docs.text: Set-up instructions for virtual machine access These instructions outline how to connect to a virtual machine (VM) for this module. Warning This process will require you to use Duo, the University’s two-factor authentication service. You will need to make sure you have enrolled with Duo with your University account and will be required to use Duo to authenticate your access to the VM. Connecting from Linux/MacOS systems Step 1: Open a terminal on your machine. Step 2: Create a directory called .ssh in your home directory (if one doesn’t already exist). $ mkdir ~/.ssh Step 3: Create a file named config within your .ssh directory. Step 4: Within this config file include the following contents ( ensure that you replace all instances of myusername with your own University username ). Host *.leeds.ac.uk !remote-access.leeds.ac.uk ProxyJump myusername@remote-access.leeds.ac.uk User "myusername" Host 20.108.153.48 ProxyJump myusername@remote-access.leeds.ac.uk Step 5: Save this file. Step 6: Run the following command in the terminal, again replacing myusername with your University username: $ ssh myusername@20.108.153.48 Step 7: You’ll then see the following message: The authenticity of host 'remote-access.leeds.ac.uk (129.11.78.165)' can't be established. ECDSA key fingerprint is SHA256:RoIHn1BDSlh/WjGHEG73OFISe6H3q3iLuM4z6tGu29Q. Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])? Step 8: Input yes and then press enter. Step 9: You will then be asked a password. Input your University password . Step 10: Once you have entered your correct password, you will receive the Duo 2 factor authentication prompt as follows: $ ssh myusername@20.108.153.48 Password: Duo two-factor login for myusername@20.108.153.48 Enter a passcode or select one of the following options: 1. Duo Push to +XX XXXX XXXXXXX 2. SMS passcodes to +XX XXXX XXXXXX (next code starts with: 2) Passcode or option (1-2): It will be simplest to just generate a passcode instead of using options 1 or 2. Therefore, use your Duo-registered device to generate a passcode and then input the passcode under the above prompt. Step 11: You will then see the following message: Warning: Permanently added '20.108.153.48' (ECDSA) to the list of known hosts. myusername@20.108.153.48's password: Step 12: This time you will need to enter your password but this is NOT your University password . Instead this is a specific password generated for you to use this VM. This specific password will have been emailed to you with a subject of ' Knowledge Representation and Reasoning - Virtual Machine Access '. Step 13: Once you have entered your password, you should then see success message of Welcome to Ubuntu . At the end you will see your command prompt now in green as: myusername@uol-ocom5201m-vm:~$ Step 14: It's recommended that you change your VM password once logged in for the first time. In order to do this type passwd and press enter. You will then be asked to enter your current password (supplied to you via email) and then enter your new chosen password. Step 15: Once you have finished with your VM session, type logout into the prompt and press enter. You’ll then be informed that the connection is closed. Step 16: When you wish to reaccess the VM, simply go to the terminal and enter ssh myusername@20.108.153.48 (replacing myusername with your own University username). You will then need to repeat steps 9,10 and 12 in order to log back into the VM. Connecting from Windows systems Info Connecting from Windows requires the installation of an SSH client. We recommend and support users using MobaXTerm. Installing MobaXTerm Step 1: Go to the MobXTerm website and select ' Download now ' under the Free download section. Step 2: Select the Portable edition . This will prompt a .zip file download. Once downloaded, extract the files in the .zip and select the .exe file. This will open up MobaXTerm. Connecting to the VM via MobaXTerm Step 1: Open MobaXTerm and in the top navigation panels, select Session . Step 2: Within the Session window, in the top navigation, select SSH . Step 3: Under the Basic SSH settings area, enter the 'Remote host' as ' 20.108.153.48 '. Select the checkbox next to 'Specify username' and then enter your University username in the associated field. Step 4: Go to the Network settings tab underneath the Basic SSH settings area. Step 5: Select the 'SSH gateway (jump)' button. Step 6: Under 'Gateway host' enter remote-access.leeds.ac.uk . Under 'Username' enter your University username . Then select 'OK'. Step 7: This will take you back to the Network settings tab. Once here, select the 'OK' button at the bottom. This will then prompt a pop-up that asks for your University password . Enter you password and select 'OK'. Note Please note that the password pop-up may take up to a few minutes to appear. Step 8: After you have correctly entered your password, you will be asked to enter a Duo passcode. Generate a Duo passcode on your Duo-registered device and enter it into the pop-up and select 'OK'. Step 9: You will then be asked if you would like to save your password for remote access. Select 'Yes'. If you do not select 'Yes' you will need to repeat this process each time you access the VM. Step 10: You will then be prompted to create a master password. This is a master password for MobaXTerm and is not related to your VM password. Create something you will be able to remember. Step 11: With the 'User session' sidebar in MobaXTerm, you should now see an item for 20.108.153.48 (myusername) . Select this item (you will need to double click). Step 12: This will open up a terminal window for the session and will require you to enter your VM password straight away. This is NOT your University password . Instead this is a specific password generated for you to use this VM. This specific password will have been emailed to you with a subject of ' Knowledge Representation and Reasoning - Virtual Machine Access '. Warning Please note that you will not be able to copy your password via Ctrl+C/Cmd+C, instead, you will need to right click and select 'Paste'. Step 13: You will then be asked if you wish to save your password for the VM accesss. Select 'yes'. Step 14: The terminal should then open a session into the VM and you will see the prompt as myusername@uol-ocom5201m-vm:~$ . Step 15: It's recommended that you change your VM password once logged in for the first time. In order to do this type passwd and press enter. You will then be asked to enter your current password (supplied to you via email) and then enter your new chosen password. Step 16: Once done with the VM, type logout into the prompt and press enter. You’ll then be informed that the connection is closed. Step 17: When revisiting MobaXTerm, select the item underneath 'User session' in the sidebar (you will need to double click). This will reopen the session into the VM. If you receive an error message when trying to reopen the session, please repeat the process from step 1 to reconnect successfully.
docs.title: VM Instructions

docs.text: About Unit 1 Learning outcomes: After completing this unit you should be able to: explain the general scope and basic historical context for the development of knowledge representation and reasoning use propositional logic for knowledge representation use Prover9 and Prolog as tools that can process formulas in propositional logic manipulate propositional formulas into a form suitable for automated reasoning by resolution.
docs.title: About Unit 1

docs.text: Learning outcomes: After completing this unit you should be able to: explain the general scope and basic historical context for the development of knowledge representation and reasoning use propositional logic for knowledge representation use Prover9 and Prolog as tools that can process formulas in propositional logic manipulate propositional formulas into a form suitable for automated reasoning by resolution.
docs.title: About Unit 1

docs.text: Lesson 1: Introducing KRR Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: explain what main topics we study identify deduction, induction, and abduction in examples understand the general historical antecedents of the topics we study. 1.1 The name KRR Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KRR) concerns the representation of knowledge in a form suitable for computational manipulation and exchange, together with the use of reasoning to process knowledge represented in this way in order to both generate new knowledge and to uncover properties of existing knowledge. Typically KRR uses techniques which originated in the mathematical study of logic and in philosophy, but the needs of AI have led to contemporary KRR diverging from these disciplines. This module deals with: The representation of knowledge, and Reasoning about knowledge using representations in formal logics. In this lesson you will be introduced to the meanings of these terms. Note The abbreviation KRR is always pronounced by saying the name of each letter individually: K R R. In computing this is like AI, which is always A I, but unlike some names such as SQL which sometimes becomes 'sequel' in speech. In the Computing literature, KRR is sometimes shortened to KR and Knowledge Representation can include reasoning in its meaning. The main academic conference on the topic is known simply as KR . 1.2 Introducing the main concepts What is inference/reasoning? Reasoning is computation with symbols representing statements, rather than with numerals. This means it is very like arithmetic. For example in binary addition (addition base 2) we have inputs combined following rules to produce an output. \[1011, 10 \leadsto 1101\] In reasoning we deal with statements like this: If it is a bank holiday then the weather is bad; it is a bank holiday; so the weather is bad By representing the statements as symbols that can be manipulated according to rules, we see processes like this: \[P \to Q, \quad P \quad \leadsto \quad Q.\] Notice the analogy with the numerical computation: two inputs are combined according to a rule to produce an output result. What is knowledge? One definition is 'things that can be known'. So, anything that could fill the gap here: ''Harry knows that …'' Another is that knowledge is a relationship between an agent and a proposition . Propositions are either true or false e.g. \(2+2=4\) , Today is sunny. More accurately: propositions are uses of sentences in specific contexts which are true or false (because of indexicals such as 'today') Questions and commands are not propositions. For instance: Close the door! Are there 26 students in the room? We don't deal with ''Harry knows how to … (play the piano)'', nor ''Harry believes that …'' (although there are logics of belief) What is representation? Representation, in the context of KRR, is modelling something in such a way that handling the model is better suited to our purpose than handling the thing itself. Representations generally abstract from what is modelled (leaving out details). They may introduce features of their own that do not correspond to anything in the modelled domain. In the well-known map of the London Underground the connections between stations are represented, but distances are not represented and directions are only sometimes roughly represented. Whether a station is North or South of the river Thames is represented, but an East-West or North-South relationship on the ground may not be indicated at all correctly. What is knowledge representation? There is no universally agreed answer, but we can make some observations It is: How to appropriately encode and utilise information in computational models of cognition. such encoding is generally explicit, symbolic, unambiguous the use and modelling is mostly engineering orientated, but sometimes aims for psychological or linguistic plausibility. Knowledge representation often involves: syntax, semantics, inference, but only syntax and inference are evident in implementation. There are many different KR languages (ways to write down or encode knowledge). some have more than one syntax (e.g. textual/graphical) different languages have different expressiveness and computational properties. 1.3 Why do we need knowledge representation? One motivation is the current awareness that we need explainable AI . That is, not just systems that can carry out complex tasks, but ones that can explain why they came to a particular decision. This can be important to avoid bias and discrimination that can arise, for example, in some machine learning situations where the data is not appropriate for the ultimate use of the system. KR can be useful to describe behaviour of sufficiently complex systems (human or otherwise) in terms of beliefs, goals, intentions, hopes. For example: the computer moved the pawn to block a threat from the white bishop even if the reason was just because of heuristic search of a game. The program diagnosed a faulty bulb because the fuse and switch are working correctly. These type of examples come from an approach known as the physical symbol system hypothesis suggested in this paper: Allen Newell and Herbert A. Simon, “Computer Science as Empirical Inquiry: Symbols and Search,” Communications of the ACM. vol. 19, No. 3, pp. 113-126, March, 1976. Available online here 1.4 KRR research at Leeds (some examples) We will return to spatial information in knowledge representation and reasoning in detail in unit 5. There have been a number of important contributions to research in this area by people working at Leeds. Some of these contributions are highlighted on this page , which links to papers on the following two research areas: The Region-Connection Calculus or RCC is a formal system of spatial relationships corresponding to ideas such as inside , touching only at the boundary , overlapping , separate from . This was developed by David Randell, Zhan Cui, and Tony Cohn , starting with initial work at Warwick University. The origins go back much further to ideas of A. N. Whitehead and others in the early part of the twentieth century. Qualitative spatial and temporal representation and reasoning (QSTR) has been used in event recognition from videos . The basic problem here is to determine what event is taking place in a video. For example, given two people engaged in some activity, can you tell if they are having an argument, sharing a meal, or playing a card game? The way QSTR is used here involves abstracting from geometrical data (bounding boxes for people and objects) in individual frames and modelling how relationships (between these bounding boxes) change over time. This enables patterns of relationships to be discovered and used as part of the mechanism to identify events. Other KRR activity at Leeds includes the following: Brandon Bennett has worked on the ontology of geographic information including projects supported by the Ordnance Survey and questions such as how to formulate definitions of concepts, for example: forest , mountain and building , in a logical way. A new project starting in 2022, led by Ian Gregory at Lancaster, involves spatial relationships in an interdisciplinary context and includes collaboration with Leeds staff John Stell and Tony Cohn , as well as other researchers at Lancaster together with Manchester, Bristol, Stanford, and IUPUI Indiana. The project, funded by ESRC and NSF, will include the use of spatial relationships in texts to represent information about narrative descriptions in language. The part of the project where Leeds is involved provides a representation of spatial relationships in historical narratives so that patterns of relationships can be discovered using KRR techniques. The historical narratives in the project include transcripts of interviews with survivors of the Holocaust, as well as nineteenth century and earlier writing about the Lake District. 1.5 Semantic technology The semantic web has not delivered all that was promised, but ontology and other semantic technologies remain a key application area for KRR. Read: Horrocks, I (2008) Ontologies and the Semantic Web Comm ACM 51( 12 ) pp58-67 December 2008 Horrocks' article raises several important issues, noted below: Data integration is a problem. Tagging with keywords is not enough and we really need to have some way to represent what the terms mean. Web search is still very limited. RDF RDF (Resource Description Framework) is a way to represent knowledge using triples. For example, knowledge that paper is made from wood, could be represented by three things in order: paper (the subject of the triple) is made from (the predicate of the triple) wood (the object of the triple) One advantage of RDF is the way a set of triples forms a graph where the nodes are labelled by things that can be subjects or objects, and the directed links are labelled by predicates. Ontology In computer science, an ontology is an engineering artifact, usually a model of (some aspect of) the world; it introduces vocabulary describing various aspects of the domain being modelled and provides an explicit specification of the intended meaning of that vocabulary (Horrocks 2008, p61). RDF (and various extensions of it) is limited as an ontology language because it has limited expressiveness . This means there are statements that it is useful to make but which are beyond what can be expressed in RDF. The Web Ontology Language OWL is a W3C standard designed for the Semantic Web, and is much more expressive than RDF. Description logics A key feature of OWL is its basis in Description Logics (DLs), a family of logic-based knowledge-representation formalisms descended from Semantic Networks and KL-ONE but that have a formal semantics based on first-order logic. These formalisms all adopt an object-oriented model like the one used by Plato and Aristotle in which the domain is described in terms of individuals, concepts (called classes in RDF), and roles (called properties in RDF) (Horrocks 2008). 1.6 Early History of KRR 1.6.1 John McCarthy 1958 John McCarthy was not merely a leading figure in KRR from the 1950s onwards, but introduced the term "Artificial Intelligence" itself. Here he writes about a proposed AI system in 1959. The quotes below are taken from Mechanisation of thought processes: proceedings of a symposium held at the National Physical Laboratory on 24 th -27 th November 1958. (1959) , in which Dr McCarthy presents his ideas, and his contemporaries at the symposium offer their responses. If you would like to read the document itself, a clearer version is also available here . ''The advice taker is a proposed program for solving problems by manipulating sentences in formal languages… … Assume I am seated at my desk at home and I wish to go to the airport. My car is at home also. The solution to the problem is to walk to the car and drive the car to the airport. First, we shall give a formal statement of the premises the advice taker uses to draw the conclusions. Then we shall discuss the heuristics which cause the advice taker to assemble these premises from the totality of facts it has available.'' But not everyone thought it would be any use at all: '' Prof. Y. Bar-Hillel: Dr. McCarthy's paper belongs in the Journal of Half-Baked Ideas … …A deductive argument, where you have first to find out what are the relevant premises, is something which many humans are not always able to carry out successfully. I do not see the slightest reason to believe, at present, machines should be able to perform things that humans find trouble in doing. I do not think there could possibly exist a program which would, given any problem, divide all the facts in the universe into those which are and those which are not relevant for the problem…" McCarthy's reply: "… I hope that once we have succeeded in making computer programs reason about the world, we will be able to reformulate epistemology as a branch of applied mathematics no more mysterious or controversial than physics…'' 1.6.2 Earlier history McCarthy's dream of reducing knowledge to mathematics is related to much earlier ideas (e.g. Leibniz' dream of reducing argument to calculation). Formal logic developed in the 19 th and 20 th centuries. Much of this later work was motivated from mathematics and philosophy rather than everyday commonsense reasoning. Boole (1847) developed an algebraic calculus for some forms of argument using statements like All Xs are Ys and Some Ys are not-Zs etc. This was mechanised by Jevons in 1869. Jevons' Logic Piano Licensed under CC 2.0 Attribution Share Alike https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:William_Stanley_Jevons_Logic_Piano.jpg But logical machines can be traced back as far as Raymond Lull (1235–1315) with a mechanical system for generating combinations of concepts. 1.7 Aspects of logics Traditionally, logic is the study of entailment (and syntax, semantics, rules of inference). Inference and other mechanisms We saw the idea that computing with propositions is analogous to computing with numbers. There are different processes; here we mostly study deduction but you should also be aware of induction (which in the sense below is not the same as the deductive mechanism called 'mathematical induction'), and abduction. Humans often use induction and abduction but it must be remembered that only in the case of deduction is there any guarantee that the conclusion is correct. However, correctness may not be the only criterion of value in some cases. It can be more useful to have a likely (but wrong) conclusion in a short time than a certainly correct answer after a very long delay. Deduction (inferring logical truths) \[\begin{array}{lll} \mathsf{All~ birds~ fly} & \qquad & \forall x \mathit{Bird}(x) \to \mathit{Flies}(x)\\ \mathsf{Tweety~ is~ a~ bird} & & \mathit{Bird}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \therefore \mathsf{Tweety~ flies} & & \mathit{Flies}(\mathit{tweety}) \end{array}\] Induction (generalising from examples) \[\begin{array}{lll} \mathsf{Tweety~ flies} & \qquad & \mathit{Flies}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \mathsf{Tweety~ is~ a~ bird} & & \mathit{Bird}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \therefore \mathsf{All~ birds~ fly} & & \forall x \mathit{Bird}(x) \to \mathit{Flies}(x) \end{array}\] Abduction (explaining observations from background theory) \[\begin{array}{lll} \mathsf{All~ birds~ fly} & \qquad & \forall x \mathit{Bird}(x) \to \mathit{Flies}(x)\\ \mathsf{Tweety~ flies} & & \mathit{Flies}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \therefore \mathsf{Tweety~ is~ a~ bird} & & \mathit{Bird}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \end{array}\] The variety of logics First order logic (FOL) has played a leading role in KR. (also called 1 st order predicate calculus). But FOL is just a starting point, and there are lots of other logics. Some of these are parts of FOL (subsets of FOL); others extend FOL with additional features (supersets of FOL). There are still other logics which are neither subsets nor supersets of FOL. Propositional logic Here the only things manipulated are propositions; things that are true or false. First order logic Here, besides statements that can be true or false, there are also individual things that the statements can refer to. There is quantification over the collection of individuals. That means you can say that all of them have some property, or that at least one of them has some property. Higher order logic This differs from the first order case by allowing quantification over sets of the individuals (second order logic), or sets of sets, or sets of sets of sets, etc of the individuals. This can make some things easier to express at the expense of being more impractical to do any computation. Modal logics (closely related to description logics) These arose traditionally in expressing statements about propositions such as necessity and possibility. For example it is necessarily the case that if Zaphod Beeblebrox has two heads then he has more than one head. But it is only possible that Douglas Adams will one day be more famous than Shakespeare. Modal logics can be used to represent concepts such as an agent knowing something, a person believing something, someone being obliged to do something, one event happening in the future or the past of something else (temporal logics), etc. Non monotonic logics In classical logic adding new facts does not make something false that was previously true. But in a lot of human situations we do this in the sense of assuming things to be true that can be rejected after getting more information. For some reason in AI this is always explained by the example of knowing that \(\mathit{Tweety}\) is a bird, then taking as true that \(\mathit{Tweety}\) flies, only later to reject that when you are introduced to \(\mathit{Tweety}\) herself and you realise you are talking to a penguin. This kind of default reasoning is not easy to model fully, but it is certainly something different from classical logic. Inference and computation A tough issue that any AI reasoning system must confront is that of tractability . A problem domain is intractable if it is not possible for a (conventional) computer program to solve it in 'reasonable' time (and with 'reasonable' use of other resources such as memory). Certain classes of logical problem are not only intractable but also undecidable. This means that there is no program that, given any instance of the problem, will in finite time either: find a solution; or terminate having determined that no solution exists. 1.8 Review of basic mathematical terminology The module uses some very basic discrete mathematics terminology. Some concepts are listed here, but for fuller explanation see any good book on the topic. Rosen's Discrete Mathematics and its Applications (2019) is one such example. Sets Remember that a set is a collection of things without any order, and in which repeated elements are not allowed. Finite sets can be written down as \(\{1, 2, 4\}\) . This is the same set as \(\{2, 1, 4\}\) . The empty set can be written \(\{ \}\) or as \(\varnothing\) . The symbol \(\in\) means 'is an element of' as in \(2 \in \{4,1,2\}\) . You should know intersection \(\cap\) and union \(\cup\) and cartesian product \(\times\) . You should know the idea of a subset , denoted \(A \subseteq B\) meaning that \(A\) is any set made entirely of elements of \(B\) , including the extreme cases of none of the elements and all of them. The difference of sets is denoted many different ways, but here by a minus sign. \(A - B\) is the set of things which are in \(A\) but not in \(B\) . In the context of a set \(U\) and a subset \(A \subseteq U\) the complement of \(A\) , sometimes written \(\overline{A}\) or \(-A\) , is the difference \(U-A\) . The notation \(\mathbb{N}\) is used for the set of natural numbers . In this module we follow the convention that zero is a natural number. In the language of set theory we can express this as \(0 \in \mathbb{N}\) . Relations Much knowledge involves binary relations. Such as: Harry is older than Sam Paper is made from wood Bradford is close to Leeds A binary relation on a set \(X\) is a set of pairs of elements of \(X\) . In other words any subset of \(X \times X\) . E.g. we might represent 'is older than' on the set \(\{\mathit{Harry}, \mathit{Sam}, \mathit{Jill}\}\) by the relation \(\{(\mathit{Harry}, \mathit{Sam}), (\mathit{Jill}, \mathit{Sam})\}\) . Diagrams with arrows are useful so that if the pair \((\mathit{Jill}, \mathit{Sam})\) is in our relation we can draw this as an arrow from \(\mathit{Jill}\) to \(\mathit{Sam}\) . Of course the direction of the arrow is important. Lists and non-binary relations A binary relation models a kind of statement, the truth of which depends on two parameters. But we may have dependence on three parameters. Birmingham is between London and Glasgow 7 is the sum of 3 and 4 A ternary relation on \(X\) is a subset of \(X^3 = X \times X \times X\) . e.g. \(\{(\mathit{Birmingham}, \mathit{London}, \mathit{Glasgow}), (\mathit{Manchester}, \mathit{Leeds}, \mathit{Liverpool})\}\) There is no generally agreed way to draw pictures of ordered triples, which is at least one reason why binary relations are easier to think about. You can think of an ordered pair as a list of length two. In general an \(n\) -tuple is a list of length \(n\) . It makes sense to allow \(n\) to be 0 or 1 sometimes. A set of lists of length 1 with elements from \(X\) is really the same as (that means 'is best thought of as being') a subset of \(X\) . We can think of this as modelling a property that elements of \(X\) may have. \[\{\mathit{Orange}, \mathit{Lemon}\} \subseteq \{\mathit{Orange}, \mathit{Apple}, \mathit{Lemon}, \mathit{Banana}\}\] This might be modelling the fact that oranges and lemons are citrus fruits and apples and bananas are not. A set of lists of length \(0\) is either \(\{\}\) or \(\{()\}\) . Either the empty set (no lists) or the set containing the unique list of length zero (the empty list). We can think of these two sets as being the truth values false and true respectively. A \(0\) -ary relation models (can model) something that is true or false (a proposition). The set of all lists of length \(n\) made out of elements of a set \(A\) is often denoted \(A^n\) where this means the \(n\) -fold cartesian product. \[A^n = \overbrace{A \times A \times \cdots \times A}^n\] Functions These are also called maps, mappings, and various other things. Writing \(f: A \to B\) indicates that \(f\) is a function which given any \(a\in A\) gives an element of \(B\) which is denoted \(f(a)\) . This can be thought of as a special kind of relation; a subset of \(A \times B\) where every \(a \in A\) occurs in exactly one pair in the subset. Pictorially, drawing a relation as a collection of arrows connecting things from one set to another, a function has exactly one arrow leaving every element of the first set. There is no requirement that the elements of the second set have any particular number of arrows coming in to each of them. If there is at least one inward arrow for every element of the second set then the function is surjective . If there is at most one it is injective . If a function is both injective and surjective it is said to be bijective .
docs.title: Lesson 1 Introducing KRR

docs.text: Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: explain what main topics we study identify deduction, induction, and abduction in examples understand the general historical antecedents of the topics we study.
docs.title: Lesson 1: Introducing KRR

docs.text: Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KRR) concerns the representation of knowledge in a form suitable for computational manipulation and exchange, together with the use of reasoning to process knowledge represented in this way in order to both generate new knowledge and to uncover properties of existing knowledge. Typically KRR uses techniques which originated in the mathematical study of logic and in philosophy, but the needs of AI have led to contemporary KRR diverging from these disciplines. This module deals with: The representation of knowledge, and Reasoning about knowledge using representations in formal logics. In this lesson you will be introduced to the meanings of these terms. Note The abbreviation KRR is always pronounced by saying the name of each letter individually: K R R. In computing this is like AI, which is always A I, but unlike some names such as SQL which sometimes becomes 'sequel' in speech. In the Computing literature, KRR is sometimes shortened to KR and Knowledge Representation can include reasoning in its meaning. The main academic conference on the topic is known simply as KR .
docs.title: 1.1 The name KRR

docs.text: Reasoning is computation with symbols representing statements, rather than with numerals. This means it is very like arithmetic. For example in binary addition (addition base 2) we have inputs combined following rules to produce an output. \[1011, 10 \leadsto 1101\] In reasoning we deal with statements like this: If it is a bank holiday then the weather is bad; it is a bank holiday; so the weather is bad By representing the statements as symbols that can be manipulated according to rules, we see processes like this: \[P \to Q, \quad P \quad \leadsto \quad Q.\] Notice the analogy with the numerical computation: two inputs are combined according to a rule to produce an output result.
docs.title: What is inference/reasoning?

docs.text: One definition is 'things that can be known'. So, anything that could fill the gap here: ''Harry knows that …'' Another is that knowledge is a relationship between an agent and a proposition . Propositions are either true or false e.g. \(2+2=4\) , Today is sunny. More accurately: propositions are uses of sentences in specific contexts which are true or false (because of indexicals such as 'today') Questions and commands are not propositions. For instance: Close the door! Are there 26 students in the room? We don't deal with ''Harry knows how to … (play the piano)'', nor ''Harry believes that …'' (although there are logics of belief)
docs.title: What is knowledge?

docs.text: Representation, in the context of KRR, is modelling something in such a way that handling the model is better suited to our purpose than handling the thing itself. Representations generally abstract from what is modelled (leaving out details). They may introduce features of their own that do not correspond to anything in the modelled domain. In the well-known map of the London Underground the connections between stations are represented, but distances are not represented and directions are only sometimes roughly represented. Whether a station is North or South of the river Thames is represented, but an East-West or North-South relationship on the ground may not be indicated at all correctly.
docs.title: What is representation?

docs.text: There is no universally agreed answer, but we can make some observations It is: How to appropriately encode and utilise information in computational models of cognition. such encoding is generally explicit, symbolic, unambiguous the use and modelling is mostly engineering orientated, but sometimes aims for psychological or linguistic plausibility. Knowledge representation often involves: syntax, semantics, inference, but only syntax and inference are evident in implementation. There are many different KR languages (ways to write down or encode knowledge). some have more than one syntax (e.g. textual/graphical) different languages have different expressiveness and computational properties.
docs.title: What is knowledge representation?

docs.text: One motivation is the current awareness that we need explainable AI . That is, not just systems that can carry out complex tasks, but ones that can explain why they came to a particular decision. This can be important to avoid bias and discrimination that can arise, for example, in some machine learning situations where the data is not appropriate for the ultimate use of the system. KR can be useful to describe behaviour of sufficiently complex systems (human or otherwise) in terms of beliefs, goals, intentions, hopes. For example: the computer moved the pawn to block a threat from the white bishop even if the reason was just because of heuristic search of a game. The program diagnosed a faulty bulb because the fuse and switch are working correctly. These type of examples come from an approach known as the physical symbol system hypothesis suggested in this paper: Allen Newell and Herbert A. Simon, “Computer Science as Empirical Inquiry: Symbols and Search,” Communications of the ACM. vol. 19, No. 3, pp. 113-126, March, 1976. Available online here
docs.title: 1.3 Why do we need knowledge representation?

docs.text: We will return to spatial information in knowledge representation and reasoning in detail in unit 5. There have been a number of important contributions to research in this area by people working at Leeds. Some of these contributions are highlighted on this page , which links to papers on the following two research areas: The Region-Connection Calculus or RCC is a formal system of spatial relationships corresponding to ideas such as inside , touching only at the boundary , overlapping , separate from . This was developed by David Randell, Zhan Cui, and Tony Cohn , starting with initial work at Warwick University. The origins go back much further to ideas of A. N. Whitehead and others in the early part of the twentieth century. Qualitative spatial and temporal representation and reasoning (QSTR) has been used in event recognition from videos . The basic problem here is to determine what event is taking place in a video. For example, given two people engaged in some activity, can you tell if they are having an argument, sharing a meal, or playing a card game? The way QSTR is used here involves abstracting from geometrical data (bounding boxes for people and objects) in individual frames and modelling how relationships (between these bounding boxes) change over time. This enables patterns of relationships to be discovered and used as part of the mechanism to identify events. Other KRR activity at Leeds includes the following: Brandon Bennett has worked on the ontology of geographic information including projects supported by the Ordnance Survey and questions such as how to formulate definitions of concepts, for example: forest , mountain and building , in a logical way. A new project starting in 2022, led by Ian Gregory at Lancaster, involves spatial relationships in an interdisciplinary context and includes collaboration with Leeds staff John Stell and Tony Cohn , as well as other researchers at Lancaster together with Manchester, Bristol, Stanford, and IUPUI Indiana. The project, funded by ESRC and NSF, will include the use of spatial relationships in texts to represent information about narrative descriptions in language. The part of the project where Leeds is involved provides a representation of spatial relationships in historical narratives so that patterns of relationships can be discovered using KRR techniques. The historical narratives in the project include transcripts of interviews with survivors of the Holocaust, as well as nineteenth century and earlier writing about the Lake District.
docs.title: 1.4 KRR research at Leeds (some examples)

docs.text: The semantic web has not delivered all that was promised, but ontology and other semantic technologies remain a key application area for KRR. Read: Horrocks, I (2008) Ontologies and the Semantic Web Comm ACM 51( 12 ) pp58-67 December 2008 Horrocks' article raises several important issues, noted below: Data integration is a problem. Tagging with keywords is not enough and we really need to have some way to represent what the terms mean. Web search is still very limited.
docs.title: 1.5 Semantic technology

docs.text: RDF (Resource Description Framework) is a way to represent knowledge using triples. For example, knowledge that paper is made from wood, could be represented by three things in order: paper (the subject of the triple) is made from (the predicate of the triple) wood (the object of the triple) One advantage of RDF is the way a set of triples forms a graph where the nodes are labelled by things that can be subjects or objects, and the directed links are labelled by predicates.
docs.title: RDF

docs.text: In computer science, an ontology is an engineering artifact, usually a model of (some aspect of) the world; it introduces vocabulary describing various aspects of the domain being modelled and provides an explicit specification of the intended meaning of that vocabulary (Horrocks 2008, p61). RDF (and various extensions of it) is limited as an ontology language because it has limited expressiveness . This means there are statements that it is useful to make but which are beyond what can be expressed in RDF. The Web Ontology Language OWL is a W3C standard designed for the Semantic Web, and is much more expressive than RDF.
docs.title: Ontology

docs.text: A key feature of OWL is its basis in Description Logics (DLs), a family of logic-based knowledge-representation formalisms descended from Semantic Networks and KL-ONE but that have a formal semantics based on first-order logic. These formalisms all adopt an object-oriented model like the one used by Plato and Aristotle in which the domain is described in terms of individuals, concepts (called classes in RDF), and roles (called properties in RDF) (Horrocks 2008).
docs.title: Description logics

docs.text: John McCarthy was not merely a leading figure in KRR from the 1950s onwards, but introduced the term "Artificial Intelligence" itself. Here he writes about a proposed AI system in 1959. The quotes below are taken from Mechanisation of thought processes: proceedings of a symposium held at the National Physical Laboratory on 24 th -27 th November 1958. (1959) , in which Dr McCarthy presents his ideas, and his contemporaries at the symposium offer their responses. If you would like to read the document itself, a clearer version is also available here . ''The advice taker is a proposed program for solving problems by manipulating sentences in formal languages… … Assume I am seated at my desk at home and I wish to go to the airport. My car is at home also. The solution to the problem is to walk to the car and drive the car to the airport. First, we shall give a formal statement of the premises the advice taker uses to draw the conclusions. Then we shall discuss the heuristics which cause the advice taker to assemble these premises from the totality of facts it has available.'' But not everyone thought it would be any use at all: '' Prof. Y. Bar-Hillel: Dr. McCarthy's paper belongs in the Journal of Half-Baked Ideas … …A deductive argument, where you have first to find out what are the relevant premises, is something which many humans are not always able to carry out successfully. I do not see the slightest reason to believe, at present, machines should be able to perform things that humans find trouble in doing. I do not think there could possibly exist a program which would, given any problem, divide all the facts in the universe into those which are and those which are not relevant for the problem…" McCarthy's reply: "… I hope that once we have succeeded in making computer programs reason about the world, we will be able to reformulate epistemology as a branch of applied mathematics no more mysterious or controversial than physics…''
docs.title: 1.6.1 John McCarthy 1958

docs.text: McCarthy's dream of reducing knowledge to mathematics is related to much earlier ideas (e.g. Leibniz' dream of reducing argument to calculation). Formal logic developed in the 19 th and 20 th centuries. Much of this later work was motivated from mathematics and philosophy rather than everyday commonsense reasoning. Boole (1847) developed an algebraic calculus for some forms of argument using statements like All Xs are Ys and Some Ys are not-Zs etc. This was mechanised by Jevons in 1869. Jevons' Logic Piano Licensed under CC 2.0 Attribution Share Alike https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:William_Stanley_Jevons_Logic_Piano.jpg But logical machines can be traced back as far as Raymond Lull (1235–1315) with a mechanical system for generating combinations of concepts.
docs.title: 1.6.2 Earlier history

docs.text: Traditionally, logic is the study of entailment (and syntax, semantics, rules of inference).
docs.title: 1.7 Aspects of  logics

docs.text: We saw the idea that computing with propositions is analogous to computing with numbers. There are different processes; here we mostly study deduction but you should also be aware of induction (which in the sense below is not the same as the deductive mechanism called 'mathematical induction'), and abduction. Humans often use induction and abduction but it must be remembered that only in the case of deduction is there any guarantee that the conclusion is correct. However, correctness may not be the only criterion of value in some cases. It can be more useful to have a likely (but wrong) conclusion in a short time than a certainly correct answer after a very long delay. Deduction (inferring logical truths) \[\begin{array}{lll} \mathsf{All~ birds~ fly} & \qquad & \forall x \mathit{Bird}(x) \to \mathit{Flies}(x)\\ \mathsf{Tweety~ is~ a~ bird} & & \mathit{Bird}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \therefore \mathsf{Tweety~ flies} & & \mathit{Flies}(\mathit{tweety}) \end{array}\] Induction (generalising from examples) \[\begin{array}{lll} \mathsf{Tweety~ flies} & \qquad & \mathit{Flies}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \mathsf{Tweety~ is~ a~ bird} & & \mathit{Bird}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \therefore \mathsf{All~ birds~ fly} & & \forall x \mathit{Bird}(x) \to \mathit{Flies}(x) \end{array}\] Abduction (explaining observations from background theory) \[\begin{array}{lll} \mathsf{All~ birds~ fly} & \qquad & \forall x \mathit{Bird}(x) \to \mathit{Flies}(x)\\ \mathsf{Tweety~ flies} & & \mathit{Flies}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \therefore \mathsf{Tweety~ is~ a~ bird} & & \mathit{Bird}(\mathit{tweety})\\ \end{array}\]
docs.title: Inference and other mechanisms

docs.text: First order logic (FOL) has played a leading role in KR. (also called 1 st order predicate calculus). But FOL is just a starting point, and there are lots of other logics. Some of these are parts of FOL (subsets of FOL); others extend FOL with additional features (supersets of FOL). There are still other logics which are neither subsets nor supersets of FOL. Propositional logic Here the only things manipulated are propositions; things that are true or false. First order logic Here, besides statements that can be true or false, there are also individual things that the statements can refer to. There is quantification over the collection of individuals. That means you can say that all of them have some property, or that at least one of them has some property. Higher order logic This differs from the first order case by allowing quantification over sets of the individuals (second order logic), or sets of sets, or sets of sets of sets, etc of the individuals. This can make some things easier to express at the expense of being more impractical to do any computation. Modal logics (closely related to description logics) These arose traditionally in expressing statements about propositions such as necessity and possibility. For example it is necessarily the case that if Zaphod Beeblebrox has two heads then he has more than one head. But it is only possible that Douglas Adams will one day be more famous than Shakespeare. Modal logics can be used to represent concepts such as an agent knowing something, a person believing something, someone being obliged to do something, one event happening in the future or the past of something else (temporal logics), etc. Non monotonic logics In classical logic adding new facts does not make something false that was previously true. But in a lot of human situations we do this in the sense of assuming things to be true that can be rejected after getting more information. For some reason in AI this is always explained by the example of knowing that \(\mathit{Tweety}\) is a bird, then taking as true that \(\mathit{Tweety}\) flies, only later to reject that when you are introduced to \(\mathit{Tweety}\) herself and you realise you are talking to a penguin. This kind of default reasoning is not easy to model fully, but it is certainly something different from classical logic.
docs.title: The variety of logics

docs.text: A tough issue that any AI reasoning system must confront is that of tractability . A problem domain is intractable if it is not possible for a (conventional) computer program to solve it in 'reasonable' time (and with 'reasonable' use of other resources such as memory). Certain classes of logical problem are not only intractable but also undecidable. This means that there is no program that, given any instance of the problem, will in finite time either: find a solution; or terminate having determined that no solution exists.
docs.title: Inference and computation

docs.text: The module uses some very basic discrete mathematics terminology. Some concepts are listed here, but for fuller explanation see any good book on the topic. Rosen's Discrete Mathematics and its Applications (2019) is one such example.
docs.title: 1.8 Review of basic mathematical terminology

docs.text: Remember that a set is a collection of things without any order, and in which repeated elements are not allowed. Finite sets can be written down as \(\{1, 2, 4\}\) . This is the same set as \(\{2, 1, 4\}\) . The empty set can be written \(\{ \}\) or as \(\varnothing\) . The symbol \(\in\) means 'is an element of' as in \(2 \in \{4,1,2\}\) . You should know intersection \(\cap\) and union \(\cup\) and cartesian product \(\times\) . You should know the idea of a subset , denoted \(A \subseteq B\) meaning that \(A\) is any set made entirely of elements of \(B\) , including the extreme cases of none of the elements and all of them. The difference of sets is denoted many different ways, but here by a minus sign. \(A - B\) is the set of things which are in \(A\) but not in \(B\) . In the context of a set \(U\) and a subset \(A \subseteq U\) the complement of \(A\) , sometimes written \(\overline{A}\) or \(-A\) , is the difference \(U-A\) . The notation \(\mathbb{N}\) is used for the set of natural numbers . In this module we follow the convention that zero is a natural number. In the language of set theory we can express this as \(0 \in \mathbb{N}\) .
docs.title: Sets

docs.text: Much knowledge involves binary relations. Such as: Harry is older than Sam Paper is made from wood Bradford is close to Leeds A binary relation on a set \(X\) is a set of pairs of elements of \(X\) . In other words any subset of \(X \times X\) . E.g. we might represent 'is older than' on the set \(\{\mathit{Harry}, \mathit{Sam}, \mathit{Jill}\}\) by the relation \(\{(\mathit{Harry}, \mathit{Sam}), (\mathit{Jill}, \mathit{Sam})\}\) . Diagrams with arrows are useful so that if the pair \((\mathit{Jill}, \mathit{Sam})\) is in our relation we can draw this as an arrow from \(\mathit{Jill}\) to \(\mathit{Sam}\) . Of course the direction of the arrow is important.
docs.title: Relations

docs.text: A binary relation models a kind of statement, the truth of which depends on two parameters. But we may have dependence on three parameters. Birmingham is between London and Glasgow 7 is the sum of 3 and 4 A ternary relation on \(X\) is a subset of \(X^3 = X \times X \times X\) . e.g. \(\{(\mathit{Birmingham}, \mathit{London}, \mathit{Glasgow}), (\mathit{Manchester}, \mathit{Leeds}, \mathit{Liverpool})\}\) There is no generally agreed way to draw pictures of ordered triples, which is at least one reason why binary relations are easier to think about. You can think of an ordered pair as a list of length two. In general an \(n\) -tuple is a list of length \(n\) . It makes sense to allow \(n\) to be 0 or 1 sometimes. A set of lists of length 1 with elements from \(X\) is really the same as (that means 'is best thought of as being') a subset of \(X\) . We can think of this as modelling a property that elements of \(X\) may have. \[\{\mathit{Orange}, \mathit{Lemon}\} \subseteq \{\mathit{Orange}, \mathit{Apple}, \mathit{Lemon}, \mathit{Banana}\}\] This might be modelling the fact that oranges and lemons are citrus fruits and apples and bananas are not. A set of lists of length \(0\) is either \(\{\}\) or \(\{()\}\) . Either the empty set (no lists) or the set containing the unique list of length zero (the empty list). We can think of these two sets as being the truth values false and true respectively. A \(0\) -ary relation models (can model) something that is true or false (a proposition). The set of all lists of length \(n\) made out of elements of a set \(A\) is often denoted \(A^n\) where this means the \(n\) -fold cartesian product. \[A^n = \overbrace{A \times A \times \cdots \times A}^n\]
docs.title: Lists and non-binary relations

docs.text: These are also called maps, mappings, and various other things. Writing \(f: A \to B\) indicates that \(f\) is a function which given any \(a\in A\) gives an element of \(B\) which is denoted \(f(a)\) . This can be thought of as a special kind of relation; a subset of \(A \times B\) where every \(a \in A\) occurs in exactly one pair in the subset. Pictorially, drawing a relation as a collection of arrows connecting things from one set to another, a function has exactly one arrow leaving every element of the first set. There is no requirement that the elements of the second set have any particular number of arrows coming in to each of them. If there is at least one inward arrow for every element of the second set then the function is surjective . If there is at most one it is injective . If a function is both injective and surjective it is said to be bijective .
docs.title: Functions

docs.text:
Lesson 2: Propositional logic This may be revision if you are familiar with this material. It is included here to make sure the notation and terminology is clear as some of this may be different from what you know. Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: use truth tables to determine whether a statement in propositional logic is a tautology, a contradiction, or a contingency understand the syntax of propositional logic and be able to spot mistakes. Propositions A proposition is a statement that (perhaps in some context) is either true or false (even if no one knows which). Examples and non-examples Today is Monday is a proposition (if we fix when Today is). The room is empty is a proposition (if we know which room). Leeds is in England is a proposition (assuming we agree on the meaning of the places mentioned). Leeds is bigger than London is a proposition. Being a proposition has nothing to do with whether it is true or false Is Leeds in China? is not a proposition, it's a question. \(x = 1\) is a proposition (if we know what \(x\) is). Let \(x =1\) is not a proposition; it's a request or an instruction. x := 1 (in a programming language) is not a proposition; it is a command or an instruction to do something A compound proposition is a statement that consists of simpler propositions combined together. For example, The room is empty and the window is broken is made by combining The room is empty with the window is broken . Formal propositional logic models combinations like this. In classical logic the combinations behave in a truth functional way. This means that the truth of a compound proposition depends only on what combination is used and on the truth of the component parts. Syntax Formal logic has a precise syntax, just like any programming language. Propositional logic builds statements out of: Letters (or words) for atomic propositions, often called propositional variables or just variables . The logical connectives \(\wedge\) (and), \(\vee\) (or), \(
eg\) (not), \(	o\) (implies / if – then –), \(\leftrightarrow\) (iff / – if and only if –). Parentheses '(' and ')' to avoid ambiguity. The statements in the logic are sometimes called formulas or formulae . These are both acceptable plurals of formula . Sometimes, instead of 'formula', people say well formed formula or wff (which you can pronounce 'woof' as in the sound made by a dog) to emphasise that there are strings of symbols, such as \[p\; \vee\; 	o \;q \;r \;
eg \qquad 	ext{ or } \qquad (\;(\;p\;(\;q\;(\;(\;\wedge\;
eg \] which are (in programming terms) 'syntax errors' and are not 'well formed'. The use of single letters for propositional variables saves a lot of effort, but can lead to less intelligible expressions. In computer science, the importance of meaningful identifiers in programming is well known. So, we may sometimes write logical expressions like this: \[\mathit{theSunIsShiningToday} 	o (
eg \mathit{itIsRaining} \vee \mathit{itIsWarmOutsideToday})\] rather than just \(s 	o (
eg r \vee w)\) . It is important not to be misled by imagining that using meaningful names for propositions makes any difference to the logical content. The meaningful names are very useful for humans, but the two logical expressions above would be treated in the same way when processed by software dealing with logic. Precedence conventions To avoid using too many parentheses we can use conventions about precedence of logical connectives. In the following table a lower number means insert parentheses earlier. \[  egin{array}{lcccccc} \hline \mathsf{Operator} & 
eg & \wedge & \vee & 	o & \leftrightarrow\ \mathsf{Precedence} & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5\ \hline \end{array} \] Examples \(
eg p \vee q \wedge r 	o s\) means \(((
eg p) \vee (q \wedge r)) 	o s\) \(p \wedge 
eg q 	o r \vee s \wedge t\) means \((p \wedge (
eg q)) 	o (r \vee (s \wedge t))\) \(p 	o 
eg q \leftrightarrow r \wedge s \vee t\) means \((p 	o (
eg q)) \leftrightarrow ((r \wedge s) \vee t)\) It is often assumed that binary operators associate to the left so that for example \(p 	o q 	o r\) means \((p 	o q) 	o r\) not \(p 	o (q 	o r)\) \(p \wedge q \wedge r\) means \((p \wedge q) \wedge r\) not \(p \wedge (q \wedge r)\) . Rather than use all the precedence rules all the time you can add more parentheses than strictly necessary, as follows. Suggested use of parentheses and precedence \(
eg\) has highest precedence among propositional connectives \(\vee\) and \(\wedge\) come next, but put in parentheses to distinguish \((p \vee q) \wedge r\) and \(p \vee (q \wedge r)\) , \(	o\) and \(\leftrightarrow\) come next, but put in parentheses to distinguish \((p 	o q) \leftrightarrow r\) and \(p 	o (q \leftrightarrow r)\) put in parentheses to distinguish \((p 	o q) 	o r\) and \(p 	o (q 	o r)\) ; and similarly for all the binary connectives, so that if the difference between \((p \vee q) \vee r\) and \(p \vee (q \vee r)\) is important then write them in this way. Semantics In classical propositional logic the meaning of the connectives is defined by truth tables as follows. You should have seen these before and be familiar with the process of working out a truth table for any compound proposition. Beware: logic can differ from everyday discourse From the knowledge representation perspective, some key points to note about the connectives are: \(\vee\) means inclusive or . The formula \(p \vee q\) is true when both \(p\) and \(q\) are true, and also when exactly one is true. If you need to say ' \(p\) or \(q\) , but not both' then you have to say explicitly, such as one of the following. \[ (p \vee q) \wedge 
eg(p \wedge q) \qquad \qquad (p \wedge 
eg q) \vee (q \wedge 
eg p)\] \(\wedge\) means and but sometimes the place we include the word in statements can be confusing. In the sentence "I bought an apple and an orange", the part "I bought an apple" is a proposition, but "an orange" (which comes after the connecting "and") is not a proposition. In the example the statement is essentially a short form of "I bought an apple and I bought an orange" in which you clearly see two propositions connected together. \(	o\) means material implication . The truth of \(p 	o q\) has nothing at all to do with whether \(p\) causes \(q\) or whether there is any relationship between what the propositions represent. The expression gets its truth value only from the separate individual truth values of \(p\) and of \(q\) . This can be confusing because in everyday speech we can use 'if — then —' in other ways. For example, if we model the statement 'if I want to speak Russian then I can speak Russian' in propositional logic as \(w 	o s\) where the statement \(w\) : 'I want to speak Russian' is false, then the statement as a whole is true. This is the case whether or not \(s\) : 'I can speak Russian' holds. Yet, in an everyday situation, someone saying 'if I want to speak Russian then I can speak Russian' who was completely unable to speak Russian at all, might not be regarded as being truthful. We will return to this issue when we discuss representation in more detail. The fact that classical propositional logic has these features does not mean it is fundamentally 'wrong'. It is a tool which is capable of representing certain aspects of the world but in the process ignores other aspects. This is found in any kind of modelling process. There are ways to model aspects of the language if — then — that are ignored in classical propositional logic, such as relevance logic , but these are complex and are beyond the scope of this module. Truth tables for connectives The truth tables are listed here, but you should be familiar with these already. \[  egin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p \wedge q\ \hline T & T & T \ T & F & F \ F & T & F \ F & F & F \ \hline \end{array} \qquad \qquad  egin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p \vee q\ \hline T & T & T \ T & F & T \ F & T & T \ F & F & F \ \hline \end{array} \qquad \qquad  egin{array}{|c|c|} \hline p & 
eg p\ \hline T & F \ F & T \ \hline \end{array}\] \[ egin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p 	o q\ \hline T & T & T \ T & F & F \ F & T & T \ F & F & T \ \hline \end{array} \qquad  egin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p \leftrightarrow q\ \hline T & T & T \ T & F & F \ F & T & F \ F & F & T \ \hline \end{array} \] Types of proposition and relationships between propositions These types of proposition are important: tautology : This means a proposition that is true under all values given to the variables it contains. For example \(p \vee 
eg p\) is a tautology. However, \(p \wedge 
eg p\) is not a tautology, and neither is \(p\) . contingency : This means a proposition that is true overall when its variables are given some truth values, but false in other cases. For example \(p \vee q\) is a contingency. However neither \(p \vee 
eg p\) nor \(p \wedge 
eg p\) is a contingency. contradiction : This means a proposition that is false overall whatever truth values its variables are given. For example \(p \wedge 
eg p\) is a contradiction. A proposition is said to be satisfiable when there is some choice of values for the variables that makes it true. All tautologies and all contingencies are satisfiable. A proposition which is not satisfiable is said to be unsatisfiable . Besides propositions individually being of a certain type, we also need to talk about relationships between propositions. We say that a proposition \(\alpha\) logically implies a proposition \( eta\) if whenever \(\alpha\) evaluates to true, then \( eta\) does so as well. When this happens we write \(\alpha \vDash  eta\) . Logical implication is a relationship between propositions, not a new way of constructing propositions. It is similar to the situation when discussing different natural languages. Someone talking in German about two English statements could be describing a relationship between English statements but they would be making a statement in German and not in English. The symbol \(\vDash\) is sometimes called a double turnstile when people talk about the symbol itself rather than what it means. A turnstile in non-mathematical contexts is a kind of revolving gate to control a flow of people. In most traditional forms a turnstile revolves about a vertical axis, like the single vertical line in the symbol. When we get to proof we also meet the single turnstile , \(\vdash\) . Either of these symbols might just be called turnstile if it causes no ambiguity and, like of lot of mathematical symbols, there is no consistency about how different people may use them in writing about logic. Sometimes the relationship of logical implication is called semantic consequence . This is because it depends on the semantics of the statements involved, which in the context of propositional logic means how the truth values of propositions are related. This is different from the notion of proof or syntactic consequence where relationships depend on the syntactic structure of the formulas. In reading out the formula \(P \vDash Q\) you can say ' \(P\) logically implies \(Q\) ' or ' \(P\) entails \(Q\) ' . An example of someone talking about the \(\vDash\) symbol as opposed to using it would be 'I am using the double turnstile to denote logical implication' . For example, the following logical implications hold: \[ 
eg p \vDash p 	o q, \qquad p \wedge q \vDash q \vee 
eg (p \wedge r), \qquad p \wedge 
eg p \vDash q. \] Note in the last example that the statement to the left of the \(\vDash\) symbol is never true. Logical implication is only claiming that when the statement on the left is true, then the statement on the right is true. You may find it helpful to think of this another way: \(\alpha \vDash  eta\) means that it is impossible for \(\alpha\) to be true for some values of the propositional variables and for \( eta\) to be false for those same values. More generally, a logical implication can involve any number of formulas on the left. So that \(\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n \vDash  eta\) asserts that whenever all of the \(\alpha_i\) are true then \( eta\) is true too. You may find it helpful to think of this another way: \(\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n \vDash  eta\) means that it is impossible for all of the \(\alpha_i\) to be true for some values of the propositional variables and for \( eta\) to be false for those same values. For example the following logical implication holds. You can check this either by drawing up truth tables, or by thinking of all the possible ways to make all of the formulas on the left come out true, and making sure that the formula on the right is true in every one of these cases. \[ P 	o (Q \vee 
eg R),\; P \wedge R,\; Q 	o S \vDash Q \wedge S \] In the special case that there are no formulas on the left, writing \({ }\vDash  eta\) just means that \( eta\) is a tautology. Truth tables provide a mechanical process Truth tables provide a finite procedure which for any formula will say whether it is a tautology or not. More generally, given any claimed logical implication \[\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n \vDash  eta\] whether this holds or not can be determined automatically: simply draw up a truth table to find out for what values of the variables involved all of the formulas on the left evaluate to true, and then see whether or not the formula, \( eta\) on the left is true in all of these cases. Propositional logic is very different from other logics that we meet later in that there are only a finite number of ways of interpreting the symbols in any formula. The propositional variables can only be true or false, so if there are \(n\) different variables there are \(2^n\) different interpretations for the formula. Each of these interpretations corresponds to a line in the truth table. Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 2, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise about the nature of propositions. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit-1 Lesson 2' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.
docs.title: Lesson 2 Propositional logic

docs.text: This may be revision if you are familiar with this material. It is included here to make sure the notation and terminology is clear as some of this may be different from what you know. Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: use truth tables to determine whether a statement in propositional logic is a tautology, a contradiction, or a contingency understand the syntax of propositional logic and be able to spot mistakes.
docs.title: Lesson 2: Propositional logic

docs.text: A proposition is a statement that (perhaps in some context) is either true or false (even if no one knows which). Examples and non-examples Today is Monday is a proposition (if we fix when Today is). The room is empty is a proposition (if we know which room). Leeds is in England is a proposition (assuming we agree on the meaning of the places mentioned). Leeds is bigger than London is a proposition. Being a proposition has nothing to do with whether it is true or false Is Leeds in China? is not a proposition, it's a question. \(x = 1\) is a proposition (if we know what \(x\) is). Let \(x =1\) is not a proposition; it's a request or an instruction. x := 1 (in a programming language) is not a proposition; it is a command or an instruction to do something A compound proposition is a statement that consists of simpler propositions combined together. For example, The room is empty and the window is broken is made by combining The room is empty with the window is broken . Formal propositional logic models combinations like this. In classical logic the combinations behave in a truth functional way. This means that the truth of a compound proposition depends only on what combination is used and on the truth of the component parts.
docs.title: Propositions
Syntax

docs.text:
To avoid using too many parentheses we can use conventions about precedence of logical connectives. In the following table a lower number means insert parentheses earlier. \[  egin{array}{lcccccc} \hline \mathsf{Operator} & 
eg & \wedge & \vee & 	o & \leftrightarrow\ \mathsf{Precedence} & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5\ \hline \end{array} \] Examples \(
eg p \vee q \wedge r 	o s\) means \(((
eg p) \vee (q \wedge r)) 	o s\) \(p \wedge 
eg q 	o r \vee s \wedge t\) means \((p \wedge (
eg q)) 	o (r \vee (s \wedge t))\) \(p 	o 
eg q \leftrightarrow r \wedge s \vee t\) means \((p 	o (
eg q)) \leftrightarrow ((r \wedge s) \vee t)\) It is often assumed that binary operators associate to the left so that for example \(p 	o q 	o r\) means \((p 	o q) 	o r\) not \(p 	o (q 	o r)\) \(p \wedge q \wedge r\) means \((p \wedge q) \wedge r\) not \(p \wedge (q \wedge r)\) . Rather than use all the precedence rules all the time you can add more parentheses than strictly necessary, as follows. Suggested use of parentheses and precedence \(
eg\) has highest precedence among propositional connectives \(\vee\) and \(\wedge\) come next, but put in parentheses to distinguish \((p \vee q) \wedge r\) and \(p \vee (q \wedge r)\) , \(	o\) and \(\leftrightarrow\) come next, but put in parentheses to distinguish \((p 	o q) \leftrightarrow r\) and \(p 	o (q \leftrightarrow r)\) put in parentheses to distinguish \((p 	o q) 	o r\) and \(p 	o (q 	o r)\) ; and similarly for all the binary connectives, so that if the difference between \((p \vee q) \vee r\) and \(p \vee (q \vee r)\) is important then write them in this way.
docs.title: Precedence conventions

docs.text: In classical propositional logic the meaning of the connectives is defined by truth tables as follows. You should have seen these before and be familiar with the process of working out a truth table for any compound proposition.
docs.title: Semantics

docs.text:
From the knowledge representation perspective, some key points to note about the connectives are: \(\vee\) means inclusive or . The formula \(p \vee q\) is true when both \(p\) and \(q\) are true, and also when exactly one is true. If you need to say ' \(p\) or \(q\) , but not both' then you have to say explicitly, such as one of the following. \[ (p \vee q) \wedge 
eg(p \wedge q) \qquad \qquad (p \wedge 
eg q) \vee (q \wedge 
eg p)\] \(\wedge\) means and but sometimes the place we include the word in statements can be confusing. In the sentence "I bought an apple and an orange", the part "I bought an apple" is a proposition, but "an orange" (which comes after the connecting "and") is not a proposition. In the example the statement is essentially a short form of "I bought an apple and I bought an orange" in which you clearly see two propositions connected together. \(	o\) means material implication . The truth of \(p 	o q\) has nothing at all to do with whether \(p\) causes \(q\) or whether there is any relationship between what the propositions represent. The expression gets its truth value only from the separate individual truth values of \(p\) and of \(q\) . This can be confusing because in everyday speech we can use 'if — then —' in other ways. For example, if we model the statement 'if I want to speak Russian then I can speak Russian' in propositional logic as \(w 	o s\) where the statement \(w\) : 'I want to speak Russian' is false, then the statement as a whole is true. This is the case whether or not \(s\) : 'I can speak Russian' holds. Yet, in an everyday situation, someone saying 'if I want to speak Russian then I can speak Russian' who was completely unable to speak Russian at all, might not be regarded as being truthful. We will return to this issue when we discuss representation in more detail. The fact that classical propositional logic has these features does not mean it is fundamentally 'wrong'. It is a tool which is capable of representing certain aspects of the world but in the process ignores other aspects. This is found in any kind of modelling process. There are ways to model aspects of the language if — then — that are ignored in classical propositional logic, such as relevance logic , but these are complex and are beyond the scope of this module.
docs.title: Beware: logic can differ from everyday discourse

docs.text: The truth tables are listed here, but you should be familiar with these already. \[ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p \wedge q\\ \hline T & T & T \\ T & F & F \\ F & T & F \\ F & F & F \\ \hline \end{array} \qquad \qquad \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p \vee q\\ \hline T & T & T \\ T & F & T \\ F & T & T \\ F & F & F \\ \hline \end{array} \qquad \qquad \begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline p & \neg p\\ \hline T & F \\ F & T \\ \hline \end{array}\] \[\begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p \to q\\ \hline T & T & T \\ T & F & F \\ F & T & T \\ F & F & T \\ \hline \end{array} \qquad \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p \leftrightarrow q\\ \hline T & T & T \\ T & F & F \\ F & T & F \\ F & F & T \\ \hline \end{array} \]
docs.title: Truth tables for connectives
