docs.title: Truth tables provide a mechanical process

docs.text:
Lesson 3: Representation Learning outcomes: In this lesson we study the use of propositional logic in knowledge representation. After this lesson you should be able to: construct representations in propositional logic for knowledge expressed in natural language (in English), check that your representations are justifiable by using the idea of a state space described by the truth values the propositions are allowed to take, identify reasons why some sentences in natural language may be impossible to represent fully using propositional logic. Knowledge representation as specifying states Propositional logic can be used to represent knowledge about the world. To do this you need to: identify one or more atomic propositions that describe the world, in the sense that knowing whether each proposition is true or false gives relevant information about the world. You can think of all the different possibilities as a state space – a set of different states that the world could be in. decide what combinations of truth values for these atomic propositions correspond to the states that need to be picked out describe the states that are to be picked out by giving one or more formulas which are true only in the case that the world is in one of the desired states. Example Suppose I want to represent the knowledge that If it is Tuesday then I always travel to Leeds, but I never travel to Leeds on other days We could use two atomic propositions: It is Tuesday today I travel to Leeds today and consider the four possible states that the world might have: \[  egin{array}{l|cc|} & \mathit{tuesdayToday} & \mathit{leedsToday}\[1ex] \hline \mathsf{State~ 1} & \mathit{True} & \mathit{True} \[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 2} & \mathit{True} & \mathit{False} \[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 3} & \mathit{False} & \mathit{True} \[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 4} & \mathit{False} & \mathit{False} \[1ex] \hline \end{array}\] In this case State 1 and State 4 are allowed by the given knowledge and State 2 and State 3 are excluded. One formula which is true in just the allowed cases is \(\qquad \mathit{tuesdayToday} \leftrightarrow \mathit{leedsToday}\) . In the example above, the task of representing the knowledge present in the statement was given in the context of using propositional logic and being given two specific propositions to use. Several scenarios we meet later have a similar form, with a particular logic being specified as well as some basic components to use. In real-world examples the task of knowledge representation is often much less tightly controlled. This can be like just being asked to represent the knowledge expressed in the statement 'If it is Tuesday then I always travel to Leeds, but I never travel to Leeds on other days' , without any specification of the formalism to be used. One danger to be aware of in writing a logical formula to represent knowledge is to make sure you are not just writing a formula that is true in the states you want to select. It also needs to be false in the states you want to exclude. In the case of the above example, \(\mathit{tuesdayToday} 	o \mathit{leedsToday}\) will be true in all the selected states, but it is also true in State 3 which should be excluded. This way of thinking of representing knowledge as selecting states gives you a way of checking whether a formula you write does represent what you intend. Sometimes the process of representation is talked about as if you need to 'translate' from natural language to formal logic. It is not always a helpful analogy to think of translating from one natural language to another, because in that case you do not have such an effective means of checking your answers for yourself. Knowledge representation and natural language In some knowledge representation tasks you may not need to think explicitly about a state space and which states are to be included and which excluded. You may, especially with some practice, be able to work more quickly from the form of the language used to express the knowledge. This can be risky unless you are careful to avoid all the ways that natural language can be ambiguous, and can also use the same words as appear in formal logic but with very different meanings. When dealing with tricky cases it may help to check how the formal logic is constraining what is allowed in possible interpretations. In this section we look at some of the ways natural language and propositional logic fail to match up. The word 'or'. Hidden threats or instructions Saying 'Do X or Y will happen' is common in situations like 'Find the documents by Monday or you will lose your job' or like 'Tidy your bedroom or you cannot go out to play' . We might be tempted to follow the form of the English and translate these like this: \[\mathit{findDocuments} \vee \mathit{loseJob},\] \[\mathit{tidyRoom} \vee 
eg \mathit{canPlay}.\] This misses two things: The statements have an aspect that is a proposition, but there is also a threat or instruction that propositional logic cannot model. Even just taking the propositional element, if the documents are found but the person still loses their job, this is entirely consistent with the statement. If the child does tidy their bedroom, but is not allowed to play, the statement is perfectly true. To avoid the second problem we need to think about what states can be described by the atomic propositions we are seeking to pick out. If we do this we see that we need to specify: \[(\mathit{findDocuments} \wedge 
eg \mathit{loseJob}) \vee (
eg \mathit{findDocuments} \wedge \mathit{loseJob}),\] or equally well: \[(\mathit{findDocuments} \vee \mathit{loseJob}) \wedge 
eg ( \mathit{findDocuments} \wedge \mathit{loseJob}).\] Of course, it is possible that the context is that the person is going to lose their job whether the documents are found or not. In that case the knowledge to be modelled is different and a different proposition is needed. The order of things (commutativity) In propositional logic \(A \vee B\) means exactly the same as \(B \vee A\) . In algebraic terms we would say that \(\vee\) is a commutative operation. But language is not like that. In examples like the following, the two versions may not have exactly the same sense. This is often because the meaning is not purely about truth values, but also includes expectations or hopes about which of two options is the more likely or the more desired. 'Enter your 27 digit security code, or apply to have your permissions reset' 'Apply to have your permissions reset, or enter your 27 digit security code' 'I will see you tomorrow, or sometime next year' 'I will see you sometime next year, or tomorrow' The use of 'either', 'neither', 'nor'. The word 'either' is often used to indicate an exclusive or. For example 'you can either have X or Y' very often means that you cannot have both X and Y. In modelling such statements you need to pay attention to the intended meaning. The use of 'neither — nor —' is always to indicate a negated disjunction. For example 'Neither Monday nor Tuesday is possible' could be modelled using atomic propositions \(\mathit{mondayPossible}\) meaning 'Monday is possible' \(\mathit{tuesdayPossible}\) meaning 'Tuesday is possible' as \[ 
eg (\mathit{mondayPossible} \vee \mathit{tuesdayPossible}).\] The word 'and' Just as with 'or', it is important to be aware of the limitations of propositional logic to model the complexity of what we say in natural language. The order of things (commutativity) Compare these two statements: 'I bought a kilo of flour and made bread' , 'I made bread and bought a kilo of flour' or these two: 'I got a new phone yesterday and I spent ages talking to my friends' , 'I spent ages talking to my friends and I got a new phone yesterday' . Notice how the two statements do not have quite the same sense. Often the word 'and' is used to convey a sequence of events; one thing happening after another thing. Sometimes, as in the above examples, this suggests a causal connection between the things. In propositional logic the connective \(\wedge\) has none of these properties. A formula \(A \wedge B\) always has the same truth value as \(B \wedge A\) , and there is no way of \(A\) and \(B\) sharing some context or meaning between them. Interaction with possibility Imagine a scenario where it is possible to get a train from Leeds to London, and it is also possible to get a train from Leeds to Edinburgh, but it is not possible to get a single train from Leeds that goes both to London and to Edinburgh. In this case the propositions \(\mathit{possibleLondon}\) meaning 'It is possible to get a train to London' , and \(\mathit{possibleEdinburgh}\) meaning 'It is possible to get a train to Edinburgh' can be combined into \(\mathit{possibleLondon} \wedge \mathit{possibleEdinburgh}\) . This compound proposition means it is possible to get a train from Leeds to London, and it is also possible to get a train from Leeds to Edinburgh. However, there is no way of combining these two propositions to convey the knowledge that no single train leaving Leeds goes both to London and to Edinburgh. Here we see the way that propositional logic is a very limited means of modelling. Later in the module we will see how this particular example is straightforward to represent in a modal logic, where the notion of possibility is handled as part of the logic. As with earlier examples, you need to be careful about what exactly the knowledge present in natural language really is. This is frequently unclear, sometimes deliberately so. You may need to guess what people probably mean. 'There are trains to London and Edinburgh from here' 'There are benefits in failing and succeeding' 'There are trains to London and to Edinburgh from here' 'There are benefits in failing and in succeeding' The use of 'if — then —'. The \(	o\) connective can be most confusing because it only models a few of the situations where we use 'if — then —' in everyday language. Sometimes means '— if and only if —' We saw the following idea expressed using 'or' earlier. If you don't find the documents by Monday then you will lose your job. You might expect this to be modelled by \(
eg \mathit{findDocuments} 	o \mathit{loseJob}\) . The problem there is if the documents are found then the person probably expects that they will not lose their job. But this is not present in the formal logical statement at all. Without any further information it is most likely that the following is what the statement expresses. \[ 
eg \mathit{findDocuments} \leftrightarrow \mathit{loseJob}. \] Here the statement in natural language has the form \(A 	o B\) but the intention is not that statement, but both than one and \(B 	o A\) . The statement \(B 	o A\) is the converse of the statement \(A 	o B\) . When the antecedent is false In a compound proposition of the form \(A 	o B\) , the proposition \(A\) is called the antecedent and the proposition \(B\) is called the consequent . The truth table tells us that when the antecedent is false, the statement as a whole is true. This happens whatever the consequent is, and it does not matter whether it is true or false. Even when we accept that propositional logic is a very crude means of modelling the complexity of what people say and mean, there can still be some potentially confusing examples. Consider this one, about the number of letters in the word 'Leeds'. For the purpose of this example, there are actually 5 letters in the word; you can say 'character' if you prefer. If 'Leeds' has 3 letters then 'Leeds' has 6 letters. We can model this with atomic propositions: \(\mathit{hasThree}\) meaning the word 'Leeds' has 3 letters , and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) meaning the word 'Leeds' has 6 letters . We can agree that both \(\mathit{hasThree}\) and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) are false statements. So it must be the case that \[ \mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix} \] is true. Something to think about It is correct, given the setting above, that If 'Leeds' has 3 letters then 'Leeds' has 6 letters. is true when modelled, in the above way, in propositional logic. But, can you explain where the fault is in the following arguments that this cannot possibly be right? The arguments should be considered separately from each other. We all know that if any word has 3 letters than the word cannot have 6 letters. This is true for any word at all, so it must be true for the word 'Leeds'. This is impossible in combination with the given statement. Thinking in terms of states (as suggested at the start of this lesson) we know that both \(\mathit{hasThree}\) and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) are false propositions. To represent this knowledge it cannot be correct to say \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) is true, because this happens in situations other than the actual ones. This is shown in the following table. \[ egin{array}{l|cc|cc|} & \mathit{hasThree} & \mathit{hasSix} & \mathsf{Actually?} & \mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\[1ex] \hline \mathsf{State~ 1} & \mathit{True} & \mathit{True} & \mathsf{NO} & \mathit{True}\[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 2} & \mathit{True} & \mathit{False}& \mathsf{NO} & \mathit{False} \[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 3} & \mathit{False} & \mathit{True}& \mathsf{NO} & \mathit{True} \[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 4} & \mathit{False} & \mathit{False}& \mathsf{YES} & \mathit{True} \[1ex] \hline \end{array} \] Reveal answers The argument here depends on making a connection between the two statements which it is simply impossible to make in propositional logic. There is no way in propositional logic to model the idea that both propositions are talking about the same thing (the same word). Each is either true or false, and any compound proposition has a truth value which depends only on the raw truth values of the atomic propositions. So the fallacy in the argument lies in thinking that the propositions in formal logic are carrying much more of their informal meaning expressed in words than they actually are. Propositional logic is unable to detect any difference between the given scenario and the following one. \(\mathit{hasThree}\) meaning the word 'Bradford' has 3 letters , and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) meaning the word 'Leeds' has 6 letters . We can agree that both \(\mathit{hasThree}\) and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) are false statements. So it must be the case that \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) is true. When we get to first order logic in unit 3 then we will be equipped to model connections between statements that talk about the same thing. For example, the idea that if a word has three letters than it cannot have six might be represented by \( orall x\, (\mathit{hasThree}(x) 	o 
eg \mathit{hasSix}(x)).\) The puzzle here is resolved by realizing that it was never claimed that \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) is modelling the fact that both \(\mathit{hasThree}\) and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) are false. To model that we could use \(
eg \mathit{hasThree} \wedge 
eg \mathit{hasSix}\) . This puzzle shows that it is necessary to be very careful about the purpose behind asserting or using any statement. It is correct to say that \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) is true given that both the atomic propositions are false. It is not correct to say that \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) models the falsity of both the atomic propositions. The word 'not'. In interpreting 'not' , be careful to distinguish formulas such as these two: \[
eg (A \vee B) \quad \mathsf{or} \quad (
eg A) \vee (
eg B),\] and similarly for \(\wedge\) . For example 'You can not have a pay rise or a holiday' is best represented by \[ 
eg (\mathit{payRise} \vee \mathit{holiday}). \] But 'You can not fail this task and be more useful' is probably best represented by \[ 
eg \mathit{failTask} \wedge \mathit{beMoreUseful}. \] Whereas 'You can not fail this task and continue working here' is probably best represented by \[ 
eg (\mathit{failTask} \wedge \mathit{continueWorking}). \] In these two cases it is essential to look beyond the form of the language and use knowledge about the most likely meaning. The word 'but' Very often 'but' corresponds to 'and' . More accurately, 'and' is the closest we can get in propositional logic to the meaning. However, 'not' can interact with 'but' and 'and' in different ways. For instance: 'I don't eat cheese but I like mushrooms' \(\qquad
eg \mathit{eatCheese} \wedge \mathit{likeMushrooms}\) 'The proposal is not legal and cheap' \(\qquad 
eg (\mathit{legalProposal} \wedge \mathit{cheapProposal})\) This could easily be different depending on the context: 'This is what we villains love to see. Your proposal is not legal and cheap' Arguably, in that case, it might be written without ambiguity using a comma: '… Your proposal is not legal, and cheap' \((
eg \mathit{legalProposal}) \wedge \mathit{cheapProposal}\;\;\) is the only interpretation then. Adding context is another way ambiguity disappears: 'I asked for a cheap legal solution, Your proposal is not legal and cheap' The last sentence has to be \(\qquad 
eg (\mathit{legalProposal} \wedge \mathit{cheapProposal})\) . 'The proposal is not legal but cheap' \(\qquad (
eg \mathit{legalProposal}) \wedge \mathit{cheapProposal}\) In this case the parentheses are not needed, but are added to emphasise the difference with the previous example. Sometimes the addition of a small word can change the meaning. 'This is not a brief but complete account' \(\quad 
eg (\mathit{briefAccount} \wedge \mathit{completeAccount})\) 'This is not a brief but a complete account' \(\quad (
eg \mathit{briefAccount}) \wedge \mathit{completeAccount}\) The word 'unless' A phrase of the form ' \(\,\mathsf{X}\) unless \(\mathsf{Y}\) ' is often represented in propositional logic by \(
eg \mathsf{Y} 	o \mathsf{X}\) . 'I walk to work unless it is raining' \(\qquad 
eg \mathit{rain} 	o \mathit{walk}\) 'It is a hippo unless it's an elephant' \(\qquad 
eg \mathit{elephant} 	o \mathit{hippo}\) 'Unless it's an elephant, it's a hippo' \(\qquad 
eg \mathit{elephant} 	o \mathit{hippo}\) 'You cannot have a pay rise unless you work harder' \(\;\; 
eg \mathit{workHarder} 	o 
eg \mathit{payRise}\) In the last case it is not clear whether working harder would actually imply a pay rise. In some settings that might be part of the meaning and the whole sentence could be represented by \(
eg \mathit{workHarder} \leftrightarrow 
eg \mathit{payRise}\) or the logically eqivalent version: \(\mathit{workHarder} \leftrightarrow \mathit{payRise}\) . However, this is not representing the idea that being given a pay rise will cause the person to work harder, and neither is it saying that working harder is causing them to get a pay rise. An extended example In this example we look at a more complex situation which is described in several sentences and brings in reasoning. The scenario Imagine a room with a light, a light switch and a TV. Consider the following propositions. \[  egin{array}{|l|l|} \mathsf{Proposition~ Symbol} & \mathsf{Meaning}\ \hline \mathit{tvOK} & \mathsf{the~ TV~ is~ working}\ \mathit{power} & \mathsf{there~ has~ not~ been~ a~ power~ cut}\ \mathit{lampLit} & \mathsf{the~ lamp~ is~ lit}\ \mathit{harryAlone} & \mathsf{Harry~ is~ alone~ in~ the~ room}\ \mathit{bulbDead} & \mathsf{the~ light~ bulb~ in~ the~ lamp~ is~ dead}\ \mathit{switchOn} & \mathsf{the~ lamp~ switch~ is~ on}\ \mathit{harryOff} & \mathsf{Harry~ switched~ the~ lamp~ off}\ \hline \end{array} \] We can represent this knowledge about the world in general If Harry is alone and didn't switch the light off, it is still switched on. \( egin{equation} (\mathit{harryAlone} \wedge 
eg \mathit{harryOff}) 	o \mathit{switchOn} \end{equation}\) If the TV is working then there can't be a power cut. \( egin{equation} \mathit{tvOK} 	o \mathit{power} \end{equation}\) If the lamp is not lit and it's switched on and there isn't a power cut then the light bulb is dead. \( egin{equation} (
eg \mathit{lampLit} \wedge \mathit{switchOn} \wedge \mathit{power}) 	o \mathit{bulbDead} \end{equation}\) Suppose we also know these facts about this particular scenario: The TV is working. \( egin{equation}\mathit{tvOK}\end{equation}\) Harry didn't switch the lamp off. \( egin{equation} 
eg \mathit{harryOff} \end{equation}\) Harry is alone in the room. \( egin{equation} \mathit{harryAlone} \end{equation}\) The lamp isn't lit. \( egin{equation} 
eg \mathit{lampLit} \end{equation}\) The question is: Must the bulb be dead? That is, can we be sure the following must be true? \[\mathit{bulbDead}\] An informal argument We can argue as follows: Because Harry is alone (6) and didn't switch the lamp off (5) we know from (1) that the lamp is switched on. So we know that: \( egin{equation} \mathit{switchOn} \end{equation}\) Because the TV's working (4) there cannot be a power cut by (2). So we know that: \( egin{equation} \mathit{power} \end{equation}\) Because the lamp is switched on (8) and it isn't lit (7) and we know from (9) that there isn't a power cut, it must be that the bulb's dead by (3). So we conclude that: \( egin{equation} \mathit{bulbDead} \end{equation}\) Looking forward In the next lesson we look at two ways in which the propositions in this scenario can be represented in a system that can automatically carry out an argument and come to the conclusion that the bulb is dead. Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 3, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise about representation in propositional logic. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit-1 Lesson 3' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.
docs.title: Lesson 3 Representation

docs.text: Learning outcomes: In this lesson we study the use of propositional logic in knowledge representation. After this lesson you should be able to: construct representations in propositional logic for knowledge expressed in natural language (in English), check that your representations are justifiable by using the idea of a state space described by the truth values the propositions are allowed to take, identify reasons why some sentences in natural language may be impossible to represent fully using propositional logic.
docs.title: Lesson 3: Representation

docs.text: Propositional logic can be used to represent knowledge about the world. To do this you need to: identify one or more atomic propositions that describe the world, in the sense that knowing whether each proposition is true or false gives relevant information about the world. You can think of all the different possibilities as a state space – a set of different states that the world could be in. decide what combinations of truth values for these atomic propositions correspond to the states that need to be picked out describe the states that are to be picked out by giving one or more formulas which are true only in the case that the world is in one of the desired states. Example Suppose I want to represent the knowledge that If it is Tuesday then I always travel to Leeds, but I never travel to Leeds on other days We could use two atomic propositions: It is Tuesday today I travel to Leeds today and consider the four possible states that the world might have: \[ \begin{array}{l|cc|} & \mathit{tuesdayToday} & \mathit{leedsToday}\\[1ex] \hline \mathsf{State~ 1} & \mathit{True} & \mathit{True} \\[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 2} & \mathit{True} & \mathit{False} \\[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 3} & \mathit{False} & \mathit{True} \\[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 4} & \mathit{False} & \mathit{False} \\[1ex] \hline \end{array}\] In this case State 1 and State 4 are allowed by the given knowledge and State 2 and State 3 are excluded. One formula which is true in just the allowed cases is \(\qquad \mathit{tuesdayToday} \leftrightarrow \mathit{leedsToday}\) . In the example above, the task of representing the knowledge present in the statement was given in the context of using propositional logic and being given two specific propositions to use. Several scenarios we meet later have a similar form, with a particular logic being specified as well as some basic components to use. In real-world examples the task of knowledge representation is often much less tightly controlled. This can be like just being asked to represent the knowledge expressed in the statement 'If it is Tuesday then I always travel to Leeds, but I never travel to Leeds on other days' , without any specification of the formalism to be used. One danger to be aware of in writing a logical formula to represent knowledge is to make sure you are not just writing a formula that is true in the states you want to select. It also needs to be false in the states you want to exclude. In the case of the above example, \(\mathit{tuesdayToday} \to \mathit{leedsToday}\) will be true in all the selected states, but it is also true in State 3 which should be excluded. This way of thinking of representing knowledge as selecting states gives you a way of checking whether a formula you write does represent what you intend. Sometimes the process of representation is talked about as if you need to 'translate' from natural language to formal logic. It is not always a helpful analogy to think of translating from one natural language to another, because in that case you do not have such an effective means of checking your answers for yourself.
docs.title: Knowledge representation as specifying states

docs.text: In some knowledge representation tasks you may not need to think explicitly about a state space and which states are to be included and which excluded. You may, especially with some practice, be able to work more quickly from the form of the language used to express the knowledge. This can be risky unless you are careful to avoid all the ways that natural language can be ambiguous, and can also use the same words as appear in formal logic but with very different meanings. When dealing with tricky cases it may help to check how the formal logic is constraining what is allowed in possible interpretations. In this section we look at some of the ways natural language and propositional logic fail to match up.
docs.title: Knowledge representation and natural language

docs.text:
Saying 'Do X or Y will happen' is common in situations like 'Find the documents by Monday or you will lose your job' or like 'Tidy your bedroom or you cannot go out to play' . We might be tempted to follow the form of the English and translate these like this: \[\mathit{findDocuments} \vee \mathit{loseJob},\] \[\mathit{tidyRoom} \vee 
eg \mathit{canPlay}.\] This misses two things: The statements have an aspect that is a proposition, but there is also a threat or instruction that propositional logic cannot model. Even just taking the propositional element, if the documents are found but the person still loses their job, this is entirely consistent with the statement. If the child does tidy their bedroom, but is not allowed to play, the statement is perfectly true. To avoid the second problem we need to think about what states can be described by the atomic propositions we are seeking to pick out. If we do this we see that we need to specify: \[(\mathit{findDocuments} \wedge 
eg \mathit{loseJob}) \vee (
eg \mathit{findDocuments} \wedge \mathit{loseJob}),\] or equally well: \[(\mathit{findDocuments} \vee \mathit{loseJob}) \wedge 
eg ( \mathit{findDocuments} \wedge \mathit{loseJob}).\] Of course, it is possible that the context is that the person is going to lose their job whether the documents are found or not. In that case the knowledge to be modelled is different and a different proposition is needed.
docs.title: Hidden threats or instructions

docs.text: In propositional logic \(A \vee B\) means exactly the same as \(B \vee A\) . In algebraic terms we would say that \(\vee\) is a commutative operation. But language is not like that. In examples like the following, the two versions may not have exactly the same sense. This is often because the meaning is not purely about truth values, but also includes expectations or hopes about which of two options is the more likely or the more desired. 'Enter your 27 digit security code, or apply to have your permissions reset' 'Apply to have your permissions reset, or enter your 27 digit security code' 'I will see you tomorrow, or sometime next year' 'I will see you sometime next year, or tomorrow'
docs.title: The order of things (commutativity)

docs.text: The word 'either' is often used to indicate an exclusive or. For example 'you can either have X or Y' very often means that you cannot have both X and Y. In modelling such statements you need to pay attention to the intended meaning. The use of 'neither — nor —' is always to indicate a negated disjunction. For example 'Neither Monday nor Tuesday is possible' could be modelled using atomic propositions \(\mathit{mondayPossible}\) meaning 'Monday is possible' \(\mathit{tuesdayPossible}\) meaning 'Tuesday is possible' as \[ \neg (\mathit{mondayPossible} \vee \mathit{tuesdayPossible}).\]
docs.title: The use of 'either', 'neither', 'nor'.

docs.text: Just as with 'or', it is important to be aware of the limitations of propositional logic to model the complexity of what we say in natural language.
docs.title: The word 'and'

docs.text: Compare these two statements: 'I bought a kilo of flour and made bread' , 'I made bread and bought a kilo of flour' or these two: 'I got a new phone yesterday and I spent ages talking to my friends' , 'I spent ages talking to my friends and I got a new phone yesterday' . Notice how the two statements do not have quite the same sense. Often the word 'and' is used to convey a sequence of events; one thing happening after another thing. Sometimes, as in the above examples, this suggests a causal connection between the things. In propositional logic the connective \(\wedge\) has none of these properties. A formula \(A \wedge B\) always has the same truth value as \(B \wedge A\) , and there is no way of \(A\) and \(B\) sharing some context or meaning between them.
docs.title: The order of things (commutativity)

docs.text: Imagine a scenario where it is possible to get a train from Leeds to London, and it is also possible to get a train from Leeds to Edinburgh, but it is not possible to get a single train from Leeds that goes both to London and to Edinburgh. In this case the propositions \(\mathit{possibleLondon}\) meaning 'It is possible to get a train to London' , and \(\mathit{possibleEdinburgh}\) meaning 'It is possible to get a train to Edinburgh' can be combined into \(\mathit{possibleLondon} \wedge \mathit{possibleEdinburgh}\) . This compound proposition means it is possible to get a train from Leeds to London, and it is also possible to get a train from Leeds to Edinburgh. However, there is no way of combining these two propositions to convey the knowledge that no single train leaving Leeds goes both to London and to Edinburgh. Here we see the way that propositional logic is a very limited means of modelling. Later in the module we will see how this particular example is straightforward to represent in a modal logic, where the notion of possibility is handled as part of the logic. As with earlier examples, you need to be careful about what exactly the knowledge present in natural language really is. This is frequently unclear, sometimes deliberately so. You may need to guess what people probably mean. 'There are trains to London and Edinburgh from here' 'There are benefits in failing and succeeding' 'There are trains to London and to Edinburgh from here' 'There are benefits in failing and in succeeding'
docs.title: Interaction with possibility

docs.text: The \(\to\) connective can be most confusing because it only models a few of the situations where we use 'if — then —' in everyday language.
docs.title: The use of 'if — then —'.

docs.text: We saw the following idea expressed using 'or' earlier. If you don't find the documents by Monday then you will lose your job. You might expect this to be modelled by \(\neg \mathit{findDocuments} \to \mathit{loseJob}\) . The problem there is if the documents are found then the person probably expects that they will not lose their job. But this is not present in the formal logical statement at all. Without any further information it is most likely that the following is what the statement expresses. \[ \neg \mathit{findDocuments} \leftrightarrow \mathit{loseJob}. \] Here the statement in natural language has the form \(A \to B\) but the intention is not that statement, but both than one and \(B \to A\) . The statement \(B \to A\) is the converse of the statement \(A \to B\) .
docs.title: Sometimes means '— if and only if —'

docs.text:
In a compound proposition of the form \(A 	o B\) , the proposition \(A\) is called the antecedent and the proposition \(B\) is called the consequent . The truth table tells us that when the antecedent is false, the statement as a whole is true. This happens whatever the consequent is, and it does not matter whether it is true or false. Even when we accept that propositional logic is a very crude means of modelling the complexity of what people say and mean, there can still be some potentially confusing examples. Consider this one, about the number of letters in the word 'Leeds'. For the purpose of this example, there are actually 5 letters in the word; you can say 'character' if you prefer. If 'Leeds' has 3 letters then 'Leeds' has 6 letters. We can model this with atomic propositions: \(\mathit{hasThree}\) meaning the word 'Leeds' has 3 letters , and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) meaning the word 'Leeds' has 6 letters . We can agree that both \(\mathit{hasThree}\) and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) are false statements. So it must be the case that \[ \mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix} \] is true. Something to think about It is correct, given the setting above, that If 'Leeds' has 3 letters then 'Leeds' has 6 letters. is true when modelled, in the above way, in propositional logic. But, can you explain where the fault is in the following arguments that this cannot possibly be right? The arguments should be considered separately from each other. We all know that if any word has 3 letters than the word cannot have 6 letters. This is true for any word at all, so it must be true for the word 'Leeds'. This is impossible in combination with the given statement. Thinking in terms of states (as suggested at the start of this lesson) we know that both \(\mathit{hasThree}\) and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) are false propositions. To represent this knowledge it cannot be correct to say \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) is true, because this happens in situations other than the actual ones. This is shown in the following table. \[ egin{array}{l|cc|cc|} & \mathit{hasThree} & \mathit{hasSix} & \mathsf{Actually?} & \mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\[1ex] \hline \mathsf{State~ 1} & \mathit{True} & \mathit{True} & \mathsf{NO} & \mathit{True}\[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 2} & \mathit{True} & \mathit{False}& \mathsf{NO} & \mathit{False} \[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 3} & \mathit{False} & \mathit{True}& \mathsf{NO} & \mathit{True} \[1ex] \mathsf{State~ 4} & \mathit{False} & \mathit{False}& \mathsf{YES} & \mathit{True} \[1ex] \hline \end{array} \] Reveal answers The argument here depends on making a connection between the two statements which it is simply impossible to make in propositional logic. There is no way in propositional logic to model the idea that both propositions are talking about the same thing (the same word). Each is either true or false, and any compound proposition has a truth value which depends only on the raw truth values of the atomic propositions. So the fallacy in the argument lies in thinking that the propositions in formal logic are carrying much more of their informal meaning expressed in words than they actually are. Propositional logic is unable to detect any difference between the given scenario and the following one. \(\mathit{hasThree}\) meaning the word 'Bradford' has 3 letters , and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) meaning the word 'Leeds' has 6 letters . We can agree that both \(\mathit{hasThree}\) and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) are false statements. So it must be the case that \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) is true. When we get to first order logic in unit 3 then we will be equipped to model connections between statements that talk about the same thing. For example, the idea that if a word has three letters than it cannot have six might be represented by \( orall x\, (\mathit{hasThree}(x) 	o 
eg \mathit{hasSix}(x)).\) The puzzle here is resolved by realizing that it was never claimed that \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) is modelling the fact that both \(\mathit{hasThree}\) and \(\mathit{hasSix}\) are false. To model that we could use \(
eg \mathit{hasThree} \wedge 
eg \mathit{hasSix}\) . This puzzle shows that it is necessary to be very careful about the purpose behind asserting or using any statement. It is correct to say that \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) is true given that both the atomic propositions are false. It is not correct to say that \(\mathit{hasThree} 	o \mathit{hasSix}\) models the falsity of both the atomic propositions.
docs.title: When the antecedent is false

docs.text:
In interpreting 'not' , be careful to distinguish formulas such as these two: \[
eg (A \vee B) \quad \mathsf{or} \quad (
eg A) \vee (
eg B),\] and similarly for \(\wedge\) . For example 'You can not have a pay rise or a holiday' is best represented by \[ 
eg (\mathit{payRise} \vee \mathit{holiday}). \] But 'You can not fail this task and be more useful' is probably best represented by \[ 
eg \mathit{failTask} \wedge \mathit{beMoreUseful}. \] Whereas 'You can not fail this task and continue working here' is probably best represented by \[ 
eg (\mathit{failTask} \wedge \mathit{continueWorking}). \] In these two cases it is essential to look beyond the form of the language and use knowledge about the most likely meaning.
docs.title: The word 'not'.

docs.text:
Very often 'but' corresponds to 'and' . More accurately, 'and' is the closest we can get in propositional logic to the meaning. However, 'not' can interact with 'but' and 'and' in different ways. For instance: 'I don't eat cheese but I like mushrooms' \(\qquad
eg \mathit{eatCheese} \wedge \mathit{likeMushrooms}\) 'The proposal is not legal and cheap' \(\qquad 
eg (\mathit{legalProposal} \wedge \mathit{cheapProposal})\) This could easily be different depending on the context: 'This is what we villains love to see. Your proposal is not legal and cheap' Arguably, in that case, it might be written without ambiguity using a comma: '… Your proposal is not legal, and cheap' \((
eg \mathit{legalProposal}) \wedge \mathit{cheapProposal}\;\;\) is the only interpretation then. Adding context is another way ambiguity disappears: 'I asked for a cheap legal solution, Your proposal is not legal and cheap' The last sentence has to be \(\qquad 
eg (\mathit{legalProposal} \wedge \mathit{cheapProposal})\) . 'The proposal is not legal but cheap' \(\qquad (
eg \mathit{legalProposal}) \wedge \mathit{cheapProposal}\) In this case the parentheses are not needed, but are added to emphasise the difference with the previous example. Sometimes the addition of a small word can change the meaning. 'This is not a brief but complete account' \(\quad 
eg (\mathit{briefAccount} \wedge \mathit{completeAccount})\) 'This is not a brief but a complete account' \(\quad (
eg \mathit{briefAccount}) \wedge \mathit{completeAccount}\)
docs.title: The word 'but'

docs.text:
A phrase of the form ' \(\,\mathsf{X}\) unless \(\mathsf{Y}\) ' is often represented in propositional logic by \(
eg \mathsf{Y} 	o \mathsf{X}\) . 'I walk to work unless it is raining' \(\qquad 
eg \mathit{rain} 	o \mathit{walk}\) 'It is a hippo unless it's an elephant' \(\qquad 
eg \mathit{elephant} 	o \mathit{hippo}\) 'Unless it's an elephant, it's a hippo' \(\qquad 
eg \mathit{elephant} 	o \mathit{hippo}\) 'You cannot have a pay rise unless you work harder' \(\;\; 
eg \mathit{workHarder} 	o 
eg \mathit{payRise}\) In the last case it is not clear whether working harder would actually imply a pay rise. In some settings that might be part of the meaning and the whole sentence could be represented by \(
eg \mathit{workHarder} \leftrightarrow 
eg \mathit{payRise}\) or the logically eqivalent version: \(\mathit{workHarder} \leftrightarrow \mathit{payRise}\) . However, this is not representing the idea that being given a pay rise will cause the person to work harder, and neither is it saying that working harder is causing them to get a pay rise.
docs.title: The word 'unless'

docs.text: In this example we look at a more complex situation which is described in several sentences and brings in reasoning.
docs.title: An extended example
The scenario

docs.text: We can argue as follows: Because Harry is alone (6) and didn't switch the lamp off (5) we know from (1) that the lamp is switched on. So we know that: \(\begin{equation} \mathit{switchOn} \end{equation}\) Because the TV's working (4) there cannot be a power cut by (2). So we know that: \(\begin{equation} \mathit{power} \end{equation}\) Because the lamp is switched on (8) and it isn't lit (7) and we know from (9) that there isn't a power cut, it must be that the bulb's dead by (3). So we conclude that: \(\begin{equation} \mathit{bulbDead} \end{equation}\) Looking forward In the next lesson we look at two ways in which the propositions in this scenario can be represented in a system that can automatically carry out an argument and come to the conclusion that the bulb is dead. Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 3, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise about representation in propositional logic. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit-1 Lesson 3' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.
docs.title: An informal argument

docs.text:
Lesson 4: Automation Learning outcomes: In this lesson we study two ways of automating logical reasoning: Prolog: a logic programming language, and Prover9: a theorem prover. After completing this lesson you should be able to: use Prolog to represent simple situations involving propositions, explain the difference between the open world assumption and the closed world assumption make use of the way Prolog works with negation as failure in simple examples use Prover9 to input propositional logic statements and check whether a given goal can be proved from these. Formative activities for Lesson 4 In this lesson you should make use of both Prover9 and of Prolog and run all of the examples presented in the lesson. You should also explore what happens if you make small alterations to the examples given. What happens if you add extra logical statements, or if you change the existing ones, or if you remove some of them? Can you understand why the behaviour changes when you do these things? If you come across things that don't seem to make sense this is valuable as you can then ask questions about this on the discussion board or by asking the module leader directly. If there is something that does not behave as you would expect, then it is important to make an example that is as simple as possible before asking about it. Try to cut down examples as far as possible to still get something you don't understand while having as little as possible that is not essential. You should also experiment with putting some of the examples from lessons 2 and 3 into Prover9 or Prolog, or both, and seeing if things behave as expected. Prolog: getting started Declarative not imperative Prolog is a logic programming language. If you have not seen other declarative languages before, you may find Prolog surprising at first. If your experience is limited to imperative languages you will be familiar with writing statements which are commands, and fragments of code may look like this: x := y + 2; y := y + 1; We understand such code as instructions to change the values stored in locations called x and y . However, in Prolog, code can look like this todayIsTuesday. tomorrowIsWednesday :- todayIsTuesday. These can be read not as commands to do something, but as logical statements. The first todayIsTuesday. is asserting a fact that the proposition today is Tuesday holds. The second tomorrowIsWednesday :- todayIsTuesday. is a clause asserting that if it is true that today is Tuesday, then it is true that tomorrow is Wednesday . In the clause the head is the part before the :- and the body is the part after the :- . Note that every fact and every clause is terminated with a full stop. Forgetting this can lead to confusion at first. It is also essential that propositions always start with a lower case letter. Interaction with Prolog A simple way of using Prolog is to put the above code into a file, and then to interactively enter a Prolog query . A query is again a logical statement, and the Prolog interpreter will respond by telling you whether the query can be deduced from the assertions in the code or not. One view of Prolog code is that it provides a database of basic facts together with rules (written as clauses) that allow new facts to be deduced. An example of interacting with Prolog can look like this: Welcome to SWI-Prolog (threaded, 64 bits, version 7.5.0) SWI-Prolog comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY. This is free software. ?- consult('/Users/scsjgs/today.pl'). true. ?- tomorrowIsWednesday. true. In the above example, the interactive prompt from the interpreter is ?- and the input typed by the user follows on the same line. Prolog tries to appear as if everything is a logical statement, but this is a bit misleading as the input consult('/Users/scsjgs/today.pl'). is more like an instruction to read in a database of facts and rules. The response true. tells the user that the code has been successfully read. The second input from the user is tomorrowIsWednesday. which is the query asking whether this proposition can be deduced from the facts and rules. SWI-Prolog You can download the free SWI-prolog implementation from www.swi-prolog.org SWISH Prolog There is an alternative way of using SWI-prolog that runs online and requires no installation. At swish.swi-prolog.org you will see an interface like this: Select for full text description of screenshot 1 A screenshot of the SWISH website as it appears when you first open it up. At the top of the page is a toolbar with drop down menus for file, edit, examples and help. To the toolbar’s right is an icon indicating the number of users online and a search box. Further to the right are icon login links for Google, Stack Overflow and an online hangout. Below this the screen is split into two, with clickable elements and instructions on the left-hand side and a box to input and run queries on the right. The query box is overlooked by the SWISH avatar, a cartoon owl. To get started with the simple example you can just follow these instructions and ignore the details you can see where it says 'Prolog Elements'. The first thing is to click on the + sign at the top of the left hand pane in the interface and below the bar that runs across the whole interface. You should see this: Select for full text description of screenshot 2 A screenshot of the SWISH website after having clicked the plus button on the left-hand side under the menu toolbar to open a new tab. This tab gives you options to create a program or notebook. Clicking program will take you to a new window to the bottom right of the screen where you can enter code. Click on Program (on the upper left where it says 'Create a Program … ') and ignore everything else. You will get a window to the bottom right of the screen, into which you can paste the two lines of prolog code we had earlier: todayIsTuesday. tomorrowIsWednesday :- todayIsTuesday. In addition, you may find it helpful to select the Edit menu and make sure that Semantic highlighting is not ticked. If it is ticked you will find that the code looks as if there are errors in it with some words in red, but this is not useful to start with. Select for full text description of screenshot 3 A screenshot of the SWISH website with the edit menu open. The edit menu features check boxes to turn a number of options on and off. The purpose of this screenshot is to ensure that you have semantic highlighting turned off. Having entered the code, you can then: enter the query in the lower of the two right hand panes, click on 'Run!' in the bottom right corner, and see the response in the upper pane on the right. Select for full text description of screenshot 4 A screenshot of the SWISH website which shows the response in the program window on the upper left-hand side after you have run a query in the box on the lower right-hand side. You can then explore some of the other features that swish allows, but you will not need all of these for the module. The idea of goals in Prolog In our very simple example, tomorrowIsWednesday. is a goal that the prolog interpreter attempts to justify as true given the facts and rules it has. You can imagine it does this as follows, searching through the database of facts and clauses to find a reason why the goal statement must hold. tomorrowIsWednesday is found as the head of a clause in the database. The body of the clause: todayIsTuesday now becomes a new goal because the clause can be read as: the goal tomorrowIsWednesday can be achieved if the (sub)goal todayIsTuesday can be achieved. The goal todayIsTuesday is achieved (can be satisfied) because this is stated as fact in the database. Notice that this is a very procedural way of thinking about the Prolog code. Writing effective Prolog code involves both thinking about logic as instructions and as declarative statements. Beyond the simple Prolog example The above example only illustrates an extremely simple example of Prolog. The language itself includes far more, such as the ability to: use variables, manipulate complex data structures involving lists, use sophisticated pattern-matching in writing rules, write code that alters the database of facts and rules, and control the way the interpreter searches through the available facts and clauses. This last aspect, altering the way goals are attempted to be satisfied, shows that Prolog programs can end up being a long way from purely logical statements. In order to make effective use of Prolog, you will need to learn something of the way the logic is processed. Prover9: getting started Prover9 is a theorem prover which works for first order logic, including propositional logic. Alongside Prover9 you can use Mace4 to search for finite models of first order theories. There are two ways to get access to Prover9. You can install it on your own computer, or you can use it via a virtual machine provided for you for this module. Installing Prover9 yourself You can download the software from the Prover9 site . There are two ways to interact with Prover9 and Mace4. The command line version should be possible to install on most Linux or MacOS computers. There is also a version with a graphical interface that may not work depending on your operating system. Installing it yourself may not be easy and the module does not assume you have been able to do this. Using Prover9 via virtual machine You will need to be familiar with basic linux commands such as ls, cd, pwd, mkdir, mv, cp, rm, more, man and so on. First, follow these step-by-step instructions for accessing a virtual machine (VM) . Log in to the virtual machine as per these instructions, then create an input file. An input file for Prover9 looks like this: formulas(assumptions). % you can add comments like this - awake -> asleep. awake. end_of_list. formulas(goals). - asleep. end_of_list. Logically this input consists of these two assumptions: \(
eg \mathit{awake} 	o \mathit{asleep}\) , \(\mathit{awake}\) . and this goal which the aim is to prove from the given assumptions: \(
eg \mathit{asleep}\) . Running Prover9 on the command line will look like prover9 < input_file > output_file where input_file and output_file are the names of files for the input and output. In this example the output is not very interesting as the goal cannot be proved. We get this: scsjgs@vm-ocom5201m:~$ prover9 < sleepexample > sleepout SEARCH FAILED ------ process 25020 exit (sos_empty) ------ Working with Prover9 We will use Prover9 substantially in some units of this module, and it will be used in part of the assessment. The method of proof that Prover9 uses is called resolution and the details of this will be studied as part of the module itself. This is also the reasoning mechanism used by Prolog. Do This To make sure you understand the basic operation of Prover9, repeat the above example, but changing the input assumptions to \(\mathit{awake} 	o 
eg \mathit{asleep}\) , \(\mathit{awake}\) . Check that the goal \(
eg \mathit{asleep}\) can now be proved. Did the elephant sit on the fence? here is a bigger example scsjgs@vm-ocom5201m:~$ more p9prop1 formulas(assumptions). ElephantOnFence -> BrokenFence. ElephantScared -> (ElephantOnFence | ElephantOnCar). (MouseInGarden & SunnyDay) -> ElephantScared. SunnyDay -> - ElephantOnCar. CatInHouse -> (MouseInGarden & BirdsInGarden). CatInHouse & SunnyDay. end_of_list. formulas (goals). BrokenFence. end_of_list. scsjgs@vm-ocom5201m:~$ prover9 < p9prop1 > out1 -------- Proof 1 -------- THEOREM PROVED ------ process 20565 exit (max_proofs) ------ If you look at the output file you will see a great deal of detail about the processing but including the proof: 1 ElephantOnFence -> BrokenFence # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 2 ElephantScared -> ElephantOnFence | ElephantOnCar # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 3 MouseInGarden & SunnyDay -> ElephantScared # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 4 SunnyDay -> -ElephantOnCar # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 5 CatInHouse -> MouseInGarden & BirdsInGarden # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 6 CatInHouse & SunnyDay # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 7 BrokenFence # label(non_clause) # label(goal). [goal]. 8 -ElephantOnFence | BrokenFence. [clausify(1)]. 9 -ElephantScared | ElephantOnFence | ElephantOnCar. [clausify(2)]. 10 -MouseInGarden | -SunnyDay | ElephantScared. [clausify(3)]. 11 -SunnyDay | -ElephantOnCar. [clausify(4)]. 12 -CatInHouse | MouseInGarden. [clausify(5)]. 14 CatInHouse. [clausify(6)]. 15 SunnyDay. [clausify(6)]. 16 -BrokenFence. [deny(7)]. 18 MouseInGarden. [back_unit_del(12),unit_del(a,14)]. 19 -ElephantOnCar. [back_unit_del(11),unit_del(a,15)]. 20 ElephantScared. [back_unit_del(10),unit_del(a,18),unit_del(b,15)]. 21 -ElephantOnFence. [back_unit_del(8),unit_del(b,16)]. 22 $F. [back_unit_del(9),unit_del(a,20),unit_del(b,21),unit_del(c,19)]. Automating the extended example We now see how the example of reasoning from Lesson 3 about whether there is a power cut or the light bulb in the lamp is dead can be handled both in Prover9 and in Prolog. We will see Prover9 first as Prolog involves an additional topic: negation as failure . Prover9 We can input the scenario and goal thus: formulas(assumptions). harryAlone & - harrySwitchedOff -> switchOn. tv -> powerOK. - lampLit & switchOn & powerOK -> bulbDead. tv. - harrySwitchedOff. harryAlone. - lampLit. end_of_list. formulas(goals). bulbDead. end_of_list. The output (which you are not expected to understand at this point) is: 1 harryAlone & -harrySwitchedOff -> switchOn # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 2 tv -> powerOK # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 3 -lampLit & switchOn & powerOK -> bulbDead # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 4 bulbDead # label(non_clause) # label(goal). [goal]. 5 -harryAlone | harrySwitchedOff | switchOn. [clausify(1)]. 6 -tv | powerOK. [clausify(2)]. 7 lampLit | -switchOn | -powerOK | bulbDead. [clausify(3)]. 8 tv. [assumption]. 9 -harrySwitchedOff. [assumption]. 10 harryAlone. [assumption]. 11 -lampLit. [assumption]. 12 -bulbDead. [deny(4)]. 13 powerOK. [back_unit_del(6),unit_del(a,8)]. 14 switchOn. [back_unit_del(5),unit_del(a,10),unit_del(b,9)]. 15 $F. [back_unit_del(7),unit_del(a,11),unit_del(b,14),unit_del(c,13),unit_del(d,12)]. The basic operation of resolution can be seen in the passage from the assumptions tv -> powerOK. tv. to the fact that powerOK holds. We can see the mechanism as follows: 2 tv -> powerOK # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 6 -tv | powerOK. [clausify(2)]. 8 tv. [assumption]. 13 powerOK. [back_unit_del(6),unit_del(a,8)]. What is happening here is this: (step 2) the implication \(\mathit{tv} 	o \mathit{powerOK}\) is assumed to hold (step 6) the implication is logically equivalent to \(
eg \mathit{tv} \vee \mathit{powerOK}\) (step 8) we have the assumption \(\mathit{tv}\) (step 13) the only way the two statements in steps 6 and 8 can be true together is for \(\mathit{powerOK}\) to be true. Prolog version We can use prolog switchOn :- harryAlone, not(harrySwitchedOff). powerOK :- tv. bulbDead :- not(lampLit), switchOn, powerOK. tv. harrySwitchedOff :- fail. harryAlone. lampLit :- fail. the interaction is: ?- consult('bulb.pl'). true. ?- bulbDead. true. The encoding of the problem in prolog looks strange. Why has it got fail twice in the input? Prolog treats logical negation in a different way to Prover9 and the approach is called negation as failure . We will come back to the power cut example once we have looked at a very simple example to explain about negation as failure. Negation in Prolog A simple comparison between Prover9 and Prolog In Prolog, enter the following in the program area of Swish :- discontiguous(tuesday/0). monday. Here monday is a proposition or a fact. The other part :- discontiguous(tuesday/0). has no logical function. It is there so that Prolog won't complain about never having heard of tuesday. Now, try the goal not(tuesday). Notice what response you get. Now contrast this with Prover9 by using this input file: clear(auto_denials). formulas(assumptions). monday. end_of_list. formulas (goals). -tuesday. end_of_list. You should run Prover9 with this example and see whether the goal is proved from the assumptions. The line clear(auto_denials). does not affect the logic, but generally helps avoid some opaque behaviour from Prover9. Note that this is giving essentially the same task as we just tried in Prolog: we state that the proposition monday holds. We can think of this as meaning today is monday if we want to. we then ask whether the negation of the proposition tuesday holds. You should find that Prover9 and Prolog disagree with each other about this example. The reason is that they understand not to mean quite different things. In Prolog it is not tuesday because it is impossible to prove that it is tuesday. In Prover9 it is not the case that it is not tuesday, because there is no way to prove that it is not tuesday from the given assumptions. The way Prolog treats not is called negation as failure , because if it fails to prove something then it takes the negation of that something to be true. The way Prover9 treats not is the conventional mathematical way for classical logic. Expressing not in Prolog. To return to the powercut example, if we need to assert that some proposition is not true we can say that any attempt to satisfy the proposition as a goal must fail: lampLit :- fail. An alternative way for a goal to fail is simply not to assert it at all. This is what happened in the small example we just saw. Explicit failure has the same effect and would make for more readable code in most cases. Prolog is taking an approach to negation that goes with the closed world assumption that things are false unless we know explicitly they are true. This is familiar in databases. For example, if an organisation wants to know if they have an employee called "Lyra Silvertongue" they might search in a database of all their employees and see if that name is there. If the name is not there they conclude that they have no such employee, despite it not being asserted directly that this person is not an employee. It is called the closed world assumption because it is assumed that the list of employees is complete and there cannot be any that are not recorded in the database. In Prolog, if we want to make a goal including some subgoals which must fail we express it like this switchOn :- harryAlone, not(harrySwitchedOff). Note that in the body of a clause we can use the special Prolog word not , but to assert a fact of the form ' \(x\) is not true' we can say x :- fail. .
docs.title: Lesson 4 Automating logic

docs.text: Learning outcomes: In this lesson we study two ways of automating logical reasoning: Prolog: a logic programming language, and Prover9: a theorem prover. After completing this lesson you should be able to: use Prolog to represent simple situations involving propositions, explain the difference between the open world assumption and the closed world assumption make use of the way Prolog works with negation as failure in simple examples use Prover9 to input propositional logic statements and check whether a given goal can be proved from these. Formative activities for Lesson 4 In this lesson you should make use of both Prover9 and of Prolog and run all of the examples presented in the lesson. You should also explore what happens if you make small alterations to the examples given. What happens if you add extra logical statements, or if you change the existing ones, or if you remove some of them? Can you understand why the behaviour changes when you do these things? If you come across things that don't seem to make sense this is valuable as you can then ask questions about this on the discussion board or by asking the module leader directly. If there is something that does not behave as you would expect, then it is important to make an example that is as simple as possible before asking about it. Try to cut down examples as far as possible to still get something you don't understand while having as little as possible that is not essential. You should also experiment with putting some of the examples from lessons 2 and 3 into Prover9 or Prolog, or both, and seeing if things behave as expected.
