docs.text: We now see how the example of reasoning from Lesson 3 about whether there is a power cut or the light bulb in the lamp is dead can be handled both in Prover9 and in Prolog. We will see Prover9 first as Prolog involves an additional topic: negation as failure .
docs.title: Automating the extended example

docs.text: We can input the scenario and goal thus: formulas(assumptions). harryAlone & - harrySwitchedOff -> switchOn. tv -> powerOK. - lampLit & switchOn & powerOK -> bulbDead. tv. - harrySwitchedOff. harryAlone. - lampLit. end_of_list. formulas(goals). bulbDead. end_of_list. The output (which you are not expected to understand at this point) is: 1 harryAlone & -harrySwitchedOff -> switchOn # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 2 tv -> powerOK # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 3 -lampLit & switchOn & powerOK -> bulbDead # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 4 bulbDead # label(non_clause) # label(goal). [goal]. 5 -harryAlone | harrySwitchedOff | switchOn. [clausify(1)]. 6 -tv | powerOK. [clausify(2)]. 7 lampLit | -switchOn | -powerOK | bulbDead. [clausify(3)]. 8 tv. [assumption]. 9 -harrySwitchedOff. [assumption]. 10 harryAlone. [assumption]. 11 -lampLit. [assumption]. 12 -bulbDead. [deny(4)]. 13 powerOK. [back_unit_del(6),unit_del(a,8)]. 14 switchOn. [back_unit_del(5),unit_del(a,10),unit_del(b,9)]. 15 $F. [back_unit_del(7),unit_del(a,11),unit_del(b,14),unit_del(c,13),unit_del(d,12)]. The basic operation of resolution can be seen in the passage from the assumptions tv -> powerOK. tv. to the fact that powerOK holds. We can see the mechanism as follows: 2 tv -> powerOK # label(non_clause). [assumption]. 6 -tv | powerOK. [clausify(2)]. 8 tv. [assumption]. 13 powerOK. [back_unit_del(6),unit_del(a,8)]. What is happening here is this: (step 2) the implication \(\mathit{tv} \to \mathit{powerOK}\) is assumed to hold (step 6) the implication is logically equivalent to \(\neg \mathit{tv} \vee \mathit{powerOK}\) (step 8) we have the assumption \(\mathit{tv}\) (step 13) the only way the two statements in steps 6 and 8 can be true together is for \(\mathit{powerOK}\) to be true.
docs.title: Prover9

docs.text: We can use prolog switchOn :- harryAlone, not(harrySwitchedOff). powerOK :- tv. bulbDead :- not(lampLit), switchOn, powerOK. tv. harrySwitchedOff :- fail. harryAlone. lampLit :- fail. the interaction is: ?- consult('bulb.pl'). true. ?- bulbDead. true. The encoding of the problem in prolog looks strange. Why has it got fail twice in the input? Prolog treats logical negation in a different way to Prover9 and the approach is called negation as failure . We will come back to the power cut example once we have looked at a very simple example to explain about negation as failure.
docs.title: Prolog version

docs.text: A simple comparison between Prover9 and Prolog In Prolog, enter the following in the program area of Swish :- discontiguous(tuesday/0). monday. Here monday is a proposition or a fact. The other part :- discontiguous(tuesday/0). has no logical function. It is there so that Prolog won't complain about never having heard of tuesday. Now, try the goal not(tuesday). Notice what response you get. Now contrast this with Prover9 by using this input file: clear(auto_denials). formulas(assumptions). monday. end_of_list. formulas (goals). -tuesday. end_of_list. You should run Prover9 with this example and see whether the goal is proved from the assumptions. The line clear(auto_denials). does not affect the logic, but generally helps avoid some opaque behaviour from Prover9. Note that this is giving essentially the same task as we just tried in Prolog: we state that the proposition monday holds. We can think of this as meaning today is monday if we want to. we then ask whether the negation of the proposition tuesday holds. You should find that Prover9 and Prolog disagree with each other about this example. The reason is that they understand not to mean quite different things. In Prolog it is not tuesday because it is impossible to prove that it is tuesday. In Prover9 it is not the case that it is not tuesday, because there is no way to prove that it is not tuesday from the given assumptions. The way Prolog treats not is called negation as failure , because if it fails to prove something then it takes the negation of that something to be true. The way Prover9 treats not is the conventional mathematical way for classical logic.
docs.title: Negation in Prolog

docs.text: To return to the powercut example, if we need to assert that some proposition is not true we can say that any attempt to satisfy the proposition as a goal must fail: lampLit :- fail. An alternative way for a goal to fail is simply not to assert it at all. This is what happened in the small example we just saw. Explicit failure has the same effect and would make for more readable code in most cases. Prolog is taking an approach to negation that goes with the closed world assumption that things are false unless we know explicitly they are true. This is familiar in databases. For example, if an organisation wants to know if they have an employee called "Lyra Silvertongue" they might search in a database of all their employees and see if that name is there. If the name is not there they conclude that they have no such employee, despite it not being asserted directly that this person is not an employee. It is called the closed world assumption because it is assumed that the list of employees is complete and there cannot be any that are not recorded in the database. In Prolog, if we want to make a goal including some subgoals which must fail we express it like this switchOn :- harryAlone, not(harrySwitchedOff). Note that in the body of a clause we can use the special Prolog word not , but to assert a fact of the form ' \(x\) is not true' we can say x :- fail. .
docs.title: Expressing not in Prolog.

docs.text:
Lesson 5: CNF and DNF Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: recognise whether a formula is in CNF or DNF or both or neither convert a given propositional formula into CNF by using the rewrite rules given in the lesson convert a given propositional formula into DNF by using the rewrite rules given in the lesson convert a given propositional formula into both principal CNF and principal DNF. Any propositional formula will be logically equivalent to many different formulas. For example, \(p \vee 
eg q\) is equivalent to \(
eg (
eg p \wedge q)\) as well as to \((p \wedge 
eg q) \vee (p \wedge q) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q)\) as well as to \((
eg q \vee p) \wedge (
eg q \vee q)\) . In automated reasoning it is often useful to use formulas which have a special format. Two of these formats are introduced here: the Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) and the Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF). Although we will only use the CNF in detail later, it is instructive to understand how CNF and DNF are conceptually, in an informal sense, 'mirror images' of each other. This duality could be discussed in a precise formal way, but we do not need it in this module. Literals, elementary sums and products We use the following terminology: A variable or the negation of a variable is called a literal . A formula that is a conjunction of literals is called an elementary product . For example \(p \wedge q \wedge 
eg p\) is an elementary product. More examples are \(p\) and \(p \wedge 
eg p\) . A formula that is a disjunction of literals is called an elementary sum . For example \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p\) is an elementary sum. More examples are \(p\) and \(p \vee 
eg p\) . Note that from now we do not usually need to distinguish equivalent formulas which just have disjunctions bracketed in different ways. For example: \(p \vee (q \vee 
eg p)\) and \((p \vee q) \vee 
eg p\) . We allow ourselves to write \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p\) instead, and similarly for conjunctions. We introduce the following normal forms , which are special formats or 'shapes' that a formula may have. A formula may have both or neither of these two forms, or it can have just one of them. A formula is said to be in conjunctive normal form if it is a conjunction of elementary sums. A formula is said to be in disjunctive normal form if it is a disjunction of elementary products. We often abbreviate these to CNF and DNF respectively. Examples \((p \vee q \vee 
eg q) \wedge (p \vee 
eg r) \wedge (r \vee q)\) is in conjunctive normal form \((p \wedge q \wedge 
eg q) \vee (p \wedge 
eg r) \vee (r \wedge q)\) is in disjunctive normal form \((p \vee (q \wedge r)) \wedge (q \vee p) \wedge (p \vee 
eg q)\) is not in CNF nor in DNF \(p \vee 
eg p \vee p \vee 
eg p\) is both in CNF and DNF \(p 	o q\) is not in CNF or DNF, but it is equivalent to \(
eg p \vee q\) which is both. Finding equivalent CNF and DNF for formulas Every formula is equivalent to one formula in CNF and also to one formula in DNF. In fact there will be many different possible CNF or DNF equivalents for a formula. Method for CNF The method to find an equivalent CNF for any formula is to apply the following rewrite rules: \(p 	o q\) is replaced by \(
eg p \vee q\) \(
eg(p \vee q)\) is replaced by \(
eg p \wedge 
eg q\) \(
eg(p \wedge q)\) is replaced by \(
eg p \vee 
eg q\) \(
eg 
eg p\) is replaced by \(p\) . \(p \vee (q \wedge r)\) is replaced by \((p \vee q) \wedge (p \vee r)\) . \((p \wedge q) \vee r\) is replaced by \((p \vee r) \wedge (q \vee r)\) . The rules are applied in the order (1) then ((2) and (3)) then (4) then ((5) and (6)) Example To find a CNF for \(p 	o (
eg(q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s)\) we can use these steps: \( egin{array}{l} p 	o (
eg(q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{Starting~ formula}\[1ex] 
eg p \vee (
eg(q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 1.}\[1ex] 
eg p \vee (
eg(
eg q \vee 
eg r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 1.}\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ((
eg
eg q \wedge 
eg 
eg r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 2.}\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ( (q \wedge r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 4~ twice.}\[1ex] (
eg p \vee (q \wedge r)) \wedge (
eg p \vee s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 5.}\[1ex] ((
eg p \vee q) \wedge (
eg p \vee r)) \wedge (
eg p \vee s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 5~ again.}\[1ex] (
eg p \vee q) \wedge (
eg p \vee r) \wedge (
eg p \vee s) & \mathsf{just~ removing~ unnecessary~ parentheses.}\[1ex] \end{array}\) Method for DNF The method to find an equivalent DNF for any formula is to apply the following rewrite rules: \(p 	o q\) is replaced by \(
eg p \vee q\) \(
eg(p \vee q)\) is replaced by \(
eg p \wedge 
eg q\) \(
eg 
eg p\) is replaced by \(p\) . \(p \wedge (q \vee r)\) is replaced by \((p \wedge q) \vee (p \wedge r)\) . \((p \vee q) \wedge r\) is replaced by \((p \wedge r) \vee (q \wedge r)\) . The rules are applied in the order (1) then ((2) and (3)) then (4) then ((5) and (6)) Example To find a DNF for \(p 	o ((q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s)\) we can follow these steps. You should check you can tell which rule is used at each step. \( egin{array}{l} p 	o ((q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s)\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ((q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s)\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ((
eg q \vee 
eg r) \wedge s)\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ( (
eg q \wedge s) \vee (
eg r \wedge s))\[1ex] 
eg p \vee (
eg q \wedge s) \vee (
eg r \wedge s)\ \end{array}\) Principal DNF and CNF In general there are lots of different formulas in DNF which are all logically equivalent to a given formula. The same is true of CNF. To get an essentially unique normal form, we need to use the principal DNF or principal CNF. These are 'essentially unique' in the sense that they only differ in the ordering of the elementary sums or products involved. To find the principal DNF These examples show that a given formula can have many logical equivalents in DNF. Examples All these four DNF are equivalent to each other \( egin{array}{l} p \vee (q \wedge r) \vee 
eg r\[1ex] (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge 
eg r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r) \vee (q \wedge r) \vee 
eg r\[1ex] (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (q \wedge r) \vee 
eg r\[1ex] p \vee (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge q \wedge r)\vee 
eg r \end{array}\) It is possible to find a DNF which is essentially unique for any formula. This is called the principal DNF and is easy to understand if we think about the connection between the conjunctions appearing and the rows in a truth table. A DNF for a formula tells us which rows in its truth table give the result true. For \(
eg p \vee (
eg q \wedge s) \vee (
eg r \wedge s)\) we can see that a row in the table will give \(T\) when \(p\) is \(F\) , or \(q\) is \(F\) and \(s\) is \(T\) , or \(r\) is \(F\) and \(s\) is \(T\) . We can express this diagrammatically; rows of other forms have \(F\) in final column. \( egin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline p&q&r&s&
eg p \vee (
eg q \wedge s) \vee (
eg r \wedge s)\ \hline F & ? & ? & ? & T\ \hline ?&F&?&T&T\ \hline ?&?&F&T&T\ \hline \end{array}\) In this example none of the conjunctions involves all of the four variables in the formula. We can see that if it did then it would correspond to a unique row in the truth table. For example, \(p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r \wedge 
eg s\) corresponds to the row in which \(p\) and \(r\) are assigned \(T\) and in which \(q\) and \(s\) are assigned \(F\) . The principal DNF for a formula is just the disjunction of all the conjunctions involving all the variables for which the corresponding row in the truth table has \(T\) as its result. Finding principal DNF of \((p 	o q) 	o r\) from the truth table. \( egin{array}{cr|c|c|c|c|c|l} &&p & q & r & p 	o q & (p 	o q) 	o r\ \hline p \wedge q \wedge r & \Longrightarrow & T & T & T & T & T & \Longleftarrow \ && T & T & F & T & F &\ p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r & \Longrightarrow & T & F & T & F & T &\Longleftarrow \ p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r & \Longrightarrow & T & F & F & F & T &\Longleftarrow \ 
eg p \wedge q \wedge r & \Longrightarrow & F & T & T & T & T &\Longleftarrow \ && F & T & F & T & F &\ 
eg p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r & \Longrightarrow & F & F & T & T & T &\Longleftarrow \ &&F & F & F & T & F &\ \hline \end{array}\) The answer of the following formula: \[ (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r) \vee (
eg p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r).\] Writing out the full truth table is not the only way to find the principal DNF. We can work from a DNF which is not principal which we obtained by rewriting the formula. Example Find the principal DNF of \((p 	o q) 	o r\) without using a truth table. First we find a DNF by rewriting: \(\left\{ egin{array}{l} (p 	o q) 	o r \[1ex] 
eg (p 	o q) \vee r \[1ex] 
eg (
eg p \vee q) \vee r \[1ex] (
eg 
eg p \wedge 
eg q) \vee r \[1ex] ( p \wedge 
eg q) \vee r \end{array}ight.\) Second: we expand each of the conjunctions so it becomes a disjunction of conjunctions where all the variables appear in each conjunction. \(( p \wedge 
eg q)\) becomes \(( p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\vee ( p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r)\) \(r\) becomes \((p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\) The second step could alternatively be done by rewriting \(( p \wedge 
eg q)\) to \(( p \wedge 
eg q) \wedge (r \vee 
eg r)\) and rewriting \(r\) to \((p \vee 
eg p) \wedge (q \vee 
eg q) \wedge r\) and then rewriting to DNF using the distributive laws. Third: write down the terms we have, throwing out any duplicates: \(( p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\vee ( p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\) Observe that the principal DNF of a tautology involving \(n\) variables will have \(2^n\) conjunctions, as we have to pick out every row in the truth table. But the principal DNF of a contradiction would want to pick out none of the rows, so we will just allow \(F\) as a principal DNF. It is correct to think of a disjunction of no terms as false, but if you find the idea confusing it's not important here. In any case, it is difficult to denote an empty disjunction by an empty space, so allowing \(F\) as a formula itself is convenient. To find principal CNF It is a similar but dual story for the principal CNF. The idea can be summarised as follows, but the example shows how it works. Principal DNF: Find all the values of variables for which the formula can be true , and say at least one of these must happen Principal CNF: Find all the values of variables for which the formula can be false , and say none of these must happen. Example Find the principal CNF of \((p 	o q) 	o r\) from the truth table. \[ egin{array}{cr|c|c|c|c|c|l} &&p & q & r & p 	o q & (p 	o q) 	o r\ \hline & & T & T & T & T & T & \ 
eg p \vee 
eg q \vee r &\Longrightarrow & T & T & F & T & F & \Longleftarrow 	ext{ to get $T$ avoid this row} \ && T & F & T & F & T & \ && T & F & F & F & T & \ && F & T & T & T & T & \ p \vee 
eg q \vee r &\Longrightarrow & F & T & F & T & F & \Longleftarrow 	ext{ to get $T$ avoid this row} \ && F & F & T & T & T & \ p \vee q \vee r &\Longrightarrow &F & F & F & T & F & \Longleftarrow 	ext{ to get $T$ avoid this row} \ \hline \end{array}\] The answer is \((
eg p \vee 
eg q \vee r) \wedge (p \vee 
eg q \vee r ) \wedge (p \vee q \vee r)\) Note that, for example, \((
eg p \vee 
eg q \vee r)\) is true for the values of the variables in every row except the one indicated. When this one conjunction is true we must have values of variables not corresponding to this false row. All the three conjunctions are true if and only if we have values of variables corresponding to none of the true rows. Just as for principal DNF you don't need to construct the truth table; you can take any CNF and add in extra variables. Observe that the principal CNF of a contradiction involving \(n\) variables will have \(2^n\) disjunctions, as we have to avoid every row in the truth table. But the principal CNF of a tautology would want to avoid none of the rows, so we will just allow \(T\) as a principal CNF. It is correct to think of a conjunction of no terms as true, but if you find the idea confusing it's not important here. A summary of the definitions in CNF and DNF This summary is also available as a separate PDF document which highlights the way the concepts come in dual pairs. The summary includes the notions of minterm and maxterm which were not used above. Download PDF Elementary sum A propositional logic formula is an elementary sum iff it is a single literal, or it consists of two or more literals like this: \(\ell_1 \vee \ell_2 \vee \cdots \vee \ell_m\) Examples: \(p\) \(
eg p\) \(p \vee p\) \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p \vee q\) \(p \vee 
eg q \vee r\) Elementary product A propositional logic formula is an elementary product iff it is a single literal, or it consists of two or more literals like this: \(\ell_1 \wedge \ell_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge \ell_m\) Examples: \(p\) \(
eg p\) \(p \wedge p\) \(p \wedge q \wedge 
eg p \wedge q\) \(p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r\) CNF A formula is in CNF iff if is \(T\) (true), or it is one elementary sum, or it consists of two or more elementary sums like this \(e_1 \wedge e_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge e_m\) Examples: \(T\) (by 1) \(p\) (by 2) \(
eg p\) (by 2) \(p \vee p\) (by 2) \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p \vee q\) (by 2) \((p \vee 
eg q \vee r) \wedge (p \vee q \vee 
eg p)\) (by 3) DNF A formula is in DNF iff if is \(F\) (false), or it is one elementary product, or it consists of two or more elementary products like this:\ \(e_1 \vee e_2 \vee \cdots \vee e_m\) Examples: \(F\) (by 1) \(p\) (by 2) \(
eg p\) (by 2) \(p \wedge p\) (by 2) \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p \vee q\) (by 3) \((p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge 
eg p)\) (by 3) Maxterm An elementary sum is a maxterm over a given set of variables iff it uses every variable in the set exactly once. Examples: \(p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(
eg p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(p \vee q\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \(p \vee q \vee 
eg r\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\) Non-Examples \(
eg p\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \(p \vee 
eg q\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\) Minterm An elementary product is a minterm over a given set of variables iff it uses every variable in the set exactly once. Examples: \(p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(
eg p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(p \wedge q\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \(p \wedge q \wedge 
eg r\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\) Non-Examples \(
eg p\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \(p \wedge 
eg q\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\) Principal CNF A formula is in principal CNF over a set of variables iff if is in CNF, and every elementary sum is a maxterm (over the set) Examples: \(T\) \(p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(p \vee q\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \((p \vee q) \wedge (
eg p \vee q)\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \((p \vee 
eg q \vee r) \wedge (p \vee q \vee 
eg p)\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\) Principal DNF A formula is in principal DNF over a set of variables iff if is in DNF, and every elementary product is a minterm (over the set) Examples: \(F\) \(p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(p \wedge q\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \((p \wedge q) \vee (
eg p \wedge q)\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \((p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge 
eg p)\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\) The 16 principal CNF and DNF formulas in two variables Here is a list of all the 16 principal CNF and all the principal DNF formulas in two variables, \(p\) and \(q\) . There is one formula of each kind for each of the 16 possible truth tables in two variables. For each of the 16 cases the truth table for the formula is given in an abbreviated format. In the following example the abbreviated format is shown alongside the more usual tabular form, with the final column showing the truth value associated to the given input values for \(p\) and \(q\) . The notation involving \(\mapsto\) is one way of describing a function. A truth table with two propositional variables spacifies a function where the inputs are the pairs of truth values and the result of the function is the value given in the final column. eg p \wedge q) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q) \end{array}\] \[  egin{array}{rc} \mathsf{Truth~ table} &  egin{array}{cccc} TT \mapsto F & TF \mapsto T & FT \mapsto F & FF \mapsto T \end{array} \[1ex] \mathsf{PCNF}& (
eg p \vee

eg q) \wedge (

eg p \wedge q) \end{array}\] \[  egin{array}{rc} \mathsf{Truth~ table} &  egin{array}{cccc}TT \mapsto T & TF \mapsto F & FT \mapsto F & FF \mapsto T \end{array} \[1ex] \mathsf{PCNF}& (

eg p \vee 
eg q) \wedge (p\vee q) \ \mathsf{PDNF}& ( p \wedge 
eg q) \vee (
eg p \wedge q) \end{array}\] Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 5, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise about CNF and DNF formulas. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit-1 Lesson 5' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.
docs.title: Lesson 5 CNF and DNF

docs.text:
Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: recognise whether a formula is in CNF or DNF or both or neither convert a given propositional formula into CNF by using the rewrite rules given in the lesson convert a given propositional formula into DNF by using the rewrite rules given in the lesson convert a given propositional formula into both principal CNF and principal DNF. Any propositional formula will be logically equivalent to many different formulas. For example, \(p \vee 
eg q\) is equivalent to \(
eg (
eg p \wedge q)\) as well as to \((p \wedge 
eg q) \vee (p \wedge q) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q)\) as well as to \((
eg q \vee p) \wedge (
eg q \vee q)\) . In automated reasoning it is often useful to use formulas which have a special format. Two of these formats are introduced here: the Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) and the Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF). Although we will only use the CNF in detail later, it is instructive to understand how CNF and DNF are conceptually, in an informal sense, 'mirror images' of each other. This duality could be discussed in a precise formal way, but we do not need it in this module.
docs.title: Lesson 5: CNF and DNF

docs.text:
We use the following terminology: A variable or the negation of a variable is called a literal . A formula that is a conjunction of literals is called an elementary product . For example \(p \wedge q \wedge 
eg p\) is an elementary product. More examples are \(p\) and \(p \wedge 
eg p\) . A formula that is a disjunction of literals is called an elementary sum . For example \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p\) is an elementary sum. More examples are \(p\) and \(p \vee 
eg p\) . Note that from now we do not usually need to distinguish equivalent formulas which just have disjunctions bracketed in different ways. For example: \(p \vee (q \vee 
eg p)\) and \((p \vee q) \vee 
eg p\) . We allow ourselves to write \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p\) instead, and similarly for conjunctions. We introduce the following normal forms , which are special formats or 'shapes' that a formula may have. A formula may have both or neither of these two forms, or it can have just one of them. A formula is said to be in conjunctive normal form if it is a conjunction of elementary sums. A formula is said to be in disjunctive normal form if it is a disjunction of elementary products. We often abbreviate these to CNF and DNF respectively. Examples \((p \vee q \vee 
eg q) \wedge (p \vee 
eg r) \wedge (r \vee q)\) is in conjunctive normal form \((p \wedge q \wedge 
eg q) \vee (p \wedge 
eg r) \vee (r \wedge q)\) is in disjunctive normal form \((p \vee (q \wedge r)) \wedge (q \vee p) \wedge (p \vee 
eg q)\) is not in CNF nor in DNF \(p \vee 
eg p \vee p \vee 
eg p\) is both in CNF and DNF \(p 	o q\) is not in CNF or DNF, but it is equivalent to \(
eg p \vee q\) which is both.
docs.title: Literals, elementary sums and products

docs.text: Every formula is equivalent to one formula in CNF and also to one formula in DNF. In fact there will be many different possible CNF or DNF equivalents for a formula.
docs.title: Finding equivalent CNF and DNF for formulas

docs.text:
The method to find an equivalent CNF for any formula is to apply the following rewrite rules: \(p 	o q\) is replaced by \(
eg p \vee q\) \(
eg(p \vee q)\) is replaced by \(
eg p \wedge 
eg q\) \(
eg(p \wedge q)\) is replaced by \(
eg p \vee 
eg q\) \(
eg 
eg p\) is replaced by \(p\) . \(p \vee (q \wedge r)\) is replaced by \((p \vee q) \wedge (p \vee r)\) . \((p \wedge q) \vee r\) is replaced by \((p \vee r) \wedge (q \vee r)\) . The rules are applied in the order (1) then ((2) and (3)) then (4) then ((5) and (6)) Example To find a CNF for \(p 	o (
eg(q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s)\) we can use these steps: \( egin{array}{l} p 	o (
eg(q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{Starting~ formula}\[1ex] 
eg p \vee (
eg(q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 1.}\[1ex] 
eg p \vee (
eg(
eg q \vee 
eg r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 1.}\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ((
eg
eg q \wedge 
eg 
eg r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 2.}\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ( (q \wedge r) \wedge s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 4~ twice.}\[1ex] (
eg p \vee (q \wedge r)) \wedge (
eg p \vee s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 5.}\[1ex] ((
eg p \vee q) \wedge (
eg p \vee r)) \wedge (
eg p \vee s) & \mathsf{using~ rule~ 5~ again.}\[1ex] (
eg p \vee q) \wedge (
eg p \vee r) \wedge (
eg p \vee s) & \mathsf{just~ removing~ unnecessary~ parentheses.}\[1ex] \end{array}\)
docs.title: Method for CNF

docs.text:
The method to find an equivalent DNF for any formula is to apply the following rewrite rules: \(p 	o q\) is replaced by \(
eg p \vee q\) \(
eg(p \vee q)\) is replaced by \(
eg p \wedge 
eg q\) \(
eg 
eg p\) is replaced by \(p\) . \(p \wedge (q \vee r)\) is replaced by \((p \wedge q) \vee (p \wedge r)\) . \((p \vee q) \wedge r\) is replaced by \((p \wedge r) \vee (q \wedge r)\) . The rules are applied in the order (1) then ((2) and (3)) then (4) then ((5) and (6)) Example To find a DNF for \(p 	o ((q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s)\) we can follow these steps. You should check you can tell which rule is used at each step. \( egin{array}{l} p 	o ((q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s)\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ((q 	o 
eg r) \wedge s)\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ((
eg q \vee 
eg r) \wedge s)\[1ex] 
eg p \vee ( (
eg q \wedge s) \vee (
eg r \wedge s))\[1ex] 
eg p \vee (
eg q \wedge s) \vee (
eg r \wedge s)\ \end{array}\)
docs.title: Method for DNF

docs.text: In general there are lots of different formulas in DNF which are all logically equivalent to a given formula. The same is true of CNF. To get an essentially unique normal form, we need to use the principal DNF or principal CNF. These are 'essentially unique' in the sense that they only differ in the ordering of the elementary sums or products involved.
docs.title: Principal DNF and CNF

docs.text:
These examples show that a given formula can have many logical equivalents in DNF. Examples All these four DNF are equivalent to each other \( egin{array}{l} p \vee (q \wedge r) \vee 
eg r\[1ex] (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge 
eg r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r) \vee (q \wedge r) \vee 
eg r\[1ex] (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (q \wedge r) \vee 
eg r\[1ex] p \vee (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge q \wedge r)\vee 
eg r \end{array}\) It is possible to find a DNF which is essentially unique for any formula. This is called the principal DNF and is easy to understand if we think about the connection between the conjunctions appearing and the rows in a truth table. A DNF for a formula tells us which rows in its truth table give the result true. For \(
eg p \vee (
eg q \wedge s) \vee (
eg r \wedge s)\) we can see that a row in the table will give \(T\) when \(p\) is \(F\) , or \(q\) is \(F\) and \(s\) is \(T\) , or \(r\) is \(F\) and \(s\) is \(T\) . We can express this diagrammatically; rows of other forms have \(F\) in final column. \( egin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline p&q&r&s&
eg p \vee (
eg q \wedge s) \vee (
eg r \wedge s)\ \hline F & ? & ? & ? & T\ \hline ?&F&?&T&T\ \hline ?&?&F&T&T\ \hline \end{array}\) In this example none of the conjunctions involves all of the four variables in the formula. We can see that if it did then it would correspond to a unique row in the truth table. For example, \(p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r \wedge 
eg s\) corresponds to the row in which \(p\) and \(r\) are assigned \(T\) and in which \(q\) and \(s\) are assigned \(F\) . The principal DNF for a formula is just the disjunction of all the conjunctions involving all the variables for which the corresponding row in the truth table has \(T\) as its result. Finding principal DNF of \((p 	o q) 	o r\) from the truth table. \( egin{array}{cr|c|c|c|c|c|l} &&p & q & r & p 	o q & (p 	o q) 	o r\ \hline p \wedge q \wedge r & \Longrightarrow & T & T & T & T & T & \Longleftarrow \ && T & T & F & T & F &\ p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r & \Longrightarrow & T & F & T & F & T &\Longleftarrow \ p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r & \Longrightarrow & T & F & F & F & T &\Longleftarrow \ 
eg p \wedge q \wedge r & \Longrightarrow & F & T & T & T & T &\Longleftarrow \ && F & T & F & T & F &\ 
eg p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r & \Longrightarrow & F & F & T & T & T &\Longleftarrow \ &&F & F & F & T & F &\ \hline \end{array}\) The answer of the following formula: \[ (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r) \vee (
eg p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r).\] Writing out the full truth table is not the only way to find the principal DNF. We can work from a DNF which is not principal which we obtained by rewriting the formula. Example Find the principal DNF of \((p 	o q) 	o r\) without using a truth table. First we find a DNF by rewriting: \(\left\{ egin{array}{l} (p 	o q) 	o r \[1ex] 
eg (p 	o q) \vee r \[1ex] 
eg (
eg p \vee q) \vee r \[1ex] (
eg 
eg p \wedge 
eg q) \vee r \[1ex] ( p \wedge 
eg q) \vee r \end{array}ight.\) Second: we expand each of the conjunctions so it becomes a disjunction of conjunctions where all the variables appear in each conjunction. \(( p \wedge 
eg q)\) becomes \(( p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\vee ( p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r)\) \(r\) becomes \((p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\) The second step could alternatively be done by rewriting \(( p \wedge 
eg q)\) to \(( p \wedge 
eg q) \wedge (r \vee 
eg r)\) and rewriting \(r\) to \((p \vee 
eg p) \wedge (q \vee 
eg q) \wedge r\) and then rewriting to DNF using the distributive laws. Third: write down the terms we have, throwing out any duplicates: \(( p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\vee ( p \wedge 
eg q \wedge 
eg r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (
eg p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r)\) Observe that the principal DNF of a tautology involving \(n\) variables will have \(2^n\) conjunctions, as we have to pick out every row in the truth table. But the principal DNF of a contradiction would want to pick out none of the rows, so we will just allow \(F\) as a principal DNF. It is correct to think of a disjunction of no terms as false, but if you find the idea confusing it's not important here. In any case, it is difficult to denote an empty disjunction by an empty space, so allowing \(F\) as a formula itself is convenient.
docs.title: To find the principal DNF

docs.text:
It is a similar but dual story for the principal CNF. The idea can be summarised as follows, but the example shows how it works. Principal DNF: Find all the values of variables for which the formula can be true , and say at least one of these must happen Principal CNF: Find all the values of variables for which the formula can be false , and say none of these must happen. Example Find the principal CNF of \((p 	o q) 	o r\) from the truth table. \[ egin{array}{cr|c|c|c|c|c|l} &&p & q & r & p 	o q & (p 	o q) 	o r\ \hline & & T & T & T & T & T & \ 
eg p \vee 
eg q \vee r &\Longrightarrow & T & T & F & T & F & \Longleftarrow 	ext{ to get $T$ avoid this row} \ && T & F & T & F & T & \ && T & F & F & F & T & \ && F & T & T & T & T & \ p \vee 
eg q \vee r &\Longrightarrow & F & T & F & T & F & \Longleftarrow 	ext{ to get $T$ avoid this row} \ && F & F & T & T & T & \ p \vee q \vee r &\Longrightarrow &F & F & F & T & F & \Longleftarrow 	ext{ to get $T$ avoid this row} \ \hline \end{array}\] The answer is \((
eg p \vee 
eg q \vee r) \wedge (p \vee 
eg q \vee r ) \wedge (p \vee q \vee r)\) Note that, for example, \((
eg p \vee 
eg q \vee r)\) is true for the values of the variables in every row except the one indicated. When this one conjunction is true we must have values of variables not corresponding to this false row. All the three conjunctions are true if and only if we have values of variables corresponding to none of the true rows. Just as for principal DNF you don't need to construct the truth table; you can take any CNF and add in extra variables. Observe that the principal CNF of a contradiction involving \(n\) variables will have \(2^n\) disjunctions, as we have to avoid every row in the truth table. But the principal CNF of a tautology would want to avoid none of the rows, so we will just allow \(T\) as a principal CNF. It is correct to think of a conjunction of no terms as true, but if you find the idea confusing it's not important here.
docs.title: To find principal CNF

docs.text: This summary is also available as a separate PDF document which highlights the way the concepts come in dual pairs. The summary includes the notions of minterm and maxterm which were not used above. Download PDF
docs.title: A summary of the definitions in CNF and DNF

docs.text:
A propositional logic formula is an elementary sum iff it is a single literal, or it consists of two or more literals like this: \(\ell_1 \vee \ell_2 \vee \cdots \vee \ell_m\) Examples: \(p\) \(
eg p\) \(p \vee p\) \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p \vee q\) \(p \vee 
eg q \vee r\)
docs.title: Elementary sum

docs.text:
A propositional logic formula is an elementary product iff it is a single literal, or it consists of two or more literals like this: \(\ell_1 \wedge \ell_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge \ell_m\) Examples: \(p\) \(
eg p\) \(p \wedge p\) \(p \wedge q \wedge 
eg p \wedge q\) \(p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r\)
docs.title: Elementary product

docs.text:
A formula is in CNF iff if is \(T\) (true), or it is one elementary sum, or it consists of two or more elementary sums like this \(e_1 \wedge e_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge e_m\) Examples: \(T\) (by 1) \(p\) (by 2) \(
eg p\) (by 2) \(p \vee p\) (by 2) \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p \vee q\) (by 2) \((p \vee 
eg q \vee r) \wedge (p \vee q \vee 
eg p)\) (by 3)
docs.title: CNF

docs.text:
A formula is in DNF iff if is \(F\) (false), or it is one elementary product, or it consists of two or more elementary products like this:\ \(e_1 \vee e_2 \vee \cdots \vee e_m\) Examples: \(F\) (by 1) \(p\) (by 2) \(
eg p\) (by 2) \(p \wedge p\) (by 2) \(p \vee q \vee 
eg p \vee q\) (by 3) \((p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge 
eg p)\) (by 3)
docs.title: DNF

docs.text:
An elementary sum is a maxterm over a given set of variables iff it uses every variable in the set exactly once. Examples: \(p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(
eg p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(p \vee q\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \(p \vee q \vee 
eg r\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\) Non-Examples \(
eg p\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \(p \vee 
eg q\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\)
docs.title: Maxterm

docs.text:
An elementary product is a minterm over a given set of variables iff it uses every variable in the set exactly once. Examples: \(p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(
eg p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(p \wedge q\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \(p \wedge q \wedge 
eg r\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\) Non-Examples \(
eg p\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \(p \wedge 
eg q\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\)
docs.title: Minterm

docs.text:
A formula is in principal CNF over a set of variables iff if is in CNF, and every elementary sum is a maxterm (over the set) Examples: \(T\) \(p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(p \vee q\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \((p \vee q) \wedge (
eg p \vee q)\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \((p \vee 
eg q \vee r) \wedge (p \vee q \vee 
eg p)\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\)
docs.title: Principal CNF

docs.text:
A formula is in principal DNF over a set of variables iff if is in DNF, and every elementary product is a minterm (over the set) Examples: \(F\) \(p\) over \(\{p\}\) \(p \wedge q\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \((p \wedge q) \vee (
eg p \wedge q)\) over \(\{p,q\}\) \((p \wedge 
eg q \wedge r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge 
eg p)\) over \(\{p,q,r\}\)
docs.title: Principal DNF

docs.text:
Here is a list of all the 16 principal CNF and all the principal DNF formulas in two variables, \(p\) and \(q\) . There is one formula of each kind for each of the 16 possible truth tables in two variables. For each of the 16 cases the truth table for the formula is given in an abbreviated format. In the following example the abbreviated format is shown alongside the more usual tabular form, with the final column showing the truth value associated to the given input values for \(p\) and \(q\) . The notation involving \(\mapsto\) is one way of describing a function. A truth table with two propositional variables spacifies a function where the inputs are the pairs of truth values and the result of the function is the value given in the final column. Sometimes the arrow \(\mapsto\) is pronounced "goes to" or "maps to". \[  egin{array}{cccc} TT \mapsto T & TF \mapsto F & FT \mapsto F & FF \mapsto T \end{array} \qquad \qquad  egin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & \; \ \hline T & T & T \ T & F & F \ F & T & F \ F & F & T \ \hline \end{array} \] \[  egin{array}{rc} \mathsf{Truth~ table} &  egin{array}{cccc} TT \mapsto T & TF \mapsto T & FT \mapsto T & FF \mapsto T \end{array}\[1ex] \mathsf{PCNF} & T \ \mathsf{PDNF} & (p\wedge q) \vee (p\wedge

eg p\vee

eg p\vee q) \wedge (p\vee 
eg q) \wedge ( p\vee q) \ \mathsf{PDNF}& p \wedge q \end{array}\] \[  egin{array}{rc} \mathsf{Truth~ table} &  egin{array}{cccc} TT \mapsto T & TF \mapsto T & FT \mapsto F & FF \mapsto F \end{array} \[1ex] \mathsf{PCNF}& ( p\vee 
eg q) \wedge (p \vee q) \ \mathsf{PDNF}& (p\wedge q) \vee ( p\wedge

eg p\wedge

eg p \wedge q) \end{array}\] Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 5, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise about CNF and DNF formulas. docs.title: The 16 principal CNF and DNF formulas in two variables

docs.text:
Lesson 1: Introducing first order logic In propositional logic there is only one kind of thing: statements (propositions). These can be true or false. The logic provides ways of building up complex statements out of simpler ones. But propositional logic is not expressive enough for many things we want to represent and reason about. First order logic , also called predicate logic or predicate calculus , is more expressive because as well as statements that can be true or false, there are also individual things for the statements to talk about. Lesson 1 Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: distinguish relations and relation symbols (and so on) propose language elements to represent given scenarios distinguish between terms and formulas use quantifiers and other parts of first order logic to write syntactically correct formulas. What first order logic adds to propositional logic In first order logic we can represent much more of the following situation than we could in propositional logic: All of the red parcels were delivered yesterday. The parcel with the new component was one of the red ones. Every parcel delivered in the last 10 days was received undamaged. A consequence of those statements is: The new component was received undamaged The novelties here (not in propositional logic) are: things (e.g. parcels) in addition to propositions and statements, properties things may have (a thing may be a parcel; it may be red), relationships between things (a particular parcel contains certain things), The words 'all', 'every' and 'one of' are used to talk about collections of individuals. To work with the statements we would also need a way of representing things such as: Yesterday is within the last 10 days If a parcel contained something and was received, then the contents were received. Relations, predicates, properties It can be confusing that many different words are often used to talk about the same things. Consider these examples: Manchester is between Liverpool and Leeds Sally is older than Fred Sally is tall It is true that it is raining We might represent these in first order logic by: \(\mathit{between}(\mathit{Manchester}, \mathit{Liverpool}, \mathit{Leeds})\) \(\mathit{olderThan}(\mathit{Sally}, \mathit{Fred})\) \(\mathit{tall}(\mathit{Sally})\) \(\mathit{itIsRaining}\) In this unit, we use the following terminology: \(\mathit{between}\) is a relation symbol of arity 3; it takes 3 arguments \(\mathit{olderThan}\) is a relation symbol of arity 2; it takes 2 arguments \(\mathit{tall}\) is a relation symbol of arity 1; it takes 1 argument \(\mathit{itIsRaining}\) is a relation symbol of arity 0; takes 0 arguments However, you might come across alternative conventions elsewhere. For example, people often: say predicate symbol instead of relation symbol drop the word 'symbol' if the difference between a relation and a name for a relation is obvious or unhelpful say property instead of predicate or relation for arity 1 say proposition symbol or propositional constant for a predicate of arity 0 call things of arity 0 constants call things of arity 1 unary call things of arity 2 binary Parcel delivery example This gives an example of how the earlier statements about parcels can be represented in first order logic. This is only one possibility. Different options include having \(\mathit{Undamaged}\) as a unary relation symbol, rather than as a constant. The treatment of time is very simple and would not necessarily extend to different examples. \( orall x\, (\mathit{parcel}(x) \wedge \mathit{red}(x) 	o \mathit{delivered}(x, \mathit{Yesterday}))\) \(\exists x\,(\mathit{parcel}(x) \wedge \mathit{red}(x) \wedge \mathit{contains}(x, \mathit{NewComponent}))\) \( orall x\, (\mathit{parcel}(x) \wedge \mathit{delivered}(x, \mathit{InLast10Days}) 	o \mathit{received}(x, \mathit{Undamaged}))\) \( orall x\, (\mathit{delivered}(x, \mathit{Yesterday}) 	o \mathit{delivered}(x, \mathit{InLast10Days}))\) \( orall x\,  orall y\, ((\mathit{contains}(x,y) \wedge \mathit{received}(x, \mathit{Undamaged})) 	o \mathit{received}(y, \mathit{Undamaged}))\) and we should be able to deduce: \(\mathit{received}(\mathit{NewComponent}, \mathit{Undamaged}))\) The language The language is the basis of the syntax of our logic. What is described is only one possibility for the syntax of first order logic. It is made out of what are called symbols (these do not always have to be single characters - a word can be a 'symbol'). Symbols come in two flavours: logical, and non-logical. The non-logical symbols depend on the application, the logical ones are always the same. Certain strings of these symbols will be valid expressions . There are two kinds of expressions: terms and formulas . Terms can refer to individuals, whereas formulas (also called formulae) model statements about individuals. Logical symbols These are the logical symbols: Punctuation : parentheses and commas: ( and ) and , Connectives : \(\vee\) , \(\wedge\) , \(
eg\) , \(	o\) Quantifiers : \( orall\) , \(\exists\) Variables : an infinite supply of these, usually written \(x\) , \(y\) etc and sometimes \(x_1\) , \(x_2\) , etc. Non-logical symbols The non-logical symbols consist of Constant symbols For specific individual things. For Example: \(\mathit{Yesterday}\) . There is no fixed convention, but I have tried to use upper case (capital) letters at the start of constants to help distinguish them from relation symbols. Relation symbols each with specified arity (no. of inputs \(\geqslant 1\) ) For relationships and properties. For Example: \(\mathit{between}\) , \(\mathit{red}\) . Proposition symbols ( \(0\) -ary relation symbols) For Example: \(\mathit{itIsRaining}\) , etc Terms Terms are linguistic entities that can refer to individual things. Don't confuse terms with formulas , which can have truth values. To give an analogy, in English 'the cat is happy' is a sentence. Within that sentence 'the cat' refers to a particular individual thing in the world. This is like being a term in first order logic. The sentence as a whole is something that is true or false. This sentence is like a formula. The rules for building terms are very simple: Every variable is a term. Every constant symbol is a term. Nothing else is a term. If we add function symbols this becomes slightly more complicated. Function symbols are an additional complication which are more useful for modelling aspects of mathematical practice than for most applications of knowledge representation in AI. To give an example, we can model one person being the mother of another in two ways. (For the purposes of the example about functions, we have to imagine that everybody has exactly one mother.) as a relationship that holds between two individuals, or as a function that given a person as input, returns their mother as output. The first of these approaches is more flexible. In the second approach we are forced to make sense of 'the mother of the mother of the mother of … the mother of someone' where there is no limit at all to the number of generations hidden in the … . Formulas Formulas are things in the language that can have truth values. Don't confuse formulas with terms, which can refer to individual things. Formulas are also sometimes called well formed formulas or wffs , just as in the case of propositional logic. The rules for formulas: Every propositional symbol is a formula. If \(P\) is a predicate symbol of arity \(n \geqslant 1\) , and \(t_1, \ldots, t_n\) are terms, then \(P(t_1, \ldots, t_n)\) is a formula. If \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) are formulas then these are formulas: \(
eg \alpha\) \((\alpha \vee  eta)\) \((\alpha \wedge  eta)\) If \(x\) is a variable, and \(\alpha\) is a formula, then \( orall x \; \alpha\) and \(\exists x \; \alpha\) are formulas. Nothing else is a formula. Note that the definition of what it means to be a formula is recursive. The informal idea to have at the moment is that \[ \left.  egin{array}{l}  orall x \;\alpha\[1ex] \exists x \;\alpha\[1ex] 
eg \alpha\[1ex] (\alpha \vee  eta)\[1ex] (\alpha \wedge  eta)\[1ex] (\alpha 	o  eta) \end{array} ight\} 	ext{ means } \left\{  egin{array}{l} 	ext{For all } x 	ext{ it is that case that } \alpha\[1ex] 	ext{There is some } x 	ext{ such that } \alpha\[1ex] 	ext{Not } \alpha\[1ex] \alpha 	ext{ or }  eta\[1ex] \alpha 	ext{ and }  eta\[1ex] \alpha 	ext{ implies }  eta \end{array} ight. \] Beware that 'or' is inclusive, 'some' means one or more, 'all' really means literally all, and not all except one or two. Parentheses get left out if there is no danger of ambiguity. Do not confuse terms with formulas You can imagine someone saying: When was the paint delivered? Not yesterday. You might be tempted to represent that by \(\mathit{delivered}(\mathit{ThePaint}, 
eg \mathit{Yesterday})\) , turning the informal 'not yesterday' into the formal \(
eg \mathit{Yesterday}\) . This does not work. You can only use the logical connectives with statements that are capable of being true or false. The word \(\mathit{Yesterday}\) stands for an individual thing (a specific time), not something that can be true or false. You need to say \(
eg \mathit{delivered}(\mathit{ThePaint}, \mathit{Yesterday}).\) Check your understanding Constant symbols : \(\mathit{Homer}\) , \(\mathit{Bart}\) , \(\mathit{Pink}\) , \(\mathit{Yellow}\) , \(\mathit{TheDonut}\) Relation symbols (arity 1): \(\mathit{isAPerson}\) , \(\mathit{isAColour}\) , \(\mathit{isEdible}\) Relation symbols (arity 2): \(\mathit{hasColour}\) , \(\mathit{likes}\) . Proposition symbols: \(\mathit{itIsTuesday}\) , and where \(x\) , \(y\) , \(z\) are variables. Which of the following are formulas according to the rules? \(\mathit{isAColour}(\mathit{Pink}) \wedge \mathit{hasColour}(\mathit{TheDonut}, \mathit{Yellow})\) \( orall x \; (\mathit{likes}(\mathit{Homer}, x) \vee 
eg \mathit{isEdible}(x) )\) \( orall x \; 
eg \exists y \; (\mathit{hasColour}(x,y) \wedge 
eg \mathit{isAColour}(y))\) \((\mathit{hasColour}(\mathit{Pink},\mathit{Yellow}) \vee \mathit{Homer}(\mathit{likes},\mathit{TheDonut}))\) \(\mathit{itIsFriday}\) \(\mathit{hasColour}(\mathit{isEdible}(x), \mathit{isAColour}(y))\) \( orall x\; ( \exists y\; ( orall z\; \mathit{isEdible}(y) \wedge \mathit{likes}(z,z)) ) \vee \mathit{isEdible}(x))\) \((((\mathit{itIsTuesday} \wedge 
eg \mathit{itIsTuesday}) \wedge 
eg 
eg \mathit{itIsTuesday}) \wedge  orall x \; \mathit{isEdible}(y))\) Reveal answers 1, 2, 3 are all formulas. 4 is not, because \(\mathit{Homer}\) is not a binary relation symbol \(\mathit{likes}\) is not a term 5 is not, because \(\mathit{itIsFriday}\) is not in the language at all. 6 is not, because \(\mathit{isEdible}(x)\) is a formula and there needs to be a term in its place \(\mathit{isAColour}(y)\) is a formula and there needs to be a term in its place 7 is not, because the parentheses are not in matching pairs 8 is a formula. Remember that being a formula is simply about the syntax. Something can be a formula without corresponding to anything useful that you could imagine anyone wanting to represent in the first place. Equality In first order logic with equality there is a special binary relation symbol, \(=\) . This is written in the infix style: we write \(x = y\) rather than \(=(x,y)\) . The \(=\) sign is always interpreted as meaning equality in the usual sense between the individual things that the language is talking about. For example, \(x=x\) will always be a true statement. But if we have a binary relation symbol \(\mathit{greaterThan}\) the situation is different. If we want it to be the case that nothing is greater than itself, making \(
eg \mathit{greaterThan}(x,x)\) true, then we have to say explicitly that this holds. The key point here is that the sign \(=\) is the only relation or predicate symbol which always comes with a particular fixed meaning. In general we will always include \(=\) as part of the language in first order logic. If there is any exercise or example in which it is not to be used then this will be stated very clearly. Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 1, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit-2 Lesson 1' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.

docs.text: In first order logic with equality there is a special binary relation symbol, \(=\) . This is written in the infix style: we write \(x = y\) rather than \(=(x,y)\) . The \(=\) sign is always interpreted as meaning equality in the usual sense between the individual things that the language is talking about. For example, \(x=x\) will always be a true statement. If we want it to be the case that nothing is greater than itself, making \(\neg \mathit{greaterThan}(x,x)\) true, then we have to say explicitly that this holds. In general we will always include \(=\) as part of the language in first order logic. If there is any exercise or example in which it is not to be used then this will be stated very clearly.
