docs.text:
Lesson 2: The semantics of first order logic \(\def\phi{\varphi} % this is needed to make the Greek letter phi work\) Learning outcomes for lesson 2 In this lesson you will: understand the concept of an interpretation work with examples of interpretations learn how to evaluate the truth value of sentences in the language learn basic logical equivalences Introduction In propositional logic, an interpretation of the symbols in the language just means saying whether each propositional variable is true or false. In first order logic, an interpretation has to provide a lot more: a set of things to be the individuals that the language is talking about, elements of this set for all the constants in the language, \(n\) -ary relations on this set for each of the \(n\) -ary relation symbols, functions on the set for any function symbols in the language. An expression in a first order language is just a string of symbols that follow certain rules. It does not by itself mean anything. However suggestive the names chosen may be, an expression by itself really means nothing. Giving the symbols things that they refer to is called giving an interpretation for the language. This extends the idea of giving values to propositions in propositional logic. Once an interpretation is given, we can talk about whether a formula is true in that interpretation. Having a notion of truth, we can talk about formulas being valid , meaning true in all interpretations. In propositional logic, an interpretation just needs to give a truth value for each atomic proposition. There are only a finite number of ways to do this, and all of them can be summarised in a truth-table for the formula. In first order logic each interpretation has to provide a set of individuals, as well as meanings for the constants and the relation symbols in the language. This means there are infinitely many interpretations, so we cannot tell if something is true in all interpretations by checking all of them. Informal idea of an interpretation To specify an interpretation you must: Give a non-empty set, \(D\) , called the domain . For each constant symbol, give the element of \(D\) for it. For each \(n\) -ary relation symbol, give an \(n\) -ary relation on \(D\) . For each propositional symbol, give a truth value. In this module we use this simple notion of interpretation. There are variants, all of which would change what is said later about first order logic. These include allowing an empty domain, and the possibility of giving types to individuals so that there are multiple domains, one for each type. There are good reasons for these ideas, but we use the simplest approach here. The formal definition of 'interpretation' is given later in this lesson. First we look at some examples to motivate the idea. The domain There is no restriction on what the elements of \(D\) are. They can be abstract things such as numbers, symbols, mathematical structures such as sets, etc. They can be physical concrete things in the real world, provided there is no ambiguity about how such things are described. They can be concepts used in the world such as a time, again provided there are unambiguous ways of referring to them. Examples of interpretations Let's use this first order language: Constants: \(\mathit{Leeds}\) , \(\mathit{Reggie}\) Unary relation symbols: \(\mathit{owl}\) , \(\mathit{city}\) Binary relation symbols: \(\mathit{livesIn}\) We can make the domain \(D = \{1, 2, 3\}\) , and specify: \[  egin{array}{rcl} I(\mathit{Leeds}) & = & 1\[1ex] I(\mathit{Reggie}) & = & 2\[1ex] I(\mathit{city}) & = & \{1\}\[1ex] I(\mathit{owl}) & = & \{2\}\[1ex] I(\mathit{livesIn}) & = & \{(2,1)\} \end{array} \] You might wonder where Reggie has come from. The city of Leeds has a coat of arms with two owls and a Latin motto "Pro Rege et Lege", and it is tempting to misinterpret this as naming the two owls, one of which feels like it should be Reggie (Rege). Picturing interpretations (example 1) Interpretations can be visualised in various ways. Here the unary predicates are indicated by labelling the domain elements for which the predicate holds. The binary relations are shown as arrows. The constant symbols point to the elements they are interpreted as. Select for full text description The diagram above is the visualisation of an interpretation. The diagram features a sketch of an oval on its side. The oval contains three numbers and the following details: The number 1, labelled city, is positioned left of the oval’s centre. A box labelled Leeds in the surrounding space is connected to the number 1 by a pointing arrow. The number 2, labelled owl, is positioned in the oval’s centre. A box labelled Reggie in the surrounding space is connected to the number 2 by a pointing arrow. There is an arrow pointing from 2 towards 1 labelled livesIn. The number 3 is positioned towards the lower left-hand side of the oval and is unlabelled. Picturing interpretations (example 2) Here is a different interpretation of the same language with the same domain. It is allowed that different constant symbols are interpreted by the same element. It is also allowed that the interpretation of a predicate symbol is empty: here nothing is an owl. Note also the \(\mathit{livesIn}\) predicate can be interpreted by any set of pairs of elements, even though the usual idea of something living in something might exclude things living in themselves. Select for full text description The diagram above is a visualisation of an interpretation. The diagram features a sketch of an oval on its side. The oval contains three numbers and the following details. The number one is positioned to the left and centre of the oval. Two dotted lines lead from the number one out of the oval to the surrounding space. The dotted line leading out and upwards is linked to a box labelled Leeds. The dotted line leading out and downwards is linked to a box labelled Reggie. The number 2, labelled city, is positioned in the oval’s centre. An arrow, labelled livesIn, connects 2 to 1. Another arrow circles downwards from two and points back up to it, and this arrow is also labelled livesIn. The number 3 is positioned to the right and centre of the oval. An arrow, labelled livesIn, pointing from two, connects to three. In the second example we have: The domain is \(D = \{1, 2, 3\}\) . the interpretation of the symbols is: \[  egin{array}{rcl} I(\mathit{Leeds}) & = & 1\[1ex] I(\mathit{Reggie}) & = & 1\[1ex] I(\mathit{city}) & = & \{2\}\[1ex] I(\mathit{owl}) & = & \{\;\}\[1ex] I(\mathit{livesIn}) & = & \{(2,1), (2, 3), (2,2)\} \end{array} \] Equality The relation \(=\) is important and you can include this as binary relation symbol in a first order language. We usually insist that \(=\) is interpreted as the equality relation on the domain, and we then talk about first order logic with equality . If the domain is \(\{1, 2, 3\}\) , the interpretation of \({=}\) looks like the following diagram. In the diagram the arrows pointing from each number to itself should really be labelled by \({=}\) in each case. Select for full text description The diagram above is a visualisation of an interpretation. The diagram features a sketch of an oval on its side. The oval contains three numbers – one, two and three. Each of the numbers is topped by a clockwise arrow that circles upwards and then points back towards the number it came from. The binary relation of equality is reflexive, symmetric and transitive. It is also well behaved with respect to substitution. This means, for example, that if \(P\) is a unary relation symbol, and \(t_1 = t_2\) holds then \(P(t_1)\) and \(P(t_2)\) must always have the same truth value. However, writing \(P(t_1) = P(t_1)\) is not allowed. This is because the two expressions related by \(=\) are formulas and not terms. Truth and validity Given an interpretation we can calculate a truth value for some formulas in the language. For example, the formula \(\mathit{Leeds} = \mathit{Reggie}\) is true in example 2, because the domain element used to interpret the constant symbol \(\mathit{Leeds}\) is the same domain element as the one used to interpret \(\mathit{Reggie}\) . In example 1 these domain elements are different, so the formula \(\mathit{Leeds} = \mathit{Reggie}\) is false in that case. We can 'extend' the interpretation \(I\) , so that \(I(\mathit{Leeds} = \mathit{Reggie})\) is true if \(I(\mathit{Leeds})\) and \(I(\mathit{Reggie})\) are the same element of \(D\) . Formulas with variables, like \(I(\mathit{livesIn}(\mathit{Reggie}, x))\) , need the idea of a valuation . The informal idea of a valuation Given an interpretation \(I\) with domain \(D\) , a valuation means a function that gives an element of \(D\) for each variable. For example, for the interpretation \(I\) in example 1, we can have a valuation \(V\) where \(V(x) = 1\) and \(V(y) = 2\) . In this valuation the formula \(\mathit{livesIn}(y, x)\) can be evaluated to true, because \((V(y), V(x)) \in I(\mathit{livesIn})\) . But \(\mathit{livesIn}(x, y)\) would be evaluated to false because \((V(x), V(y)) 
otin I(\mathit{livesIn})\) . Free and bound variables In simple arithmetic with natural numbers we might come across a statement such as \[x + 1 = 2x.\] This on its own, as a statement, is neither true nor false because we have not said how to understand \(x\) . Is the statement saying that the equality holds for all values of \(x\) ? Is the statement saying that it is possible to find some \(x\) making it true? Is the statement in a context where a particular value of \(x\) is known, and the statement asserts that for this value the equality holds? Or maybe it is not a statement at all. Maybe it is an instruction to find one or all values of \(x\) making it true. In first order logic, any variable which is not quantified in an explicit way is said to be a free variable . Variables which are associated to quantifiers are called bound variables . In all the following four examples every \(x\) is a free variable and every \(y\) is a bound variable. \( orall y\; (r(x,y)\vee r(y,x))\) \(p(x) 	o \exists y\; r(y,x)\) \( orall y\; (y = y)\) \(x = x\) Being free or bound is not really a property of a variable itself, but of a particular occurrence of a variable in a formula. The same variable can appear bound and free in different places in the same formula. We shall try to avoid tricky examples like this next one but it illustrates that we need to be careful. \[p(y,y) \wedge  orall y \; \exists z (r(z,y) 	o \exists y \; s(y,x))\] In that example the two \(y\,\) s in \(p(y,y)\) are both free the \(y\) in \(r(z,y)\) is bound by the universal quantification \( orall y\) the \(y\) in \(s(y,x)\) is bound by the existential quantification \(\exists y\) The example is logically indistinguishable from \[p(y,y) \wedge  orall w \; \exists z (r(z,w) 	o \exists v \; s(v,x))\] in which the \(y\) bound to the universal quantifier has been replaced by \(w\) and the \(y\) bound to the existential quantifier has been replaced by \(v\) . In many cases it is safe to replace bound variables by different named variables but we would not be able to replace all the bound \(y\,\) by \(z\,\) s in the example as the \(y\) originally bound by the \( orall y\) gets captured by the \(\exists z\) . The informal idea of truth Given a formula with no free variables and an interpretation, we can work out a truth value for the formula. The definition is recursive, breaking down the truth value of a formula in terms of simpler formulas. For example, if \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) are formulas, then the definition shows how to calculate the truth value of \(\alpha \wedge  eta\) in terms of the truth values of \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) . Formal definition of interpretation Given a first order language, \(\mathscr{L}\) , an interpretation for \(\mathscr{L}\) is a pair \((D, I)\) where \(D\) is a non-empty set, called the domain , and \(I\) is a function which: to each constant symbol \(c\) of \(\mathscr{L}\) assigns an element \(I(c) \in D\) , to each \(n\) -ary predicate symbol \(P\) , ( \(n > 0\) ), assigns an \(n\) -ary relation \(I(P) \subseteq D^n\) , to each propositional constant \(q\) , assigns a truth value \(I(q)\) . Sometimes \(I\) is called a structure . Valuations defined formally Given a structure \((D, I)\) for a language \(\mathscr{L}\) , a valuation is a function from the set of all variables to \(D\) . Given a valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) , a variable \(x\) , and \(d \in D\) , we define the valuation \(\mathcal{V}[x \mapsto d]\) to be the same as \(\mathcal{V}\) for every variable other than \(x\) , and \(x\) gets sent to \(d\) . \[ \mathcal{V}[x \mapsto d]\, (u) = \left\{  egin{array}{ll} \mathcal{V}(u) & 	ext{ if $u$ is not $x$,}\ d & 	ext{ if $u$ is $x$.} \end{array} ight. \] If you imagine \(\mathcal{V}\) as a table of input-output pairs, this is the idea of updating one of these pairs. Remember that a term is either a variable or a constant symbol. Given \(\mathcal{A} = (D, I)\) , and a valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) , it is convenient to use the notation \(\mathcal{V}(t)\) when \(t\) is any term as follows \[ \mathcal{V}(t) = \left\{  egin{array}{ll} \mathcal{V}(u) & 	ext{ if $t$ is a variable $u$,}\[2ex] I(c) & 	ext{ if $t$ is a constant symbol $c$.} \end{array} ight. \] Definition of truth For a structure \(\mathcal{A} = (D, I)\) , and a valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) , and a formula \(\phi\) , the notation \[\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \phi\] will mean that \(\phi\) is true in the structure \(\mathcal{A}\) under the valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) . This relationship is defined as follows in the cases that \(\phi\) is of the form \(P(t_1, \ldots, t_n)\) for an \(n\) -ary predicate symbol \(P\) , or \(\phi\) is \(q\) for a propositional constant \(q\) . \[ egin{array}{ll} \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash P(t_1, \ldots, t_n) & 	ext{ iff } (\mathcal{V}(t_1),\ldots, \mathcal{V}(t_n)) \in I(P)\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash q & 	ext{ iff } I(q) \end{array}\] For formulas \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) we define \[ egin{array}{ll} \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \wedge  eta & 	ext{ iff both } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha 	ext { and } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash  eta \[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \vee  eta & 	ext{ iff } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha 	ext { or } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash  eta 	ext{ or both}\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha 	o  eta & 	ext{ iff } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha 	ext { does not hold, or } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash  eta 	ext{ does, or both} \[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \leftrightarrow  eta & 	ext{ iff } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha 	ext { and } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash  eta 	ext{ either both hold or neither does} \[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash 
eg \alpha & 	ext{ iff it is not the case that } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha\[3ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash  orall x\, \alpha & 	ext{ iff for every } d \in D 	ext{ it holds that } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V}[x \mapsto d] \vDash \alpha\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \exists x\, \alpha & 	ext{ iff for some } d \in D 	ext{ it holds that } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V}[x \mapsto d] \vDash \alpha \end{array}\] We can use \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} 
vDash \alpha\) to indicate that \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha\) does not hold. The turnstile notation The symbol \(\vDash\) is sometimes called 'double turnstile', and it has a counterpart 'single turnstile', \(\vdash\) , which we meet later. The symbol \(\vDash\) is used in some ways that can be confusing when compared with the usage \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \phi\) above. Remember that a sentence is a formula with no free variables. \(\vDash \phi\) means that for all structures, \(\mathcal{A}\) , and all valuations \(\mathcal{V}\) for that structure, \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \phi\) . When \(\phi\) is a sentence, \(\phi\) will get the same truth value under all valuations. However we still need to deal with valuations even for sentences because of the way they appear in the definition of truth for quantified statements. Writing \(\mathcal{A} \vDash \phi\) will mean \(\phi\) is true in the structure \(\mathcal{A}\) (under any valuation). We then say \(\mathcal{A}\) is a model of \(\phi\) . For formulas \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) we say \(\psi\) logically implies \(\phi\) if for every stucture and valuation such that \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \psi\) , it is also the case that \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \phi\) . Logical implication is denoted \(\psi \vDash \phi\) . This notation can be confusing because we use both \(\mathcal{A} \vDash \phi\) and \(\psi \vDash \phi\) with very different entities on the left of the turnstile. There is no problem here, but be sure you are aware of the difference between a formula and an interpretation or a structure. If \(\Gamma\) is a set of sentences, we write \(\Gamma \vDash \phi\) to mean every model for all of the sentences in \(\Gamma\) is also a model of \(\phi\) . Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 2, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit-2 Lesson 2' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.
docs.title: Lesson 2 Semantics of FOL

docs.text: \(\def\phi{\varphi} % this is needed to make the Greek letter phi work\) Learning outcomes for lesson 2 In this lesson you will: understand the concept of an interpretation work with examples of interpretations learn how to evaluate the truth value of sentences in the language learn basic logical equivalences
docs.title: Lesson 2: The semantics of first order logic

docs.text: In propositional logic, an interpretation of the symbols in the language just means saying whether each propositional variable is true or false. In first order logic, an interpretation has to provide a lot more: a set of things to be the individuals that the language is talking about, elements of this set for all the constants in the language, \(n\) -ary relations on this set for each of the \(n\) -ary relation symbols, functions on the set for any function symbols in the language. An expression in a first order language is just a string of symbols that follow certain rules. It does not by itself mean anything. However suggestive the names chosen may be, an expression by itself really means nothing. Giving the symbols things that they refer to is called giving an interpretation for the language. This extends the idea of giving values to propositions in propositional logic. Once an interpretation is given, we can talk about whether a formula is true in that interpretation. Having a notion of truth, we can talk about formulas being valid , meaning true in all interpretations. In propositional logic, an interpretation just needs to give a truth value for each atomic proposition. There are only a finite number of ways to do this, and all of them can be summarised in a truth-table for the formula. In first order logic each interpretation has to provide a set of individuals, as well as meanings for the constants and the relation symbols in the language. This means there are infinitely many interpretations, so we cannot tell if something is true in all interpretations by checking all of them.
docs.title: Introduction

docs.text: To specify an interpretation you must: Give a non-empty set, \(D\) , called the domain . For each constant symbol, give the element of \(D\) for it. For each \(n\) -ary relation symbol, give an \(n\) -ary relation on \(D\) . For each propositional symbol, give a truth value. In this module we use this simple notion of interpretation. There are variants, all of which would change what is said later about first order logic. These include allowing an empty domain, and the possibility of giving types to individuals so that there are multiple domains, one for each type. There are good reasons for these ideas, but we use the simplest approach here. The formal definition of 'interpretation' is given later in this lesson. First we look at some examples to motivate the idea.
docs.title: Informal idea  of an interpretation

docs.text: There is no restriction on what the elements of \(D\) are. They can be abstract things such as numbers, symbols, mathematical structures such as sets, etc. They can be physical concrete things in the real world, provided there is no ambiguity about how such things are described. They can be concepts used in the world such as a time, again provided there are unambiguous ways of referring to them.
docs.title: The domain

docs.text: Let's use this first order language: Constants: \(\mathit{Leeds}\) , \(\mathit{Reggie}\) Unary relation symbols: \(\mathit{owl}\) , \(\mathit{city}\) Binary relation symbols: \(\mathit{livesIn}\) We can make the domain \(D = \{1, 2, 3\}\) , and specify: \[ \begin{array}{rcl} I(\mathit{Leeds}) & = & 1\\[1ex] I(\mathit{Reggie}) & = & 2\\[1ex] I(\mathit{city}) & = & \{1\}\\[1ex] I(\mathit{owl}) & = & \{2\}\\[1ex] I(\mathit{livesIn}) & = & \{(2,1)\} \end{array} \] You might wonder where Reggie has come from. The city of Leeds has a coat of arms with two owls and a Latin motto "Pro Rege et Lege", and it is tempting to misinterpret this as naming the two owls, one of which feels like it should be Reggie (Rege).
docs.title: Examples of interpretations

docs.text: Interpretations can be visualised in various ways. Here the unary predicates are indicated by labelling the domain elements for which the predicate holds. The binary relations are shown as arrows. The constant symbols point to the elements they are interpreted as. Select for full text description The diagram above is the visualisation of an interpretation. The diagram features a sketch of an oval on its side. The oval contains three numbers and the following details: The number 1, labelled city, is positioned left of the oval’s centre. A box labelled Leeds in the surrounding space is connected to the number 1 by a pointing arrow. The number 2, labelled owl, is positioned in the oval’s centre. A box labelled Reggie in the surrounding space is connected to the number 2 by a pointing arrow. There is an arrow pointing from 2 towards 1 labelled livesIn. The number 3 is positioned towards the lower left-hand side of the oval and is unlabelled.
docs.title: Picturing interpretations (example 1)

docs.text: Here is a different interpretation of the same language with the same domain. It is allowed that different constant symbols are interpreted by the same element. It is also allowed that the interpretation of a predicate symbol is empty: here nothing is an owl. Note also the \(\mathit{livesIn}\) predicate can be interpreted by any set of pairs of elements, even though the usual idea of something living in something might exclude things living in themselves. Select for full text description The diagram above is a visualisation of an interpretation. The diagram features a sketch of an oval on its side. The oval contains three numbers and the following details. The number one is positioned to the left and centre of the oval. Two dotted lines lead from the number one out of the oval to the surrounding space. The dotted line leading out and upwards is linked to a box labelled Leeds. The dotted line leading out and downwards is linked to a box labelled Reggie. The number 2, labelled city, is positioned in the oval’s centre. An arrow, labelled livesIn, connects 2 to 1. Another arrow circles downwards from two and points back up to it, and this arrow is also labelled livesIn. The number 3 is positioned to the right and centre of the oval. An arrow, labelled livesIn, pointing from two, connects to three. In the second example we have: The domain is \(D = \{1, 2, 3\}\) . the interpretation of the symbols is: \[ \begin{array}{rcl} I(\mathit{Leeds}) & = & 1\\[1ex] I(\mathit{Reggie}) & = & 1\\[1ex] I(\mathit{city}) & = & \{2\}\\[1ex] I(\mathit{owl}) & = & \{\;\}\\[1ex] I(\mathit{livesIn}) & = & \{(2,1), (2, 3), (2,2)\} \end{array} \]
docs.title: Picturing interpretations  (example 2)

docs.text: The relation \(=\) is important and you can include this as binary relation symbol in a first order language. We usually insist that \(=\) is interpreted as the equality relation on the domain, and we then talk about first order logic with equality . If the domain is \(\{1, 2, 3\}\) , the interpretation of \({=}\) looks like the following diagram. In the diagram the arrows pointing from each number to itself should really be labelled by \({=}\) in each case. Select for full text description The diagram above is a visualisation of an interpretation. The diagram features a sketch of an oval on its side. The oval contains three numbers – one, two and three. Each of the numbers is topped by a clockwise arrow that circles upwards and then points back towards the number it came from. The binary relation of equality is reflexive, symmetric and transitive. It is also well behaved with respect to substitution. This means, for example, that if \(P\) is a unary relation symbol, and \(t_1 = t_2\) holds then \(P(t_1)\) and \(P(t_2)\) must always have the same truth value. However, writing \(P(t_1) = P(t_1)\) is not allowed. This is because the two expressions related by \(=\) are formulas and not terms.
docs.title: Equality

docs.text: Given an interpretation we can calculate a truth value for some formulas in the language. For example, the formula \(\mathit{Leeds} = \mathit{Reggie}\) is true in example 2, because the domain element used to interpret the constant symbol \(\mathit{Leeds}\) is the same domain element as the one used to interpret \(\mathit{Reggie}\) . In example 1 these domain elements are different, so the formula \(\mathit{Leeds} = \mathit{Reggie}\) is false in that case. We can 'extend' the interpretation \(I\) , so that \(I(\mathit{Leeds} = \mathit{Reggie})\) is true if \(I(\mathit{Leeds})\) and \(I(\mathit{Reggie})\) are the same element of \(D\) . Formulas with variables, like \(I(\mathit{livesIn}(\mathit{Reggie}, x))\) , need the idea of a valuation .
docs.title: Truth and validity

docs.text: Given an interpretation \(I\) with domain \(D\) , a valuation means a function that gives an element of \(D\) for each variable. For example, for the interpretation \(I\) in example 1, we can have a valuation \(V\) where \(V(x) = 1\) and \(V(y) = 2\) . In this valuation the formula \(\mathit{livesIn}(y, x)\) can be evaluated to true, because \((V(y), V(x)) \in I(\mathit{livesIn})\) . But \(\mathit{livesIn}(x, y)\) would be evaluated to false because \((V(x), V(y)) \notin I(\mathit{livesIn})\) .
docs.title: The informal idea of a valuation

docs.text: In simple arithmetic with natural numbers we might come across a statement such as \[x + 1 = 2x.\] This on its own, as a statement, is neither true nor false because we have not said how to understand \(x\) . Is the statement saying that the equality holds for all values of \(x\) ? Is the statement saying that it is possible to find some \(x\) making it true? Is the statement in a context where a particular value of \(x\) is known, and the statement asserts that for this value the equality holds? Or maybe it is not a statement at all. Maybe it is an instruction to find one or all values of \(x\) making it true. In first order logic, any variable which is not quantified in an explicit way is said to be a free variable . Variables which are associated to quantifiers are called bound variables . In all the following four examples every \(x\) is a free variable and every \(y\) is a bound variable. \(\forall y\; (r(x,y)\vee r(y,x))\) \(p(x) \to \exists y\; r(y,x)\) \(\forall y\; (y = y)\) \(x = x\) Being free or bound is not really a property of a variable itself, but of a particular occurrence of a variable in a formula. The same variable can appear bound and free in different places in the same formula. We shall try to avoid tricky examples like this next one but it illustrates that we need to be careful. \[p(y,y) \wedge \forall y \; \exists z (r(z,y) \to \exists y \; s(y,x))\] In that example the two \(y\,\) s in \(p(y,y)\) are both free the \(y\) in \(r(z,y)\) is bound by the universal quantification \(\forall y\) the \(y\) in \(s(y,x)\) is bound by the existential quantification \(\exists y\) The example is logically indistinguishable from \[p(y,y) \wedge \forall w \; \exists z (r(z,w) \to \exists v \; s(v,x))\] in which the \(y\) bound to the universal quantifier has been replaced by \(w\) and the \(y\) bound to the existential quantifier has been replaced by \(v\) . In many cases it is safe to replace bound variables by different named variables but we would not be able to replace all the bound \(y\,\) by \(z\,\) s in the example as the \(y\) originally bound by the \(\forall y\) gets captured by the \(\exists z\) .
docs.title: Free and bound variables

docs.text: Given a formula with no free variables and an interpretation, we can work out a truth value for the formula. The definition is recursive, breaking down the truth value of a formula in terms of simpler formulas. For example, if \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are formulas, then the definition shows how to calculate the truth value of \(\alpha \wedge \beta\) in terms of the truth values of \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) .
docs.title: The informal idea of truth

docs.text: Given a first order language, \(\mathscr{L}\) , an interpretation for \(\mathscr{L}\) is a pair \((D, I)\) where \(D\) is a non-empty set, called the domain , and \(I\) is a function which: to each constant symbol \(c\) of \(\mathscr{L}\) assigns an element \(I(c) \in D\) , to each \(n\) -ary predicate symbol \(P\) , ( \(n > 0\) ), assigns an \(n\) -ary relation \(I(P) \subseteq D^n\) , to each propositional constant \(q\) , assigns a truth value \(I(q)\) . Sometimes \(I\) is called a structure .
docs.title: Formal definition of interpretation

docs.text: Given a structure \((D, I)\) for a language \(\mathscr{L}\) , a valuation is a function from the set of all variables to \(D\) . Given a valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) , a variable \(x\) , and \(d \in D\) , we define the valuation \(\mathcal{V}[x \mapsto d]\) to be the same as \(\mathcal{V}\) for every variable other than \(x\) , and \(x\) gets sent to \(d\) . \[ \mathcal{V}[x \mapsto d]\, (u) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \mathcal{V}(u) & \text{ if $u$ is not $x$,}\\ d & \text{ if $u$ is $x$.} \end{array} \right. \] If you imagine \(\mathcal{V}\) as a table of input-output pairs, this is the idea of updating one of these pairs. Remember that a term is either a variable or a constant symbol. Given \(\mathcal{A} = (D, I)\) , and a valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) , it is convenient to use the notation \(\mathcal{V}(t)\) when \(t\) is any term as follows \[ \mathcal{V}(t) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \mathcal{V}(u) & \text{ if $t$ is a variable $u$,}\\[2ex] I(c) & \text{ if $t$ is a constant symbol $c$.} \end{array} \right. \]
docs.title: Valuations defined formally

docs.text: For a structure \(\mathcal{A} = (D, I)\) , and a valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) , and a formula \(\phi\) , the notation \[\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \phi\] will mean that \(\phi\) is true in the structure \(\mathcal{A}\) under the valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) . This relationship is defined as follows in the cases that \(\phi\) is of the form \(P(t_1, \ldots, t_n)\) for an \(n\) -ary predicate symbol \(P\) , or \(\phi\) is \(q\) for a propositional constant \(q\) . \[\begin{array}{ll} \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash P(t_1, \ldots, t_n) & \text{ iff } (\mathcal{V}(t_1),\ldots, \mathcal{V}(t_n)) \in I(P)\\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash q & \text{ iff } I(q) \end{array}\] For formulas \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) we define \[\begin{array}{ll} \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \wedge \beta & \text{ iff both } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \text { and } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \beta \\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \vee \beta & \text{ iff } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \text { or } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \beta \text{ or both}\\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \to \beta & \text{ iff } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \text { does not hold, or } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \beta \text{ does, or both} \\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \leftrightarrow \beta & \text{ iff } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha \text { and } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \beta \text{ either both hold or neither does} \\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \neg \alpha & \text{ iff it is not the case that } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha\\[3ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \forall x\, \alpha & \text{ iff for every } d \in D \text{ it holds that } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V}[x \mapsto d] \vDash \alpha\\[1ex] \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \exists x\, \alpha & \text{ iff for some } d \in D \text{ it holds that } \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V}[x \mapsto d] \vDash \alpha \end{array}\] We can use \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \nvDash \alpha\) to indicate that \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \alpha\) does not hold.
docs.title: Definition of truth

docs.text: The symbol \(\vDash\) is sometimes called 'double turnstile', and it has a counterpart 'single turnstile', \(\vdash\) , which we meet later. The symbol \(\vDash\) is used in some ways that can be confusing when compared with the usage \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \phi\) above. Remember that a sentence is a formula with no free variables. \(\vDash \phi\) means that for all structures, \(\mathcal{A}\) , and all valuations \(\mathcal{V}\) for that structure, \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \phi\) . When \(\phi\) is a sentence, \(\phi\) will get the same truth value under all valuations. However we still need to deal with valuations even for sentences because of the way they appear in the definition of truth for quantified statements. Writing \(\mathcal{A} \vDash \phi\) will mean \(\phi\) is true in the structure \(\mathcal{A}\) (under any valuation). We then say \(\mathcal{A}\) is a model of \(\phi\) . For formulas \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) we say \(\psi\) logically implies \(\phi\) if for every stucture and valuation such that \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \psi\) , it is also the case that \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{V} \vDash \phi\) . Logical implication is denoted \(\psi \vDash \phi\) . This notation can be confusing because we use both \(\mathcal{A} \vDash \phi\) and \(\psi \vDash \phi\) with very different entities on the left of the turnstile. There is no problem here, but be sure you are aware of the difference between a formula and an interpretation or a structure. If \(\Gamma\) is a set of sentences, we write \(\Gamma \vDash \phi\) to mean every model for all of the sentences in \(\Gamma\) is also a model of \(\phi\) . Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 2, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit-2 Lesson 2' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.
docs.title: The turnstile notation

docs.text:
Lesson 3: Representation Learning outcomes for lesson 3 After completing this lesson you should be able to: use first order logic to represent knowledge about various situations. One of the challenges in using first order logic to represent knowledge, is that the knowledge is often described in natural language (English in our case) which can be ambiguous. We also see that sometimes exactly the same phrase in English has to be represented in the logic in quite different ways. This means that the process of writing logical expressions to represent knowledge is not a matter of translating English words into logic in a fixed way – you have to think about the meaning (sometimes the most likely of several meanings in the context). We will go through various examples. These examples involve geometric shapes and this type of example exists in various forms called "Tarski's world". You can experience an interactive version . Note that this may not run in all browsers and you may need to download the example to run it with java. Although some links on the web page do not work, the java program itself works correctly. To be clear: this is an additional resource and you do not need to use it to understand the examples below. Shapes scenarios We work with the following scenario, two examples of which are shown in the diagrams below. We are representing the arrangement of shapes in simple pictures, with the following constraints: A picture can contain any number of shapes greater than zero. Every shape in a picture is one of these kinds: a triangle, a circle, or a square. There can be any possible mixture of kinds in a picture. For example, there can be shapes of every kind, or there might only be shapes of one kind. two shapes may touch each other, but not overlap one shape can be inside another, but when this happens nothing can be inside the innermost one the relationships inside and touching can only be true of different things. Nothing is inside itself and nothing touches itself. Select for full text description The diagram contains two rectangles side by side, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. The rectangle on the left-hand side contains a triangle pointing towards one o’clock surrounded by a large circle. The triangle and the large circle do not touch each others boundaries. The large circle is touching a smaller circle on its lower right-hand side, which is in turn touching the lower left-hand side of a large square which contains a smaller square within itself. The smaller square is not touching the boundaries of the larger square. The rectangle on the right-hand side contains a triangle floating on its own in the same position as the triangle described previously. A smaller circle floats below and to the right-hand side of the triangle. To the right of the circle is the lower left-hand corner of a larger square which contains a smaller triangle in the lower left and a circle, the same size as the one previously described, in the upper right-hand corner. At first we are not going to represent these constraints themselves. We are assuming these constraints are given and then representing additional statements about pictures. We do this by going through a sequence of examples which get more complex as we go on. \[  egin{array}{|llll|} \mathsf{Relation~ Symbol} &\mathsf{Arity} &\mathsf{Example} &\mathsf{Meaning~ of~ example}\ \hline \mathit{circle} & 1 & \mathit{circle}(x) & x \mathsf{~ is~ a~ circle} \ \mathit{triangle} & 1 & \mathit{triangle}(x) & x \mathsf{~ is~ a~ triangle} \ \mathit{square} & 1 & \mathit{square}(x) & x \mathsf{~ is~ a~ square} \ \mathit{touches} & 2 & \mathit{touches}(x,y) & x \mathsf{~ touches~ } y \ \mathit{inside} & 2 & \mathit{inside}(x,y) & x \mathsf{~ is~ inside~ } y \ \hline \end{array} \] We next take some formulas and take the domain of interpretation to be the set of all shapes in a picture. We look at whether the formulas are true in some or all of these interpretations given the constraints stated above. Note that being true in all these interpretations is not the same as being logically valid – being valid means being true in all possible interpretations including ones where something can be a triangle and a square at the same time. \(\def\cl{\mathit{circle}}\) \(\def	l{\mathit{triangle}}\) \(\def\sr{\mathit{square}}\) \(\def	ch{\mathit{touches}}\) \(\def\ins{\mathit{inside}}\) Single quantifier examples about types of shape: universal case \( orall x\, \cl(x)\) This means every shape is a circle. This is true in interpretations 1 and 2, and false in 3 and 4. Select for full text description The diagram contains four equal-sized rectangles labelled one to four in two rows, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. 1) A large circle is placed towards the upper left-hand corner of this rectangle. This circle contains another circle half its size inside it. To the right-hand side are two more circles, one directly above the other with a gap in between. 2) This rectangle contains a solitary small circle towards its lower edge, slightly left of centre. 3) This rectangle is identical to rectangle one, except it contains a triangle on the far right-hand side, adjacent but not touching the two separate circles. 4) This rectangle contains a triangle in the lower left-hand corner. Another larger triangle is located above it, slightly to the right and pointing towards one o’clock. On the right-hand side is a large square which contains a smaller triangle positioned slightly right of centre within it. None of the shapes are touching. The formula \( orall x\, \cl(x)\) is straightforward, but we can add a negation sign to it in two different ways which it is important to distinguish. \(
eg  orall x\, \cl(x)\) will be true exactly when \( orall x\, \cl(x)\) is false and vice versa. This formula is true in interpretations 3 and 4, and false in 1 and 2. The formula \(
eg  orall x\, \cl(x)\) means that not everything is a circle. \( orall x\, 
eg \cl(x)\) which means no shape is a circle. This formula is true in interpretation 4, and false in 1, 2 and 3. \( orall x\, (	l(x) \vee \sr(x))\) and \(( orall x\, 	l(x)) \vee ( orall x\, \sr(x))\) These two formulas mean different things: \( orall x\, (	l(x) \vee \sr(x))\) means each shape is a triangle or a square. It is true in examples 1 and 3 below, and false in 2 and 4. \(( orall x\, 	l(x)) \vee ( orall x\, \sr(x))\) means that all of the shapes are triangles or all of the shapes are squares. This is true for example 3 and false in 1, 2, and 4 of the following interpretations. Select for full text description The diagram contains four equal-sized rectangles labelled one to four in two rows, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. 1) In this rectangle two equally-sized small squares are positioned in the lower left-hand corner side by side but with space in between. Above them is a triangle pointing towards one o’clock. To the right-hand side is a large square with a small triangle in its lower left-hand corner. None of the shapes are touching. 2) This rectangle contains a small square in the lower left-hand corner. Above it and slightly to the right is a triangle pointing towards one o’clock. Back towards the lower edge is a small circle still slightly left of centre. To the right of the circle is the lower left-hand corner of a large square which contains a small triangle positioned towards its own lower left-hand corner. None of the shapes are touching 3) This rectangle contains two large squares side by side. The square on the left contains a smaller square in its lower right-hand corner. The square on the right contains a smaller square centrally aligned but slightly more towards its top edge. None of the shapes are touching. 4) This rectangle contains five equally-sized circles in a random grouping. None of them are touching. \(( orall x\, 	l(x)) 	o ( orall x\, \sr(x))\) and \( orall x\, (	l(x) 	o \sr(x))\) Here you need to remember the definition of when a formula \(\alpha 	o  eta\) is true. Such a formula is true whenever \(\alpha\) is false, and it is also true whenever \( eta\) is true. The only case \(\alpha 	o  eta\) is false is when \(\alpha\) is true and \( eta\) is false. Select for full text description The diagram contains four equal-sized rectangles labelled one to four in two rows, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. 1) In this rectangle there is a square in the lower left-hand corner with a triangle inside it with space around it. Next to this square on the right there is a slightly smaller square. Above these two squares there is another square positioned more towards the top edge of the rectangle. To the right-hand side, positioned slightly below centre, there is a larger square which contains another smaller square aligned slightly to the left and above centre inside it. None of these shapes are touching. 2) This rectangle contains triangles. In the upper left-hand corner is a medium-sized triangle pointing towards one o’clock. Below this in the bottom left-hand corner is a small triangle. Next to the small triangle is a medium-sized triangle pointing towards one o’clock with a smaller triangle inside it. The top corner of this triangle is pointing up and touching the side of the larger triangle it is placed inside. To the right of this, now on the rectangle’s right-hand side, is another small triangle, centrally aligned. Above it with space in between is another slightly larger triangle. 3) This rectangle contains two squares near the left-hand edge, one on top of the other but with space in between. Alongside them with a gap in between is another square the same size. To the far right-hand side is a larger square with a smaller square, centrally aligned but slightly towards the top, inside it. 4) This rectangle contains seven circles connected together. There are two circles next to each other in the lower left-hand corner. The next circle is to the upper right which has another circle stacked on top of it. Below and to the right there are two circles next to each other and another circle is stacked on top of the right-hand one at about one o’clock on its outer edge. \(( orall x\, 	l(x)) 	o ( orall x\, \sr(x))\) is true in the interpretations 1, 3, and 4 above because \( orall x\, 	l(x)\) is false in each of these. In interpretation 3 there is another reason why \(( orall x\, 	l(x)) 	o ( orall x\, \sr(x))\) is true: because \( orall x\, \sr(x)\) is true there. In interpretation 2 the formula \(( orall x\, 	l(x)) 	o ( orall x\, \sr(x))\) is false because everything is a triangle but not everything is a square. \( orall x\, (	l(x) 	o \sr(x))\) is true in the interpretations 3 and 4, but false in both 1 and 2. In cases 3 and 4 the formula \(	l(x)\) is false for every possible \(x\) in the domain, so \(	l(x) 	o \sr(x)\) is true for every element of the domain. In both 1 and 2 we can find an element of the domain for which \(	l(x)\) is true but \(\sr(x)\) is false. In 1 there is only one such element; in 2 there are six. Single quantifier examples about types of shape: existential case \(\exists x\, \cl(x)\) This means at least one shape is a circle. This is true for interpretations 1, 2 and 3 below, and false in 4. Select for full text description The diagram contains four equal-sized rectangles labelled one to four in two rows, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. 1) A large circle is placed towards the upper left-hand corner of this rectangle. This circle contains another circle half its size inside it. To the right-hand side are two more circles, one directly above the other with a gap in between. 2) This rectangle contains a solitary small circle towards its lower edge, slightly left of centre. 3) This rectangle is identical to rectangle one, except it contains a triangle on the far right-hand side, adjacent but not touching the two separate circles. 4) This rectangle contains a triangle in the lower left-hand corner. Another larger triangle is located above it, slightly to the right and pointing towards one o’clock. On the right-hand side is a large square which contains a smaller triangle positioned slightly right of centre within it. None of the shapes are touching. The formula \(\exists x\, \cl(x)\) is straightforward, but we can add a negation sign to it in two different ways which it is important to distinguish. \(
eg \exists x\, \cl(x)\) will be true exactly when there is no circle in the picture. This formula is true in interpretation 4, and false in the others. \(\exists x\, 
eg \cl(x)\) which means some shape is not a circle. This formula is true in interpretations 3 and 4, and false in 1 and 2. \(\exists x\, (	l(x) \wedge \sr(x))\) and \((\exists x\, 	l(x)) \wedge (\exists x\, \sr(x))\) The formula \(\exists x\, (	l(x) \wedge \sr(x))\) says that there is a shape which is both a triangle and a square. This will always be false for the interpretations we are considering. \((\exists x\, 	l(x)) \wedge (\exists x\, \sr(x))\) means that there is a triangle and there is a square. This time there is no requirement that the triangle and the square have to be the same thing to make the formula true. In the interpretations shown below, this is true in 1, and false in the others. Select for full text description The diagram contains four equal-sized rectangles labelled one to four in two rows, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. 1) In this rectangle there is a square in the lower left-hand corner with a triangle in the middle with space around it. Next to this square on the left is another slightly smaller square. Above these squares towards the top edge but still on the left-hand side of the rectangle is another medium-sized square. To the right-hand side is a large square aligned just below centre. The large square has a smaller square aligned slightly to the left and above centre inside it. None of the shapes are touching. 2) This rectangle contains a mixture of circles and triangles. In the lower left-hand corner is a circle, above which is a larger triangle pointing towards one o’clock. Next to the triangle on the left is a circle which has a smaller triangle directly below it. Towards the rectangle’s right-hand corner is a triangle the same size and pointing the same direction as the one in the top left-hand corner previously described. Above this towards the rectangle’s top edge and to the left is a medium-sized triangle pointing directly upwards. None of the shapes are touching. 3) On the left-hand side of this rectangle there are two squares one above the other. To the left of them is another similarly sized square. To the right, now on the rectangle’s far right-hand side, is a larger square with another smaller square inside it, positioned just above centre. None of the shapes are touching. 4) This rectangle contains a circle in the lower left-hand corner, one just left of centre towards the top edge, another just right of centre towards the bottom and a fourth placed above centre on the right-hand side. All the circles are the same size with plenty of space in between. \((\exists x\, 	l(x)) 	o (\exists x\, \sr(x))\) and \(\exists x\, (	l(x) 	o \sr(x))\) The interaction between \(\exists\) and \(	o\) can be confusing. While it is not all logically wrong to write a formula of the shape \(\exists x \; (p(x) 	o q(x))\) , it is almost certainly not saying anything useful. The example here should help you understand what it does mean. The formula \((\exists x\, 	l(x)) 	o (\exists x\, \sr(x))\) captures the idea that if a picture has a triangle then it also has a square. This is true for pictures with no triangles at all, and it's true for any picture where there is both a square and a triangle. Otherwise it's false. This formula expresses a natural constraint where you can easily imagine the pictures for which it is true. \(\exists x\, (	l(x) 	o \sr(x))\) looks similar to the other formula, but it is not nearly so obvious for which pictures it will be true. In the interpretations pictured below, this is true in 1, 2, and 3, but false in 4. In interpretation 4 the formula is false because it is impossible to find an element of the domain making \(	l(x) 	o \sr(x)\) true. In every case \(x\) has to be a triangle and not a square. In the interpretations 1, 2 and 3, there is at least one value for \(x\) where the formula is true. In interpretation 2 there is only one element making \(	l(x)\) false; in 1 and 3 there are several elements which are not triangles. Select for full text description The diagram contains four equal-sized rectangles labelled one to four in two rows, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. 1) In this rectangle there is a square in the lower left-hand corner with a triangle floating inside it. Next to this square on the left is another slightly smaller square. Above these squares towards the top edge but still on the left-hand side of the rectangle is another medium-sized square. To the right-hand side is a large square aligned just below centre. The large square has a smaller square aligned slightly to the left and above centre inside it. None of the shapes are touching. 2) This rectangle contains one circles and five triangles. In the lower left-hand corner is a circle, above which is a larger triangle pointing towards one o’clock. Next to the triangle on the left is a circle which has a smaller triangle below it. Towards the rectangle’s right-hand corner is a triangle the same size and pointing the same direction as the one in the top left-hand corner previously described. Above this towards the rectangle’s top edge and to the left is a medium-sized triangle pointing directly upwards. All of the shapes have space in between them. 3) On the left-hand side of this rectangle there are two squares one above the other. To the left of them is another similarly sized square. To the right, now on the rectangle’s right-hand side, is a larger square with another smaller square inside it, positioned just above centre. None of the shapes are touching. 4) This rectangle contains five triangles. There is a small triangle in the lower left-hand corner and a larger triangle above it, both pointing upwards. To the right of this is a small triangle and a larger triangle below, both of these are pointing downwards with. To the far right is a larger triangle pointing to one o’clock. None of the triangles are touching. Nested combinations of quantifiers \(\exists x\, \exists y\, \exists z\, (	l(x) \wedge 	l(y) \wedge 	ch(x,z) \wedge 	ch(y,z))\) This might appear to say that there are two triangles that both touch the same third shape. However, there is no requirement that the \(x\) , \(y\) , and \(z\) are all different from each other. In first order logic it is possible that the same individual is referred to by two different variables. So the formula can be true where there is only one triangle, but that triangle touches some other shape. It is also possible that the formula is true because one triangle touches another triangle. In the interpretations 1 and 2 below the formula is true. Select for full text description This diagram contains two equal-sized rectangles labelled one and two, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. 1) In this rectangle there is a large square on the left-hand side with two triangles, one smaller than the other, inside it. The two triangles are not touching each other, and they are not touching the square they are inside. To the upper right there is a smaller square with a very small triangle inside it, not touching the square. In the lower right-hand corner of the rectangle there is a small triangle which is touching the lower left-hand corner of a larger square. This square contains a triangle, one corner of which is touching the square’s right-hand edge. 2) On the left-hand side of this rectangle there is a large square which has five equally-sized triangles inside it. Towards the top edge are two triangles spaced apart. Below this is a triangle pointing down. This is touching another triangle pointing up on its slanted edge. Adjacent but not touching this on the right is another triangle pointing upwards not touching anything. To the right is a smaller square that contains one triangle. This is pointing downwards and touching the square on its bottom edge, slightly right of center. To say there are two different triangles that both touch something, we need to make this explicit. One way is like this: \( egin{array}{l} \;\ \exists x\, \exists y\, \exists z\, \ \quad(	l(x) \wedge 	l(y) \wedge 	ch(x,z) \wedge 	ch(y,z) \wedge 
eg (x = y)). \ \;\ \end{array}\) This formula is now only true in interpretation 1 above. If we need to say that \(x\) , \(y\) , and \(z\) are all different from each other, then this can be expressed by \(
eg (x = y) \wedge 
eg (y = z) \wedge 
eg (z = x)\) . \(\exists x\, (\sr(x) \wedge  orall y\, (	l(y) 	o \ins(y,x)))\) This says that there is a particular square within which all the triangles can be found. Sometimes statements such as 'every triangle is in a square' can be ambiguous about whether there is a single square containing every triangle, or alternatively, each triangle is inside some square, but different triangles might be in different squares. When representing such situations it may be important to clarify what is meant, or to select the more likely meaning in the given context. If a newspaper headline reads 'There is a rat within 20 feet of everybody' it is most likely to mean that for each person in the country it is possible to find a rat within 20 feet of them. It is not likely to mean what it literally says: that one particular rat is so enormous as to be within 20 feet of everyone at once. In this example if you take the English statement 'There is a rat … ' and try to translate word-for-word into \(\exists x\,(\mathit{rat}(x) \wedge \ldots\) , then the result is most unlikely to represent the intended meaning. \( orall y\, (	l(y) 	o \exists x\, (\sr(x) \wedge \ins(y,x)))\) This says that every triangle is inside some square, but this need not be the same square for every triangle. It allows the possibility that all the triangles are in the same square, or that each triangle is inside a different square. In the following interpretations, this formula is true in 1, 2, and 4. In 4 it is true because there are no triangles. Select for full text description This diagram contains four equal-sized rectangles labelled one to four, each of which contains an assortment of other shapes. 1) This rectangle contains a large square on the left-hand side. It has five equally-sized triangles inside it. Towards the top edge are two triangles spaced apart. Below the one on the left is another triangle pointing down. To the right and lower down is a triangle pointing up, and to the upper right of this is another triangle pointing up. There is space between all the triangles. To the square’s right is another smaller square. This square has a small circle inside it towards the top edge but not touching the sides. 2) On the left-hand side of this rectangle there is a large square which has five equally-sized triangles inside it. Towards the top edge are two triangles spaced apart. Below this is a triangle pointing down. This is touching another triangle pointing up on its slanted edge. Adjacent to this on the right is another triangle pointing upwards not touching anything. To the right is a smaller square that contains one triangle. This is pointing downwards and touching the square on its bottom edge, slightly right of center. 3) In this rectangle there is a large square on the left-hand side with two triangles, one smaller than the other, inside it. To the upper right there is a smaller square with a very small triangle inside it. In the lower right-hand corner of the rectangle there is a small triangle which is touching the lower left-hand corner of a larger square. This square contains a triangle, one corner of which is touching the square’s right-hand edge. 4) This rectangle contains five circles. Towards the lower left-hand corner two circles are connected side by side. Slightly above with space in between, there are another two circles which are connected side by side as well. To the upper right, now towards the rectangle’s top edge slightly right of center, there is another circle isolated from the others. In representing situations in logic it is important to be careful about the difference between situations that might sound similar at first. For example, saying that every triangle in inside some square is different to saying that every square has some triangle inside it. This latter statement can be represented by \( orall x\, (\sr(x) 	o \exists y\, (	l(y) \wedge \ins(y,x)))\) . Exercise Draw four examples of interpretations that satisfy the following: Each interpretation has at least one shape. The four interpretations show all possible combinations of truth values for the two formulas \( egin{array}{l}  orall y\, (	l(y) 	o \exists x\, (\sr(x) \wedge \ins(y,x))), \;\mathsf{and}\  orall x\, (\sr(x) 	o \exists y\, (	l(y) \wedge \ins(y,x))). \end{array}\) The examples are minimal in the sense that removing any shapes would mean the above conditions are no longer satisfied. Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 3, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit-2 Lesson 3' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.

We will go through various examples. These examples involve geometric shapes and this type of example exists in various forms called "Tarski's world". You can experience an interactive version . Note that this may not run in all browsers and you may need to download the example to run it with java. Although some links on the web page do not work, the java program itself works correctly. To be clear: this is an additional resource and you do not need to use it to understand the examples below.

The large circle is touching a smaller circle on its lower right-hand side, which is in turn touching the lower left-hand side of a large square which contains a smaller square within itself. The smaller square is not touching the boundaries of the larger square. A smaller circle floats below and to the right-hand side of the triangle.
