docs.text:
First order natural deduction \(\def\phi{\varphi}\) We have seen natural deduction proofs for propositional logic. For first order logic there are extra rules to deal with the quantifiers. Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: write proofs for simple first order formulas in the natural deduction style correctly label every step of a natural deduction proof with the rule that allows it find errors in attempts at natural deduction proofs that are faulty. 3.1 The informal ideas behind the rules For each of the two quantifiers there are two kinds of argument which are modelled by natural deduction rules. The kinds of arguments will be introduced, and then we will see how they have rules that capture these. 3.1.1 Universal elimination Idea The argument goes like this: We know that for all \(x\) the statement \(\phi\) holds. We have a term \(t\) that we need to use in our argument. So we know that the statement \(\phi\) holds if we replace \(x\) by \(t\) everywhere in the statement. The idea is that we know \(\phi\) talking about \(x\) holds for all elements of the domain, so if we make the same statement but about a particular thing, \(t\) , it must also hold. Examples These take place in a setting where the stated things are known. We know that for all \(x\) , \(x^2 \geq 0\) . We are talking about the constant 42 in our argument. So we know that \(42^2 \geq 0\) . We know that for all \(x\) , \(x^2 \geq 0\) . We are talking about the term \(y +1\) in our argument. So we know that \((y+1)^2 \geq 0\) . But you do have to be careful: We know that for every \(x\) , we can find an element \(y\) where \(x 
eq y\) . Since we know that for every \(x\) , it must hold in particular for \(y\) . So we know that we can find an element \(y\) where \(y 
eq y\) . No. This is not a valid argument. We substituted \(y\) for \(x\) in a setting where the \(y\) became bound by another quantifier. 3.1.2 Universal introduction Idea This is when you have this kind of argument: We are using \(x\) for a completely arbitrary element of the domain, being careful not to assume it has any special properties. We show that \(x\) has to have the property stated in the formula \(\phi\) . Because \(x\) was arbitrary, we conclude that the formula \(\phi\) must hold for all \(x\) . Example Imagine we are talking about natural numbers. Let's use \(x\) for an arbitrary number. Now, suppose \(x\) is odd, so \(\mathit{Odd}(x)\) holds. We can find \(k\) such that \(x = 2k + 1\) . Then \(x^2 = 2(2k^2 +k) + 1\) . We have shown \(\mathit{Odd}(x) 	o \mathit{Odd}(x^2)\) . As \(x\) was arbitrary, we conclude that \[ orall x\, (\mathit{Odd}(x) 	o \mathit{Odd}(x^2))\] You have to be very careful that you are not assuming anything special about \(x\) when making this type of argument. Now a second example: Security example assume \(x\) is arbitrary person and imagine they are inside Room 27 but the only route from the outside to room 27 passes through corridor 15 (information from map of building) conclude: everyone in room 27 has passed through corridor 15 3.1.3 Existential elimination Idea The argument goes like this: We know there is some \(x\) with the property stated by \(\phi\) . Let's introduce a new name, \(a\) , for a particular thing with this property. We deduce that some statement, \(\psi\) , (not involving \(a\) ) must hold by an argument using the assumption that \(a\) has property \(\phi\) . We conclude that \(\psi\) holds on its own, without the assumption about \(a\) having property \(\phi\) . There are differences about how this is explained in different books: one way is to allow \(a\) to be a new constant (so strictly speaking we have to expand the language), another is to say \(a\) is a variable – you can call it \(x\) , but this can be confusing in some cases. Example Fred can see everyone in room 27: \( orall x\, (\mathit{IsIn}(x, room27) 	o CanSee(fred,x ))\) . There is someone in room 27: \(\exists y\, \mathit{IsIn}(y, room27)\) . ( \(\ast\) ) Let's call this person \(p27\) and work under the assumption \(\mathit{IsIn}(p27, room27)\) . We know ( \( orall\) elimination) that: \(\mathit{IsIn}(p27, room27) 	o \mathit{CanSee}(fred,p27 )\) . We deduce ( \(	o\) elimination) that: \(\mathit{CanSee}(fred,p27 )\) We get ( \(\exists\) introduction) that: \(\exists x\, \mathit{CanSee}(fred,x )\) , Fred can see someone, but this is under the assumption \(\mathit{IsIn}(p27, room27)\) . Now, we use \(\exists\) elimination to conclude: \(\exists x\, \mathit{CanSee}(fred,x )\) , Fred can see someone. This no longer depends on the assumption in \(\ast\) . 3.1.4 Existential Introduction Idea The argument goes like this: We have a statement \(\phi\) about some particular term \(t\) . We conclude there is some \(x\) with the property stated by \(\phi\) where we replace \(t\) by \(x\) . Example We know Fred is in room 27: \(\mathit{IsIn(Fred, Room27)}\) . We conclude there is someone in Room 27: \(\exists x\, \mathit{IsIn}(x, \mathit{Room27})\) . 3.2 The rules for quantifiers expressed formally 3.2.1 Existential Rules Introduction Rule \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$\phi(t)$} \RightLabel{$\; \exists I$} \UIC{$\exists x\, \phi (x)$} \end{prooftree} \] Here \(\phi(t)\) means some formula containing a term \(t\) , and \(\phi (x)\) means the same formula but with \(t\) replaced by \(x\) . For example (assuming \(1\) and \(2\) are constants in the language and \(+\) is a function symbol) \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$1 + 1 = 2$} \RightLabel{$\; \exists I$} \UIC{$\exists x\, (x + x = 2)$} \end{prooftree} \] Elimination Rule The idea of the \(a\) here is as an arbitrary constant. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$\exists x\, \phi (x)$} \AXC{$[\phi(a) ]_n \quad$} 
oLine \UIC{$\vdots \quad$} 
oLine \UIC{$\psi \quad$} \RightLabel{$\;\exists E_n$} \BIC{$\psi$} \end{prooftree}\] This rule can only be used under certain conditions. All of the following must happen in a correct use of the rule. \(a\) does not occur in \(\phi(x)\) , and \(a\) does not occur in \(\psi\) , and \(a\) does not occur in any undischarged assumption (except \(\phi(a)\) itself) used in the derviation of \(\psi\) from \(\phi(a)\) . Example The existential quantifier rules are shown in action in this example: \(\exists x\, 
eg P(x) \vdash \exists x\, (P(x) 	o Q(x))\) \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$\exists 
eg P(x)$} \AXC{$[
eg P(a)]_3$} \AXC{$[P(a)]_2$} \AXC{$[
eg Q(a)]_1$} \RightLabel{$\;{\wedge}I$} \BIC{$P(a) \wedge 
eg Q(a)$} \RightLabel{$\;{\wedge}Er$} \UIC{$P(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge}I$} \BIC{$
eg P(a) \wedge P(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg}E_1$} \UIC{$Q(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; {	o}I_2$} \UIC{$P(a) 	o Q(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; {\exists}I$} \UIC{$\exists x (P(x) 	o Q(x))$} \RightLabel{$\; {\exists}E_3$} \BIC{$\exists x (P(x) 	o Q(x))$} \end{prooftree} \] 3.2.2 Universal Rules Introduction Here, \(\phi(x)\) means the formula \(\phi(a)\) with every occurrence of the constant \(a\) replaced by the variable \(x\) . \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$\phi(a)$} \RightLabel{$ orall I$} \UIC{$ orall x\, \phi(x)$} \end{prooftree} \] The rule can only be used: Provided that the constant \(a\) does not occur in any undischarged assumption used in the derivation of \(\phi(a)\) . Elimination \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$ orall x \, \phi(x)$} \RightLabel{$ orall E$} \UIC{$\phi(t)$} \end{prooftree} \] In this rule \(t\) is a term that gets substituted for \(x\) in \(\phi(x)\) to give \(\phi(t)\) . This is only allowed if \(t\) does not have any variables which become bound when substituted for \(x\) . 3.3 Examples of misusing the rules Do not do this! This faulty proof appears to show that something is different from itself in a setting where each thing has something different from it. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$ orall x \, \exists y\, 
eg (x = y)$} \RightLabel{$ orall E$} \UIC{$\exists y\, 
eg (y = y)$} \end{prooftree} \] The problem in this example is substituting \(y\) for \(x\) when \(x\) is within the scope of the existential quantification of \(y\) . Do not do this! This is a faulty proof. It would follow from this that all numbers are zero. We assume a language in which there is a constant, \(0\) , and the equality predicate, \(=\) . \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$0 = 0$} \AXC{$[a = 0]_1$} \RightLabel{$ { orall}I $ } \UIC{$ orall x (x = 0)$} \RightLabel{$ {	o}I_1 $ } \UIC{$a = 0 	o  orall x (x = 0)$} \RightLabel{$ { orall}I $ } \UIC{$ orall x\, (x = 0 	o  orall x (x = 0))$} \RightLabel{$  orall E $ } \UIC{$0 = 0 	o  orall x\, (x = 0) $} \RightLabel{$ {	o}E $ } \BIC{$ orall x (x = 0)$} \end{prooftree} \] There is just one problem with the proof. The first use of the \( orall I\) rule goes from the assumption \(a = 0\) to the statement \( orall x\, (x=0)\) . This is not allowed because the assumption \(a = 0\) is undischarged at this point. We would be assuming that \(a\) has a special property and then deducing that everything must have that property. Note that the assumption \(a = 0\) is discharged later in the proof, but is undischarged when the \( orall I\) rule is first used. The problem here has nothing to do with special properties of the number zero or of equality. In a language with a constant \(k\) and a binary predicate \(P\) the idea of the proof could be used (wrongly) to show \[ P(k,k) \vdash  orall x\, P(x,k).\] 3.4 Interaction between quantifiers The proof shown in this section justifies \(
eg  orall x P(x) \vdash \exists x 
eg P(x)\) . This useful fact gives us a derived rule: \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$
eg  orall x P(x)$} \RightLabel{$
eg orall \exists 
eg$} \UIC{$\exists x 
eg P(x)$} \end{prooftree} \] To keep things simple it is written here for a unary predicate \(P\) . It works with any formula which may contain several free occurrences of \(x\) . \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[
eg P(a)]_2$} \RightLabel{$\; {\exists} I$} \UIC{$\exists x 
eg P(x)$} \AXC{$[
eg \exists x 
eg P(x)]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E_2$} \BIC{$P(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; { orall} I$} \UIC{$ orall x P(x)$} \AXC{$
eg  orall x P(x)$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E_1$} \BIC{$\exists x 
eg P(x)$} \end{prooftree} \] Let's see how the proof works. I will do this by revealing each part of the proof as the idea behind that part is explained. Assume, contrary to what we want to get, that \(
eg \exists x 
eg P(x)\) . Imagine picking an arbitrary element, \(a\) , in the domain. Suppose (or "assume", it means the same here) it is the case that \(
eg P(a)\) holds. If this happens we know that \(\exists x 
eg P(x)\) holds. Now we have both \(\exists x 
eg P(x)\) and \(
eg \exists x 
eg P(x)\) , which is a contradiction. So something we have assumed must be rejected. The proof works by rejecting the assumption \(
eg P(a)\) . you could try seeing what happens if instead you reject the assumption \(
eg \exists x 
eg P(x)\) at this point, but that would be a different proof and it does not appear to be a promising direction. As we are rejecting \(
eg P(a)\) we have \(P(a)\) . But the element \(a\) is arbitrary; at this point we are under no special assumptions about it, so we get \( orall x P(x)\) . But now we have both \( orall x P(x)\) and \(
eg  orall x P(x)\) , which is a contradiction. So something we have assumed must be rejected. This time we do reject the assumption \(
eg \exists x 
eg P(x)\) and we conclude \(\exists x 
eg P(x)\) . 3.5 Example Proofs 3.5.1 Example \[  orall x\,  orall y\,  orall z\, (R(x,y) \wedge R(y,z) 	o R(x,z)),\; \exists x\, \exists y\, (R(x,y) \wedge R(y,x)) \vdash \exists x\, R(x,x) \] \[  egin{prooftree} %---------------------------------------------------- \AXC{$\exists x\, \exists y\, (R(x,y) \wedge R(y,x))$} \AXC{$[\exists y\, (R(a,y) \wedge R(y,a))]_2$} \AXC{$[R(a,b) \wedge R(b,a)]_1$} \AXC{$ orall x\,  orall y\,  orall z\, (R(x,y) \wedge R(y,z) 	o R(x,z))$} \RightLabel{$ { orall}E$} \UIC{$ orall y\,  orall z\, (R(a,y) \wedge R(y,z) 	o R(a,z))$} \RightLabel{$ { orall}E $ } \UIC{$ orall z\, (R(a,b) \wedge R(b,z) 	o R(a,z))$} \RightLabel{$ { orall}E $ } \UIC{$R(a,b) \wedge R(b,a) 	o R(a,a)$} \RightLabel{$ {	o}E $ } \BIC{$R(a,a)$} \RightLabel{${\exists}I$} \UIC{$\exists x\, R(x,x)$} \RightLabel{$ {\exists}E_1 $ } \BIC{$\exists x\, R(x,x)$} \RightLabel{$ {\exists}E_2 $ } \BIC{$\exists x\, R(x,x)$} \end{prooftree} \] 3.5.2 Example \( orall x\,  orall y\, (P(x) \vee Q(y)) \vdash  orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y)\) First the idea behind the proof. \[  egin{array}{l} \mathsf{Either~ }  orall x\, P(x)\mathsf{~or~ not.}\ \qquad \mathsf{If~ }  orall x\, P(x) \mathsf{~then~}  orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y) \ \qquad \mathsf{If~ } 
eg  orall x\, P(x) \mathsf{~then~} \exists x 
eg P(x).\ \qquad \quad \mathsf{Let's~call~this~element~} a \mathsf{~and~let~} b \mathsf{~be~an~arbitrary~element.}\ \qquad \quad \mathsf{As~}  orall x\,  orall y\, (P(x) \vee Q(y)) \mathsf{~it~must~hold~that~} P(a) \vee Q(b).\ \qquad \quad \mathsf{Consider~the~two~cases } P(a) \mathsf{and} Q(b) .\ \qquad \quad \qquad \mathsf{The~case~ } P(a) \mathsf{and~ cannot~arise,~as~we~would~have~ } P(a) \wedge 
eg P(a). \ \qquad \quad \qquad\mathsf{Hence~} Q(b) , \mathsf{~but~} b \mathsf{~is~arbitrary,~so~ } orall y\, Q(y) . \ \qquad \quad \mathsf{So~in~the~case~that~} 
eg  orall x\, P(x) , \mathsf{~we~have~}~ orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y)\ \mathsf{Hence,~in~either~case,~}  orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y) \ \end{array} \] And now the detailed proof itself. $$  egin{prooftree} \AXC{ \(\quad\) } \RightLabel{$ EM $ } \UIC{$ orall x\, P(x) \vee 
eg  orall x\, P(x) $} \AXC{$[ orall x\, P(x)]_2$} \RightLabel{$ {\vee}I $ } \UIC{$ orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y)$} \AXC{$ orall x\,  orall y\, (P(x) \vee Q(y))$} \RightLabel{$ { orall}E $ } \UIC{$ orall y\, (P(a) \vee Q(y)) $} \RightLabel{$ { orall}E $ } \UIC{$P(a) \vee Q(b)$} \AXC{$[P(a)]_1$} \AXC{$[
eg  orall x P(x)]_2$} \RightLabel{$ {
eg  orall \exists 
eg} $ } \UIC{$\exists x 
eg P(x)$} \RightLabel{$ {\exists}E $ } \UIC{$
eg P(a)$} \RightLabel{$ {\wedge}I $ } \BIC{$P(a) \wedge 
eg P(a) $} \RightLabel{$ EFQL $ } \UIC{$Q(b)$} \AXC{$[Q(b)]_1$} \RightLabel{$ {\vee}E_1 $ } \TIC{$Q(b) $} \RightLabel{$ { orall}I $ } \UIC{$ orall y\, Q(y)$} \RightLabel{$ {\vee}I $ } \UIC{$ orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y) $} \RightLabel{$ {\vee}E_2 $ } \TIC{$ orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y) $} \end{prooftree} $$ Formative exercise Below are some suggestions for further practice in constructing natural deduction proofs. Solutions will be provided for all of these in Unit 4. If you have any questions before then, you can raise them in this week's webinar. In some cases there may be alternative solutions that are equally correct, so do not assume that if your answer is different then it is wrong. In each case you should construct a proof using the rules including labels for all steps so that you say which rule has been used and which assumptions (if any) have been discharged. The first ones are simple. The strategy is roughly to get rid of the quantifiers (but making sure you do only moves that are allowed by the rules) and then do a proof that only involves propositional ideas, and finally replace the quantifiers (again strictly abiding by the rules). \( orall x \; (P(x) 	o Q(x)) \vdash  orall x (
eg Q(x) 	o 
eg P(x))\) . \(\exists x\; (P(x) \vee Q(x)) \vdash \exists x P(x)\) . Finally a first order example that should help you with part of the second assessment. \( orall x\ (
eg P(x) \vee 
eg Q(x)) \vdash (\exists x P(x)) 	o (\exists y 
eg Q(y))\) . You have just finished lesson 3, which contains several examples given in detail. To make sure you understand how natural deduction proofs work in first order logic, you may want to go back and study these. It is also good to come up with your own simple examples of formulas that you can see should be provable (by thinking about the semantics) and then looking for natural deduction proofs of these.
docs.title: Lesson 3 Natural deduction (FOL)

docs.text: \(\def\phi{\varphi}\) We have seen natural deduction proofs for propositional logic. For first order logic there are extra rules to deal with the quantifiers. Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: write proofs for simple first order formulas in the natural deduction style correctly label every step of a natural deduction proof with the rule that allows it find errors in attempts at natural deduction proofs that are faulty.
docs.title: First order natural deduction

docs.text: For each of the two quantifiers there are two kinds of argument which are modelled by natural deduction rules. The kinds of arguments will be introduced, and then we will see how they have rules that capture these.
docs.title: 3.1 The informal  ideas behind the rules

docs.text: The argument goes like this: We know that for all \(x\) the statement \(\phi\) holds. We have a term \(t\) that we need to use in our argument. So we know that the statement \(\phi\) holds if we replace \(x\) by \(t\) everywhere in the statement. The idea is that we know \(\phi\) talking about \(x\) holds for all elements of the domain, so if we make the same statement but about a particular thing, \(t\) , it must also hold.
docs.title: Idea

docs.text: These take place in a setting where the stated things are known. We know that for all \(x\) , \(x^2 \geq 0\) . We are talking about the constant 42 in our argument. So we know that \(42^2 \geq 0\) . We know that for all \(x\) , \(x^2 \geq 0\) . We are talking about the term \(y +1\) in our argument. So we know that \((y+1)^2 \geq 0\) . But you do have to be careful: We know that for every \(x\) , we can find an element \(y\) where \(x \neq y\) . Since we know that for every \(x\) , it must hold in particular for \(y\) . So we know that we can find an element \(y\) where \(y \neq y\) . No. This is not a valid argument. We substituted \(y\) for \(x\) in a setting where the \(y\) became bound by another quantifier.
docs.title: Examples

docs.text: This is when you have this kind of argument: We are using \(x\) for a completely arbitrary element of the domain, being careful not to assume it has any special properties. We show that \(x\) has to have the property stated in the formula \(\phi\) . Because \(x\) was arbitrary, we conclude that the formula \(\phi\) must hold for all \(x\) .
docs.title: Idea

docs.text: Imagine we are talking about natural numbers. Let's use \(x\) for an arbitrary number. Now, suppose \(x\) is odd, so \(\mathit{Odd}(x)\) holds. We can find \(k\) such that \(x = 2k + 1\) . Then \(x^2 = 2(2k^2 +k) + 1\) . We have shown \(\mathit{Odd}(x) \to \mathit{Odd}(x^2)\) . As \(x\) was arbitrary, we conclude that \[\forall x\, (\mathit{Odd}(x) \to \mathit{Odd}(x^2))\] You have to be very careful that you are not assuming anything special about \(x\) when making this type of argument. Now a second example:
docs.title: Example

docs.text: assume \(x\) is arbitrary person and imagine they are inside Room 27 but the only route from the outside to room 27 passes through corridor 15 (information from map of building) conclude: everyone in room 27 has passed through corridor 15
docs.title: Security example

docs.text: The argument goes like this: We know there is some \(x\) with the property stated by \(\phi\) . Let's introduce a new name, \(a\) , for a particular thing with this property. We deduce that some statement, \(\psi\) , (not involving \(a\) ) must hold by an argument using the assumption that \(a\) has property \(\phi\) . We conclude that \(\psi\) holds on its own, without the assumption about \(a\) having property \(\phi\) . There are differences about how this is explained in different books: one way is to allow \(a\) to be a new constant (so strictly speaking we have to expand the language), another is to say \(a\) is a variable – you can call it \(x\) , but this can be confusing in some cases.
docs.title: Idea

docs.text: Fred can see everyone in room 27: \(\forall x\, (\mathit{IsIn}(x, room27) \to CanSee(fred,x ))\) . There is someone in room 27: \(\exists y\, \mathit{IsIn}(y, room27)\) . ( \(\ast\) ) Let's call this person \(p27\) and work under the assumption \(\mathit{IsIn}(p27, room27)\) . We know ( \(\forall\) elimination) that: \(\mathit{IsIn}(p27, room27) \to \mathit{CanSee}(fred,p27 )\) . We deduce ( \(\to\) elimination) that: \(\mathit{CanSee}(fred,p27 )\) We get ( \(\exists\) introduction) that: \(\exists x\, \mathit{CanSee}(fred,x )\) , Fred can see someone, but this is under the assumption \(\mathit{IsIn}(p27, room27)\) . Now, we use \(\exists\) elimination to conclude: \(\exists x\, \mathit{CanSee}(fred,x )\) , Fred can see someone. This no longer depends on the assumption in \(\ast\) .
docs.title: Example

docs.text: The argument goes like this: We have a statement \(\phi\) about some particular term \(t\) . We conclude there is some \(x\) with the property stated by \(\phi\) where we replace \(t\) by \(x\) .
docs.title: Idea

docs.text: We know Fred is in room 27: \(\mathit{IsIn(Fred, Room27)}\) . We conclude there is someone in Room 27: \(\exists x\, \mathit{IsIn}(x, \mathit{Room27})\) .
docs.title: Example
3.2 The rules for quantifiers expressed formally

docs.text: Introduction Rule \[ \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$\phi(t)$} \RightLabel{$\; \exists I$} \UIC{$\exists x\, \phi (x)$} \end{prooftree} \] Here \(\phi(t)\) means some formula containing a term \(t\) , and \(\phi (x)\) means the same formula but with \(t\) replaced by \(x\) . For example (assuming \(1\) and \(2\) are constants in the language and \(+\) is a function symbol) \[ \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$1 + 1 = 2$} \RightLabel{$\; \exists I$} \UIC{$\exists x\, (x + x = 2)$} \end{prooftree} \] Elimination Rule The idea of the \(a\) here is as an arbitrary constant. \[ \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$\exists x\, \phi (x)$} \AXC{$[\phi(a) ]_n \quad$} \noLine \UIC{$\vdots \quad$} \noLine \UIC{$\psi \quad$} \RightLabel{$\;\exists E_n$} \BIC{$\psi$} \end{prooftree}\] This rule can only be used under certain conditions. All of the following must happen in a correct use of the rule. \(a\) does not occur in \(\phi(x)\) , and \(a\) does not occur in \(\psi\) , and \(a\) does not occur in any undischarged assumption (except \(\phi(a)\) itself) used in the derviation of \(\psi\) from \(\phi(a)\) .
docs.title: 3.2.1 Existential Rules

docs.text:
The existential quantifier rules are shown in action in this example: \(\exists x\, 
eg P(x) \vdash \exists x\, (P(x) 	o Q(x))\) \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$\exists 
eg P(x)$} \AXC{$[
eg P(a)]_3$} \AXC{$[P(a)]_2$} \AXC{$[
eg Q(a)]_1$} \RightLabel{$\;{\wedge}I$} \BIC{$P(a) \wedge 
eg Q(a)$} \RightLabel{$\;{\wedge}Er$} \UIC{$P(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge}I$} \BIC{$
eg P(a) \wedge P(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg}E_1$} \UIC{$Q(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; {	o}I_2$} \UIC{$P(a) 	o Q(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; {\exists}I$} \UIC{$\exists x (P(x) 	o Q(x))$} \RightLabel{$\; {\exists}E_3$} \BIC{$\exists x (P(x) 	o Q(x))$} \end{prooftree} \]
docs.title: Example

docs.text: Here, \(\phi(x)\) means the formula \(\phi(a)\) with every occurrence of the constant \(a\) replaced by the variable \(x\) . \[ \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$\phi(a)$} \RightLabel{$\forall I$} \UIC{$\forall x\, \phi(x)$} \end{prooftree} \] The rule can only be used: Provided that the constant \(a\) does not occur in any undischarged assumption used in the derivation of \(\phi(a)\) .
docs.title: Introduction

docs.text: \[ \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$\forall x \, \phi(x)$} \RightLabel{$\forall E$} \UIC{$\phi(t)$} \end{prooftree} \] In this rule \(t\) is a term that gets substituted for \(x\) in \(\phi(x)\) to give \(\phi(t)\) . This is only allowed if \(t\) does not have any variables which become bound when substituted for \(x\) .
docs.title: Elimination

docs.text: Do not do this! This faulty proof appears to show that something is different from itself in a setting where each thing has something different from it. \[ \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$\forall x \, \exists y\, \neg (x = y)$} \RightLabel{$\forall E$} \UIC{$\exists y\, \neg (y = y)$} \end{prooftree} \] The problem in this example is substituting \(y\) for \(x\) when \(x\) is within the scope of the existential quantification of \(y\) . Do not do this! This is a faulty proof. It would follow from this that all numbers are zero. We assume a language in which there is a constant, \(0\) , and the equality predicate, \(=\) . \[ \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$0 = 0$} \AXC{$[a = 0]_1$} \RightLabel{$ {\forall}I $ } \UIC{$\forall x (x = 0)$} \RightLabel{$ {\to}I_1 $ } \UIC{$a = 0 \to \forall x (x = 0)$} \RightLabel{$ {\forall}I $ } \UIC{$\forall x\, (x = 0 \to \forall x (x = 0))$} \RightLabel{$ \forall E $ } \UIC{$0 = 0 \to \forall x\, (x = 0) $} \RightLabel{$ {\to}E $ } \BIC{$\forall x (x = 0)$} \end{prooftree} \] There is just one problem with the proof. The first use of the \(\forall I\) rule goes from the assumption \(a = 0\) to the statement \(\forall x\, (x=0)\) . This is not allowed because the assumption \(a = 0\) is undischarged at this point. We would be assuming that \(a\) has a special property and then deducing that everything must have that property. Note that the assumption \(a = 0\) is discharged later in the proof, but is undischarged when the \(\forall I\) rule is first used. The problem here has nothing to do with special properties of the number zero or of equality. In a language with a constant \(k\) and a binary predicate \(P\) the idea of the proof could be used (wrongly) to show \[ P(k,k) \vdash \forall x\, P(x,k).\]

eg$} \UIC{$\exists x 
eg P(x)$} \end{prooftree} \] To keep things simple it is written here for a unary predicate \(P\) . It works with any formula which may contain several free occurrences of \(x\) . \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[
eg P(a)]_2$} \RightLabel{$\; {\exists} I$} \UIC{$\exists x 
eg P(x)$} \AXC{$[
eg \exists x 
eg P(x)]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E_2$} \BIC{$P(a)$} \RightLabel{$\; { orall} I$} \UIC{$ orall x P(x)$} \AXC{$
eg  orall x P(x)$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E_1$} \BIC{$\exists x 
eg P(x)$} \end{prooftree} \] Let's see how the proof works. I will do this by revealing each part of the proof as the idea behind that part is explained. Assume, contrary to what we want to get, that \(

eg P(x)\) . Imagine picking an arbitrary element, \(a\) , in the domain. Suppose (or "assume", it means the same here) it is the case that \(
eg P(a)\) holds. If this happens we know that \(\exists x 
eg P(x)\) holds. Now we have both \(\exists x 
eg P(x)\) and \(
eg \exists x 
eg P(x)\) , which is a contradiction. So something we have assumed must be rejected. The proof works by rejecting the assumption \(
eg P(a)\) . you could try seeing what happens if instead you reject the assumption \(

eg P(x)\) at this point, but that would be a different proof and it does not appear to be a promising direction. As we are rejecting \(

eg  orall x P(x)\) , which is a contradiction. So something we have assumed must be rejected. This time we do reject the assumption \(

docs.title: 3.4 Interaction between quantifiers

docs.text: \[ \forall x\, \forall y\, \forall z\, (R(x,y) \wedge R(y,z) \to R(x,z)),\; \exists x\, \exists y\, (R(x,y) \wedge R(y,x)) \vdash \exists x\, R(x,x) \] \[ \begin{prooftree} %---------------------------------------------------- \AXC{$\exists x\, \exists y\, (R(x,y) \wedge R(y,x))$} \AXC{$[\exists y\, (R(a,y) \wedge R(y,a))]_2$} \AXC{$[R(a,b) \wedge R(b,a)]_1$} \AXC{$\forall x\, \forall y\, \forall z\, (R(x,y) \wedge R(y,z) \to R(x,z))$} \RightLabel{$ {\forall}E$} \UIC{$\forall y\, \forall z\, (R(a,y) \wedge R(y,z) \to R(a,z))$} \RightLabel{$ {\forall}E $ } \UIC{$\forall z\, (R(a,b) \wedge R(b,z) \to R(a,z))$} \RightLabel{$ {\forall}E $ } \UIC{$R(a,b) \wedge R(b,a) \to R(a,a)$} \RightLabel{$ {\to}E $ } \BIC{$R(a,a)$} \RightLabel{${\exists}I$} \UIC{$\exists x\, R(x,x)$} \RightLabel{$ {\exists}E_1 $ } \BIC{$\exists x\, R(x,x)$} \RightLabel{$ {\exists}E_2 $ } \BIC{$\exists x\, R(x,x)$} \end{prooftree} \]

eg P(a). \ \qquad \quad \qquad\mathsf{Hence~} Q(b) , \mathsf{~but~} b \mathsf{~is~arbitrary,~so~ } orall y\, Q(y) . \ \qquad \quad \mathsf{So~in~the~case~that~} 
eg  orall x\, P(x) , \mathsf{~we~have~}~ orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y)\ \mathsf{Hence,~in~either~case,~}  orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y) \ \end{array} \] And now the detailed proof itself. $$  egin{prooftree} \AXC{ \(\quad\) } \RightLabel{$ EM $ } \UIC{$ orall x\, P(x) \vee 
eg  orall x\, P(x) $} \AXC{$[ orall x\, P(x)]_2$} \RightLabel{$ {\vee}I $ } \UIC{$ orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y)$} \AXC{$ orall x\,  orall y\, (P(x) \vee Q(y))$} \RightLabel{$ { orall}E $ } \UIC{$ orall y\, (P(a) \vee Q(y)) $} \RightLabel{$ { orall}E $ } \UIC{$P(a) \vee Q(b)$} \AXC{$[P(a)]_1$} \AXC{$[
eg  orall x P(x)]_2$} \RightLabel{$ {
eg  orall \exists 
eg} $ } \UIC{$\exists x 
eg P(x)$} \RightLabel{$ {\exists}E $ } \UIC{$
eg P(a)$} \RightLabel{$ {\wedge}I $ } \BIC{$P(a) \wedge 
eg P(a) $} \RightLabel{$ EFQL $ } \UIC{$Q(b)$} \AXC{$[Q(b)]_1$} \RightLabel{$ {\vee}E_1 $ } \TIC{$Q(b) $} \RightLabel{$ { orall}I $ } \UIC{$ orall y\, Q(y)$} \RightLabel{$ {\vee}I $ } \UIC{$ orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y) $} \RightLabel{$ {\vee}E_2 $ } \TIC{$ orall x\, P(x) \vee  orall y\, Q(y) $} \end{prooftree} $$ Formative exercise Below are some suggestions for further practice in constructing natural deduction proofs. Solutions will be provided for all of these in Unit 4. If you have any questions before then, you can raise them in this week's webinar. In some cases there may be alternative solutions that are equally correct, so do not assume that if your answer is different then it is wrong. In each case you should construct a proof using the rules including labels for all steps so that you say which rule has been used and which assumptions (if any) have been discharged. The first ones are simple. The strategy is roughly to get rid of the quantifiers (but making sure you do only moves that are allowed by the rules) and then do a proof that only involves propositional ideas, and finally replace the quantifiers (again strictly abiding by the rules). \( orall x \; (P(x) 	o Q(x)) \vdash  orall x (
eg Q(x) 	o 
eg P(x))\) . \(\exists x\; (P(x) \vee Q(x)) \vdash \exists x P(x)\) . Finally a first order example that should help you with part of the second assessment. \( orall x\ (
eg P(x) \vee 
eg Q(x)) \vdash (\exists x P(x)) 	o (\exists y 
eg Q(y))\) . You have just finished lesson 3, which contains several examples given in detail. To make sure you understand how natural deduction proofs work in first order logic, you may want to go back and study these. It is also good to come up with your own simple examples of formulas that you can see should be provable (by thinking about the semantics) and then looking for natural deduction proofs of these.
docs.title: 3.5.2 Example

docs.text:
Natural deduction Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: Build natural deduction proofs in tree format for propositional logic using formal rules at every step. Use temporary assumptions in these proofs and discharge them when the rules permit. Check natural deduction proofs for errors. Introduction to natural deduction In this lesson we study formal proofs set out in a tree format and in a style called natural deduction. It is called natural deduction because the structure of the formal proof is close to the kind of argument that might be made in an informal way – when you are trying to explain the reason for something to somebody else. As with any style of proof, natural deduction proofs are used to justify that a conclusion follows from some hypotheses, or in some cases from no hypotheses. In the example studied in lesson 1, the proof justified: \[R ightarrow S, S ightarrow 
eg R \vdash 
eg R.\] In this example there are two hypotheses: \(R ightarrow S\) and \(S ightarrow 
eg R\) , and a conclusion: \(
eg R\) . The key features to watch out for in natural deduction are: Temporary assumptions can be made which parts of the proof depend on, but which are not needed to justify the ultimate conclusion. When temporary assumptions are no longer needed they are discharged . In the proof a discharged assumption appears between square brackets \([\;\;]\) . In addition to the modus ponens example in Lesson 1, there are other inference rules. These exist for each logical connective, and come in two flavours, or kinds: Introduction rules , in which the connective is introduced into the conclusion of the rule Elimination rules , in which an occurrence of the connective in an antecedent of the rule is eliminated and does not appear in the conclusion of the rule. Three examples of natural deduction proofs as trees Before presenting all the details of the rules, we return to the three proofs shown in Lesson 1 of this unit. In each case there is a video in which the construction of the tree is shown alongside the proof as constructed in Lesson 1. Example 1 Download transcript (PDF). The proof shown in the video is this one: \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[R]$} \AXC{$[R]$} \AXC{$R 	o S$} \BIC{$S$} \AXC{$S 	o 
eg R$} \BIC{$
eg R$} \BIC{$R \wedge 
eg R$} \UIC{$
eg R$} \end{prooftree} \] The proof justifies that \(R 	o S,\; S 	o 
eg R \vdash 
eg R\) . You may notice that the horizontal lines in the proof above have different thicknesses. This has no significance, and may not be noticeable depending on your browser, screen, etc. The correspondence between the argument written in words and parts of the proof is shown in this image: Select for full text description On the right hand side of the image a proof is displayed. The proof justifies that from R implies S and S implies not R we can derive that not R. The proof goes as follows: Line one: the label “Hypothesis 1”. Line two: open square bracket, R, closed square bracket. R implies S. The label on line 1 “Hypothesis 1” is shown above the formula R implies S. A black derivation line is displayed underneath the formulae open square bracket, R, closed square bracket and R implies S. Next to this there is the label “Hypothesis 2”. Line three: S. This is displayed underneath the derivation line of line two. Next to this there is S implies not R. The label on line two “Hypothesis 2” is shown above the formula S implies not R. A black derivation line is displayed underneath S and S implies not R. Line four: open square bracket, R, closed squared bracket. Next to this there is the formula not R. Not R is displayed underneath the derivation line of line three. A black derivation line is displayed underneath the formulae open square bracket, R, closed squared bracket and not R. Line five: R and not R. This is displayed underneath the derivation line of line four. A black derivation line is displayed underneath the formula R and not R. Line six: not R. This is displayed underneath the derivation line of line five. On the left hand side of the image there is the following writing. Line one says: “Suppose this is red”. There are two red arrows point to the formulae open square bracket, R, closed square bracket, displayed on lines two and four of the proof previously described. Line two says “By one, it is square”. There is a red arrow pointing to the formula S displayed on line three of the proof previously described. Line three says: “By two, it is not red”. There is a red arrow pointing to the formula not R displayed on line four of the proof previously described. Line four says: “Our assumption is impossible”. There is a red arrow pointing to the formula R and not R displayed on line five of the proof previously described. Line five says: “Hence it is not red”. There is a red arrow pointing to the formula not R displayed on line six of the proof previously described. Example 2 Download transcript (PDF). The proof shown in the video is this one: \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$S \vee 
eg S$} \AXC{$[
eg S]$} \AXC{$[R]$} \AXC{$R 	o S$} \BIC{$S$} \BIC{$S \wedge 
eg S$} \UIC{$
eg R$} \AXC{$[S]$} \AXC{$S 	o 
eg R$} \BIC{$
eg R$} \TIC{$
eg R$} \end{prooftree} \] The proof shows that \(S \vee 
eg S,\; R 	o S,\; S 	o 
eg R \vdash 
eg R\) . Example 3 Download transcript (PDF). The proof shown in the video is this one: \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$(A \wedge B) \vee C$} \AXC{$[A \wedge B]$} \UIC{$A$} \UIC{$A \vee C$} \AXC{$[A \wedge B]$} \UIC{$B$} \UIC{$B \vee C$} \BIC{$(A \vee C) \wedge (B \vee C)$} \AXC{$[C]$} \UIC{$A \vee C$} \AXC{$[C]$} \UIC{$B \vee C$} \BIC{$(A \vee C) \wedge (B \vee C)$} \TIC{$(A \vee C) \wedge (B \vee C)$} \end{prooftree} \] The proof shows that \((A \wedge B) \vee C \vdash (A \vee C) \wedge (B \vee C)\) . The natural deduction rules for each connective Each connective has one or more rules to introduce the connective, and one or more rules to eliminate the connective. Each rule has a name which appears to the right of the horizontal line. The name consists of the symbol for the connective followed by \(I\) (Introduction) or \(E\) (Elimination), with possible additional letters to indicate a left hand version ( \(\ell\) ) or a right hand one ( \(r\) ). When a rule allows you to discharge an assumption, the name of the rule can carry a subscript number to keep track of which assumption is being discharged by a particular occurrence of a rule. The following video explains the rules. Download transcript (PDF). Rules for AND \[  egin{array}{c} \mathsf{Introduction}\[2ex]  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$P$} \AXC{$Q$} \RightLabel{$\;\;\wedge I$} \BIC{$P \wedge Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad  egin{array}{c} \mathsf{Elimination}\[2ex]  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$P \wedge Q$} \RightLabel{$\;\;\wedge Er$} \UIC{$P$} \end{prooftree} \quad  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$P \wedge Q$} \RightLabel{$\;\;\wedge E\ell$} \UIC{$Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \] Rules for OR \[  egin{array}{c} \mathsf{Introduction}\[9ex]  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$P$} \RightLabel{$\;\vee Ir$} \UIC{$P \vee Q$} \end{prooftree} \;\;  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$Q$} \RightLabel{$\;\vee I\ell$} \UIC{$P \vee Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \qquad \qquad  egin{array}{c} \mathsf{Elimination}\[2ex]  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A \vee B$} \AXC{$[A]_n$} 
oLine \UIC{$\vdots$} 
oLine \UIC{$P$} \AXC{$[B]_n$} 
oLine \UIC{$\vdots$} 
oLine \UIC{$P$} \RightLabel{$\;{\vee}E_n$} \TIC{$P$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \] The elimination rule for \(\vee\) looks complex but comes from the following simple scenario: You know that \(A \vee B\) is true (but not which is true, or if both are) You want to prove \(P\) You can prove \(P\) by assuming \(A\) is true You can prove \(P\) by assuming \(B\) is true Then you are entitled to conclude that \(P\) holds and discharge the temporary assumptions about \(A\) and \(B\) . Rules for NOT \[  egin{array}{c} \mathsf{Introduction}\[2ex]  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[A]_{n}$} 
oLine \UIC{$\vdots$} 
oLine \UIC{$P \wedge 
eg P$} \RightLabel{$\; 
eg I_{n}$} \UIC{$
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad  egin{array}{c} \mathsf{Elimination}\[2ex]  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[
eg A]_{n}$} 
oLine \UIC{$\vdots$} 
oLine \UIC{$P \wedge 
eg P$} \RightLabel{$\; 
eg E_{n}$} \UIC{$A$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \] The idea behind both the introduction rule and the elimination rule shown above, is the reasoning strategy known as reductio ad absurdum in which an assumption must be rejected because it leads to a contradiction, or, in other words, an absurdity. In the introduction rule we start off with an assumption that \(A\) is true, and derive a contradiction, so we conclude \(A\) is false and we can discharge the assumption \(A\) . In the elimination rule we start off with an assumption that \(A\) is false, and derive a contradiction, so we conclude \(A\) is true and we can discharge the assumption \(
eg A\) . Rules for IMPLIES \[  egin{array}{c} \mathsf{Introduction}\[2ex]  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[P]_{n}$} 
oLine \UIC{$\vdots$} 
oLine \UIC{$Q$} \RightLabel{$\; {	o} I_{n}$} \UIC{$P 	o Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad  egin{array}{c} \mathsf{Elimination}\[9ex]  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$P$} \AXC{$P 	o Q$} \RightLabel{$\; {	o}E$} \BIC{$Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \] The elimination rule for \(	o\) is the rule we met in Lesson 1 under the name modus ponens . The introduction rule allows us to assume that \(P\) is true and argue why \(Q\) must then be true, and from that to conclude that \(P 	o Q\) holds without the assumption \(P\) which is discharged. The introductory examples in complete detail Example 1 Returning now to the example at the start of this lesson, we can now present it in the form below in which each step is labelled by the rule which justifies the step. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[R]_1$} \AXC{$[R]_1$} \AXC{$R 	o S$} \RightLabel{$\; {	o}E$} \BIC{$S$} \AXC{$S 	o 
eg R$} \RightLabel{$\; {	o}E$} \BIC{$
eg R$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge}I$} \BIC{$R \wedge 
eg R$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg}I_1$} \UIC{$
eg R$} \end{prooftree} \] In a natural deduction proof, every step needs to be justified by one of the rules. Example 2 \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$S \vee 
eg S$} \AXC{$[
eg S]$} \AXC{$[R]$} \AXC{$R 	o S$} \RightLabel{$\;{	o} E$} \BIC{$S$} \RightLabel{$\;{\wedge} I$} \BIC{$S \wedge 
eg S$} \RightLabel{$\;{
eg} I$} \UIC{$
eg R$} \AXC{$[S]$} \AXC{$S 	o 
eg R$} \RightLabel{$\;{	o} E$} \BIC{$
eg R$} \RightLabel{$\;{\vee} E$} \TIC{$
eg R$} \end{prooftree} \] Example 3 This is left as an exercise for you. Go back to the example and check that you can see which rule is being used at each stage. More examples of natural deduction proofs Example 4: anything follows from a contradiction Here are two proofs that \(\quad A, \; 
eg A \vdash B\) Notice in both proofs the assumption \(
eg B\) , the negation of the desired conclusion, is introduced into a conjunction, is then immediately eliminated, and is discharged at the end. We do not actually need the assumption of \(
eg B\) to derive the contradiction \(A \wedge 
eg A\) , but these examples are instructive in showing how the rules work as tools for manipulating formulas. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A$} \AXC{$[
eg B]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg B$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} Er$} \UIC{$A$} \AXC{$
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E_1$} \UIC{$B$} \end{prooftree} \qquad \qquad  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A$} \AXC{$
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \AXC{$[
eg B]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$(A \wedge 
eg A) \wedge 
eg B$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} Er$} \UIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E_1$} \UIC{$B$} \end{prooftree} \] These examples have been includes as it is easy to see how they fit the pattern of the rule \({
eg}E\) where you can see the conclusion \(B\) together with the discharged assumption \(
eg B\) . However, the rule \({
eg}E\) allows you to use an assumption such as \(
eg B\) any number of times, including zero. The same holds for the discharge of assumptions in other rules. This means that we can have the following proof that \(A, \; 
eg A \vdash B\) . \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A$} \AXC{$
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E$} \UIC{$B$} \end{prooftree} \] Example 5 Proof that \(\quad A \vee B, \; 
eg A \vdash B\) Here the proof in the previous example appears as part of the larger proof. In this example we assume we have both \(A \vee B\) and \(
eg A\) then argue that \(B\) must hold. The pattern of the argument is that since \(A\) or \(B\) we can consider these two cases separately. In the case that \(A\) holds, then because we are assuming \(
eg A\) we have a contradiction from which anything can be derived. In particular we can derive \(B\) . In the second of the two cases we immediately have that \(B\) holds. Since we have analysed both cases and found \(B\) in each case, we know \(B\) holds without any temporary assumption about which of the two cases holds. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A \vee B$} \AXC{$[A]_1$} \AXC{$
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E$} \UIC{$B$} \AXC{$[B]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\vee} E_1$} \TIC{$B$} \end{prooftree} \] Example 6 Proof that \(\quad A 	o B, 
eg B \vdash 
eg A\) \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[A]_1$} \AXC{$A 	o B$} \RightLabel{$\; {	o} E$} \BIC{$B$} \AXC{$
eg B$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$B \wedge 
eg B$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} I_1$} \UIC{$
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \] You should be able to take this example and demonstrate the principle of reasoning by contraposition. This is the principle that \(A 	o B\) and \(
eg B 	o 
eg A\) are equivalent statements. In terms of proofs this means demonstrating both \(A 	o B \vdash 
eg B 	o 
eg A\) and \(
eg B 	o 
eg A \vdash A 	o B\) . Negated disjunction examples: 7 and 8 Proof that \(\quad 
eg (A \vee B) \vdash 
eg A\) The strategy here should be familiar from previous examples. We want to conclude \(
eg A\) , so we assume \(A\) and manage to show that this leads to a contradiction. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[A]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\vee} Ir$} \UIC{$A \vee B$} \AXC{$
eg(A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$(A \vee B) \wedge 
eg ( A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\; 
eg I_1$} \UIC{$
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \] The fact that from \(
eg(A \vee B)\) we can deduce \(
eg A\) is useful in some proofs we build later. To avoid having to write the whole proof again, we will call the derived rule \({
eg}{\vee}Er\) . Written in the same format as the fundamental rules it looks like this: \[ egin{prooftree} \AXC{$
eg(A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}{\vee}Er$} \UIC{$
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \] Proof that \(\quad 
eg (A \vee B) \vdash 
eg B\) \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[B]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\vee} I\ell$} \UIC{$A \vee B$} \AXC{$
eg(A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$(A \vee B) \wedge 
eg ( A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\; 
eg I_1$} \UIC{$
eg B$} \end{prooftree} \] Just as in the previous example, we can make a derived rule from this so that it can be reused easily. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$
eg(A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}{\vee}E\ell$} \UIC{$
eg B$} \end{prooftree} \] Example 9: double negation elimination Proof that \(\quad 
eg 
eg A \vdash A, \quad\) In this case the proof as it might be written in a less symbolic way is shown alongside. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[
eg A]_1$} \AXC{$
eg 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{\wedge}I$} \BIC{$
eg A \wedge 
eg 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}E_1$} \UIC{$A$} \end{prooftree} \qquad  egin{array}{l} \mathsf{We~ are~ given}\; 
eg 
eg A,\; \mathsf{suppose~ also~ that~ }
eg A.\ \mathsf{Then~ we~ have~ both}\; 
eg A\; \mathsf{and} \;
eg 
eg A.\ \mathsf{That~ is~ a~ contradiction;~ so} \; 
eg A\; \mathsf{cannot~ hold,}\ \mathsf{ and~ we~ must~ have~} A. \end{array} \] \[  egin{array}{l} \mathsf{This~ makes~ another~ useful~ derived~ rule.}\ \mathsf{It~ is~ called~ Double~ Negation~ Elimination,}\ \mathsf{which~ we~ abbreviate~ as~ DNE.} \end{array} \qquad  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$
eg 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\;\;\mathsf{DNE}$} \UIC{$A$} \end{prooftree} \] Example 10: double negation introduction Proof that \(\quad A \vdash 
eg 
eg A \quad\) This is not as much use in practice as the elimination of double negation ( \(\mathsf{DNE}\) ). However, you should notice how the proof of the ability to introduce double negation has a very similar structure. In this case we use the negation introduction rule ( \({
eg}I\) ); to establish \(\mathsf{DNE}\) we used negation elimination ( \({
eg}E\) ). \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A$} \AXC{$[
eg A]_1$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{\wedge}I$} \BIC{$ A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}I_1$} \UIC{$
eg 
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \qquad  egin{array}{l} \mathsf{We~ are~ given}\; A,\; \mathsf{suppose~ also~ that~ }
eg A.\ \mathsf{Then~ we~ have~ both}\; A\; \mathsf{and} \;
eg A.\ \mathsf{That~ is~ a~ contradiction;~ so} \; 
eg A\; \mathsf{cannot~ hold,}\ \mathsf{ and~ we~ must~ have~} 
eg 
eg A. \end{array} \] Example 11: excluded middle (EM) Proof that \(\quad \vdash A \vee 
eg A\) Excluded middle is so called because it expresses the idea that a logical statement is either true or its negation is true, and there is no possibility in the middle between these extremes. Excluded middle is sometimes called by the Latin name tertium non datur , which means that there is no third option. The idea behind the proof is to use contradiction. We assume that \(
eg(A \vee 
eg A)\) holds. We then use the earlier proofs which gave the derived rules \({
eg}{\vee}Er\) and \({
eg}{\vee}E\ell\) . These allow us to deduce both \(
eg A\) and \(
eg 
eg A\) . This is a contradiction: both \(
eg A\) and its negation. So our original assumption is impossible and we conclude that \(A \vee 
eg A\) holds. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[
eg(A \vee 
eg A)]_1$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}{\vee}Er$} \UIC{$
eg A$} \AXC{$[
eg(A \vee 
eg A)]_1$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}{\vee}E\ell$} \UIC{$ 
eg 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{\wedge}I$} \BIC{$
eg A \wedge 
eg 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}E_1$} \UIC{$A \vee 
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \] Formative exercise Below are some suggestions for further practice in constructing natural deduction proofs. Solutions will be provided for all of these in Unit 4. If you have any questions before then, you can raise them in this week's webinar. In some cases there may be alternative solutions that are equally correct, so do not assume that if your answer is different then it is wrong. In each case you should construct a proof using the rules including labels for all steps so that you say which rule has been used and which assumptions (if any) have been discharged. \(\vdash A 	o A\) . \(B \vdash A 	o B\) . \(\vdash B 	o (A 	o B\) ). These two to prove are already suggested in lesson 2 example 6: \(A 	o B \vdash 
eg B 	o 
eg A\) and \(
eg B 	o 
eg A \vdash A 	o B\) . \((A 	o B) 	o A \vdash B 	o A\) . \(A \wedge B \vdash A 	o B\) . \((A 	o B) 	o C \vdash A 	o (B 	o C)\) . \(A \vee B \vdash (A 	o B) \vee (B 	o A)\) . \(A \wedge B \vdash (A 	o B) \wedge (B 	o A)\) . \(
eg A \vdash A 	o B\) .

Not R is displayed underneath the derivation line of line three. A black derivation line is displayed underneath the formulae open square bracket, R, closed squared bracket and not R. Line five: R and not R. This is displayed underneath the derivation line of line four. A black derivation line is displayed underneath the formula R and not R. Line six: not R. This is displayed underneath the derivation line of line five. On the left hand side of the image there is the following writing. Line one says: “Suppose this is red”. The proof shown in the video is this one: \[ \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$(A \wedge B) \vee C$} \AXC{$[A \wedge B]$} \UIC{$A$} \UIC{$A \vee C$} \AXC{$[A \wedge B]$} \UIC{$B$} \UIC{$B \vee C$} \BIC{$(A \vee C) \wedge (B \vee C)$} \AXC{$[C]$} \UIC{$A \vee C$} \AXC{$[C]$} \UIC{$B \vee C$} \BIC{$(A \vee C) \wedge (B \vee C)$} \TIC{$(A \vee C) \wedge (B \vee C)$} \end{prooftree} \] The proof shows that \((A \wedge B) \vee C \vdash (A \vee C) \wedge (B \vee C)\) .
docs.title: Example 3

docs.text: Each connective has one or more rules to introduce the connective, and one or more rules to eliminate the connective. Each rule has a name which appears to the right of the horizontal line. The name consists of the symbol for the connective followed by \(I\) (Introduction) or \(E\) (Elimination), with possible additional letters to indicate a left hand version ( \(\ell\) ) or a right hand one ( \(r\) ). When a rule allows you to discharge an assumption, the name of the rule can carry a subscript number to keep track of which assumption is being discharged by a particular occurrence of a rule. The following video explains the rules. Download transcript (PDF).
docs.title: The natural deduction rules for each connective

docs.text: \[ \begin{array}{c} \mathsf{Introduction}\\[2ex] \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$P$} \AXC{$Q$} \RightLabel{$\;\;\wedge I$} \BIC{$P \wedge Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \begin{array}{c} \mathsf{Elimination}\\[2ex] \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$P \wedge Q$} \RightLabel{$\;\;\wedge Er$} \UIC{$P$} \end{prooftree} \quad \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$P \wedge Q$} \RightLabel{$\;\;\wedge E\ell$} \UIC{$Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \]
docs.title: Rules for AND
Rules for OR
Rules for NOT

docs.text: \[ \begin{array}{c} \mathsf{Introduction}\\[2ex] \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$[P]_{n}$} \noLine \UIC{$\vdots$} \noLine \UIC{$Q$} \RightLabel{$\; {\to} I_{n}$} \UIC{$P \to Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \begin{array}{c} \mathsf{Elimination}\\[9ex] \begin{prooftree} \AXC{$P$} \AXC{$P \to Q$} \RightLabel{$\; {\to}E$} \BIC{$Q$} \end{prooftree} \end{array} \] The elimination rule for \(\to\) is the rule we met in Lesson 1 under the name modus ponens . The introduction rule allows us to assume that \(P\) is true and argue why \(Q\) must then be true, and from that to conclude that \(P \to Q\) holds without the assumption \(P\) which is discharged.

eg R$} \RightLabel{$\; {	o}E$} \BIC{$

eg}I_1$} \UIC{$
eg R$} \end{prooftree} \] In a natural deduction proof, every step needs to be justified by one of the rules.

More examples of natural deduction proofs

docs.text:
Here are two proofs that \(\quad A, \; 
eg A \vdash B\) Notice in both proofs the assumption \(
eg B\) , the negation of the desired conclusion, is introduced into a conjunction, is then immediately eliminated, and is discharged at the end. We do not actually need the assumption of \(
eg B\) to derive the contradiction \(A \wedge 
eg A\) , but these examples are instructive in showing how the rules work as tools for manipulating formulas. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A$} \AXC{$[
eg B]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg B$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} Er$} \UIC{$A$} \AXC{$
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E_1$} \UIC{$B$} \end{prooftree} \qquad \qquad  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A$} \AXC{$
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \AXC{$[
eg B]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$(A \wedge 
eg A) \wedge 
eg B$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} Er$} \UIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E_1$} \UIC{$B$} \end{prooftree} \] These examples have been includes as it is easy to see how they fit the pattern of the rule \({
eg}E\) where you can see the conclusion \(B\) together with the discharged assumption \(
eg B\) . However, the rule \({
eg}E\) allows you to use an assumption such as \(
eg B\) any number of times, including zero. The same holds for the discharge of assumptions in other rules. This means that we can have the following proof that \(A, \; 
eg A \vdash B\) . \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A$} \AXC{$
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E$} \UIC{$B$} \end{prooftree} \]
docs.title: Example 4: anything follows from a contradiction

docs.text:
Proof that \(\quad A \vee B, \; 
eg A \vdash B\) Here the proof in the previous example appears as part of the larger proof. In this example we assume we have both \(A \vee B\) and \(
eg A\) then argue that \(B\) must hold. The pattern of the argument is that since \(A\) or \(B\) we can consider these two cases separately. In the case that \(A\) holds, then because we are assuming \(
eg A\) we have a contradiction from which anything can be derived. In particular we can derive \(B\) . In the second of the two cases we immediately have that \(B\) holds. Since we have analysed both cases and found \(B\) in each case, we know \(B\) holds without any temporary assumption about which of the two cases holds. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$A \vee B$} \AXC{$[A]_1$} \AXC{$
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$A \wedge 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} E$} \UIC{$B$} \AXC{$[B]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\vee} E_1$} \TIC{$B$} \end{prooftree} \]

eg B$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$B \wedge 
eg B$} \RightLabel{$\; {
eg} I_1$} \UIC{$
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \] You should be able to take this example and demonstrate the principle of reasoning by contraposition. This is the principle that \(A 	o B\) and \(
eg B 	o 
eg A\) are equivalent statements. In terms of proofs this means demonstrating both \(A 	o B \vdash 
eg B 	o 
eg A\) and \(
eg B 	o 
eg A \vdash A 	o B\) .

docs.text:
Proof that \(\quad

eg A\) , so we assume \(A\) and manage to show that this leads to a contradiction. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[A]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\vee} Ir$} \UIC{$A \vee B$} \AXC{$

eg ( A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\;

eg A$} \end{prooftree} \] The fact that from \(

eg A\) is useful in some proofs we build later. To avoid having to write the whole proof again, we will call the derived rule \({
eg}{\vee}Er\) . Written in the same format as the fundamental rules it looks like this: \[ egin{prooftree} \AXC{$
eg(A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}{\vee}Er$} \UIC{$
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \] Proof that \(\quad 
eg (A \vee B) \vdash 
eg B\) \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[B]_1$} \RightLabel{$\; {\vee} I\ell$} \UIC{$A \vee B$} \AXC{$
eg(A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\; {\wedge} I$} \BIC{$(A \vee B) \wedge 
eg ( A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\;

eg B$} \end{prooftree} \] Just as in the previous example, we can make a derived rule from this so that it can be reused easily. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$
eg(A \vee B)$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{

eg B$} \end{prooftree} \]

Proof that \(\quad \vdash A \vee 
eg A\) Excluded middle is so called because it expresses the idea that a logical statement is either true or its negation is true, and there is no possibility in the middle between these extremes. Excluded middle is sometimes called by the Latin name tertium non datur , which means that there is no third option. The idea behind the proof is to use contradiction. We assume that \(
eg(A \vee 
eg A)\) holds. We then use the earlier proofs which gave the derived rules \({
eg}{\vee}Er\) and \({
eg}{\vee}E\ell\) . These allow us to deduce both \(
eg A\) and \(
eg 
eg A\) . This is a contradiction: both \(
eg A\) and its negation. So our original assumption is impossible and we conclude that \(A \vee 
eg A\) holds. \[  egin{prooftree} \AXC{$[
eg(A \vee 
eg A)]_1$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}{\vee}Er$} \UIC{$
eg A$} \AXC{$[
eg(A \vee 
eg A)]_1$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}{\vee}E\ell$} \UIC{$ 
eg 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{\wedge}I$} \BIC{$
eg A \wedge 
eg 
eg A$} \RightLabel{$\;\;{
eg}E_1$} \UIC{$A \vee 
eg A$} \end{prooftree} \] Formative exercise Below are some suggestions for further practice in constructing natural deduction proofs. Solutions will be provided for all of these in Unit 4. If you have any questions before then, you can raise them in this week's webinar. In some cases there may be alternative solutions that are equally correct, so do not assume that if your answer is different then it is wrong. In each case you should construct a proof using the rules including labels for all steps so that you say which rule has been used and which assumptions (if any) have been discharged. \(\vdash A 	o A\) . \(B \vdash A 	o B\) . \(\vdash B 	o (A 	o B\) ). These two to prove are already suggested in lesson 2 example 6: \(A 	o B \vdash 
eg B 	o 
eg A\) and \(
eg B 	o 
eg A \vdash A 	o B\) . \((A 	o B) 	o A \vdash B 	o A\) . \(A \wedge B \vdash A 	o B\) . \((A 	o B) 	o C \vdash A 	o (B 	o C)\) . \(A \vee B \vdash (A 	o B) \vee (B 	o A)\) . \(A \wedge B \vdash (A 	o B) \wedge (B 	o A)\) . \(
eg A \vdash A 	o B\) .

docs.text:
Propositional resolution Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: convert questions about deduction in propositional logic into clausal form so that resolution can be applied identify all possible ways to resolve one clause with another carry out the resolution refutation procedure on a given set of clauses. Introduction to resolution Download transcript (PDF). Literals A literal is a wff which is either an atom (an atomic proposition) or the negation of an atom. Atoms are also called positive literals and negated atoms are called negative literals . This does not mean literals have to be a single letter. The wff \(\mathit{tuesday} \vee \mathit{friday}\) contains two literals. Examples Which of the following wffs are positive literals, which are negative literals, and which are not literals? \(a \vee b\) \(a\) \(
eg 
eg b\) \(a 	o 
eg b\) \(
eg b\) \(b\) Select to reveal answers \(a \vee b\) Not a literal \(a\) A positive literal \(
eg 
eg b\) Not a literal (only allowed a single \(
eg\) in a negative literal) \(a 	o 
eg b\) Not a literal \(
eg b\) A negative literal \(b\) A positive literal Clauses A clause is a set of literals. There are lots of different notations, but clauses will be denoted with square brackets. For example \([a, 
eg a, b]\) and \([a, 
eg b]\) are clauses. The reason for not writing these sets with { and } is that we later need sets of clauses and we will use { and } for those. A clause is used to stand for the disjunction of its elements. For example the clause \([a, 
eg b]\) is a useful way of referring to both the equivalent (but different) wffs \(a \vee 
eg b\) and \(
eg b \vee a\) . The clause \([a, b, c]\) refers to several different wffs including \(a \vee (b \vee c)\) , and \((a \vee b) \vee c\) , and \(b \vee (a \vee c)\) . The empty clause, \([\,]\) is allowed and is equivalent to false. Horn clauses and prolog Horn clauses are clauses with at most one positive literal. In the table below, since \(A 	o B\) is equivalent to \(
eg A \vee B\) , the Horn clauses on the left can be expressed as the wffs in the second column. Their Prolog expression appears in the third column. \[  egin{array}{l|l|l|l} \mathsf{Horn ~clause}& \mathsf{wff}& \mathsf{Prolog~ expression } & \mathsf{this~is~a}\ \hline a \vee 
eg b & b 	o a & 	exttt{ a :- b.} & \mathsf{rule}\ \hline a & \mathsf{true} 	o a & 	exttt{ a.} & \mathsf{fact}\ \hline 
eg b \vee 
eg a \vee c & (b \wedge a ) 	o c & 	exttt{ c :- b, a.} & \mathsf{rule}\ \hline 
eg a & a 	o \mathsf{false} & 	exttt{ :- a. } & \mathsf{goal}\ \hline \end{array} \] Prolog implements resolution refutation. E.g. try to prove the goal \(a\) from statements \(b\) , \(b 	o a\) . b. a :- b. :- a. You can put that in one file, but more usually you would have a file with the statements: b. a :- b. and then at the interactive prompt ?- type a Clausal resolution Resolution is an inference rule that lies at the heart of Prolog and many other systems for automating deduction. Here we give the propositional case of resolution. The following rule is applicable when \(\alpha\) is an atom, and \(\Sigma_1\) and \(\Sigma_2\) are clauses. \[  rac{[\alpha] \cup \Sigma_{1} \quad[
eg \alpha] \cup \Sigma_{2}}{\Sigma_{1} \cup \Sigma_{2}} 	ext { resolution } \] The atom \(\alpha\) is called the atom resolved upon and the clause \(\Sigma_1 \cup \Sigma_2\) is the resolvent of the clauses \([\alpha]\cup \Sigma_1\) and \([
eg \alpha] \cup \Sigma_2\) . Examples Where possible, carry out resolution on these pairs of clauses. \([a, b]\) and \([
eg b, c]\) \([a]\) and \([
eg a, b]\) \([a, b, c, d]\) and \([
eg a, b, e]\) \([a, b, 
eg c]\) and \([a, 
eg b, c, d]\) \([a]\) and \([
eg a]\) \([a, 
eg a]\) and \([a, 
eg a]\) \([a, 
eg b]\) and \([b, 
eg a]\) Select to reveal answers \([a,c]\) \([b]\) \([b,c,d,e]\) \([a,c,
eg c, d]\) and \([a,b,
eg b, d]\) can be obtained. \([\;]\) Nothing new can be obtained. You just get \([a, 
eg a]\) again. \([a,
eg a]\) and \([b, 
eg b]\) are obtained Conjunctive normal form Download transcript (PDF). Conjunctive Normal Form Resolution applies only to clauses. A clause is essentially a disjunction of literals, but not all wffs are of this form. What about \(a 	o b\) or \(a \vee (b \wedge 
eg a)\) ? In fact every wff can be converted to an equivalent conjunction of clauses. This format is called CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form). Recall: equivalent means that in any given interpretation the two wffs will get the same truth value. Clausal form We saw CNF in unit 1 already, so we can translate a set of formulas into clausal form . We write \[ (a \vee b) \wedge (c \vee d) \wedge (a \vee d \vee e) \] in the form \[ \{\; [a, b],\; [c, d],\; [a, d, e]\;\} \] We can use a set of sets like this to represent any propositional formula in a convenient form. The innermost sets are treated as disjunctions of their elements, the whole is treated as the conjunction of its elements. \([\;]\) (empty disjunction) represents false, and \(\{\;\}\) (empty conjunction) represents true, and \(\{\;[\;]\;\}\) is a non-empty conjunction representing false. Resolution refutation procedure Download transcript (PDF). Given a finite set of wffs \(\Delta\) and a wff \(f\) carry out this procedure: Convert \(\Delta\) to CNF, giving a set of clauses, \(\Phi\) . Convert the negation of \(f\) to CNF giving \(\Psi\) . Let \(\Gamma = \Phi \cup \Psi\) Iteratively apply resolution to the clauses in \(\Gamma\) adding the resolvents to \(\Gamma\) . Continue until no more resolution is possible or until the empty clause is a resolvent. Properties of the resolution rule Resolution is sound but not complete in the following sense: The statement 'resolution is sound' means that if we have any set \(\Delta\) of clauses and we use the resolution rule to build a proof of a clause \(f\) from \(\Delta\) then \(\Delta\) will logically entail \(f\) . To justify the soundness of resolution, it's sufficient to show that if \([\alpha] \cup \Sigma_1\) and \([
eg \alpha] \cup \Sigma_2\) are true in some interpretation, then \(\Sigma_1 \cup \Sigma_2\) is also true in that interpretation. The resolution rule is not complete as \(a \wedge b\) logically entails \(a \vee b\) but we cannot take the clauses \([a], [b]\) and generate the clause \([a, b]\) by resolution. Properties of resolution refutation The properties of resolution refutation are: If \(\Delta\) logically entails \(f\) , then the procedure will generate the empty clause. This property is the completeness of resolution refutation . If \(\Delta\) doesn't logically entail \(f\) then the procedure will terminate without producing the empty clause. See example \(\Delta=\{a \wedge b\}\) , and \(f=a \vee b\) to understand the distinction between resolution rule alone and the resolution refutation procedure. Examples in resolution refutation Now you can try the resolution refutation procedure with the examples below: Remember \(\Delta\) is a set of formulas (a set of wffs), not a set of clauses, and \(f\) is also an arbitrary formula. \(\Delta = \{a \wedge b\}\) , and \(f = a \vee b\) . \(\Delta = \{a 	o (b \vee c), a, 
eg b\}\) , and \(f = c\) . \(\Delta = \{a \vee b\}\) , and \(f = a \wedge b\) . \(\Delta = \{ \}\) , and \(f = a 	o a\) . \(\Delta = \{a 	o b, b 	o 
eg a\}\) , and \(f = a \wedge 
eg a\) . \(\Delta = \{a \wedge 
eg b, b 	o c\}\) , and \(f = c\) . Select to reveal answer to example 1 CNF for \(\Delta\) ; nothing to do here \(a \wedge b\) is already a conjunction of disjunctions of literals. \(\Delta\) as clauses: \(\{[a],[b]\}\) negate \(a \vee b\) to \(
eg(a \vee b)\) put into CNF: \(
eg a \wedge 
eg b\) . As clauses: \(\{[
eg a], [
eg b]\}\) \(\Gamma = \{[a],[b], [
eg a], [
eg b]\}\) Resolve \([
eg a], [a]\) yields \([\;]\) empty clause Result makes sense: if both \(a\) and \(b\) hold, then at least one of them does Select to reveal answer to example 2 CNF for \(\Delta\) ; we need to replace \(a 	o (b \vee c)\) by \(
eg a \vee b \vee c\) \(\Delta\) as clauses: \(\{[
eg a, b, c], [a], [
eg b]\}\) negate \(c\) to \(
eg c\) put into CNF and express as clauses: \(\{[
eg c]\}\) \(\Gamma = \{[
eg a, b, c], [a], [
eg b], [
eg c]\}\) Resolve \([
eg a, b, c], [a]\) yields \([b, c]\) Resolve \([b, c], [
eg b]\) yields \([c]\) Resolve \([c], [
eg c]\) yields \([\;]\) Result makes sense: if \(a\) implies \(b\) or \(c\) but \(b\) does not hold, then \(c\) must. Select to reveal answer to example 3 CNF for \(\Delta\) ; nothing to do this time \(\Delta\) as clauses: \(\{[a, b]\}\) negate \(a \wedge b\) to \(
eg (a \wedge b)\) put into CNF and express as clauses: \(\{[
eg a, 
eg b]\}\) \(\Gamma = \{[a, b], [
eg a, 
eg b]\}\) Resolve \([a, b], [
eg a, 
eg b]\) yields \([b, 
eg b]\) Resolve \([a, b], [
eg a, 
eg b]\) yields \([a, 
eg a]\) We end up with a set of clauses: \([a, b], [
eg a, 
eg b], [b, 
eg b], [a, 
eg a]\) and nothing further can be generated. We conclude that \(a \vee b\) does not entail \(a \wedge b\) . Result makes sense: if we only know \(a\) or \(b\) then there is no reason why we must then know that both \(a\) and \(b\) . Select to reveal answer to example 4 CNF for \(\Delta\) ; nothing to do this time as it is the empty set of formulas. \(\Delta\) as a set of clauses: \(\{\;\}\) . There are no clauses. Do not confuse this with having the empty clause. negate \(a 	o a\) to \(
eg (a 	o a)\) put into CNF: \(a \wedge 
eg a\) and express as clauses: \(\{[a], [
eg a]\}\) \(\Gamma = \{[a], [
eg a]\}\) Resolve \([a], [
eg a]\) yields \([\;]\) We got the empty clause, so the empty set of formulas entails \(a 	o a\) Result makes sense: \(a 	o a\) is a tautology. It always holds and we need not make any assumptions to ensure it holds. Select to reveal answer to example 5 CNF for \(\Delta\) ; \(a 	o b\) is \(
eg a \vee b\) ; \(b 	o 
eg a\) is \(
eg b \vee 
eg a\) \(\Delta\) as clauses: \(\{[

eg(a \wedge

eg c]\}\) and can go no further Conclude: formula is not entailed. Formative exercise Now you have finished lesson 4, you can test your knowledge in a formative exercise. Return to Minerva, and using the side bar, navigate to 'Formative exercises'. From here you can select 'Formative Unit 3 Lesson 4' , and attempt the questions. Please note that while this activity is in Gradescope, there is no grade associated with it.

docs.title: Introduction to resolution

docs.text:
A literal is a wff which is either an atom (an atomic proposition) or the negation of an atom. Atoms are also called positive literals and negated atoms are called negative literals . This does not mean literals have to be a single letter. The wff \(\mathit{tuesday} \vee \mathit{friday}\) contains two literals. Examples Which of the following wffs are positive literals, which are negative literals, and which are not literals? \(a \vee b\) \(a\) \(
eg 
eg b\) \(a 	o 
eg b\) \(
eg b\) \(b\) Select to reveal answers \(a \vee b\) Not a literal \(a\) A positive literal \(
eg 
eg b\) Not a literal (only allowed a single \(
eg\) in a negative literal) \(a 	o 
eg b\) Not a literal \(
eg b\) A negative literal \(b\) A positive literal
docs.title: Literals

docs.text:
A clause is a set of literals. There are lots of different notations, but clauses will be denoted with square brackets. For example \([a, 
eg a, b]\) and \([a, 
eg b]\) are clauses. The reason for not writing these sets with { and } is that we later need sets of clauses and we will use { and } for those. A clause is used to stand for the disjunction of its elements. For example the clause \([a, 
eg b]\) is a useful way of referring to both the equivalent (but different) wffs \(a \vee 
eg b\) and \(
eg b \vee a\) . The clause \([a, b, c]\) refers to several different wffs including \(a \vee (b \vee c)\) , and \((a \vee b) \vee c\) , and \(b \vee (a \vee c)\) . The empty clause, \([\,]\) is allowed and is equivalent to false.
docs.title: Clauses

docs.text:
Horn clauses are clauses with at most one positive literal. In the table below, since \(A 	o B\) is equivalent to \(
eg A \vee B\) , the Horn clauses on the left can be expressed as the wffs in the second column. Their Prolog expression appears in the third column. \[  egin{array}{l|l|l|l} \mathsf{Horn ~clause}& \mathsf{wff}& \mathsf{Prolog~ expression } & \mathsf{this~is~a}\ \hline a \vee 
eg b & b 	o a & 	exttt{ a :- b.} & \mathsf{rule}\ \hline a & \mathsf{true} 	o a & 	exttt{ a.} & \mathsf{fact}\ \hline 
eg b \vee 
eg a \vee c & (b \wedge a ) 	o c & 	exttt{ c :- b, a.} & \mathsf{rule}\ \hline 
eg a & a 	o \mathsf{false} & 	exttt{ :- a. } & \mathsf{goal}\ \hline \end{array} \] Prolog implements resolution refutation. E.g. try to prove the goal \(a\) from statements \(b\) , \(b 	o a\) . b. a :- b. :- a. You can put that in one file, but more usually you would have a file with the statements: b. a :- b. and then at the interactive prompt ?- type a
docs.title: Horn clauses and prolog

docs.text:
Resolution is an inference rule that lies at the heart of Prolog and many other systems for automating deduction. Here we give the propositional case of resolution. The following rule is applicable when \(\alpha\) is an atom, and \(\Sigma_1\) and \(\Sigma_2\) are clauses. \[  rac{[\alpha] \cup \Sigma_{1} \quad[
eg \alpha] \cup \Sigma_{2}}{\Sigma_{1} \cup \Sigma_{2}} 	ext { resolution } \] The atom \(\alpha\) is called the atom resolved upon and the clause \(\Sigma_1 \cup \Sigma_2\) is the resolvent of the clauses \([\alpha]\cup \Sigma_1\) and \([
eg \alpha] \cup \Sigma_2\) . Examples Where possible, carry out resolution on these pairs of clauses. \([a, b]\) and \([
eg b, c]\) \([a]\) and \([
eg a, b]\) \([a, b, c, d]\) and \([
eg a, b, e]\) \([a, b, 
eg c]\) and \([a, 
eg b, c, d]\) \([a]\) and \([
eg a]\) \([a, 
eg a]\) and \([a, 
eg a]\) \([a, 
eg b]\) and \([b, 
eg a]\) Select to reveal answers \([a,c]\) \([b]\) \([b,c,d,e]\) \([a,c,
eg c, d]\) and \([a,b,
eg b, d]\) can be obtained. \([\;]\) Nothing new can be obtained. You just get \([a, 
eg a]\) again. \([a,
eg a]\) and \([b, 
eg b]\) are obtained
docs.title: Clausal resolution

docs.text: Download transcript (PDF). Conjunctive Normal Form Resolution applies only to clauses. A clause is essentially a disjunction of literals, but not all wffs are of this form. What about \(a \to b\) or \(a \vee (b \wedge \neg a)\) ? In fact every wff can be converted to an equivalent conjunction of clauses. This format is called CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form). Recall: equivalent means that in any given interpretation the two wffs will get the same truth value.
docs.title: Conjunctive normal form

docs.text: We saw CNF in unit 1 already, so we can translate a set of formulas into clausal form . We write \[ (a \vee b) \wedge (c \vee d) \wedge (a \vee d \vee e) \] in the form \[ \{\; [a, b],\; [c, d],\; [a, d, e]\;\} \] We can use a set of sets like this to represent any propositional formula in a convenient form. The innermost sets are treated as disjunctions of their elements, the whole is treated as the conjunction of its elements. \([\;]\) (empty disjunction) represents false, and \(\{\;\}\) (empty conjunction) represents true, and \(\{\;[\;]\;\}\) is a non-empty conjunction representing false.
docs.title: Clausal form

docs.text: Download transcript (PDF). Given a finite set of wffs \(\Delta\) and a wff \(f\) carry out this procedure: Convert \(\Delta\) to CNF, giving a set of clauses, \(\Phi\) . Convert the negation of \(f\) to CNF giving \(\Psi\) . Let \(\Gamma = \Phi \cup \Psi\) Iteratively apply resolution to the clauses in \(\Gamma\) adding the resolvents to \(\Gamma\) . Continue until no more resolution is possible or until the empty clause is a resolvent.
docs.title: Resolution refutation procedure

docs.text: Resolution is sound but not complete in the following sense: The statement 'resolution is sound' means that if we have any set \(\Delta\) of clauses and we use the resolution rule to build a proof of a clause \(f\) from \(\Delta\) then \(\Delta\) will logically entail \(f\) . To justify the soundness of resolution, it's sufficient to show that if \([\alpha] \cup \Sigma_1\) and \([\neg \alpha] \cup \Sigma_2\) are true in some interpretation, then \(\Sigma_1 \cup \Sigma_2\) is also true in that interpretation. The resolution rule is not complete as \(a \wedge b\) logically entails \(a \vee b\) but we cannot take the clauses \([a], [b]\) and generate the clause \([a, b]\) by resolution.
docs.title: Properties of the resolution rule

docs.text: The properties of resolution refutation are: If \(\Delta\) logically entails \(f\) , then the procedure will generate the empty clause. This property is the completeness of resolution refutation . If \(\Delta\) doesn't logically entail \(f\) then the procedure will terminate without producing the empty clause. See example \(\Delta=\{a \wedge b\}\) , and \(f=a \vee b\) to understand the distinction between resolution rule alone and the resolution refutation procedure.
docs.title: Properties of resolution refutation

docs.text:

Select to reveal answer to example 4 CNF for \(\Delta\) ; nothing to do this time as it is the empty set of formulas. \(\Delta\) as a set of clauses: \(\{\;\}\) . There are no clauses. Do not confuse this with having the empty clause. negate \(a 	o a\) to \(
