If an exercise, question, or assessment requires the use of a particular description logic in which only certain constructs are allowed, then this will be stated explicitly. 2.5.4 Comparison with RDFS In lesson 1 of this unit we already met the idea that RDFS, is very limited in what it can express. The example there was that we cannot say the classes of cats and of people are disjoint. In description logic this is simply \[\mathsf{Cat} \sqcap \mathsf{Person} \sqsubseteq  ot\] Some other examples of statements that are known to be impossible to make in RDFS are the following. These examples are found in R. Arp, B. Smith, A. D. Spear: 'Building ontologies with Basic Formal Ontology' MIT Press, 2015, (page 155). Every person has a mother who is also a person Every person has exactly two biological parents You should be able write these in description logic using the constructs we have seen in this lesson. 2.6 Wording and representation These examples show English phrases corresponding to concepts and statements in Description Logic. Note in particular that the word 'and' can indicate different things depending on the context. Undergraduate students are students : \[\mathsf{UnderGrad} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{Student}\] Modules and students are disjoint : \[\mathsf{Module} \sqcap \mathsf{Student} \equiv  ot\] Every student is a postgrad or undergrad (or possibly both) : \[\mathsf{Student} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{UnderGrad} \sqcup \mathsf{PostGrad}\] Undergrads and postgrads are students (beware the and here modelled by \(\sqcup\) ): \[\mathsf{UnderGrad} \sqcup \mathsf{PostGrad} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{Student}\] People who are staff and students are postgrads (notice and modelled by \(\sqcap\) ): \[\mathsf{Staff} \sqcap \mathsf{Student} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{PostGrad}\] or more accurately: \[\mathsf{Person} \sqcap \mathsf{Staff} \sqcap \mathsf{Student} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{PostGrad}\] but: Staff and students are people : \[\mathsf{Staff} \sqcup \mathsf{Student} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{Person}\] Exercise Here are some atomic concepts (classes) and roles (properties). \[ egin{array}{l} \mathsf{Animal}\ \mathsf{Insect} \ \mathsf{Human} \ \mathsf{LivingThing} \ \mathsf{Carnivore} \ \mathsf{Herbivore} \ \mathsf{Plant} \[3ex] \mathsf{eats} \ \mathsf{isEatenBy}\ \end{array} \] Express in Description Logic the following statements: Animals are living things Plants and animals are living things. Nothing is both a plant and an animal. 'Herbivore' means 'animal that only eats plants'. 'Carnivore means 'living thing that eats animals (but may eat other things as well). Select to reveal answer 1 \[\mathsf{Animal} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{LivingThing}\] Select to reveal answer 2 \[\mathsf{Animal} \sqcup \mathsf{Plant} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{LivingThing}\] Select to reveal answer 3 \[\mathsf{Animal} \sqcap \mathsf{Plant} \equiv  ot\] Select to reveal answer 4 \[\mathsf{Herbivore} \equiv \mathsf{Animal} \sqcap  orall \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Plant}\] Select to reveal answer 5 \[\mathsf{Carnivore} \equiv \mathsf{LivingThing} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Animal}\] Exercise continued Invent appropriate names for the following additional concepts (classes). In these the 'are / is' means 'are exactly / is defined to be'. Write down the appropriate axioms. Edible plants are plants that humans eat. Carnivorous plants are plants that eat animals. Insectivores are living things that eat insects. An exclusive insectivore is one with a diet consisting entirely of insects. Special insects are insects where some of the things that eat them only eat insects. Select to reveal answer 1 \[\mathsf{EdiblePlant} \equiv \mathsf{Plant} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{isEatenBy} . \mathsf{Human}\] Select to reveal answer 2 \[\mathsf{CarnivorousPlant} \equiv \mathsf{Plant} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Animal}\] Select to reveal answer 3 \[\mathsf{Insectivore} \equiv \mathsf{LivingThing} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Insect}\] Select to reveal answer 4 \[\mathsf{ExclusiveInsectivore} \equiv \mathsf{Insectivore} \sqcap  orall \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Insect}\] Select to reveal answer 5 \[\mathsf{SpecialInsect} \equiv \exists \mathsf{isEatenBy} . \mathsf{ExclusiveInsectivore}\]
docs.title: Lesson 2 Description Logic

docs.text: Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: Explain the meaning of the terms "concept" and "role" as used in description logics. Write definitions of concepts in description logic using a variety of constructs for both concepts and roles. Identify when characteristics of roles are needed, such as being transitive, functional, or symmetric. Explain why the statement "Mary likes all cats" is possible to express in some description logics but not in others.
docs.title: Description Logic

docs.text: Description Logic exists to describe two kinds of thing: Concepts For example: \(\mathsf{Human}\) , \(\mathsf{Pizza}\) , \(\mathsf{Grandmother}\) , \(\mathsf{MeatTopping}\) . Think of a concept as a set of things (its instances). The word 'concept' means the same as 'class' in this context. Roles For example: \(\mathsf{hasTopping}\) , \(\mathsf{isMotherOf}\) , \(\mathsf{isGrandmotherOf}\) , \(\mathsf{likesToEat}\) . Think of a role as a binary relation between things.
docs.title: 2.1.1 The ingredients

docs.text: Concepts are built up in the following ways: Atomic concepts These are names of concepts e.g. \(\mathsf{Human}\) , \(\mathsf{Pizza}\) . Universal concept This is denoted by: \(\top\) Instances are all things in the ontology. Bottom concept This is denoted by: \(\bot\) This is the class with no instances, also called the empty concept. Negated concept This is denoted by: \(\neg C\) where \(C\) is any concept Instances are things not in \(C\) . Concept intersection This is denoted by: \(C \sqcap D\) where \(C\) and \(D\) are any concepts. The things in this class are those things that are instances of both \(C\) and of \(D\) . Note that this is written \(\sqcap\) and not \(\cap\) . Concept union This is denoted by: \(C \sqcup D\) where \(C\) and \(D\) are any concepts. The things in this class are those things that are instances of \(C\) or of \(D\) (or both). Note that this is written \(\sqcup\) and not \(\cup\) . Universal value restriction This is denoted by: \(\forall r.C\) where \(C\) is a concept and \(r\) is a role. The things in this class are all those things where everything to which they stand in relation \(r\) belongs to the class \(C\) . For example, to talk about the class of things that only eat vegetables we could say: \[ \forall \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Vegetable}. \] Although the symbol \(\forall\) is the same as in first order logic, be careful that the way it is used here in the syntax is very different. In particular, \(\mathsf{eats}\) is not a variable. To understand the connection with first order logic imagine a language with two predicates \(\mathit{eats}\) and \(\mathit{Vegetable}\) . The concept above would correspond to the set of those \(x\) for which the following holds \[ \forall y\, (\mathit{eats}(x,y) \to \mathit{Vegetable}(y)). \] Existential value restriction This is denoted by: \(\exists r.C\) where \(C\) is a concept and \(r\) is a role. The things in this class are all those things where something to which they stand in relation \(r\) belongs to the class \(C\) . For example, to talk about the class of things that eat at least one thing which is a vegetable we could say: \[ \exists \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Vegetable}. \] Although the symbol \(\exists\) is the same as in first order logic, be careful that the way it is used here in the syntax is very different. In particular, \(\mathsf{eats}\) is not a variable. To understand the connection with first order logic imagine a language with two predicates \(\mathit{eats}\) and \(\mathit{Vegetable}\) . The concept above would correspond to the set of those \(x\) for which the following holds: \[ \exists y\, (\mathit{eats}(x,y) \wedge \mathit{Vegetable}(y)). \]
docs.title: 2.1.2 Building concepts

docs.text: Suppose we have a class: \(\mathsf{Mansion}\) and a property \(\mathsf{owns}\) . Two individuals are related by this property just when the first owns the second. For example a particular person owning a specific car. We can build a new class from \(\mathsf{Mansion}\) and \(\mathsf{owns}\) . This is the class consisting of every individual who \(\mathsf{owns}\) a \(\mathsf{Mansion}\) . In Description Logic we could write \(\exists\, \mathsf{owns} \cdot \mathsf{Mansion}\) This means the class of everyone who owns a mansion, or people where something they own is a mansion . We could call this class \(\mathsf{Rich}\) , or better say it's a subclass of \(\mathsf{Rich}\) . In Protégé the same class is written: owns some Mansion
docs.title: Existential flavour of restriction

docs.text: Suppose we have a class: \(\mathsf{Worthless}\) and a property \(\mathsf{owns}\) . We can build a new class from \(\mathsf{Worthless}\) and \(\mathsf{owns}\) . This is the class consisting of every individual who only \(\mathsf{owns}\) things that are \(\mathsf{Worthless}\) . In Description Logic we could write \(\forall\, \mathsf{owns} \cdot \mathsf{Worthless}\) This means the class of everyone who owns only worthless things, or people where everything they own is worthless . We could call this class \(\mathsf{Poor}\) . It includes anyone owning nothing. In Protégé the same class is written: owns only Worthless Notice how this is different from: owns some Worthless More restriction examples More examples of the power of Description Logic to define new classes out of existing ones and properties. Teaching staff are staff who teach some module: \[\mathsf{TeachingStaff} \equiv \mathsf{Staff} \sqcap \exists\, \mathsf{teaches} . \mathsf{Module}\] A PG-only module is a module only studied by postgraduate students: \[\mathsf{PGOnlyModule} \equiv \mathsf{Module} \sqcap \forall\, \mathsf{studiedBy} . \mathsf{PostGrad}\]
docs.title: Universal flavour of restriction

docs.text: If \(R\) is a property (role) and \(n\) a non-negative integer then: \[\leq n \; R \quad\mathsf{~and~}\quad \geq n \; R \quad\mathsf{~and~}\quad = n \; R\qquad \mathsf{~are~concepts.}\] The concept \(\leq n \; R\) consists of all those entities having the \(R\) relationship to at most \(n\) things. The other two concepts have the same idea, except we would be talking about 'at least \(n\) things' or 'exactly \(n\) things' . For example, if we want to say a criminal with at least three cars is successful, we could do this: \[\mathsf{Criminal} \sqcap (\geq 3 \; \mathsf{hasCar}) \sqsubseteq \mathsf{SuccessfulCriminal}.\] A qualified number restriction is one of these: \[\leq n \; R . C \qquad \geq n \; R . C \qquad = n \; R . C\] These differ from the previous ones in having a concept \(C\) as well as a role \(R\) . We can then talk about not simply having three cars, but having three cars which belong to some class. For example: \[\geq 3 \; \mathsf{hasCar} . \mathsf{Electric}.\] For another example, the concept of having exactly four bedrooms and all rooms (not just bedrooms) being spacious. \[(= 4 \; \mathsf{hasRoom} . \mathsf{BedRoom}) \sqcap \forall\, \mathsf{hasRoom}.\mathsf{Spacious}\] Note that \(\geq 1 \; R.C\) is equivalent to \(\exists \, R.C\) Exercise Someone belongs to this class: \[\geq 2 \, \mathsf{hasChild} . (\geq 2 \, \mathsf{hasChild} . \mathsf{Female})\] How could you express in natural English what this tells you about their children and grandchildren? Someone else belongs to this class: \[\geq 2 \, \mathsf{hasChild} . (\geq 2 \, \mathsf{hasLivedIn} . \mathsf{City}).\] What can you say about the total number of different cities which have been lived in by at least one of their children? The structure of the two concepts above is very similar in the way they are defined, but the total number of cities and the total number of grandchildren do not have the same properties (assuming usual conventions about parents of children). Use first order logic to express the additional constraint that the binary predicate \(\mathit{hasChild}\) satisfies compared with the binary predicate \(\mathit{hasLivedIn}\) . Select to reveal answer 1 They must have at least two children, and each of their children has at least two daughters. They must have at least four granddaughters is what we would like to say, but the description logic does not enforce this. It is logically possible that these four are not actually all different. Select to reveal answer 2 They must have at least two children each of whom has lived in at least two different cities. The total number of cities that have been lived in by at least one of their children is at least two. This number might only be two if each of their children had lived in the same two cities as the other children. Select to reveal answer 3 In first order logic we need to express the following: if \(y\) and \(z\) are different children of the same person, then the children of \(y\) are all different from the children of \(z\) . \[ \begin{array}{l} \forall x \forall y \forall z\; ((\mathit{hasChild}(x, y) \wedge \mathit{hasChild}(x,z) \wedge \neg (y=z)) \to\\ \qquad \qquad \forall c \forall d\; (\mathit{hasChild}(y, c) \wedge \mathit{hasChild}(z, d)) \to \neg (c=d))) \end{array} \]
docs.title: 2.1.3 Number restrictions

docs.text: Terminological axioms allow us to state: Concept equivalence This is written \(C \equiv D\) Asserting that means concepts have the same instances. Concept subsumption This is written \(C \sqsubseteq D\) . Asserting that means \(C\) is a sub-concept of \(D\) .
docs.title: 2.1.4 Sub-class relationships

docs.text: There are many different flavours of Description Logic and one way these differ is in the ways they allow you to construct roles out of other roles. We will see some of these constructions later, but to get started we only need the constructions on roles.
docs.title: 2.1.5 Building roles

docs.text: This example shows how you can use the ways of constructing concepts to define some concepts from some given atomic ones. Description Logic Example: TBox \(\mathsf{Atomic ~Concepts ~and ~Roles:} \quad \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \mathsf{Person} &\mathsf{(concept)} \\ \mathsf{Female} &\mathsf{(concept)} \\ \mathsf{hasChild}&\mathsf{(role)}\\ \end{array} \right.\) \(\begin{array}{rcl} \mathsf{Woman} & \equiv & \mathsf{Person} \sqcap \mathsf{Female} \\ \mathsf{Mother} & \equiv & \mathsf{Woman} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{hasChild}.\mathsf{Person} \\ \mathsf{Parent} & \equiv & \mathsf{Person} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{hasChild}.\mathsf{Person}\\ \mathsf{ParentOfOnlyDaughters} & \equiv & \mathsf{Person} \sqcap \forall \mathsf{hasChild}.\mathsf{Female} \\ \mathsf{Grandmother} & \equiv & \mathsf{Mother} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{hasChild}.\mathsf{Parent}\\ \end{array}\) A typical Description Logic specification consists of: the TBox , which is a set of terminological axioms as in the above example, and also the ABox , which is a set of assertions about individuals as in the following. Description Logic example: ABox \(\qquad\qquad\begin{array}{l} \mathsf{Woman}(\mathsf{MARGE})\\ \mathsf{Woman}(\mathsf{LISA})\\ \mathsf{hasChild}(\mathsf{MARGE}, \mathsf{LISA}) \end{array}\) This example indicates the basic ideas, but there is a lot that you might want to say about the concepts and roles. For example: Every person has exactly two people whose child they are. The \(\mathsf{hasChild}\) role cannot relate anyone to themself or to their own parents. We shall see how to express these later.
docs.title: 2.2 TBox and ABox example

docs.text: A relationship means a way in which individuals might be related, for example: Parent of, child of, brother of, sister of, ancestor of, etc. Older than, younger than, taller than, heavier than, etc. Studies, teaches, studies with. Is inside, is next to, overlaps with, etc. Is part of, has part. Earlier than, later than, at same time as, during, etc. Author of, editor of, illustrator of. Actor in, director of, script writer of. Was written in, was first screened. Follows, is followed by, knows (person), is friend of, etc. Unfortunately terminology is not uniform. The following three words are used to mean the same thing by different communities: Relationship (this is used in Entity-Relationship modelling). Property (this is used in OWL, the Web Ontology Language in Protégé). Role (this is used in many Description Logics). A relationship can have features (such as being transitive, or being reflexive). As OWL uses the word property already we will say characteristics instead of properties for these features. This is what Protégé does as you can see in the following screenshot: Select for full text description of screenshot 4.2.1. The image is a screenshot. There are two headings, each of which has a list underneath. On the left-hand side the heading is Characteristics . There is a list of checkboxes underneath the heading. In descending order, these are labelled: Functional , Inverse functional , Transitive , Symmetric , Asymmetric , Reflexive , Irreflexive . On the right-hand side the heading is Description . There is a list of options underneath which are greyed out. Each of these also has a plus button alongside it, as if each option could be expanded. In descending order, these are: Equivalent To , SubProperty Of , Inverse Of , Domains (intersection) , Ranges (intersection) , Disjoint With and SuperProperty Of (Chain) . The seven characteristics listed are all used in the usual sense of these words in books and other resourses on discrete mathematical structures. The characteristic inverse functional is often called injective in a lot of settings, and also gets called one-to-one in some places.
docs.title: 2.3.1 Introduction

docs.text: Before we review the definitions of the characteristics, it is important to note when it is appropriate to say that a relationship has one of these characteristics in an ontology: The relationship 'ancestor of' has the property: If A is an 'ancestor of' B, and B is an 'ancestor of' C Then A is an 'ancestor of' C. This is not about: accidental features of particular individuals that happen to be in an ontology at one time. This is about all individuals that could ever possibly be related. Compare that with the relationship 'has heard of' between people (used in the usual sense of the phrase, and applied to a large number of people): it might just happen that we have an ontology in which it is true of all the individuals there at present that if A has 'heard of' B, and B has 'heard of' C, then A has 'heard of' C; but this does not mean we should be asserting that 'heard of' has the same characteristic as 'ancestor of' does. We only assert that a relationship has a characteristic if it is forced to have it by virtue of the meaning of the relationship as it can apply to any individuals whatsoever.
docs.title: 2.3.2 When to use characteristics

docs.text: A relationship \(R\) is functional means: if \(A\) is any individual, then it never happens that there are individuals \(B\) and \(C\) which are different from each other, and \(R(A, B)\) and \(R(A,C)\) , A relationship \(R\) is inverse functional means: if \(A\) is any individual, then it never happens that there are individuals \(B\) and \(C\) which are different from each other, and \(R(B, A)\) and \(R(C, A)\) . A relationship \(R\) is transitive means: if \(A\) , \(B\) , \(C\) are any individuals (including possibly some of them being equal to others) then it always happens that if \(R(A,B)\) and \(R(B,C)\) then \(R(A,C)\) . A relationship \(R\) is symmetric means: if \(A\) and \(B\) are any individuals (including possibly both being the same) then it always happens that if \(R(A,B)\) then \(R(B,A)\) . A relationship \(R\) is asymmetric means: if \(A\) and \(B\) are any individuals (including possibly both being the same) then it never happens that both \(R(A,B)\) and \(R(B,A)\) . A relationship \(R\) is reflexive means: if \(A\) is any individuals then it always happens that \(R(A,A)\) holds. A relationship \(R\) is irreflexive means: if \(A\) is any individuals then it never happens that \(R(A,A)\) holds. All of these definitions need to be read using the language in a very literal way, just as in the way logic is used. Beware of these points: The characteristic irreflexive is not the same as 'not reflexive'. A relation can be not reflexive without being irreflexive. The characteristic asymmetric is not the same as 'not symmetric'. A relation can be not symetric without being asymmetric. The key part of the definition of transitive says: 'If \(R(A,B)\) and \(R(B,C)\) '. In the event that these do not both hold for particular \(A\) , \(B\) , \(C\) , or that there are no individuals at all for which both hold, neither of these things does anything to prevent \(R\) being transitive (remember how implication works in propositonal logic). The key part of the definition of symmetric also uses 'if' at a similar key point.
docs.title: 2.3.3 The seven characteristics

docs.text: Exercise For each of the following (with the most obvious meaning), which of the seven characteristics should it have? brotherOf hasMother hasParent hasChild ancestorOf sameAgeAs youngerThan isMarriedTo Select to reveal answers For the first one, brotherOf , the full reasons why we only want it to be irreflexive are given in detail. In the other cases only the desired characteristics are given. brotherOf Functional? Someone can be the brother of two different people. We do not require it to be functional. Inverse functional? Someone can have two different people who are brothers of them. We do not require it to be inverse functional. Transitive? Assuming no one is a brother of themself, we do not require the relation to be transitive. Symmetric? Think of two people \(a\) and \(b\) , who are brother and sister. If \((a,b)\) is in the relation then \((b,a)\) is not in the relation in this situation. We do not require it to be symmetric. Asymmetric? Two people can be brothers of each other. We do not require it it be asymmetric. Reflexive? We do not say someone is a brother to themself. We do not require it it be reflexive. Irreflexive? Yes, we do want this because we never say someone is a brother to themself. hasMother Functional if modelling a situation where people never have more than one mother Asymmetric Irreflexive hasParent Asymmetric Irreflexive hasChild Asymmetric Irreflexive ancestorOf Transitive Asymmetric Irreflexive sameAgeAs Transitive Symmetric Reflexive youngerThan Transitive Symmetric Reflexive isMarriedTo Symmetric In many cultures both functional and inverse functional. Exercise Draw a relation with three individuals which is functional, inverse functional, transitive, symmetric and reflexive. Draw a relation with three individuals with none of the seven characteristics. Draw a relation with three individuals which is only functional, asymmetric, irreflexive and transitive. Select to reveal answer 1 Three dots each with one loop. No other arrows. Select to reveal answer 2 Three dots, one having a loop. One of the other dots has an arrow to the one with a loop. The other dot without a loop has an arrow from the one with a loop to it. In other words, three dots and two arrows like this \(\bullet \to \bullet \to \bullet\) plus a third arrow which goes from the middle dot to itself. Select to reveal answer 3 Three dots and two arrows like this: \(\bullet \to \bullet \leftarrow \bullet\)
docs.title: Exercises on the characteristics
2.4 Other features of and constructions with roles

docs.text: It is possible to say that individuals connected by a property must belong to specified classes. We might want to say that a property \(\mathsf{teaches}\) should be from the class \(\mathsf{Staff}\) to class \(\mathsf{Module}\) . In this case \(\mathsf{Staff}\) is the Domain and \(\mathsf{Module}\) is the Range. The Domain and the Range can be the same, but this is NOT the same as the property being reflexive! One Description Logic notation for domain and range is: \(\mathsf{Domain}(R,C)\) . Meaning that whenever \(R\) relates individual \(x\) to individual \(y\) , then \(x\) belongs to \(C\) . \(\mathsf{Range}(R,C)\) . Meaning that whenever \(R\) relates individual \(x\) to individual \(y\) , then \(y\) belongs to \(C\) .
docs.title: 2.4.1 Domain and range

docs.text: We know: If \(A \text{ is } \mathrel{\mathsf{motherOf}} B\) then \(A \text{ is } \mathrel{\mathsf{parentOf}} B\) . In Description Logic: The \(\mathsf{motherOf}\) property is a sub-property of \(\mathsf{parentOf}\) and we write this as: \[\mathsf{motherOf} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{parentOf}.\] Another way of saying the same thing is: \(\mathsf{parentOf}\) property is a super property of \(\mathsf{motherOf}\) . In Description Logic we use \(\sqsubseteq\) for inclusion of properties as well as for classes.
docs.title: 2.4.2 Sub-property and super property

docs.text: In Description Logic, OWL and Protégé (but not in lots of other places): Given properties \(R\) and \(S\) , \(R \circ S\) is the property where \(x\) is linked to \(z\) if: \(x\) is linked to something \(y\) by \(R\) , and \(y\) is linked to \(z\) by \(S\) This is nice for pictures but opposite to the most popular (if arguably misguided) convention for function composition. Chain composition example Classes: Module , Student , Degree Properties: takenBy , compulsoryFor , studiedBy A module can be studied by a student. A degree can be taken by a student. A module can be compulsory for a degree. How do we say this? - If a module is compulsory for a degree taken by a student then the module must be studied by the student. \[\mathsf{compulsoryFor} \circ \mathsf{takenBy} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{studiedBy}\] In Protégé: \(\mathsf{studiedBy}\) needs to be a super property of the chain \(\mathsf{compulsoryFor} \circ \mathsf{takenBy}\) The composition or chain \(\mathsf{compulsoryFor} \circ \mathsf{takenBy}\) is a new property relating every module which is compulsory for some degree to every student taking that degree. Composition is a powerful idea. See how it works in this exercise. Composition exercises Assuming parentOf , childOf , motherOf and daughterOf have conventional meanings, which of these correspond to or include the properties granddaughterOf , grandmotherOf , siblingOf , grandparentOf , or other easily named things? childOf o childOf . parentOf o childOf . childOf o parentOf . daughterOf o childOf . motherOf o parentOf . parentOf o brotherOf .
docs.title: 2.4.3 Composing properties (chains)

docs.text: Concept: Knowing someone who has a child who plays football : \[\qquad \exists \; (\mathsf{knows} \circ \mathsf{hasChild}) . \mathsf{PlaysFootball}\] concept: Having a child who knows someone who plays football : \[\qquad \exists \; (\mathsf{hasChild} \circ \mathsf{knows}) . \mathsf{PlaysFootball}\] In first order logic the first example corresponds to those \(x\) for which: \[\qquad \exists y \; \exists z\; (\mathsf{knows}(x,y) \wedge \mathsf{hasChild}(y,z) \wedge \mathsf{PlaysFootball}(z))\] How in Description Logic could we formulate the concept of: knowing someone who plays football who has a child ?
docs.title: Some more examples

docs.text: If \(R\) and \(S\) are roles then we can construct these roles: \(R \sqcup S\) (union) Two things are related by this role if they are related by \(R\) or by \(S\) or by both. \(R \sqcap S\) (intersection) Two things are related by this role if they are related by both \(R\) and \(S\) . \(\neg R\) (complement) Two things are related by this role if they are not related \(R\) .
docs.title: 2.4.4 Boolean operations

docs.text: If \(R\) is a role then we can construct these roles: \(R^+\) (transitive closure). Two things \(x\) and \(y\) are related by this role if there is a sequence of \(n+1\) things \[ x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n\] where \(x = x_0\) , \(x_n = y\) , \(n \geqslant 1\) and \(R(x_i, x_{i+1})\) for all \(0 \leqslant i < n\) . \(R^\ast\) (reflexive transitive closure). Two things \(x\) and \(y\) are related by this role if there is a sequence of \(n+1\) things \[ x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n\] where \(x = x_0\) , \(x_n = y\) , \(n \geqslant 0\) and \(R(x_i, x_{i+1})\) for all \(0 \leqslant i < n\) . The difference between \(R^\ast\) and \(R^+\) is \(R^\ast(x,x)\) always holds, but \(R^+(x,x)\) only holds in the case that \(R(x,x)\) .
docs.title: 2.4.5 Transitive closure

docs.text: If \(R\) is a role then we can have these roles: \(R^{-1}\) or \(R^-\) (inverse / converse). This is the role where \(R^-(x,y)\) iff \(R(y,x)\) . \(\mathsf{id}(C)\) (identity role on concept \(C\) ). This is the role where the domain and range are \(C\) and \(x\) is related to \(y\) iff \(x = y\) .
docs.title: 2.4.6 Identity and inverse

docs.text: There are many different systems called description logics and they differ among themselves according to the different constructs allowed. We have introduced a variety of features to indicate what is possible, but not all description logics allow all the features we have discussed. A widely used starting point is a language denoted by \(\mathcal{AL}\) . This only allows the formation of concepts as follows: atomic concepts the top, \(\top\) , and bottom, \(\bot\) , concepts negation, \(\neg\) , applied only to atomic concepts intersection of concepts: \(C \sqcap D\) universal restriction: \(\forall R . C\) existential restriction in the limited form \(\exists R . \top\) In this logic unions of concepts are not allowed and expressions like \(\neg (C \cap D)\) are also not allowed. There are no constructions on roles, just atomic names for roles. In the literature on description logics there is a convention about naming logics by using particular letters in the name to indicate what features are allowed. For example, extending the logic \(\mathcal{AL}\) by allowing negation for arbitrary concepts results in a logic called \(\mathcal{ALC}\) where the \(\mathcal{C}\) stands for 'complement'.
docs.title: 2.5.1 The variety of different logics

docs.text: The statement 'Mary likes all cats' has become part of the culture almost as much as 'Tweety' being a bird when discussing non-monotontic logic, or the names 'Alice' and 'Bob' for opposed players in games. The significance of Mary liking all cats is that in several description logics, including \(\mathcal{ALC}\) , it is impossible to express the concept of liking all cats, and hence there is no way to say that Mary belongs to this concept. To be precise, it is impossible to do this in terms of: a role \(\mathsf{likes}\) , and concept \(\mathsf{Cat}\) . Using these two things we can build the concept \(\forall\; \mathsf{likes} . \mathsf{Cat}\) . This is not the concept of liking all cats. This is the concept of liking only cats. An individual who fitted the concept \(\forall\; \mathsf{likes} . \mathsf{Cat}\) might only like one thing. That one thing would certainly have to be a cat, but there might be any number of cats that the individual did not like. If we have a more expressive description logic where roles can be negated, then it is possible to construct the concept \(\forall\; \neg \mathsf{likes} . \neg \mathsf{Cat}\) . Saying Mary belongs to the class defined by this concept is to say Mary only dislikes non-cats or, in more natural but less concise English: Everything that Mary does not like is not a cat. This is equivalent to saying Mary likes all cats. Because if we have any cat, then Mary cannot dislike it. So it has to be one of the things that Mary likes. Don't forget that this is quite different from saying Mary only likes cats. If Mary likes all cats, then there may be things other than cats which Mary likes. There is always a trade-off between expressivity and the abilty to have reasoning tools that compute using a particular language. In some cases adding constructs makes reasoning impossible to do efficiently, that is in a reasonable amount of time. In other cases, the inclusion of certain features actually makes some reasoning problems impossible altogether.
docs.title: 2.5.2 Mary likes all cats

docs.text: In this module the term "Description Logic" without any further qualification will allow the use of any of the constructs mentioned in this unit. If an exercise, question, or assessment requires the use of a particular description logic in which only certain constructs are allowed, then this will be stated explicitly.
docs.title: 2.5.3 What "Description Logic" means here.

docs.text: In lesson 1 of this unit we already met the idea that RDFS, is very limited in what it can express. The example there was that we cannot say the classes of cats and of people are disjoint. In description logic this is simply \[\mathsf{Cat} \sqcap \mathsf{Person} \sqsubseteq \bot\] Some other examples of statements that are known to be impossible to make in RDFS are the following. These examples are found in R. Arp, B. Smith, A. D. Spear: 'Building ontologies with Basic Formal Ontology' MIT Press, 2015, (page 155). Every person has a mother who is also a person Every person has exactly two biological parents You should be able write these in description logic using the constructs we have seen in this lesson.
docs.title: 2.5.4 Comparison with RDFS

docs.text: These examples show English phrases corresponding to concepts and statements in Description Logic. Note in particular that the word 'and' can indicate different things depending on the context. Undergraduate students are students : \[\mathsf{UnderGrad} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{Student}\] Modules and students are disjoint : \[\mathsf{Module} \sqcap \mathsf{Student} \equiv \bot\] Every student is a postgrad or undergrad (or possibly both) : \[\mathsf{Student} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{UnderGrad} \sqcup \mathsf{PostGrad}\] Undergrads and postgrads are students (beware the and here modelled by \(\sqcup\) ): \[\mathsf{UnderGrad} \sqcup \mathsf{PostGrad} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{Student}\] People who are staff and students are postgrads (notice and modelled by \(\sqcap\) ): \[\mathsf{Staff} \sqcap \mathsf{Student} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{PostGrad}\] or more accurately: \[\mathsf{Person} \sqcap \mathsf{Staff} \sqcap \mathsf{Student} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{PostGrad}\] but: Staff and students are people : \[\mathsf{Staff} \sqcup \mathsf{Student} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{Person}\] Exercise Here are some atomic concepts (classes) and roles (properties). \[\begin{array}{l} \mathsf{Animal}\\ \mathsf{Insect} \\ \mathsf{Human} \\ \mathsf{LivingThing} \\ \mathsf{Carnivore} \\ \mathsf{Herbivore} \\ \mathsf{Plant} \\[3ex] \mathsf{eats} \\ \mathsf{isEatenBy}\\ \end{array} \] Express in Description Logic the following statements: Animals are living things Plants and animals are living things. Nothing is both a plant and an animal. 'Herbivore' means 'animal that only eats plants'. 'Carnivore means 'living thing that eats animals (but may eat other things as well). Select to reveal answer 1 \[\mathsf{Animal} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{LivingThing}\] Select to reveal answer 2 \[\mathsf{Animal} \sqcup \mathsf{Plant} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{LivingThing}\] Select to reveal answer 3 \[\mathsf{Animal} \sqcap \mathsf{Plant} \equiv \bot\] Select to reveal answer 4 \[\mathsf{Herbivore} \equiv \mathsf{Animal} \sqcap \forall \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Plant}\] Select to reveal answer 5 \[\mathsf{Carnivore} \equiv \mathsf{LivingThing} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Animal}\] Exercise continued Invent appropriate names for the following additional concepts (classes). In these the 'are / is' means 'are exactly / is defined to be'. Write down the appropriate axioms. Edible plants are plants that humans eat. Carnivorous plants are plants that eat animals. Insectivores are living things that eat insects. An exclusive insectivore is one with a diet consisting entirely of insects. Special insects are insects where some of the things that eat them only eat insects. Select to reveal answer 1 \[\mathsf{EdiblePlant} \equiv \mathsf{Plant} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{isEatenBy} . \mathsf{Human}\] Select to reveal answer 2 \[\mathsf{CarnivorousPlant} \equiv \mathsf{Plant} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Animal}\] Select to reveal answer 3 \[\mathsf{Insectivore} \equiv \mathsf{LivingThing} \sqcap \exists \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Insect}\] Select to reveal answer 4 \[\mathsf{ExclusiveInsectivore} \equiv \mathsf{Insectivore} \sqcap \forall \mathsf{eats} . \mathsf{Insect}\] Select to reveal answer 5 \[\mathsf{SpecialInsect} \equiv \exists \mathsf{isEatenBy} . \mathsf{ExclusiveInsectivore}\]
docs.title: 2.6 Wording and representation

docs.text: Protégé Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: Use the Protégé software for defining a class hierarchy. Use Protégé to define properties and their characteristics. Use a reasoner with Protégé to compute inferences. Explain the difference between open world reasoning and closed world reasoning in the context of Protégé. Installing Protégé 5.5.0 It is straightforward to install the latest version of Protégé on your own computer. At http://protegeproject.github.io/protege/ you will find all the instructions you need. Creating an ontology On starting Protégé you will see something like this: Fig 4.3.1. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.1. This screenshot shows what the Protégé application window looks like when you open it up. The panel on the upper left has the Active ontology tab open. The panel on the upper right is for Ontology metrics . The panel on the bottom has the Ontology imports tab open. Note that the top half of the window has several tabs, including: Active ontology, Entities, Classes, Individuals by class. Make sure the Active ontology tab is selected and by clicking on the + sign next to Annotations add a suitable annotation: Fig 4.3.2. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot Fig 4.3.2. This screenshot shows the Create Annotation window that opens after you select the plus icon next to Annotations . Here you can add a suitable annotation in the text box on the right hand side. Create some classes Select the Classes tab which should bring up the Class hierarchy . Fig 4.3.3. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.3. This screenshot has the Classes tab open. There are two Class hierarchy panels on the left-hand side. On the upper right is the Annotations panel and below this is the Description panel. Select Thing (the top element of the hierarchy). Select the Add-subclass icon just above Thing and add a new class: Fig 4.3.4. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.4. This screenshot shows what happens after you select Thing , the top element of the hierarchy, and then select the Add sub-class icon above Thing and add a new sub-class. A Create a new Class panel pops up with a text box to enter a new Class name. Protégé provides a very flexible interface in which you are able to control the arrangement of individual windows. Windows can be split horizontally or vertically as well as floated off separately from the main window. Some of this flexibility can be confusing for beginners, but you are encouraged to explore the various features. Add further classes through adding sibling classes and subclasses so that you arrive at the following (but do not worry if the ordering of siblings is different). Fig 4.3.5. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.5. This screenshot shows the Class hierarchy panel and the class hierarchy after numerous sibling classes and sub classes have been added. The hierarchy shown is: owl:Thing BeautifulThing UsefulThing Building House PuristHouse GoodHouse ValuableHouse Station School. Adding properties Informally we want to say the following: A valuable house is one which is near a school and a station. A good house is one in which everything is useful or beautiful. A purist house is one in which everything is useful and beautiful. Beware of the language here. If we say something is close to a school and a station we generally expect the school and the station to be different things. We certainly would not insist that they have to be the same thing. If we say everything is useful and beautiful we do mean that any single thing is both useful and also beautiful. Instead of the Classes tab, you need to select the Entities tab. When you do this and select the top item in the Object property hierarchy , owl:topObjectProperty , you should see this: Fig 4.3.6. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.6. This screenshot shows the Entities tab open. On the left-hand side the Object properties panel is open and the top item in the object property hierarchy, owl:topObjectProperty , is selected. On the right-hand side there are four further panels open in this view. At the top is the Domains and ranges panel. In the centre is the Annotations panel. At the bottom the Characteristics and Description panels are side by side. Note that in this view you can see the seven characteristics functional, inverse functional, transitive, etc, which we studied earlier. There are various kinds of property in OWL. Here we use object properties which correspond to roles in description logic relating individuals to individuals. You can create a new sub-property by clicking on the leftmost of the three icons which you can see here. Fig 4.3.7. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.7. This screenshot shows the Entities tab open, focusing on the Object properties panel. There are three icons on the panel toolbar. The icon on the far left, Add sub property , should be selected. Create the two properties isInside and isCloseTo , which are siblings of each other. Fig 4.3.8. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.8. This screenshot focuses on the Object properties panel. After selecting the add sub-property icon, two sub-properties, isInside and isCloseTo , have been added. These are siblings in the hierarchy under owl:topObjectProperty . Characteristics of properties Next we want to define the property contains as being inverse to isInside . The idea is that \(x\) isInside \(y\) is the same as \(y\) contains \(x\) . Select isInside and click on the + sign next to Inverse Of . You can then create the contains property which is the inverse of isInside . Fig 4.3.9. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.9. This screenshot shows the window that is displayed when the sub-property isInside is selected. The + icon next to InverseOf in the Description panel on the lower right-hand side is also selected. You can then create the contains property which is the inverse of isInside . Tick the appropriate boxes to make isInside and contains both transitive. Should isCloseTo be transitive? No -- can you see why? Let's make isCloseTo symmetric: Fig 4.3.10. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.10. This screenshot shows the Characteristics and Description window when the property isCloseTo is selected. The checkbox Symmetric in the Characteristics panel has been selected. Using the reasoner If the isCloseTo property is symmetric, it must be its own inverse. Use the reasoner to check this. The way this appears may be different depending on which operating system you are using. Fig 4.3.11. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.11. This screenshot shows the drop down Reasoner menu on the Protégé application. Use the reasoner to check whether the isCloseTo property that is symmetric is its own inverse. To do this select Start reasoner if this is the first time you’ve used reasoner. If it’s not the first time select Synchronize reasoner . The first time you use the reasoner you need to select Start Reasoner ; subsequently it will be Synchronise Reasoner . After using the reasoner you may need to click again on isCloseTo in the hierarchy before you get to this: Fig 4.3.12. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.12. This screenshot shows the isCloseTo property in the Object properties selected after using the reasoner. The Object properties panel is on the left-hand side and there are four panels on the right-hand side: Domains and ranges is at the top, Annotations in the centre, with the Characteristics* and **Description panels below, side by side. The key part is here: Fig 4.3.13. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.13. This screenshot shows a close-up of the Description panel after running the reasoner. The reasoner has deduced that isCloseTo is inverse of itself. If selected the ? icon on the right-hand side reveals an explanation. The reasoner has deduced that isCloseTo is inverse to itself. If you click on the ? you will get an explanation of the reasoning behind the deduction. In this case the explanation is very short. Restrictions Next we want to say that a valuable house must be close to a school. This means the class ValuableHouse is a subclass, as follows if we use the notation of description logic: \[\mathsf{ValuableHouse} \sqsubseteq \exists \mathsf{closeTo} . \mathsf{School}\] In Protégé you need to go back to the Classes tab and select ValuableHouse from the class hierarchy. Now click on the + where it says SubClass Of . In the hierarchy we have already ensured that: \[\mathsf{ValuableHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{House}\] but we are about to add another subclass relationship. Fig 4.3.14. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.14. This screenshot shows the Class hierarchy tab with ValuableHouse selected on the left-hand side. On the right-hand side is the Description panel. Select the + icon next to SubClass Of to add a new subclass relationship. Select the Object restriction creator in the window that pops up. The Restricted property should be isCloseTo . The Restriction filler should be School . The Restriction type should be Some (existential) . Fig 4.3.15. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.15. This screenshot shows the window that pops up after selecting the + icon next to SubClass Of on the Description panel. Select the Object restriction creator panel. This brings up the Restricted property panel on the left and the Restriction filler panel on the right-hand side. isCloseTo should be selected as the Restricted property and School as the Restriction filler . Below this the Restriction type selection should be Some (existential) . Note that isCloseTo some School is really just an alternative syntax. for: \(\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}\) Fig 4.3.16. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.16. This screenshot shows a close-up of the Description panel when the property ValuableHouse is selected and after the existential property isCloseTo , restriction fillers School and existential type some have been added in the Object restriction creator window. Add the other existential restriction: isCloseTo some Station in a similar way so you have: Fig 4.3.17. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.17. This screenshot shows a close-up of the Description panel when the property ValuableHouse is selected and after the existential property isCloseTo , restriction fillers School and Station , and existential type some have been added in the Object restriction creator window. Now create a new class which is sibling to valuable house called ExpensiveHouse . Specify that it is a subclass of: \[\exists \, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . (\mathsf{School} \sqcap \mathsf{Station})\] This time, use the Class expression editor , instead of the Object restriction creator , to enter the appropriate text to achieve this. Fig 4.3.18. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.18. This screenshot shows the Class expression editor of a new class ExpensiveHouse . Enter isCloseTo some (School and Station) into the Class expression editor . Defined classes So far our use of restriction types has asserted that: \[\mathsf{ValuableHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{House} \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station})\] and \[\mathsf{ExpensiveHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{House} \sqcap \exists \, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . (\mathsf{School} \sqcap \mathsf{Station})\] This does not mean that every expensive house is a valuable house, even though: \[\exists \, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . (\mathsf{School} \sqcap \mathsf{Station}) \sqsubseteq (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station})\] It is certainly the case that: \[\mathsf{ExpensiveHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{House} \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station})\] but we have not said that: \[\mathsf{House} \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station}) \sqsubseteq \mathsf{ValuableHouse} \] In this case we can do that by asserting the equivalence: \[\mathsf{ValuableHouse} \equiv \mathsf{House} \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station})\] This asserts that \(\mathsf{ValuableHouse}\) can be defined in terms of other classes. Back in Protégé, from Edit in the top menu, you can select Convert to defined class from the drop down menu. Fig 4.3.19. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.19. This screenshot shows the drop down Edit menu of the Protégé application. There is a Convert to defined class option in this menu. This option 'converts the superclasses of the selected class to an equivalent class'. In the screenshot ValuableHouse is the class selected in the Class hierarchy menu. Also make ExpensiveHouse into a defined class. The hierarchy looks like this now: Fig 4.3.20. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.20. This screenshot shows the Class hierarchy panel when ExpensiveHouse is selected. The Description panel is adjacent to it. ExpensiveHouse and ValuableHouse have been converted to defined classes. This is indicated by three horizontal bars in the dot next to the property name. Now, what happens if we synchronise the reasoner? Fig 4.3.21. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.21. This screenshot shows the Class hierarchy (inferred) panel after the reasoner has been synchronised. ExpensiveHouse is now a subproperty of ValuableHouse , which is selected. Note we are looking at the inferred hierarchy here. Adding individuals Going now to the Individuals tab, it should be clear enough what you need to do to create three individuals with properties as follows: \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) : this is a \(\mathsf{House}\) \(\mathsf{Red\_House}\) : this is a \(\mathsf{School}\) \(\mathsf{Red\_House}\) : this is a \(\mathsf{School}\) \(\mathsf{West\_Avenue}\) : this is a \(\mathsf{Station}\) \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) is close to \(\mathsf{Red\_House}\) \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) is close to \(\mathsf{West\_Avenue}\) Fig 4.3.22 A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.22. This screenshot shows the Individuals by class tab and shows you how to create an individual. In this case the individual is 42_Acacia_Avenue , with the properties this is a House , isCloseTo Red_House and isCloseTo West_Avenue . In the upper left-hand corner is the Class hierarchy panel where House is selected. Below this is the Direct instances panel with 42_Acacia_Avenue selected. On the upper right-hand side is the Annotations panel. Below this are two panels alongside each other: the Descriptions panel is on the left-hand side and the Object property assertions panel is on the right-hand side. In the **Object property assertions panel there are two assertions: isCloseTo Red_House and isCloseTo West_Avenue . The Object property assertions you need to make appear in the bottom right of the screen shot Fig 4.3.22 . Now synchronise the reasoner to see that 42_Acacia_Avenue is a valuable house, and by clicking on the ? , by this inference, you can get the following explanation: Fig 4.3.23. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.23. This screenshot shows you the explanation after selecting the question mark in the reasoner after synchronising 42_Acacia_Avenue . The options Show regular justifications and All justifications are selected. The following is displayed as the explanation: 42_Acacia_Avenue Type House 42_Acacia_Avenue isCloseTo Red_House 42_Acacia_Avenue isCloseTo West_Avenue Red_House Type School West_Avenue Type Station ValuableHouse EquivalentTo House and (isCloseTo some School) and (isCloseTo some Station). Inconsistency One of the values of reasoning for ontologies is to be able to detect when you have created an individual with contradictory properties. It is possible in Protégé to state that a class is empty without the ontology actually being inconsistent. Although this makes sense logically, it is unlikely to be intended in practical ontology construction. You can try the following experiment and see how Protégé highlights the empty class in the class hierachy. Create a new class \(\mathsf{Unicorn}\) . Assert that this class is equivalent to \(\mathsf{owl{:}Nothing}\) . Note that class names are case-sensitive, so be careful not to try \(\mathsf{owl{:}nothing}\) etc. You should be able to run the reasoner without any fundamental objections appearing. Now create an individual of the class \(\mathsf{Unicorn}\) . You will need a name for your unicorn. Synchronise the reasoner, and you should get: Fig 4.3.24. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.24. This screenshot shows an inconsistency message after the reasoner has been synchronised for an individual of the class Unicorn . The first part of the message says: Your ontology is inconsistent which means that the OWL reasoner will no longer be able to provide any useful information about the ontology. Three options are then provided: 'Click the Explain button to try the Protégé explanation facility.'' 'If you think you know what the problem is, click Cancel to fix the ontology yourself.'' 'Some reasoners come with command line tools that will provide complete explanations for inconsistent ontologies.'' There are two buttons below this, Cancel and Explain. We have tried to say that there are no unicorns and that there is an individual unicorn. Protégé alerts us to this inconsistency. In this case we deliberately introduced the inconsistency, but in creating complex ontologies such problems can occur and would make the ontology useless if not corrected. Disjoint classes In the \(\mathsf{Building}\) example above there were classes \(\mathsf{Station}\) and \(\mathsf{School}\) . If we want to say that nothing is both a school and a station, Protégé provides a way to do this via the Description pane for one of the classes. Click on the +* next to Disjoint With in the Description pane for a class and you can assert that the class is disjoint from another class. If you do this, while keeping the defined class \(\mathsf{ExpensiveHouse}\) : \[\mathsf{ExpensiveHouse} \equiv \mathsf{House} \sqcap \exists \, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . (\mathsf{School} \sqcap \mathsf{Station})\] then you will find that the reasoner objects to this as \(\mathsf{ExpensiveHouse}\) must be empty. In developing an ontology for an area where knowledge is not fixed, it can happen that classes are initially correctly described as disjoint but as more knowledge is acquired the ontology needs to be updated. Imagine the development of an ontology where classes \(\mathsf{EggLayingAnimal}\) and \(\mathsf{Mammal}\) were designated disjoint by someone unfamiliar with the platypus and other inhabitants of the intersection of these two classes. On being introduced to one of these examples, our imaginary ontologist would need to remove the disjointness requirement and maybe create a new class: \[\mathsf{Monotreme} \equiv \mathsf{Mammal} \sqcap \mathsf{EggLayingAnimal}\] Open world reasoning Continue to develop the ontology so that the classes \(\mathsf{GoodHouse}\) and \(\mathsf{PuristHouse}\) have these definitions. \[\mathsf{GoodHouse} \equiv \mathsf{House} \sqcap \forall\, \mathsf{contains} . (\mathsf{UsefulThing} \sqcup \mathsf{BeautifulThing})\] \[\mathsf{PuristHouse} \equiv \mathsf{House} \sqcap \forall\, \mathsf{contains} . (\mathsf{UsefulThing} \sqcap \mathsf{BeautifulThing})\] You can do this using the method shown earlier, or you can type directly in the Class expression editor which comes up if you click on the + next to Equivalent To in the Description pane like this: Fig 4.3.25. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.25. This screenshot shows the Class expression editor if you select the + icon next to Equivalent To in the Description panel. The Class expression editor contains House and (contains only (UsefulThing and BeautifulThing)) Check that the reasoner deduces: \[\mathsf{PuristHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{GoodHouse}\] Remember that you have to inspect the inferred class hierarchy not the asserted one to see this. Introduce some individual items which \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) contains. For example: a piano, a dishwasher, a cat, a cactus, etc. Say that some of these are useful/beautiful or neither according to taste. It is optional in this exercise to follow William Morris who wrote Hopes and Fears for Art, 1882 'Now unless we are musical, and need a piano (in which case, as far as beauty is concerned, we are in a bad way) …' You are encouraged to play with different choices of things in 42 Acacia Avenue and the classes to which they belong. You can see if the inferred hierarchy changes depending on these choices. To take a concrete example, suppose that: \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) contains a cat which is a \(\mathsf{BeautifulThing}\) , and also contains a piano which is a \(\mathsf{UsefulThing}\) , and we make no other assertions at all about individuals in that house. The only things we have said are in the house are either beautiful or useful. Does \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) get assigned to the class: \[\mathsf{GoodHouse} \equiv \mathsf{House} \sqcap \forall\, \mathsf{contains} . (\mathsf{UsefulThing} \sqcup \mathsf{BeautifulThing})\] by the reasoner? You should find that the answer is 'no' . Because of the open world reasoning used in Protégé and in ontologies based on description logic, this is what is expected to happen. This is because the possibility remains open that there are objects lurking in the house which are neither useful nor beautiful but which are not recorded in the ontology. Next we see one way to assert that the house contains nothing other than a cat and a piano. Create one new class called \(\mathsf{42Contents}\) . The idea is that instances of this class are just the things inside \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) . You can just make this a subclass of \(\mathsf{owl{:}Thing}\) and not a subclass of any of the other classes in the ontology. Using the Class Expression Editor assert that \(\mathsf{42Contents}\) is equivalent to two classes: Fig 4.3.26. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.26. This screenshot shows the Description panel for the class 42Contents after using the Class expression editor . Two classes that are equivalent to 42Contents are displayed: isInside some {42_Acacia_Avenue} and {cat, piano} . We have thus ensured that: \[\{\mathsf{cat},\, \mathsf{piano}\} \equiv \exists\, \mathsf{isInside} . \{\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\}.\] We have used Enumerated Classes which allow us to create a class consisting only of specified individuals. The reasoner can now deduce that 42 Acacia Avenue belongs to \(\mathsf{GoodHouse}\) . Further work on Protégé This part of the lesson has only introduced the most basic features of ontology construction. To learn more about Protégé you should consult the well-known pizza ontology example described in this document. You will find much of the first 50 or 60 pages covers issues similar to the ones already seen in this lesson. Beyond that are some useful new techniques for expressing knowledge in Protégé that we have not seen.
docs.title: Lesson 3 Protege

docs.text: Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: Use the Protégé software for defining a class hierarchy. Use Protégé to define properties and their characteristics. Use a reasoner with Protégé to compute inferences. Explain the difference between open world reasoning and closed world reasoning in the context of Protégé.
docs.title: Protégé

docs.text: It is straightforward to install the latest version of Protégé on your own computer. At http://protegeproject.github.io/protege/ you will find all the instructions you need.
docs.title: Installing Protégé 5.5.0

docs.text: On starting Protégé you will see something like this: Fig 4.3.1. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.1. This screenshot shows what the Protégé application window looks like when you open it up. The panel on the upper left has the Active ontology tab open. The panel on the upper right is for Ontology metrics . The panel on the bottom has the Ontology imports tab open. Note that the top half of the window has several tabs, including: Active ontology, Entities, Classes, Individuals by class. Make sure the Active ontology tab is selected and by clicking on the + sign next to Annotations add a suitable annotation: Fig 4.3.2. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot Fig 4.3.2. This screenshot shows the Create Annotation window that opens after you select the plus icon next to Annotations . Here you can add a suitable annotation in the text box on the right hand side.
docs.title: Creating an ontology

docs.text: Select the Classes tab which should bring up the Class hierarchy . Fig 4.3.3. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.3. This screenshot has the Classes tab open. There are two Class hierarchy panels on the left-hand side. On the upper right is the Annotations panel and below this is the Description panel. Select Thing (the top element of the hierarchy). Select the Add-subclass icon just above Thing and add a new class: Fig 4.3.4. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.4. This screenshot shows what happens after you select Thing , the top element of the hierarchy, and then select the Add sub-class icon above Thing and add a new sub-class. A Create a new Class panel pops up with a text box to enter a new Class name. Protégé provides a very flexible interface in which you are able to control the arrangement of individual windows. Windows can be split horizontally or vertically as well as floated off separately from the main window. Some of this flexibility can be confusing for beginners, but you are encouraged to explore the various features. Add further classes through adding sibling classes and subclasses so that you arrive at the following (but do not worry if the ordering of siblings is different). Fig 4.3.5. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.5. This screenshot shows the Class hierarchy panel and the class hierarchy after numerous sibling classes and sub classes have been added. The hierarchy shown is: owl:Thing BeautifulThing UsefulThing Building House PuristHouse GoodHouse ValuableHouse Station School.
docs.title: Create some classes

docs.text: Informally we want to say the following: A valuable house is one which is near a school and a station. A good house is one in which everything is useful or beautiful. A purist house is one in which everything is useful and beautiful. Beware of the language here. If we say something is close to a school and a station we generally expect the school and the station to be different things. We certainly would not insist that they have to be the same thing. If we say everything is useful and beautiful we do mean that any single thing is both useful and also beautiful. Instead of the Classes tab, you need to select the Entities tab. When you do this and select the top item in the Object property hierarchy , owl:topObjectProperty , you should see this: Fig 4.3.6. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.6. This screenshot shows the Entities tab open. On the left-hand side the Object properties panel is open and the top item in the object property hierarchy, owl:topObjectProperty , is selected. On the right-hand side there are four further panels open in this view. At the top is the Domains and ranges panel. In the centre is the Annotations panel. At the bottom the Characteristics and Description panels are side by side. Note that in this view you can see the seven characteristics functional, inverse functional, transitive, etc, which we studied earlier. There are various kinds of property in OWL. Here we use object properties which correspond to roles in description logic relating individuals to individuals. You can create a new sub-property by clicking on the leftmost of the three icons which you can see here. Fig 4.3.7. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.7. This screenshot shows the Entities tab open, focusing on the Object properties panel. There are three icons on the panel toolbar. The icon on the far left, Add sub property , should be selected. Create the two properties isInside and isCloseTo , which are siblings of each other. Fig 4.3.8. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.8. This screenshot focuses on the Object properties panel. After selecting the add sub-property icon, two sub-properties, isInside and isCloseTo , have been added. These are siblings in the hierarchy under owl:topObjectProperty .
docs.title: Adding properties

docs.text: Next we want to define the property contains as being inverse to isInside . The idea is that \(x\) isInside \(y\) is the same as \(y\) contains \(x\) . Select isInside and click on the + sign next to Inverse Of . You can then create the contains property which is the inverse of isInside . Fig 4.3.9. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.9. This screenshot shows the window that is displayed when the sub-property isInside is selected. The + icon next to InverseOf in the Description panel on the lower right-hand side is also selected. You can then create the contains property which is the inverse of isInside . Tick the appropriate boxes to make isInside and contains both transitive. Should isCloseTo be transitive? No -- can you see why? Let's make isCloseTo symmetric: Fig 4.3.10. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.10. This screenshot shows the Characteristics and Description window when the property isCloseTo is selected. The checkbox Symmetric in the Characteristics panel has been selected.
docs.title: Characteristics of properties

docs.text: If the isCloseTo property is symmetric, it must be its own inverse. Use the reasoner to check this. The way this appears may be different depending on which operating system you are using. Fig 4.3.11. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.11. This screenshot shows the drop down Reasoner menu on the Protégé application. Use the reasoner to check whether the isCloseTo property that is symmetric is its own inverse. To do this select Start reasoner if this is the first time you’ve used reasoner. If it’s not the first time select Synchronize reasoner . The first time you use the reasoner you need to select Start Reasoner ; subsequently it will be Synchronise Reasoner . After using the reasoner you may need to click again on isCloseTo in the hierarchy before you get to this: Fig 4.3.12. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.12. This screenshot shows the isCloseTo property in the Object properties selected after using the reasoner. The Object properties panel is on the left-hand side and there are four panels on the right-hand side: Domains and ranges is at the top, Annotations in the centre, with the Characteristics* and **Description panels below, side by side. The key part is here: Fig 4.3.13. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.13. This screenshot shows a close-up of the Description panel after running the reasoner. The reasoner has deduced that isCloseTo is inverse of itself. If selected the ? icon on the right-hand side reveals an explanation. The reasoner has deduced that isCloseTo is inverse to itself. If you click on the ? you will get an explanation of the reasoning behind the deduction. In this case the explanation is very short.
docs.title: Using the reasoner

docs.text: Next we want to say that a valuable house must be close to a school. This means the class ValuableHouse is a subclass, as follows if we use the notation of description logic: \[\mathsf{ValuableHouse} \sqsubseteq \exists \mathsf{closeTo} . \mathsf{School}\] In Protégé you need to go back to the Classes tab and select ValuableHouse from the class hierarchy. Now click on the + where it says SubClass Of . In the hierarchy we have already ensured that: \[\mathsf{ValuableHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{House}\] but we are about to add another subclass relationship. Fig 4.3.14. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.14. This screenshot shows the Class hierarchy tab with ValuableHouse selected on the left-hand side. On the right-hand side is the Description panel. Select the + icon next to SubClass Of to add a new subclass relationship. Select the Object restriction creator in the window that pops up. The Restricted property should be isCloseTo . The Restriction filler should be School . The Restriction type should be Some (existential) . Fig 4.3.15. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.15. This screenshot shows the window that pops up after selecting the + icon next to SubClass Of on the Description panel. Select the Object restriction creator panel. This brings up the Restricted property panel on the left and the Restriction filler panel on the right-hand side. isCloseTo should be selected as the Restricted property and School as the Restriction filler . Below this the Restriction type selection should be Some (existential) . Note that isCloseTo some School is really just an alternative syntax. for: \(\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}\) Fig 4.3.16. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.16. This screenshot shows a close-up of the Description panel when the property ValuableHouse is selected and after the existential property isCloseTo , restriction fillers School and existential type some have been added in the Object restriction creator window. Add the other existential restriction: isCloseTo some Station in a similar way so you have: Fig 4.3.17. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.17. This screenshot shows a close-up of the Description panel when the property ValuableHouse is selected and after the existential property isCloseTo , restriction fillers School and Station , and existential type some have been added in the Object restriction creator window. Now create a new class which is sibling to valuable house called ExpensiveHouse . Specify that it is a subclass of: \[\exists \, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . (\mathsf{School} \sqcap \mathsf{Station})\] This time, use the Class expression editor , instead of the Object restriction creator , to enter the appropriate text to achieve this. Fig 4.3.18. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.18. This screenshot shows the Class expression editor of a new class ExpensiveHouse . Enter isCloseTo some (School and Station) into the Class expression editor .
docs.title: Restrictions

docs.text: So far our use of restriction types has asserted that: \[\mathsf{ValuableHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{House} \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station})\] and \[\mathsf{ExpensiveHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{House} \sqcap \exists \, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . (\mathsf{School} \sqcap \mathsf{Station})\] This does not mean that every expensive house is a valuable house, even though: \[\exists \, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . (\mathsf{School} \sqcap \mathsf{Station}) \sqsubseteq (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station})\] It is certainly the case that: \[\mathsf{ExpensiveHouse} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{House} \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station})\] but we have not said that: \[\mathsf{House} \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station}) \sqsubseteq \mathsf{ValuableHouse} \] In this case we can do that by asserting the equivalence: \[\mathsf{ValuableHouse} \equiv \mathsf{House} \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{School}) \sqcap (\exists\, \mathsf{isCloseTo} . \mathsf{Station})\] This asserts that \(\mathsf{ValuableHouse}\) can be defined in terms of other classes. Back in Protégé, from Edit in the top menu, you can select Convert to defined class from the drop down menu. Fig 4.3.19. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.19. This screenshot shows the drop down Edit menu of the Protégé application. There is a Convert to defined class option in this menu. This option 'converts the superclasses of the selected class to an equivalent class'. In the screenshot ValuableHouse is the class selected in the Class hierarchy menu. Also make ExpensiveHouse into a defined class. The hierarchy looks like this now: Fig 4.3.20. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.20. This screenshot shows the Class hierarchy panel when ExpensiveHouse is selected. The Description panel is adjacent to it. ExpensiveHouse and ValuableHouse have been converted to defined classes. This is indicated by three horizontal bars in the dot next to the property name. Now, what happens if we synchronise the reasoner? Fig 4.3.21. A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.21. This screenshot shows the Class hierarchy (inferred) panel after the reasoner has been synchronised. ExpensiveHouse is now a subproperty of ValuableHouse , which is selected. Note we are looking at the inferred hierarchy here.
docs.title: Defined classes

docs.text: Going now to the Individuals tab, it should be clear enough what you need to do to create three individuals with properties as follows: \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) : this is a \(\mathsf{House}\) \(\mathsf{Red\_House}\) : this is a \(\mathsf{School}\) \(\mathsf{Red\_House}\) : this is a \(\mathsf{School}\) \(\mathsf{West\_Avenue}\) : this is a \(\mathsf{Station}\) \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) is close to \(\mathsf{Red\_House}\) \(\mathsf{42\_Acacia\_Avenue}\) is close to \(\mathsf{West\_Avenue}\) Fig 4.3.22 A screenshot of the Protégé application window. Please see the full text description below. Select for full text description of screenshot 4.3.22. This screenshot shows the Individuals by class tab and shows you how to create an individual. In this case the individual is 42_Acacia_Avenue , with the properties this is a House , isCloseTo Red_House and isCloseTo West_Avenue . In the upper left-hand corner is the Class hierarchy panel where House is selected. Below this is the Direct instances panel with 42_Acacia_Avenue selected. On the upper right-hand side is the Annotations panel. Below this are two panels alongside each other: the Descriptions panel is on the left-hand side and the Object property assertions panel is on the right-hand side. In the **Object property assertions panel there are two assertions: isCloseTo Red_House and isCloseTo West_Avenue . The Object property assertions you need to make appear in the bottom right of the screen shot Fig 4.3.22 .
