docs.title: 1.8 Exercises

docs.text:
Time Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: Explain the distinction between time as represented by specific date and times, and time as represented by qualitative descriptions. Represent temporal information in first order logic, including using a HoldsAt predicate. Identify which Allen relation holds between time intervals in a given example. 2.1 Introduction This lesson is about representing knowledge about time \(\def\HA{\mathit{HoldsAt}}\) \(\def\phi{\varphi}\) A classical proposition is either true or false. So it cannot be true sometimes and false at other times. Hence a statement such as It is snowing in Leeds does not really express a classical proposition. Its truth depends on when the statement is made (assume here the location is clear). A corresponding classical proposition would be something like: It was snowing in Leeds at 11:22am 8/2/2032 This statement, if true, is eternally true. In this lesson we consider three ways to deal with time: adding a time parameter to all predicates in a first order logic using a special \(\mathit{HoldsAt}\) predicate qualitative representation with time intervals, in a similar way to the qualitative space in the previous lesson. 2.2 Adding a time parameter We can explicitly add time references to first-order formulas. For example \[\mathit{Happy}(\mathit{John}, t)\] could mean John is happy at time \(t\) . In this representation each predicate is given an extra argument place specifying the time at which it is true. If we do this we need to describe properties of time itself in first-order logic. 2.2.1 First order axioms about time To talk about the ordering of time points we introduce the special relation \(\leq\) . Being a (linear) order it satisfies the following axioms. \( egin{array}{rll} 	ext{1} &  orall t_1\, orall t_2\, orall t_3\, ((t_1 \leq t_2 \wedge t_2 \leq t_3) 	o t_1 \leq t_3) & \mathsf{ (transitivity)}\[2ex] 	ext{2} &  orall t_1\, orall t_2\, (t_1 \leq t_2 \vee t_2 \leq t_1) & \mathsf{ (linearity)}\[2ex] 	ext{3} &  orall t_1\, orall t_2\, ((t_1 \leq t_2 \wedge t_2 \leq t_1) 	o t_1 = t_2) & \mathsf{ (anti-symmetry)} \end{array}\) We can define a strict ordering relation by: \(\qquad \qquad t_1 < t_2 \quad \mathsf{ iff } \quad t_1 \leq t_2 \wedge 
eg(t_1 = t_2)\) Depending on what we want to model, we can add additional axioms such as these: Time will continue for ever in the future: \(\qquad \qquad orall t \, \exists t' \, (t < t')\) . The infinite divisibility of time (usually called density ): \(\qquad \qquad orall t_1\,  orall t_2 ((t_1 < t_2) 	o \exists t_3\, ((t_1 < t_3) \wedge (t_3 < t_2))\) . 2.2.2 Examples Example Sue is happy but will be sad: \(\qquad\mathit{Happy}(\mathit{Sue}, 0) \wedge \exists t\,((0 < t) \wedge \mathit{Sad}(\mathit{Sue}, t))\) Here I use \(0\) to stand for the present time. We can describe more complex temporal constraints of a causal nature, as in the next example. Example When the sun comes out I am happy until it rains \( orall t \, (S(t) 	o  orall u\,((t\leq u \wedge 
eg \exists r\, ((t\leq r) \wedge (r\leq u) \wedge R(r))) 	o H(u)))\) The predicates \(S(x), R(x), H(x)\) correspond to: the sun is shining at time \(x\) it is raining at time \(x\) , I am happy at time \(x\) . 2.3 Using a Holds-At predicate Rather than adding time to each predicate, several AI researchers have found it more convenient to use a special type of relation between propositions and time points: \(\qquad \HA(\mathit{Happy}(\mathit{John}), t)\) . This looks like it is stepping outside first order logic, because the first parameter of \(\HA\) looks like a formula itself. When you see things like \(\qquad \HA(\mathit{Sunny} \wedge \mathit{Warm}, t)\) , you should remember that putting logical connectives inside the arguments to predicates is simply not syntactically correct in first order logic. So this appears at first sight to be complete nonsense. However, it is not nonsense, but rather a useful short-hand. Everything could be translated into perfectly correct first order logic. To do this we could take the expressions that appear as the first arguments of \(\HA\) to be terms built with function symbols. These function symbols would model the logical connectives, but they would be different from the actual connectives in first order logic. For example, the axiom we see below, where \(	o\) appears twice, \(\qquad (\HA(\phi, t) \wedge \HA(\phi 	o \psi, t)) 	o \HA(\psi, t)\) would need to be a convenient way of asserting: \(\qquad (\HA(\phi, t) \wedge \HA(\mathit{implies}(\phi,\psi), t)) 	o \HA(\psi, t)\) . To develop this in detail would be time-consuming and is not necessary for this module. We will treat the usual way of writing this as a short-hand. Axioms for Holds-At \((\HA(\phi, t) \wedge \HA(\phi 	o \psi, t)) 	o \HA(\psi, t)\) \(
eg \HA(\phi \wedge 
eg \phi, t)\) \(\HA(\phi, t) \vee \HA(
eg \phi, t)\) \(\HA(\phi, t) \leftrightarrow \HA(\HA(\phi, t) , t')\) \(t \leq t' \leftrightarrow \HA((t \leq t'), t'')\) \( orall t\, (\HA(\phi, t')) 	o \HA( orall t \,\phi, t')\) The \(\HA\) predicate can be used to model general relationships between formulas. For example to capture a possible specification of the relation \(\phi\) causes \(\psi\) . We could use: \[ orall t\, \HA(\phi, t) 	o \exists t'\, (t\leq t' \wedge \HA(\psi, t')).\] 2.4 Qualitative Reasoning about Time 2.4.1 The Allen relations When talking about intervals and events in time we use words such as before and during to relate one event to another. For example during my time at university I attended a module on knowledge representation , or the flooding happened before the new building was constructed . Statements like these are making qualitative relationships between events that occur over intervals of time. The event the flooding took place over an interval and the event construction of the new building took place over another interval, and the qualitative relationship between these two intervals is that the first happened before the second one. There are 13 possible relations between two intervals as shown in the diagram below. These are called the Allen relations and were described in the paper by J. F. Allen Maintaining knowledge about temporal intervals , Communications of the ACM, 26(11):832–843, 1983. In the diagram below each of the seven rows shows a period of time and within that two intervals. One of these intervals is shown below the horizontal line representing the whole period, and the other interval is shown above the horizontal line. On the left hand side of the diagram is the name Allen gave to the relationship of the lower interval to the upper one. Note that the order is important here: saying \(A\) happened before \(B\) is quite different from saying \(B\) happened before \(A\) . On the right hand side of the diagram is the name given to the relationship of the upper interval to the lower one. The equals relation is symmetric but none of the other 12 relations has this property. Select for full text description of diagram. This diagram describes 13 possible relations between two intervals. These are called Allen relations. The diagram has seven rows showing a period of time as a horizontal line. Each of the lines has two bars on it: the bar below the line is red and represents the whole period; the bar above the line is green and represents the other interval. On the left-hand side of the diagram is the name given to the relationship of the red bar to the green bar. On the right-hand side of the diagram is the name given to the relationship of the green bar to the red bar. The first line is labelled before on the left and after on the right. The red bar is on the lower left-hand side of the line and the green bar is on the upper right-hand side. There is a space in between the two. The second line is labelled during on the left and contains on the right. A short red bar is aligned in the line’s centre. A green bar is also aligned centrally above it. The third line is labelled ends on the left and ended by on the right. A short red bar is aligned just right of centre. The green bar above is longer and aligned centrally and equal to the red bar on its right side. The fourth line is labelled starts on the left and started by on the right. A short red bar is aligned to the left of centre. The green bar above is longer and aligned centrally and double the length of the red bar on its left side. The fifth line is labelled overlaps on the left and overlapped by on the right. A long red bar extends from near to the start of the line on the left to just right of centre. A green bar of equal length extends from near to the start of the line on the right to left of centre. The two bars thus overlap in the centre. The sixth line is labelled meets on the left and met by on the right. A red bar half the width of the line extends from the left side of the line to the centre. A green bar half the width of the line extends from the right side of the line so that it meets the red line in the centre. The seventh line is labelled equals on the left and equals on the right. The red and green bar are the same width and aligned together in the centre of the line. 2.4.2 Compositional reasoning An example of compositional reasoning: A occurs before B and B contains C What relationship has A to C? Answer: A occurs before C Note there is not always just one answer to such questions. In the paper by Allen cited above you can find a table, called a composition table , in which the possible answers to such questions are calculated for every possible pair of the 13 relations between time intervals. Composition tables have been calculated for many other systems of qualitative relations, including spatial ones we met in the previous lesson. These tables have proved to be of practical use in reasoning tasks involving qualitative concepts. 2.5 Tense logic We will later (in Unit 6) see another way of representing time called Tense Logic This has two modalities , one looking into the past and one into the future. To explain how that works we need some general ideas on modal logic , which is the topic of the next lesson.

docs.text: We will later (in Unit 6) see another way of representing time called Tense Logic This has two modalities , one looking into the past and one into the future. To explain how that works we need some general ideas on modal logic , which is the topic of the next lesson.
docs.title: 2.5 Tense logic

docs.text:
Modal logic \(\def\HA{\mathit{HoldsAt}}\) \(\def\Past{\mathop {\mathbf{P}}}\) \(\def\Future{\mathop {\mathbf{F}}}\) \(\def\Going{\mathop {\mathbf{G}}}\) \(\def\HasBeen{\mathop {\mathbf{H}}}\) \(\def\mBox{\mathop{\Box}}\) \(\def\mDiamond{\mathop{\Diamond}}\) \(\def\leq{\leqslant}\) \(\def\geq{\geqslant}\) \(\def\phi{\varphi}\) Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: Construct simple examples of frames and valuations for propositional modal logic. Evaluate the truth of modal logic formulas in a given interpretation. Explain how some expressions in description logic are essentially formulas in modal logic. Write first order formulas that correspond to modal logic formulas in some special cases where this is possible. 3.1 Syntax of modal logic In this lesson, we will study propositional modal logic . There are various kinds of first order modal logics, but the propositional case is the most relevant here. Although it is called propositional it is capable of expressing some things that are not possible in ordinary propositional logic, and in some ways even beyond first order logic. Description logics are one example of modal logics being used in knowledge representation. Another example, we meet later in this unit are tense logics. These are logics for expressing things like: In the future there will be pelicans in the park. At every future time, the values of \(x\) and \(y\) in the program execution will be equal. At some point in the past people lived here and there was a canal but not a railway. We start with a simple form of modal logic that captures the main idea, but which is too simple to fit the way that description logics are examples of modal logic. 3.1.1 The language A language for propositional modal logic consists of: Propositional variables, that stand for atomic propositions just as in ordinary propositional logic Logical connectives as before: \(\quad 
eg \quad \vee \quad \wedge \quad 	o \quad \leftrightarrow\) Two additional logical symbols: \(\Box\) , called box , and \(\lozenge\) , called diamond . The usual parentheses to avoid ambiguity. 3.1.2 The formulas The formulas of modal logic are expressions that can be true or false. Unlike first order logic there are no terms in the language that can refer to individual things. The rules for construction of formulas are: Every atomic proposition is a formula If \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) are formulas then the following are all formulas: \[
eg \alpha \qquad \alpha \vee  eta \qquad \alpha \wedge  eta \qquad \alpha 	o  eta \qquad \alpha \leftrightarrow  eta \qquad \Box \alpha \qquad \lozenge \alpha\] Parentheses can be added as necessary. The precedence rules follow those of first order logic with the box and diamond having the same precedence that the quantifiers have there. 3.1.2.1 Examples If \(A\) , \(B\) , \(C\) are atomic propositions then the following are all examples of formulas in propositional modal logic: \[  egin{array}{l} A\[2ex] 
eg B\ (A 	o B) \leftrightarrow C\[2ex] \Box A\[2ex] \lozenge B\[2ex] (\Box A) \vee 
eg A\[2ex] \Box (A \vee 
eg A)\[2ex] (\Box \lozenge \Box \lozenge 
eg A) \wedge ((\Box \Box B) 	o \lozenge (B \vee \Box C)) \end{array} \] 3.2 Semantics 3.2.1 Informal idea In ordinary propositional logic, when we give the meaning of an atomic proposition we simply say whether it is true or false. In modal logic we can think of propositions not simply being true or false, but being true at some places and false at others. The idea of place here is not necessarily anything to do with space, it's more a way of thinking about how the semantics works. The 'places' might be different times, or objects in the world. The semantics for modal logic has two ingredients that go beyond semantics for propositional logic. These are: set, often denoted \(W\) , which models the idea of "places" where propositions can be true, and a binary relation on \(W\) , often denoted \(R\) , so that \(R \subseteq W 	imes W\) . Why use \(W\) ? The letter 'W' is chosen because the elements of the set are sometimes referred to as worlds . This comes from the philosophical history of modal logic where the logic was used to talk about statements being true in two senses: in all possible worlds, or in some possible worlds. For example, it could be claimed that \(2+2=4\) is necessarily true (assuming the meanings of the symbols involved) and could not possibly be otherwise. On the other hand we can imagine a world in which the number \(4\) is commonly denoted by a different symbol (as of course it is in various cultures). In a lot of computer science applications, 'worlds' does not fit the context and they are instead called states . Modal logic is similar to first order logic in having this set of entities, but in the modal logic we study (at first) there is no way to refer to individuals in this set. The relation, \(R\) , is often called the accessibility relation and this models the idea that some of the 'places' are accessible from a given 'place'. A simple example is if \(W\) models a set of times, and the relation \(R\) models a relation of being later among the times. The accessibility relation can also model a role as in description logic. For example, the 'worlds' might be people and the accessibility relation could model the idea of being a parent. In modal logic, unlike propositional logic and first order logic, there is a kind of local truth. That is, a formula can be true at one particular world but it may not be true at all worlds. 3.2.2 Formal definition of semantics Let's have the formal definition and then come back to some examples. Given a language as described above, an interpretation provides: frame , that is a pair \((W, R)\) where a set \(W\) a binary relation \(R\subseteq W 	imes W\) , a valuation \(V\) which provides for each atomic proposition, \(p\) , a subset of \(W\) so \(V(p) \subseteq W\) . then the semantics is: \[  egin{array}{lcl} \mathcal{M}, s \vDash p & \mathsf{ iff } & s \in V(p)\ \mathcal{M}, s \vDash 
eg \alpha & \mathsf{ iff } & \mathcal{M}, s 
vDash \alpha\ \mathcal{M}, s \vDash \alpha \vee  eta & \mathsf{ iff } & \mathcal{M}, s \vDash \alpha \mathsf{~or~ } \mathcal{M}, s \vDash  eta\ \mathcal{M}, s \vDash \alpha \wedge  eta & \mathsf{ iff } & \mathcal{M}, s \vDash \alpha \mathsf{ ~and~ } \mathcal{M}, s \vDash  eta\ \mathcal{M}, s \vDash \alpha 	o  eta & \mathsf{ iff } & \mathcal{M}, s 
vDash \alpha \mathsf{~or ~} \mathcal{M}, s \vDash  eta\ \mathcal{M}, s \vDash \lozenge \alpha & \mathsf{ iff } & \mathsf{There~ exists~ } t \in W \mathsf{~such ~that~} R(s,t) \mathsf{~and~} \mathcal{M}, t \vDash \alpha\ \mathcal{M}, s \vDash \Box \alpha & \mathsf{ iff } & \mathsf{For ~ every ~} t \in W \mathsf{~such~that~} R(s,t), \mathsf{~it~holds~that~} \mathcal{M}, t \vDash \alpha\ \end{array} \] The key points to understand are that (for a given valuation): \(\lozenge p\) is true at \(w\) when \(p\) is true somewhere accessible from \(w\) \(\Box p\) is true at \(w\) when \(p\) is true everywhere accessible from \(w\) This means that: \(\lozenge\) has some similarities with \(\exists\) in first order logic. \(\Box\) has some similarities with \( orall\) in first order logic. 3.2.3 Truth, satisfaction and validity In ordinary propositional logic, we said a formula \(\phi\) was satisfiable if it was true under some valuation, and valid if it was true under all valuations. In propositional modal logic, a formula \(\phi\) can be: True at a particular world in a frame \(F\) under valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) . True at all worlds in a frame \(F\) under valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) ( satisfied in \(F\) by \(\mathcal{V}\) ). True at all worlds in a frame \(F\) under all valuations ( valid in \(F\) ). Valid in all frames ( universally valid ). In propositional modal logic, the notion of satisfiable (able to be true) for a formula \(\phi\) could be confusing. In the above terminology able to be true and able to be satisfied are used in different ways. This means we may need to be careful to distinguish these cases. 3.3 Example (parents, children and grandchildren) In this example the states are modelling individual people, and the accessibility relation models one person being a parent of another. In the image below the states are drawn as small circles, and the accessibility relation is shown by drawing arrows. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it have arrows pointing down to a child circle. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. You can see that: There are three people who are grandparents at the top. There are four people who are parents but not grandparents. There are five people who are children but not parents at the bottom. The diagram provides a frame in which we are going to interpret formulas. Let's start with a single proposition \(p\) and give a valuation by drawing circles around the states where \(p\) is true. Look at the image below. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement my age is less than 10 is true. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it have arrows pointing down to a circle that is circled. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. The circle on the left of these is circled. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles, both of which are themselves circled. In the image above there are four states where \(p\) is true. To make it more concrete, let's say \(p\) is modelling the statement: My age is less than 10. You can see how the truth of such a statement depends on who is saying it. That is, it is true in some states, or at some worlds, but not at others. What we do next is see how we can use modal logic to build formulas that model statements more complex than just the speaker being under 10 years old. Our first example is: I have a child whose age is less than 10. We can model this by \(\lozenge p\) , and the next image shows where \(\lozenge p\) holds. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement I have a child whose age is less than 10 is true. In this diagram all the parent circles are circled. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it have arrows pointing down to a child circle. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Now what does \(\Box p\) look like? This can be thought of as modelling: All of my children are under 10. But this can feel odd because the semantics of \(\Box\) means that \(\Box p\) will be true for anyone who has no children at all. So you may feel that it's better to say it is modelling: Either I have no children at all, or all the ones I do have are under 10. If we really wanted to model this: I have children and they are all under 10. We could use \((\lozenge p) \wedge \Box p\) . But you have to remember that this is saying they have at least one child, not necessarily more than one. If all we wanted to say was "I have children" we would need \(\lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) . In terms of the pictures this is true wherever at least one arrow is leaving the state. The states where \(\Box p\) is true are shown in the next image. You should be able to draw a different picture for \((\lozenge p) \wedge \Box p\) yourself. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement Either I have no children at all, or all the ones I do have are under 10 is true. In this diagram all the child circles and three of the parents circles are themselves circled. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it are both themselves circled and have arrows pointing down to a child circle. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle which is itself circled. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. You can see, following the discussion after the previous picture, that we can model related statements about grandchildren. I have some (at least one) grandchildren: \(\lozenge \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) I have grandchildren and they are all under 10: \((\lozenge \lozenge p) \wedge \Box \Box p\) . If we look at \(\Box \Box p\) we get this next image. It is modelling the following statement: Either I have no grandchildren at all, or all the ones I do have are under 10. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle which is itself circled. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. For a final example, consider the formula \(\lozenge (p \vee 
eg p) 	o \lozenge p\) . Because of the properties of \(	o\) this formula holds: for any person with no children, and for any person who has a child under 10. We can interpret it as modelling: if I have a child then I have a child who is under 10. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement any person with no children, or any person who has a child under 10 is true. In this diagram all the parent and child circles are circled. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This is the only circle in this diagram which is not itself circled. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle which is itself circled. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. 3.4 Exercises Universally valid formulas These formulas are all valid in any frame, whatever the accessibility relation, and whatever the valuations for the propositional variables. \(\qquad p \vee 
eg p\) \(\qquad \lozenge \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p) 	o \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) \(\qquad \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p) \vee \Box (p \wedge 
eg p)\) \(\qquad \Box (p 	o q) 	o (\Box p 	o \Box q)\) To make sure you understand how modal logic works you should see if you can explain why each one has to be valid in all situations. You may find it helpful to think in terms of pictures with arrows like the ones drawn above. In pictorial terms we can understand when certain formulas would be true at a particular state. Taking one step is to be understood as moving along one arrow in the accessibility relation. The accessibility relation is allowed to have loops, so taking one step might not lead anywhere new. From here you can take one step: \(\lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) . From here you can take two steps: \(\lozenge \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) . From here you cannot go anywhere: \(\Box (p \wedge 
eg p)\) . Formulas that cannot be true everywhere The following two formulas are not universally valid. \(\qquad p \wedge 
eg p\) \(\qquad p \wedge \lozenge 
eg p\) The first formula is never going to be true at any state at all, however you choose a valuation for any frame. The second one is never going to be true at all states in any situation. Can you explain why this must be so for \(p \wedge \lozenge 
eg p\) ? Can you find a simple example of a frame and a valuation where \(p \wedge \lozenge 
eg p\) holds at some states but not others? Can you find an example of a frame where \(p \wedge \lozenge 
eg p\) is always false at all states under all possible valuations? Formulas that are valid in some but not all frames For each of the following formulas you should be able to: find examples of particular frames where the formula is valid. That means true at every state and whatever the valuation is, and find examples of frames which do not have all those properties. In each case drawing simple examples can be helpful. \(\qquad \lozenge \lozenge p 	o \lozenge p\) \(\qquad p 	o \lozenge p\) \(\qquad \Box p\) 3.5 Comparison with first order logic In some cases modal logic and first order logic are just different ways of doing the same thing. In other cases there are significant differences in what can be represented in each. If we have a first order language with unary predicates \(\mathit{Red}\,\) and \(\mathit{Green}\) then in any interpretation we can say that certain formulas represent given statements. Here are some examples: Something is red: \(\qquad \exists x \; \mathit{Red}(x)\) . Everything is green: \(\qquad  orall x\; \mathit{Green}(x)\) . Everything is either red or green: \(\qquad  orall x\; (\mathit{Green}(x) \vee \mathit{Red}(x))\) More than one thing is red: \(\qquad \exists x \exists y \; (\mathit{Red}(x) \wedge \mathit{Red}(y) \wedge 
eg (x = y))\) . With an additional binary predicate \(\mathit{atLeastAsBig}\) we can express things like: Any red thing is at least as big as all the green things: \(\qquad  orall x  orall y\;((\mathit{Green}(x) \wedge \mathit{Red}(y)) 	o \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(y,x))\) 'At least as big as' has the transitive property: \(\qquad  orall x  orall y  orall z\;((\mathit{atLeastAsBig}(x,y) \wedge \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(y,z)) 	o \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(x,z))\) Everything is at least as big as itself: \(\qquad  orall x\; \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(x,x)\) To model similar things in a modal logic we can think of the states (or worlds) as the individual things. Instead of the first order unary predicate \(\mathit{Red}\,\) we can use a proposition \(\mathit{Red}\) . If this proposition holds at a particular state we can think of the state proclaiming "I am red". Similarly for being green. The idea that everything is green would correspond to the formula \(\mathit{Green}\) being true at every state. But there is no formula in modal logic that will be true in an arbitrary interpretation if and only if there are two different red things. This is because in the modal logic we have described there is no way to talk explicitly about the states themselves. In first order logic, on the other hand, we have variables and constants that allow us to talk about the individuals in the domain of any interpretation. If we can make assumptions about the accessibility relation then more can be expressible. For example, suppose we know the relation \(R\) in a frame \((W,R)\) is the universal relation, \(R = W 	imes W\) , or everything is related to everything. In this case the statement "something is red" would correspond to \(\lozenge \mathit{Red}\) being valid in that particular frame. However, we cannot write a formula in modal logic that forces the accessibility relation to be the universal relation. Modal logic is quite limited with respect to what can be said at the level of a particular interpretation. That is the problem of: Given a frame and a valuation can you find a formula that is valid in that interpretation if and only if the frame has certain properties? It is known, for example, that you cannot write a formula that detects (by being valid or not) whether a given interpretation has a reflexive accessibility relation. On the other hand, if the valuation is not fixed, so we have a frame but not an interpretation, there is a simple formula which is valid under all valuations for that frame if and only if the frame has a reflexive accessibility relation. One formula that does this is \(p 	o \lozenge p\) . In a similar way to validity in a frame under all valuations being able to express reflexivity, other properties such as being transitive can be handled as well. It is known that this formula \(\qquad \qquad \lozenge \lozenge p 	o \lozenge p\) is valid under all interpretations in a given frame if and only if the accessibility relation \(R\) is transitive. However, it is possible to find a particular valuation for a frame where \(R\) is not transitive but the formula holds at all states in that valuation. This can be done with only three states. Going back to the example of \(\mathit{atLeastAsBig}\) above: In first order logic: we have a formula that is true in a given interpretation if the 'at least as big' relation is reflexive: \( orall x\; \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(x,x)\) In modal logic: we can model the 'at least as big' relation as the accessibility relation, but it is impossible to write a formula that is true at all states in a given interpretation if the 'at least as big' relation is reflexive. Modal logic is still valuable in knowledge representation, but it is important to appreciate that there are a lot of subtleties when talking about the ability of different logics to express different kinds of statements. 3.6 More than one accessibility relation Beyond the most basic modal logic you may have several different diamonds \(\langle i angle\) (and their dual boxes \([ i ]\) ). The semantics then involves several accessibility relations on the same set of 'worlds'. There are two cases where this is important in this module: 1) This is another perspective on description logic. In fact description logic is really just a different notation for a modal logic of this kind, as indicated by these comparisons. \[  egin{array}{ccc} &&\ \langle \mathsf{hasChild} angle \, \mathsf{UnderTen} & \qquad & \exists \,\mathsf{hasChild} . \mathsf{UnderTen}\[2ex] [ \mathsf{hasChild} ] \, \mathsf{UnderTen} & \qquad &  orall\, \mathsf{hasChild} . \mathsf{UnderTen}\end{array} \] 2) In modelling time where you can have one relation relating a time to all future times, and another relation relating a time to all past times. We will explore this example further in the final unit.
docs.title: Lesson 3 Modal logic

docs.text: \(\def\HA{\mathit{HoldsAt}}\) \(\def\Past{\mathop {\mathbf{P}}}\) \(\def\Future{\mathop {\mathbf{F}}}\) \(\def\Going{\mathop {\mathbf{G}}}\) \(\def\HasBeen{\mathop {\mathbf{H}}}\) \(\def\mBox{\mathop{\Box}}\) \(\def\mDiamond{\mathop{\Diamond}}\) \(\def\leq{\leqslant}\) \(\def\geq{\geqslant}\) \(\def\phi{\varphi}\) Learning outcomes: After completing this lesson you should be able to: Construct simple examples of frames and valuations for propositional modal logic. Evaluate the truth of modal logic formulas in a given interpretation. Explain how some expressions in description logic are essentially formulas in modal logic. Write first order formulas that correspond to modal logic formulas in some special cases where this is possible.
docs.title: Modal logic

docs.text: In this lesson, we will study propositional modal logic . There are various kinds of first order modal logics, but the propositional case is the most relevant here. Although it is called propositional it is capable of expressing some things that are not possible in ordinary propositional logic, and in some ways even beyond first order logic. Description logics are one example of modal logics being used in knowledge representation. Another example, we meet later in this unit are tense logics. These are logics for expressing things like: In the future there will be pelicans in the park. At every future time, the values of \(x\) and \(y\) in the program execution will be equal. At some point in the past people lived here and there was a canal but not a railway. We start with a simple form of modal logic that captures the main idea, but which is too simple to fit the way that description logics are examples of modal logic.
docs.title: 3.1 Syntax of modal logic

docs.text: A language for propositional modal logic consists of: Propositional variables, that stand for atomic propositions just as in ordinary propositional logic Logical connectives as before: \(\quad \neg \quad \vee \quad \wedge \quad \to \quad \leftrightarrow\) Two additional logical symbols: \(\Box\) , called box , and \(\lozenge\) , called diamond . The usual parentheses to avoid ambiguity.
docs.title: 3.1.1 The language

docs.text: The formulas of modal logic are expressions that can be true or false. Unlike first order logic there are no terms in the language that can refer to individual things. The rules for construction of formulas are: Every atomic proposition is a formula If \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are formulas then the following are all formulas: \[\neg \alpha \qquad \alpha \vee \beta \qquad \alpha \wedge \beta \qquad \alpha \to \beta \qquad \alpha \leftrightarrow \beta \qquad \Box \alpha \qquad \lozenge \alpha\] Parentheses can be added as necessary. The precedence rules follow those of first order logic with the box and diamond having the same precedence that the quantifiers have there.
docs.title: 3.1.2 The formulas

docs.text:
If \(A\) , \(B\) , \(C\) are atomic propositions then the following are all examples of formulas in propositional modal logic: \[  egin{array}{l} A\[2ex] 
eg B\ (A 	o B) \leftrightarrow C\[2ex] \Box A\[2ex] \lozenge B\[2ex] (\Box A) \vee 
eg A\[2ex] \Box (A \vee 
eg A)\[2ex] (\Box \lozenge \Box \lozenge 
eg A) \wedge ((\Box \Box B) 	o \lozenge (B \vee \Box C)) \end{array} \]
docs.title: 3.1.2.1 Examples

docs.title: 3.2.2 Formal definition of semantics

docs.text: In ordinary propositional logic, we said a formula \(\phi\) was satisfiable if it was true under some valuation, and valid if it was true under all valuations. In propositional modal logic, a formula \(\phi\) can be: True at a particular world in a frame \(F\) under valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) . True at all worlds in a frame \(F\) under valuation \(\mathcal{V}\) ( satisfied in \(F\) by \(\mathcal{V}\) ). True at all worlds in a frame \(F\) under all valuations ( valid in \(F\) ). Valid in all frames ( universally valid ). In propositional modal logic, the notion of satisfiable (able to be true) for a formula \(\phi\) could be confusing. In the above terminology able to be true and able to be satisfied are used in different ways. This means we may need to be careful to distinguish these cases.

The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. You can see that: There are three people who are grandparents at the top. There are four people who are parents but not grandparents. There are five people who are children but not parents at the bottom. The diagram provides a frame in which we are going to interpret formulas. Let's start with a single proposition \(p\) and give a valuation by drawing circles around the states where \(p\) is true. Look at the image below. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement my age is less than 10 is true. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it have arrows pointing down to a circle that is circled. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. The circle on the left of these is circled. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles, both of which are themselves circled. In the image above there are four states where \(p\) is true. To make it more concrete, let's say \(p\) is modelling the statement: My age is less than 10. You can see how the truth of such a statement depends on who is saying it. That is, it is true in some states, or at some worlds, but not at others. What we do next is see how we can use modal logic to build formulas that model statements more complex than just the speaker being under 10 years old. Our first example is: I have a child whose age is less than 10. We can model this by \(\lozenge p\) , and the next image shows where \(\lozenge p\) holds. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement I have a child whose age is less than 10 is true. In this diagram all the parent circles are circled. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it have arrows pointing down to a child circle. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Now what does \(\Box p\) look like? This can be thought of as modelling: All of my children are under 10. But this can feel odd because the semantics of \(\Box\) means that \(\Box p\) will be true for anyone who has no children at all. So you may feel that it's better to say it is modelling: Either I have no children at all, or all the ones I do have are under 10. If we really wanted to model this: I have children and they are all under 10. We could use \((\lozenge p) \wedge \Box p\) . But you have to remember that this is saying they have at least one child, not necessarily more than one. If all we wanted to say was "I have children" we would need \(\lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) . In terms of the pictures this is true wherever at least one arrow is leaving the state. The states where \(\Box p\) is true are shown in the next image. You should be able to draw a different picture for \((\lozenge p) \wedge \Box p\) yourself. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement Either I have no children at all, or all the ones I do have are under 10 is true. In this diagram all the child circles and three of the parents circles are themselves circled. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it are both themselves circled and have arrows pointing down to a child circle. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle which is itself circled. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. You can see, following the discussion after the previous picture, that we can model related statements about grandchildren. I have some (at least one) grandchildren: \(\lozenge \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) I have grandchildren and they are all under 10: \((\lozenge \lozenge p) \wedge \Box \Box p\) . If we look at \(\Box \Box p\) we get this next image. It is modelling the following statement: Either I have no grandchildren at all, or all the ones I do have are under 10. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement Either I have no grandchildren at all, or all the ones I do have are under 10 is true. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This is the only circle in this diagram which is not itself circled. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it are both themselves circled and have arrows pointing down to a child circle. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle which is itself circled. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. For a final example, consider the formula \(\lozenge (p \vee 
eg p) 	o \lozenge p\) . Because of the properties of \(	o\) this formula holds: for any person with no children, and for any person who has a child under 10. We can interpret it as modelling: if I have a child then I have a child who is under 10. Select for full text description of diagram. In this diagram circles model individual people and arrows the parental relationship between them. The circles at the top of the diagram model grandparents, the circles in the middle model parents and the circles at the bottom model children. Circles which are themselves circled indicate that the statement any person with no children, or any person who has a child under 10 is true. In this diagram all the parent and child circles are circled. At the top of the diagram on the left-hand side is a grandparent circle. This is the only circle in this diagram which is not itself circled. This circle has two downward arrows pointing at parent circles below. To the left of this is another parent circle that doesn’t have a grandparent. This circle and the one next to it are both themselves circled and have arrows pointing down to a child circle. The third parent circle in the middle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles. Back to the top of the diagram now on the right-hand side there are two grandparent circles. These each have an arrow pointing downwards to the same parent circle which is itself circled. This parent circle has two arrows pointing downwards to two separate child circles.
docs.title: 3.3 Example (parents, children and grandchildren)

docs.text:
Universally valid formulas These formulas are all valid in any frame, whatever the accessibility relation, and whatever the valuations for the propositional variables. \(\qquad p \vee 
eg p\) \(\qquad \lozenge \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p) 	o \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) \(\qquad \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p) \vee \Box (p \wedge 
eg p)\) \(\qquad \Box (p 	o q) 	o (\Box p 	o \Box q)\) To make sure you understand how modal logic works you should see if you can explain why each one has to be valid in all situations. You may find it helpful to think in terms of pictures with arrows like the ones drawn above. In pictorial terms we can understand when certain formulas would be true at a particular state. Taking one step is to be understood as moving along one arrow in the accessibility relation. The accessibility relation is allowed to have loops, so taking one step might not lead anywhere new. From here you can take one step: \(\lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) . From here you can take two steps: \(\lozenge \lozenge (p \vee 
eg p)\) . From here you cannot go anywhere: \(\Box (p \wedge 
eg p)\) . Formulas that cannot be true everywhere The following two formulas are not universally valid. \(\qquad p \wedge 
eg p\) \(\qquad p \wedge \lozenge 
eg p\) The first formula is never going to be true at any state at all, however you choose a valuation for any frame. The second one is never going to be true at all states in any situation. Can you explain why this must be so for \(p \wedge \lozenge 
eg p\) ? Can you find a simple example of a frame and a valuation where \(p \wedge \lozenge 
eg p\) holds at some states but not others? Can you find an example of a frame where \(p \wedge \lozenge 
eg p\) is always false at all states under all possible valuations? Formulas that are valid in some but not all frames For each of the following formulas you should be able to: find examples of particular frames where the formula is valid. That means true at every state and whatever the valuation is, and find examples of frames which do not have all those properties. In each case drawing simple examples can be helpful. \(\qquad \lozenge \lozenge p 	o \lozenge p\) \(\qquad p 	o \lozenge p\) \(\qquad \Box p\)
docs.title: 3.4 Exercises

docs.text: In some cases modal logic and first order logic are just different ways of doing the same thing. In other cases there are significant differences in what can be represented in each. If we have a first order language with unary predicates \(\mathit{Red}\,\) and \(\mathit{Green}\) then in any interpretation we can say that certain formulas represent given statements. Here are some examples: Something is red: \(\qquad \exists x \; \mathit{Red}(x)\) . Everything is green: \(\qquad \forall x\; \mathit{Green}(x)\) . Everything is either red or green: \(\qquad \forall x\; (\mathit{Green}(x) \vee \mathit{Red}(x))\) More than one thing is red: \(\qquad \exists x \exists y \; (\mathit{Red}(x) \wedge \mathit{Red}(y) \wedge \neg (x = y))\) . With an additional binary predicate \(\mathit{atLeastAsBig}\) we can express things like: Any red thing is at least as big as all the green things: \(\qquad \forall x \forall y\;((\mathit{Green}(x) \wedge \mathit{Red}(y)) \to \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(y,x))\) 'At least as big as' has the transitive property: \(\qquad \forall x \forall y \forall z\;((\mathit{atLeastAsBig}(x,y) \wedge \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(y,z)) \to \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(x,z))\) Everything is at least as big as itself: \(\qquad \forall x\; \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(x,x)\) To model similar things in a modal logic we can think of the states (or worlds) as the individual things. Instead of the first order unary predicate \(\mathit{Red}\,\) we can use a proposition \(\mathit{Red}\) . If this proposition holds at a particular state we can think of the state proclaiming "I am red". Similarly for being green. The idea that everything is green would correspond to the formula \(\mathit{Green}\) being true at every state. But there is no formula in modal logic that will be true in an arbitrary interpretation if and only if there are two different red things. This is because in the modal logic we have described there is no way to talk explicitly about the states themselves. In first order logic, on the other hand, we have variables and constants that allow us to talk about the individuals in the domain of any interpretation. If we can make assumptions about the accessibility relation then more can be expressible. For example, suppose we know the relation \(R\) in a frame \((W,R)\) is the universal relation, \(R = W \times W\) , or everything is related to everything. In this case the statement "something is red" would correspond to \(\lozenge \mathit{Red}\) being valid in that particular frame. However, we cannot write a formula in modal logic that forces the accessibility relation to be the universal relation. Modal logic is quite limited with respect to what can be said at the level of a particular interpretation. That is the problem of: Given a frame and a valuation can you find a formula that is valid in that interpretation if and only if the frame has certain properties? It is known, for example, that you cannot write a formula that detects (by being valid or not) whether a given interpretation has a reflexive accessibility relation. On the other hand, if the valuation is not fixed, so we have a frame but not an interpretation, there is a simple formula which is valid under all valuations for that frame if and only if the frame has a reflexive accessibility relation. One formula that does this is \(p \to \lozenge p\) . In a similar way to validity in a frame under all valuations being able to express reflexivity, other properties such as being transitive can be handled as well. It is known that this formula \(\qquad \qquad \lozenge \lozenge p \to \lozenge p\) is valid under all interpretations in a given frame if and only if the accessibility relation \(R\) is transitive. However, it is possible to find a particular valuation for a frame where \(R\) is not transitive but the formula holds at all states in that valuation. This can be done with only three states. Going back to the example of \(\mathit{atLeastAsBig}\) above: In first order logic: we have a formula that is true in a given interpretation if the 'at least as big' relation is reflexive: \(\forall x\; \mathit{atLeastAsBig}(x,x)\) In modal logic: we can model the 'at least as big' relation as the accessibility relation, but it is impossible to write a formula that is true at all states in a given interpretation if the 'at least as big' relation is reflexive. Modal logic is still valuable in knowledge representation, but it is important to appreciate that there are a lot of subtleties when talking about the ability of different logics to express different kinds of statements.
docs.title: 3.5 Comparison with first order logic

docs.text: Beyond the most basic modal logic you may have several different diamonds \(\langle i \rangle\) (and their dual boxes \([ i ]\) ). The semantics then involves several accessibility relations on the same set of 'worlds'. There are two cases where this is important in this module: 1) This is another perspective on description logic. In fact description logic is really just a different notation for a modal logic of this kind, as indicated by these comparisons. \[ \begin{array}{ccc} &&\\ \langle \mathsf{hasChild} \rangle \, \mathsf{UnderTen} & \qquad & \exists \,\mathsf{hasChild} . \mathsf{UnderTen}\\[2ex] [ \mathsf{hasChild} ] \, \mathsf{UnderTen} & \qquad & \forall\, \mathsf{hasChild} . \mathsf{UnderTen}\end{array} \] 2) In modelling time where you can have one relation relating a time to all future times, and another relation relating a time to all past times. We will explore this example further in the final unit.
docs.title: 3.6 More than one accessibility relation

docs.text: About Unit 6 Learning Outcomes After completing this unit you should be able to: Use more advanced Prolog constructs. Build your own implementation to evaluate modal logic formulas. See how tense logic works as a special case of modal logic. See how Prolog terms can be manipulated in Prolog to check the syntax of formulas in your implementation. Overview This unit provides experience in writing Prolog and also in understanding some of the concepts around relations and modal logic. It should help develop your understanding of description logic and will introduce you to tense logic. The unit will guide you through a series of stages in which you are given a number of tasks. In order to help you do these tasks, some basic Prolog programming is introduced first.

It should help develop your understanding of description logic and will introduce you to tense logic. The unit will guide you through a series of stages in which you are given a number of tasks. docs.title: Overview

docs.text: 1. Prolog ideas you will need We have not seen much Prolog so far. The examples here will help you with tasks later in the unit, but if you need further guidance, there is documentation online that can help you. Remember that names starting with a capital letter are always variables. 1.1 Comma in body of clause To say: If X is green and X is a monster then X is a green monster. we can write this clause: greenMonster(X) :- green(X), monster(X). You should test it works by adding suitable facts like: green(thing1). green(thing2). green(thing3). monster(thing2). monster(thing3). monster(thing4). That means you add those to the database of facts and clauses. If you are using SWISH Prolog this will be on the left-hand side. You can run queries such as these (one at a time) to see what happens. greenMonster(thing2). greenMonster(thing4). greenMonster(thing42). greenMonster(M). In the last case you can see more than one answer by selecting 'next' in SWISH. 1.2 Multiple clauses for same predicate We often need to write multiple clauses with the same head and different bodies. To say: Things which are green are dangerous and things that are monsters are dangerous we can put: dangerous(X) :- green(X). dangerous(X) :- monster(X). Try this out with the facts you added in 1.1 above. 1.3 Getting output Sometimes writing things out is helpful, especially in debugging code. Try out the following and then try altering it so that it prints something different. greenMonWrite(X) :- write(X), writeln(" is a green monster"). testMon(X) :- greenMonster(X), greenMonWrite(X). 1.4 The built-in predicates findall and foreach To write out all the green monsters the foreach built-in predicate is valuable: foreach(greenMonster(M), greenMonWrite(M)). Alternatively you can define: writeAllTheGreenMonsters :- foreach(greenMonster(M), greenMonWrite(M)). Do something similar with finding all the dangerous things as defined in 1.2 and writing them out. You will get some twice because there is more than one reason why some are dangerous. Instead of writing out all of the dangerous things you can do this to get a list of them: findall(X, dangerous(X),ListOfThem). You will find: ListOfThem = [thing1, thing2, thing3, thing2, thing3, thing4] 1.5 List processing Prolog can deal with lists, which are always enclosed in square brackets and with the elements separated by commas. Lists can contain other lists as elements, and the empty list, [ ], is often seen, especially as the base case in recursively defined predicates. What if we do not want the duplicates in the list of all dangerous things? removeDups([], []). removeDups([FirstOne | Rest ], RestDone) :- member(FirstOne, Rest), removeDups(Rest, RestDone). removeDups([FirstOne | Rest ], [FirstOne | RestDone]) :- removeDups(Rest, RestDone). This is a very common structure, processing one list into another. The built in predicate member tests to see if something is a member of a list. A list with one or more elements will match a pattern [H|T] by taking: H to be the first element of the list. T to be the list (possibly empty) that results by removing H from the list. Try these goals to see what happens: [H|T] = [1,2,3]. [H|T] = [1]. [H|T] = [ ]. [H|T] = [[1],2,3]. [H|T] = [1,[]]. [H|T] = [[ ]]. [H|[H2|TT]] = [1,2,3]. [H|[H2|[TT]]] = [1,2,3]. 1.6 Anonymous variables Suppose we have a list of just two things and we want to know if there is a dangerous thing there. One way is simply: beware([X,Y]) :- dangerous(X). beware([X,Y]) :- dangerous(Y). This works (try it to see) but produces a warning, and it is better to write beware([X,_]) :- dangerous(X). beware([_,Y]) :- dangerous(Y). with an anonymous variable , the underscore. Beware that if the underscore occurs several times in a single clause or goal, they can all be successfully matched with different things. 1.7 Using the cut, !, to prevent backtracking The pattern in the following includes the exclamation mark ! which is called cut . This means that if X is green then the second clause is never tried when backtracking occurs: checkGreen(X) :- green(X), !. checkGreen(X) :- write("Warning: "), write(X), writeln(" is not green"), fail. You can experience the effect of the cut by trying this goal checkGreen(thing1),monster(thing1). with two scenarios: with and without the ! in the first clause. 1.8 Equals If you try this as a goal X = a. it succeeds with X instantiated to a. You can use this to unify expressions, but Prolog does not check for cyclic cases. The following succeeds but can be confusing: X = p(X). The following is easy to understand; try it as a goal. p(a,Y) = p(X,b). If you try this as a goal X == a. it fails because the Prolog terms are not the same.
docs.title: Lesson 1 Prolog ideas you will need

docs.text: 2. Modelling modal logic in Prolog In this lesson you will write a Prolog program that is capable of finding whether a given expression is true at a state in a given interpretation. The description logic we saw in unit 4 is a special kind of modal logic, and we will see how to model time later in this unit. 2.1 Setting up states and relations We start with four states: state(fido). state(rover). state(felix). state(wally). We can have a language with propositions that hold for some states: holds(dog, fido). holds(dog, rover). holds(cat, felix). holds(cat, wally). The next ingredient we need is some relations between the states. We can do this: rel(chases, felix, fido). rel(chases, fido, rover). rel(likes, wally, fido). rel(older, wally, fido). These mean that the chases relation holds between Felix and Rover in that order. If we drew a picture there could be an arrow labelled "chases" going from Felix to Fido. So far we have set up a structure which can be seen as an interpretation for a modal logic language in which there are: Propositions: \(\mathit{dog}\) , and \(\mathit{cat}\) . The usual logical connectives \(\vee\) , \(\wedge\) , \(\neg\) , \(\to\) . Boxes and diamonds: \([\mathit{chases}]\) , \([\mathit{likes}]\) , \([\mathit{older}]\) \(\langle \mathit{chases} \rangle\) , \(\langle \mathit{likes} \rangle\) , \(\langle \mathit{older} \rangle\) . We are giving a valuation in which: \(\mathit{dog}\) is true at the states fido and rover , and in which \(\mathit{cat}\) is true at the states felix and wally . In our interpretation we have provided three actual binary relations on the set of all states. These are going to be needed to interpret the modal operators. To test the pieces that you are going to write, you may need to add to or change the states, propositions and relations as you experiment. 2.2 The propositional connectives Your task here is to write clauses so that the holds predicate will handle expressions involving the logical connectives. The clauses must be able to deal with complex logical expressions such as implies( and( not(implies(dog,not(cat))), or(not(dog), or(bird,hippo))), thing) but of course you need to work out each connective separately with one or more clauses for the holds predicate. The following goals should all succeed: holds( and(dog, not(cat)), fido). holds( or(cat, dog), wally). holds( not(dog), felix). holds( implies(dog, cat), wally). The following should all fail: holds( and(dog, cat), fido). holds( or(not(cat), dog), wally). holds( not(not(dog)), felix). holds( implies(cat, dog), wally). To test thoroughly you may need to introduce some more atomic propositions and give the states where they hold. You may also need to introduce more states. Make sure all the connectives work exactly as the truth tables say they should. Your definitions must work whatever the states and atomic propositions are. 2.3 Holding everywhere or validity Write predicates holdsEverywhere(Formula) holdsSomewhere(Formula) which succeed if and only if the formula is true at all states or at some state respectively. You should find that these succeed: holdsEverywhere( or(cat, not(cat)) ). holdsSomewhere( cat ). 2.4 Adding the diamond and the box Now extend the language so we can have formulas like these: diamond( Relation, Formula) box( Relation, Formula) You need to extend the holds predicate so that these succeed: holds( diamond( chases, dog ), felix). holds( box( older, dog ), wally). These say that Felix chases some dog. Wally is only older than dogs. are both true. Beware that this is saying they are true in this particular interpretation. This is quite different from saying they must be true in all interpretations, which would be the effect of stating these as assertions in description logic. To test thoroughly you will need to add some more states, propositions and relations. Don't forget nesting is allowed, so your rules have to work correctly with formulas like diamond( chases, box( older, dog ) ) and possibly including all the earlier connectives. 2.5 Operations on relations So far we have used only the explicitly given relations. We have met operations of inverse and composition of roles in description logic. So we extend the language to allow not just relation names but also these expressions: compose(Rel1, Rel2) inverse(Rel) You need to write clauses for the rel predicate to make this work. The following goals would succeed (given the initially stated facts): rel(compose(likes, chases), wally, rover). rel(inverse(likes), fido, wally). These would be saying: Wally likes someone who chases Rover. Fido is liked by Wally. Again, be careful that what we are doing is describing a particular interpretation. We are not setting out axioms that are required to hold in all cases. You should be able to include further operations on relations so that expressions can include: union(Rel1, Rel2) intersect(Rel1, Rel2) not(Rel) To be clear what these mean: Two things are related in the union of relations if those two things are related in at least one of the relations. Two things are related in the union of relations if those two things are related in both relations. Two things are related in the negation of a relation, \(R\) , if those two things are not related in \(R\) . Make sure you test the operations on relations carefully with suitable examples, and check they work when you use them in logical expressions. Goals of the following form should work correctly: holds( diamond( compose(likes,chases), dog), wally). In other words: Wally likes someone who chases a dog. 2.6 Properties of relations In Protégé we saw the significance of the seven characteristics that roles may have. You should now write predicates as follows: reflexive(R) transitive(R) symmetric(R) functional(R) invfun(R) irrefl(R) asymm(R) Each of these should succeed for a relation R just when it has the associated property.
docs.title: Lesson 2 Modelling modal logic in Prolog

docs.text: In this lesson you will write a Prolog program that is capable of finding whether a given expression is true at a state in a given interpretation. The description logic we saw in unit 4 is a special kind of modal logic, and we will see how to model time later in this unit.
docs.title: 2. Modelling modal logic in Prolog

docs.text: We start with four states: state(fido). state(rover). state(felix). state(wally). We can have a language with propositions that hold for some states: holds(dog, fido). holds(dog, rover). holds(cat, felix). holds(cat, wally). The next ingredient we need is some relations between the states. We can do this: rel(chases, felix, fido). rel(chases, fido, rover). rel(likes, wally, fido). rel(older, wally, fido). These mean that the chases relation holds between Felix and Rover in that order. If we drew a picture there could be an arrow labelled "chases" going from Felix to Fido. So far we have set up a structure which can be seen as an interpretation for a modal logic language in which there are: Propositions: \(\mathit{dog}\) , and \(\mathit{cat}\) . The usual logical connectives \(\vee\) , \(\wedge\) , \(\neg\) , \(\to\) . Boxes and diamonds: \([\mathit{chases}]\) , \([\mathit{likes}]\) , \([\mathit{older}]\) \(\langle \mathit{chases} \rangle\) , \(\langle \mathit{likes} \rangle\) , \(\langle \mathit{older} \rangle\) . We are giving a valuation in which: \(\mathit{dog}\) is true at the states fido and rover , and in which \(\mathit{cat}\) is true at the states felix and wally . In our interpretation we have provided three actual binary relations on the set of all states. These are going to be needed to interpret the modal operators. To test the pieces that you are going to write, you may need to add to or change the states, propositions and relations as you experiment.
docs.title: 2.1 Setting up states and relations

docs.text: Your task here is to write clauses so that the holds predicate will handle expressions involving the logical connectives. The clauses must be able to deal with complex logical expressions such as implies( and( not(implies(dog,not(cat))), or(not(dog), or(bird,hippo))), thing) but of course you need to work out each connective separately with one or more clauses for the holds predicate. The following goals should all succeed: holds( and(dog, not(cat)), fido). holds( or(cat, dog), wally). holds( not(dog), felix). holds( implies(dog, cat), wally). The following should all fail: holds( and(dog, cat), fido). holds( or(not(cat), dog), wally). holds( not(not(dog)), felix). holds( implies(cat, dog), wally). To test thoroughly you may need to introduce some more atomic propositions and give the states where they hold. You may also need to introduce more states. Make sure all the connectives work exactly as the truth tables say they should. Your definitions must work whatever the states and atomic propositions are.
docs.title: 2.2 The propositional connectives

docs.text: Write predicates holdsEverywhere(Formula) holdsSomewhere(Formula) which succeed if and only if the formula is true at all states or at some state respectively. You should find that these succeed: holdsEverywhere( or(cat, not(cat)) ). holdsSomewhere( cat ).
docs.title: 2.3 Holding everywhere or validity

docs.text: Now extend the language so we can have formulas like these: diamond( Relation, Formula) box( Relation, Formula) You need to extend the holds predicate so that these succeed: holds( diamond( chases, dog ), felix). holds( box( older, dog ), wally). These say that Felix chases some dog. Wally is only older than dogs. are both true. Beware that this is saying they are true in this particular interpretation. This is quite different from saying they must be true in all interpretations, which would be the effect of stating these as assertions in description logic. To test thoroughly you will need to add some more states, propositions and relations. Don't forget nesting is allowed, so your rules have to work correctly with formulas like diamond( chases, box( older, dog ) ) and possibly including all the earlier connectives.
docs.title: 2.4 Adding the diamond and the box

docs.text: So far we have used only the explicitly given relations. We have met operations of inverse and composition of roles in description logic. So we extend the language to allow not just relation names but also these expressions: compose(Rel1, Rel2) inverse(Rel) You need to write clauses for the rel predicate to make this work. The following goals would succeed (given the initially stated facts): rel(compose(likes, chases), wally, rover). rel(inverse(likes), fido, wally). These would be saying: Wally likes someone who chases Rover. Fido is liked by Wally. Again, be careful that what we are doing is describing a particular interpretation. We are not setting out axioms that are required to hold in all cases. You should be able to include further operations on relations so that expressions can include: union(Rel1, Rel2) intersect(Rel1, Rel2) not(Rel) To be clear what these mean: Two things are related in the union of relations if those two things are related in at least one of the relations. Two things are related in the union of relations if those two things are related in both relations. Two things are related in the negation of a relation, \(R\) , if those two things are not related in \(R\) . Make sure you test the operations on relations carefully with suitable examples, and check they work when you use them in logical expressions. Goals of the following form should work correctly: holds( diamond( compose(likes,chases), dog), wally). In other words: Wally likes someone who chases a dog.
docs.title: 2.5 Operations on relations

docs.text: In Protégé we saw the significance of the seven characteristics that roles may have. You should now write predicates as follows: reflexive(R) transitive(R) symmetric(R) functional(R) invfun(R) irrefl(R) asymm(R) Each of these should succeed for a relation R just when it has the associated property.
docs.title: 2.6 Properties of relations

docs.text: 3. Time In tense logic there four tense operators: \(\mathbf{F} \varphi\) meaning \(\varphi\) is true at some time in the future. \(\mathbf{P} \varphi\) meaning \(\varphi\) is true at some time in the past. \(\mathbf{G} \varphi\) meaning \(\varphi\) is true at every time in the future. \(\mathbf{H} \varphi\) meaning \(\varphi\) is true at every time in the past. We are going to have to use a finite number of times here so we can check all the times. The logic itself is not limited in this way. If we have ten times, we can set up an interpretation of tense logic as follows and use our earlier Prolog predicates to evaluate expressions. state(t0). state(t1). state(t2). state(t3). state(t4). state(t5). state(t6). state(t7). state(t8). state(t9). We can then add a relation from each time to every future time. This would be painful, so we can do this: state(t(X)) :- 0 =< X, X =< 9. rel(before, t(X), t(Y)) :- X < Y. You should now be able to use the earlier box, diamond, and inverse relation ideas to define things to make these work. holds(future(X), S) holds(past(X), S) holds(goingToBe(X), S) holds(hasAlwaysBeen(X), S) To test your definitions and your understanding, how would you express the following in tense logic (using suitable atomic propositions)? I have never been to Iceland. To win the lottery you must have bought a ticket. Drinking alcohol and driving are each legal separately, but not at the same time. Every time I was in Manchester it was raining. Test your formulas by setting up models in which the formulas are true and ones in which they are false. For example you could set up the following for the last example: holds(inManchester, state(t0)). holds(inManchester, state(t1)). holds(inManchester, state(t2)). holds(raining,state(t2)). holds(raining, state(t1)). You should find that your formulation of “Every time I was in Manchester it was raining” does not hold at state t1.
docs.title: Lesson 3 Time

We are going to have to use a finite number of times here so we can check all the times. The logic itself is not limited in this way. If we have ten times, we can set up an interpretation of tense logic as follows and use our earlier Prolog predicates to evaluate expressions. state(t0). state(t1). state(t2). state(t3). state(t4). state(t5). state(t6). state(t7). state(t8). state(t9). We can then add a relation from each time to every future time. This would be painful, so we can do this: state(t(X)) :- 0 =< X, X =< 9. rel(before, t(X), t(Y)) :- X < Y. You should now be able to use the earlier box, diamond, and inverse relation ideas to define things to make these work. holds(future(X), S) holds(past(X), S) holds(goingToBe(X), S) holds(hasAlwaysBeen(X), S) To test your definitions and your understanding, how would you express the following in tense logic (using suitable atomic propositions)? I have never been to Iceland. To win the lottery you must have bought a ticket. Drinking alcohol and driving are each legal separately, but not at the same time. Every time I was in Manchester it was raining. Test your formulas by setting up models in which the formulas are true and ones in which they are false. For example you could set up the following for the last example: holds(inManchester, state(t0)). holds(inManchester, state(t1)). holds(inManchester, state(t2)). holds(raining,state(t2)). holds(raining, state(t1)). You should find that your formulation of “Every time I was in Manchester it was raining” does not hold at state t1.
docs.title: 3. Time

docs.text: 4. Checking the language syntax This lesson may be a bit more challenging, but can be completed without knowing any more Prolog than the ideas introduced earlier and some new ones below. We can try this query: holds(not(hippo), john). This will succeed when there is no state john and no predicate hippo . So we cannot distinguish failure from having the value 'false' and failure from not being a valid expression. Extend your program so that when given a goal such as the above you get a useful error message such as hippo is not a proposition john is not a state but the holds predicate should work as before on acceptable expressions. You can extend this so that it checks the logical elements too. For example it could detect and explain the syntax errors in these: holds(and(cat,dog,cat), wally). holds(nonsense(cat,dog), fido). holds(diamond(cat,cat), wally). holds(fido, dog). You may find the following built-in predicates useful: atom(X). % is X an atom? functor(T, F, A). % succeeds if T is a term, with functor F % having arity A. The case of arity zero is allowed. not(fred(bill), cat) =.. X. % will produce = [not, fred(bill), cat] For Prolog the predicate atom will succeed when its argument is hippo but will fail if it is a variable or a complex term. See these goals for examples to try out: atom(hippo). atom(and(dog, cat)). atom(and). atom(Something).
docs.title: Lesson 4 Checking the language syntax
